A mental or psychological impairment 2. In the case of alcoholism, drug addiction, or other substance abuse, the term disability only applies to a person who: - a. Is recovering or has recovered, AND - b. Is currently free of such abuse

Commanding Officer Defined

For the purpose of this procedure, the Commanding Officer is the precinct, police service area, or transit district commanding officer within whose jurisdiction the incident occurs.

Response Chain

The response chain follows a specific escalation path:

1. UMOS evaluates condition, takes police action, stabilizes
   ↓
2. UMOS determines if possible bias → Requests PATROL SUPERVISOR to respond
   ↓
3. Patrol Supervisor stabilizes, determines if additional personnel needed
   ↓
4. Patrol Supervisor requests CO/DUTY CAPTAIN to respond if possible bias
   ↓
5. Patrol Supervisor notifies DESK OFFICER of incident
   ↓
6. CO/Duty Captain determines if should be referred to Hate Crimes
   ↓
7. If public official involved → confer with Intelligence Division before classifying

Exam Alert: For a possible bias incident reported by another police agency, the Patrol Supervisor shall respond. At the request of the Patrol Supervisor OR a Captain or above from the other agency, the CO/Duty Captain (NYPD) will respond and determine whether the occurrence should be designated as non-bias or referred to the Hate Crime Unit as a possible bias incident.

Commanding Officer / Duty Captain Responsibilities

Upon notification of a potential bias incident, the CO or Duty Captain must:

  1. Determine if occurrence is a possible bias incident that should be referred to the Hate Crime Unit for further investigation
  2. NOTE: When considering incidents involving public officials, confer with the Intelligence Division prior to classifying the occurrence as a possible bias incident

Sergeant Focus: The Intelligence Division consultation for public officials is a new emphasis in the 2025 revision. This is a shall requirement, not discretionary.

Patrol Supervisor Duties (Possible Bias)

  1. Direct that a COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A) be prepared and include whether occurrence was determined to be a possible bias incident

UMOS Duties (Possible Bias)

  1. Take digital photographs on Department issued smartphone for the following, if it is determined to be a possible bias incident:
      1. Criminal Mischief, 3rd Degree (Section 145.05, Penal Law)
      1. Criminal Mischief, 4th Degree (Section 145.00, Penal Law)
      1. Aggravated Harassment, 1st Degree (Section 240.31, Penal Law), and/or
      1. Aggravated Harassment, 2nd Degree (Section 240.30, Penal Law)
  2. Prepare and enter COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET into OMNIFORM System
      1. Upload digital photographs via OMNIFORM Complaints menu and electronically attach to COMPLAINT REPORT (PD313-152)

Desk Officer (Possible Bias)

  1. Ensure digital photographs are uploaded into OMNIFORM System and sign-off COMPLAINT REPORT

If the Occurrence IS Referred to Hate Crime Unit as a Possible Bias Incident

The CO/Duty Captain must:

  1. Request additional resources to stabilize the location or defuse the incident (community affairs, crime prevention officers, additional command personnel, training unit, Strategic Response Group, if available, or others, as appropriate)
  2. Request detective squad personnel to respond
  3. Request Evidence Collection Team (ECT) to respond and process scene for evidence
  4. Notify Operations Unit and obtain possible bias incident log number
  5. Prepare UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT (PD370-152) after conferral with precinct detective commander
      1. Subject of Report will be “POSSIBLE BIAS INCIDENT-LOG NO. ___.”
  6. Forward UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT to Chief of Detectives and Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit direct, and forward additional copies through channels
  7. Forward duplicate copies of UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT through channels to:
      1. Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau
      1. Precinct commanding officer
      1. Police service area/transit district commanding officer, as applicable
  8. Direct crime prevention officer, where appropriate, to personally contact and advise complainant concerning actions to take to prevent reoccurrence

Exam Alert: Operations Unit personnel will notify: patrol borough command, Patrol Services Bureau Duty Chief, Housing Bureau or Transit Bureau (as applicable), detective borough commander, Intelligence Division, Commanding Officer Hate Crime Unit, Deputy Commissioner Community Affairs Bureau, and Police Commissioner.

Detective Squad Member (Possible Bias)

  1. Conduct an immediate investigation and confer with Hate Crime Unit personnel
  2. Forward additional copies of COMPLAINT REPORT and OmniForm Complaint Revision (if applicable) to Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit direct within ten days of incident

Exam Alert: The detective forwards to C.O. Hate Crime Unit DIRECT (not through channels) for possible bias incidents.

ECT Personnel

  1. Respond to scene as directed
  2. Process scene for evidence and generate ECT run number
  3. Inform initial responding unit to invoice evidence as Investigatory Evidence

CO/Hate Crime Unit (Determination)

  1. Evaluate situation and make determination after conferral with Chief of Detectives to:
      1. Assume complete control of investigation, OR
      1. Participate jointly with precinct detective squad personnel, OR
      1. Have precinct detective personnel assume full responsibility for the investigation
  2. Visit and personally interview victims of possible bias incident
  3. Prepare and forward comprehensive follow-up report on Typed Letterhead within ten days of incident, including post-incident actions of all Department units, and the current status of investigation to Chief of Detectives and Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit, through channels

Exam Alert: If the victim does not want the perpetrator arrested and there is appropriate legal justification for the arrest, the Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit, may direct that the arrest be made. The primary considerations are the prevention of further violence and the safety of all parties concerned.

Crime Prevention Officer (Possible Bias)

  1. Prepare and forward a report on Typed Letterhead within ten days of incident to Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit, indicating action taken concerning incident and whether a security survey was conducted

Exam Alert: If no Crime Prevention Officer is assigned to the command, the Special Operations Lieutenant prepares and forwards the report per step 28.

CO/Hate Crime Unit (Final Determination)

  1. Make determination of whether an occurrence is a bias incident or a non-bias incident based upon all factors obtained during investigation, after conferral with the Chief of Detectives
  2. Visit and personally interview victims of confirmed bias incidents upon completion of investigation
      1. In appropriate cases, a Hate Crime Unit supervisor may be designated to interview victims
  3. Prepare report on Typed Letterhead indicating determination, and forward through channels to:
      1. Chief of Patrol
      1. Chief of Detectives
      1. Chief of Housing/Chief of Transit, if appropriate
      1. Patrol borough concerned
      1. Housing/transit borough, as applicable
      1. Precinct of occurrence
      1. Police service area/transit district, as applicable

When CO/Duty Captain Determines Incident is NOT Bias Motivated

  1. Notify detective squad
  2. Notify Operations Unit that offense is not a bias incident
      1. Contact Hate Crime Unit, between 0800 hours and 2400 hours, seven days a week, for log number on all non-bias incidents
  3. Direct crime prevention officer to personally contact and advise complainant concerning actions to take to prevent reoccurrence, if offense was committed at a religious institution or a sensitive location
  4. Prepare an UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT and forward to Chief of Detectives and Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit direct
      1. Forward one additional copy of UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT through channels

Precinct Detective (Non-Bias)

  1. Conduct appropriate investigation
  2. Forward copies of COMPLAINT REPORT OmniForm Complaint Revision (if applicable) to Chief of Detectives and Hate Crime Unit, through channels, within ten days of incident

Crime Prevention Officer (Non-Bias)

  1. Prepare and forward a report on Typed Letterhead within ten days of incident to Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit, if offense was committed at a religious institution or sensitive location indicating action taken and whether security survey was conducted

CO of the Command Concerned (NOT Duty Captain)

  • Must PERSONALLY VISIT victims of possible bias incident
  • Within 10 days of incident: prepare comprehensive follow-up Typed Letterhead including post-incident actions and current status of investigation
  • Forward to Chief of Detectives and Commanding Officer, Hate Crime Unit, through channels

Reporting Timeline

  • Reports are due within TEN (10) DAYS of the incident for: CO, Crime Prevention Officer, AND Detective

Bias vs. Non-Bias Comparison Table

Role NON-BIAS POSSIBLE BIAS
C.O. sends report to Chief of Det + HC (direct) + 1 copy through channels Chief of Det + HC (direct) + copies through channels
Crime Prevention Officer sends report to Only if religious institution/sensitive location: C.O., Hate Crime Unit C.O., Hate Crime Unit
Detective forwards 61/OCR to Chief of Detectives AND Hate Crime Unit, through channels C.O., Hate Crime Unit direct
C.O. notifies Squad + call Hate Crimes (0800x2400) for Non-Bias Log # Squad to RESPOND + call Operations for Bias Log #
Detective conducts An APPROPRIATE investigation An IMMEDIATE investigation

Prior Test (Captain’s Exam): When an offense committed against a religious institution is investigated as possible bias but the duty captain determines it is NOT bias motivated, all of the following are true EXCEPT: the precinct detective will forward a copy of the 61 and OCR to C.O., Hate Crimes. – Correct answer is that this is FALSE because for non-bias, the detective forwards to C.O., Hate Crime Unit AND Chief of Detectives, through channels (not just C.O. HCU). The exam takes facts from non-bias and confirmed bias procedures and mixes them up. Know the addressees for each role’s reports.

Exam Alert: Four key testable points: (1) Only the Hate Crime Unit CO determines bias classification, (2) the Precinct CO (NOT Duty Captain) must personally visit victims, (3) the 10-day reporting deadline applies to the CO, Crime Prevention Officer, AND Detective, and (4) for incidents involving public officials, confer with the Intelligence Division before classifying as possible bias.

Penal Law Refresher for Bias Crimes

Charge Elements
Criminal Mischief 4th (PL 145.00) Intentional damage up to $250 (reckless damage of $250 is NOT a crime)
Criminal Mischief 3rd (PL 145.05) Intentional damage above $250 up to and including $1,500; above $1,500 is 2nd degree
Aggravated Harassment 2nd (PL 240.30) Kick, push, etc. for reasons of race, etc. (usually would be Agg Hass 3, a violation, but done for bias = Misdemeanor)
Aggravated Harassment 1st (PL 240.31) Damage a religious place more than $50; burn a cross in public view; draw a swastika or noose; prior Agg Hass 2 (Sub 3) with prior of 1st degree or Agg Hass 2 Sub 3 in last 10 years

P.G. 207-29: Investigation of Carjackings

Carjacking investigations involve a coordinated response between patrol, the detective squad, and specialized units. Key supervisor responsibilities include:

  • Ensuring immediate notification of the detective squad
  • Coordinating a canvass of the area
  • Broadcasting vehicle and suspect descriptions
  • Ensuring the stolen vehicle alarm is transmitted promptly
  • Coordinating with neighboring commands when the crime occurs near precinct boundaries

P.G. 207-33: Complaints Involving Sex Crimes

(Updated R.O. 43, effective 06/19/25)

4 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical for supervisors.

Definition

For purposes of this procedure, the term sex crime shall include those offenses enumerated in: - Article 130 of the New York State Penal Law - Article 230 of the New York State Penal Law - Article 255 of the New York State Penal Law

UMOS Response

  1. Render reasonable aid and prepare AIDED REPORT, if applicable
      1. Take victim to hospital, if necessary
      1. Encourage necessary medical attention when victim is reluctant
      1. Inform the victim that under NYS Law they are entitled to have a sexual assault advocate present during the interview
  2. Attempt to apprehend perpetrator(s), if present
  3. Establish a crime scene, if necessary
      1. Ensure physical evidence (DNA, fingerprints, discharged shell casings, cell phones, computers, hairs, clothing, masks, etc.) is not contaminated, lost, altered, destroyed, etc.
      1. Comply with P.G. 218-33, Processing Sexual Offense Collection Kits in Sex Offense Cases, if applicable
  4. Request response of patrol supervisor
  5. Notify desk officer, precinct of occurrence

Patrol Supervisor Duties

  1. Respond to location and coordinate patrol response
  2. Promptly report ALL cases involving a sex crime to Special Victims Unit and provide preliminary details
      1. Notify desk officer, PSA/transit district when sex crime incident occurs on housing/transit jurisdiction, as appropriate
  3. Supervise crime scene, if established, pending the arrival of Detective Bureau personnel
      1. Ensure crime scene is safeguarded
      1. Ensure potential physical evidence (DNA, fingerprints, discharged shell casings, cell phones, computers, hairs, clothing, masks, etc.) is not contaminated, lost, altered, destroyed, etc.
  4. Have COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A) prepared, if Special Victims Unit is not responding

Sergeant Focus: The patrol supervisor has dual responsibilities: (1) promptly report to SVU, and (2) safeguard the crime scene pending detective arrival. The supervisor shall ensure evidence preservation – this is mandatory.

Special Victims Unit Response

  1. Notify patrol supervisor, precinct of occurrence and provide investigative unit’s estimated time of arrival to incident
  2. Notify desk officer, precinct of occurrence and provide investigative unit’s estimated time of arrival to incident

Assigned Investigator (SVU/UMOS) Duties

  1. Provide victim with a written notice detailing the name, address and telephone number(s) of the nearest Rape Crisis Center
      1. Document referral in the Details section of COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET
  2. Advise victim of sexual offense(s) as defined in Article 130 and Sections 230.34, 255.25, 255.26, and 255.27 of NYS Penal Law who has relocated, or is planning to relocate, due to safety concerns that they may be eligible to participate in the New York State Address Confidentiality Program to shield their addresses
      1. Refer victim to the NYS Department of State’s website for additional information
  3. Advise victim of sexual offense(s) as defined in Article 130 and Sections 230.34, 230.34-a, 255.25, 255.26, and 255.27 of NYS Penal Law that they will be provided with the New York State Sexual Assault Survivor Bill of Rights and referrals for supportive services when they meet with a member of the Special Victims Division
  4. Enter under Notifications To section on rear of COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET:
      1. Name of member notified at SVU, the SVU Squad assigned, investigator assigned and case number, if available
      1. Include any other investigative unit assigned, investigator assigned and case number, if applicable (e.g., IAB, Crime Scene Unit, etc.)

Hospital Walk-In Sex Crime Complaints (No Prior Police Contact)

  1. CO, Communications Section: Ensure all calls received at 911 involving a hospital walk-in complaint of a sex crime are referred by the 911 dispatcher directly to the Special Victims Unit
      1. Uniformed members assigned to patrol will NOT be directed to respond to the location and interview the complainant/victim
  2. Special Victims Unit: Coordinate the response of SVU investigative squad concerned

Exam Alert: The hospital walk-in procedure is a common test question. Remember: 911 sends it directly to SVU, patrol does NOT respond. The CO of Communications Section ensures this routing.

If Arrest Is Effected by UMOS on Patrol for Any Sex Crime

  1. Arresting Officer: Promptly report details of arrest directly from the scene to the desk officer, precinct of occurrence, in all circumstances, to facilitate immediate notification to the Special Victims Unit
  2. Desk Officer: Promptly report effected arrest for any sex crime to Special Victims Unit
      1. Notify desk officer, PSA/transit district when sex crime incident occurs on housing/transit jurisdiction, as appropriate
  3. SVU: Notify SVU investigative squad concerned, provide details and coordinate response
  4. Assigned Investigator: Advise arresting officer and determine if case enhancement is needed
  5. Arresting Officer: Enter under Notifications To section on rear of COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET:
      1. Name of member notified at SVU, the SVU Squad assigned, investigator assigned and case number, if available
      1. Include any other investigative unit assigned, investigator assigned and case number, if applicable (e.g., IAB, Crime Scene Unit, etc.)

SVU as Central Repository

SVU is responsible for investigating sexual offense patterns and the tracking of all sex crimes. SVU will be the central repository of information and intelligence regarding complaints involving sex crimes.

Exam Alert: Members of the service must contact SVU if assistance is required regarding the proper classification of complaints involving a sex crime. The member shall request to speak to a SVU supervisor. The name of the SVU member who assisted must be placed in the Details section of the COMPLAINT REPORT.

Key Operational Policies

  • Refer the complaint, not the complainant – Under no circumstances will a sex crime complainant be referred or directed to report to another Department unit or facility
  • All members shall have a compassionate and non-judgmental attitude and demeanor toward all complainants of sex crimes
  • Any complaint involving sex related offenses, including secondary offenses that are sex related, requires notification to SVU
  • The precinct detective squad will notify SVU and Police Impersonation Investigation Unit (PIIU) for complaints involving sex crimes and the impersonation of a law enforcement officer
  • SVU will investigate robberies committed by police impersonators which include any sexual assault; PIIU will assist

Victim Rights Summary

Right Details
Sexual Assault Advocate Victim must be informed of right to have advocate present during interview; authorized by NYS Law
Address Confidentiality Victims who relocate due to safety may participate in NYS Address Confidentiality Program
Sexual Assault Survivor Bill of Rights Provided when victim meets with SVU member
Rape Crisis Center Referral Written notice with name, address, and phone of nearest center

Supervisor Quick Reference — Investigation Thresholds

Threshold Triggers
$100,000 Major Case Unit: truck contents, commercial burglary
$10,000 Detective squad referral: burglary in Manhattan
$5,000 Detective squad referral: burglary citywide (except Manhattan)
60+ years old Detective squad referral: robbery victim age
6 months Detective squad referral: same victim re-victimized within this period
Under 11 SVU notification: child abuse victim age
10 days Bias incident report deadline

Section 207 — Missing Persons and Alert Systems

Covers P.G. 207-23, 207-31, 207-39

P.G. 207-23: Missing Persons

(Updated R.O. 121, effective 11/21/25)

7 pages in the Patrol Guide — high priority for the Sergeant exam.

Definition of Missing Person

A person missing from a NYC Residence AND: - 17 and under OR 65 and older, OR - Regardless of age if: - Mentally or physically impaired (cognitively impaired/developmentally disabled, disabled to extent hospitalization may be required, incapable of self-care or clear communication) - Drowning / possible victim of drowning - Suicide / indicated intention of committing suicide - Unique/unusual case - Unaccountable/involuntary disappearance

Exam Alert: A temporary resident (hotel/rooming house) IS considered a missing person. Make a Missing Person Report and direct the complainant to notify local police covering the permanent residence.

No Minimum Time Requirement

There are NO MINIMUM TIME LIMITS before accepting a missing person report. A report must be taken immediately upon request.

Exam Alert: This is one of the most commonly tested points. There is NO waiting period (no 24-hour rule or any other delay). Accept the report immediately.

Who is NOT a Missing Person

  • Non-City Resident – Refer complainant to hometown police (we help if they ask); search if known locations of missing within NYC require it
  • Wanted – For crimes or wanted on a warrant
  • Victim of Crime – Found to be a crime victim; notify D.O. (residence and occurrence if different) and Missing Persons Squad; prepare OCR to close missing report and prepare NEW 61 for the crime with NEW 61#
  • Person 18 or older who left home voluntarily due to domestic, financial, or similar reason

Exam Alert: Never refer a complaint directly to MPS (Missing Persons Squad). The exclusions above are for persons who likely fled voluntarily, but they shall NOT be used as justification for failing to accept a missing report when the totality of circumstances indicates the person may in fact be missing.

Where Missing Person Reports Are Recorded

Missing person reports are recorded at the missing person’s RESIDENT precinct.

Command Responsibility

The Commanding Officer of the residence precinct/PSA is ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE for directing the overall effort to locate the missing person.

Who Can Be a Complainant

The complainant is NOT limited to family members. In addition to family, can be: - Legal or temporary guardian - Representative of Department of Education - Hospital administrator - Roommate, home care attendant - Any person reasonably expected to know if the person is actually missing

UMOS Field Investigation Steps

  1. Interview complainant – Obtain as much background as possible
  2. Description – Clothing worn AND photo (accurate, digital preferred)
  3. Language/Communication – What language the missing usually uses; ability to communicate in English
  4. Missing Person’s Contact Info – Email address and password, social media usernames/passwords, cellphone number (ascertain if it can be tracked; try to access last known location)
  5. Incidents/Disputes – Prior missing incidents (if so, where found); any disputes that may relate to disappearance
  6. Locations – Where last seen, public transportation (get type, route, possible destinations), old residences, school info, locations known to frequent
  7. Associates – Family/friends/contact info; biological parents/closest relative/guardian; ACS caseworkers if applicable
  8. Medical/Psychological – Alzheimer’s/related dementia: check Safe Return Program (MedicAlert + Alzheimer’s Association) for bracelet/necklace; obtain Safe Return ID#; Operation Safe Child info

See Also: PG 202-10 – Community Affairs Officer administers the Safe Return Program, but we do not call the Community Affairs Officer for the information.

UMOS Search Actions

  • Immediately search the building/structure and immediate area
  • Request Patrol Supervisor to respond
  • Request Dispatcher to broadcast description to members on patrol
  • Public Transportation: If missing may be using public transit, have Radio Dispatcher broadcast to Pct/PSA/TD along route AND:
    • Subways: Confer with a SUPERVISOR assigned to Transit Bureau
    • Buses: Confer with a SUPERVISOR assigned to Traffic Management Center
  • Computer checks – Conduct name and address check on Dept smartphone/tablet
  • Notify Desk Officer of details

Patrol Supervisor Duties (Command of Occurrence)

  • Search: Ensure initial search was conducted
  • Consult C.O./X.O./Duty Captain
  • Additional personnel: Level 1 mobilization if C.O./X.O./Duty Captain approves
    • If exigent circumstances and immediate need (kidnapping, etc.): MAY do Level 1 without prior approval, then consult Captain ASAP
  • Transmit Missing Message via Domain Awareness System (DAS) Messenger including: digital photo, full name, DOB, age, gender, physical description (height, weight, eye/hair color, hairstyle, clothes, distinguishing characteristics), physical/mental condition, locations (last seen, residence, frequented means of transportation), Safe Return info if available, and circumstances
  • Search (prior to CO arrival): Request immediate search where missing was last observed AND missing person’s residence (include travel route in search pattern)
  • Maintain records: Search area/routes, premises searched, identities of persons questioned
  • Coordinate search with: PCT/PSA/TD C.O./X.O./Duty Captain, D.O. of Pct of occurrence, D.O.s of residence if different, Operations Unit, additional units as appropriate

Exam Alert: If the missing person is a child 9 years old or younger, the CO/XO/Duty Captain of Pct/PSA/TD of occurrence performs the Patrol Supervisor’s duties (the Captain takes over).

Desk Officer Duties (Command of Occurrence)

  • Check Department records (determine if missing has been subject of police action; query databases for prior law enforcement contacts)
  • Notify:
    • Detective Squad (precinct of occurrence) to respond and assist in search (if no detective available, notify detective borough dispatcher)
    • D.O. of missing person’s resident precinct/PSA (if different from occurrence)
    • Operations Unit
    • If missing is a juvenile, notify Juvenile Desk
    • Missing Persons Squad (if MPS unavailable, contact Real Time Crime Center and request records search)
    • Inform MPS of: ID of member conducting investigation/search (name and rank of patrol supervisor, CO/XO/Duty Capt)

Three Reports Required (ALL Missing Cases)

In ALL missing cases, a Complaint Report AND Missing Report must be made even if the missing is found before the reports are prepared or entered into OMNIFORM/ECMS:

  1. Complaint Report Worksheet (61)
  2. Missing/Unidentified Person Report Worksheet
  3. Juvenile Report System Worksheet (JR) – if missing is at least 7 but less than 16 (comply with PG 215-03 if youth may be missing due to abuse, neglect, maltreatment)
  • Use the same 61# for both the Complaint Report and Missing Person Report
  • Enter both into OMNIFORM under the same number
  • If found prior to making/transmitting reports: JR, Missing, 61 – assigned Pct Det closes case in ECMS with how/where found
  • Reports filed at Pct/PSA concerned

Desk Officer Review (Command of Occurrence vs. Residence)

Role Duty
D.O. Command of Occurrence Review and sign: Complaint Report (also reviewed/finalized by D.O. of Resident Pct), Missing Person’s Report (ensure MPS case # and detective assigned are noted), Juvenile Report (enter Pct/PSA serial # before forwarding)
D.O. Residence Command Immediately review the 61 and finalize within 2 hours of receipt

Exam Alert: The D.O. of the command of occurrence reviews and signs all 3 reports. But the D.O. of the residence command immediately reviews the 61 and must finalize it within 2 hours. Notify MPS and D.O. at Resident Precinct that reports have been completed.

If Missing Not Found by Change of Tour

  • D.O. Pct of Occurrence AND D.O. Pct of Residence: Confer with incoming D.O.s
  • Incoming D.O.s: Ensure details given at R/C to outgoing platoon (Don’t Drop the Hand Off)
  • Incoming CO/XO/Duty Capt and Detective: Direct additional search and determine scope and duration

Missing vs. Unidentified Comparison

Feature 207-23 Missing 216-03 Unidentified
JRSW Less than 16 years old Less than 18 years old
Detective OCR/ECMS 7 working days 3 working days

P.G. 207-31: Silver Alert System

(Updated R.O. 66, effective 08/01/25)

4 pages in the Patrol Guide.

Definition — Vulnerable Senior

A vulnerable senior for Silver Alert purposes is a person who is: - 65 years of age or older with dementia (Alzheimer’s disease or similar condition) - Missing under circumstances indicating imminent danger to their health or safety

Who Can Activate a Silver Alert

ONLY a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may activate a Silver Alert.

No lower rank and no unit outside the Detective Bureau has this authority.

Timing Requirement

A Silver Alert must be issued within 24 HOURS of the determination that the person qualifies for the alert.

Tactical Consideration

The most prudent approach is to activate the Silver Alert AFTER patrol and field resources have been exhausted. The alert system supplements — rather than replaces — immediate search efforts.

Under-65 Exception

A Silver Alert can also be activated for persons under 65 who have dementia, if the Detective Zone Commanding Officer reasonably believes activation will be beneficial to the investigation.

Exam Alert: Key testable points: (1) Captain or above from Detective Bureau activates, (2) 24-hour window, (3) under-65 exception exists with Detective Zone CO approval.

P.G. 207-39: Blue Alert System

(Updated R.O. 115, effective 11/04/25)

Purpose

The Blue Alert System is activated when a law enforcement officer has been: - Killed in the line of duty, OR - Seriously injured in the line of duty, OR - Missing in the line of duty

AND the suspect has not been apprehended.

Activation

The Blue Alert disseminates critical information to the public and law enforcement to assist in locating the suspect. It is reserved for situations where the threat to officer safety is ongoing.

Supervisor Quick Reference — Missing Persons Decision Tree

Person reported missing
├── Is the missing person a NYC resident?
│   ├── NO → Do NOT accept report; direct to local police
│   └── YES → Accept IMMEDIATELY (no time limit)
│       ├── Record at RESIDENT precinct
│       ├── CO of residence precinct is ULTIMATELY responsible
│       └── Do NOT refer complainant to Missing Persons Squad
│
├── Is the complainant a family member?
│   └── Does NOT matter — anyone can be a complainant
│
└── Is this a vulnerable senior (65+ with dementia)?
    ├── YES → Silver Alert eligible
    │   └── Captain+ from Detective Bureau activates within 24 hours
    └── NO, but under-65 with dementia?
        └── Detective Zone CO may authorize Silver Alert if beneficial

Alert Systems Comparison

Feature Silver Alert Blue Alert
Subject Vulnerable senior (65+ with dementia) Law enforcement officer
Trigger Missing under dangerous circumstances Killed, seriously injured, or missing; suspect at large
Who activates Captain+ from Detective Bureau Department-level activation
Time requirement Within 24 hours of determination Immediate upon qualifying event
Under-65 exception Yes, with Detective Zone CO approval N/A

Section 207 — Notification Requirements

Covers P.G. 207-06, 207-24

P.G. 207-06: Notifications

3 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical notification matrix for supervisors.

Desk Officer Notifications to Operations Unit

The Desk Officer must notify the Operations Unit for the following situations:

Situation Additional Notification
Counterfeit U.S. obligations Secret Service
DOH employee crime or impersonation Inspector General, DOH + IAB
Tax official crimes Inspector General, Dept. of Finance + IAB
Postal property or employee crimes Postal Inspector + IAB
Contraband weapons ATF (Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives)
Product tampering New York State Police
Home invasion robbery Detective Bureau Wheel

Direct Notifications (No Operations Unit)

Situation Notify Directly
Child abuse (victim under 18) New York State Child Abuse Register
Police impersonation IAB Command Center
Private carting / wholesale markets / gambling boats Business Integrity Commission (via DB Wheel)

Specialized Crime Notifications

Situation Notify
ATM larceny Grand Larceny Squad (via email)
Skimmer device / financial information recording device Financial Crimes Task Force
Sexual assault of prisoner in custody Criminal Justice Bureau

Exam Alert: The notification matrix is heavily tested. Focus on which situations require Operations Unit notification versus direct notification, and which require IAB involvement (DOH, tax officials, postal matters all require IAB).

IAB Notification Pattern

Notice that three categories require both Operations Unit AND IAB notification: 1. DOH employee/impersonation 2. Tax official crimes 3. Postal property/employee crimes

Police impersonation goes directly to IAB (no Operations Unit).

P.G. 207-24: Notifications from Caseworkers (Adult Protective Services / Dept. for the Aging)

(Updated R.O. 104, effective 10/20/25)

When notifications are received from caseworkers of Adult Protective Services or the Department for the Aging: - The information is documented and appropriate action is taken - These notifications often involve vulnerable adults who may be victims of abuse, neglect, or exploitation - Patrol supervisors ensure that responding officers conduct welfare checks and document findings

Sergeant Focus: These notifications frequently involve elderly or incapacitated persons. Ensure responding members are sensitive to the circumstances and thorough in documentation.

Supervisor Quick Reference — Notification Matrix

Notifications Requiring Operations Unit

Counterfeit U.S. obligations ────────→ Operations Unit + Secret Service
DOH employee/impersonation ──────────→ Operations Unit + IG DOH + IAB
Tax official crimes ─────────────────→ Operations Unit + IG Finance + IAB
Postal property/employee crimes ─────→ Operations Unit + Postal Inspector + IAB
Contraband weapons ──────────────────→ Operations Unit + ATF
Product tampering ───────────────────→ Operations Unit + NYS Police
Home invasion robbery ───────────────→ Operations Unit + DB Wheel

Direct Notifications (Bypass Operations Unit)

Child abuse (under 18) ──────────────→ NYS Child Abuse Register
Police impersonation ────────────────→ IAB Command Center
Private carting/wholesale/gambling ──→ Business Integrity Commission (via DB Wheel)
ATM larceny ─────────────────────────→ Grand Larceny Squad (email)
Skimmer device ──────────────────────→ Financial Crimes Task Force
Sexual assault of prisoner ──────────→ Criminal Justice Bureau

Memory Aid: When Does IAB Get Notified?

Situation IAB?
DOH employee/impersonation YES
Tax official crimes YES
Postal property/employee YES
Police impersonation YES (direct)
All other notification categories NO

Section 207 — Key Terms, Forms, and Abbreviations

Core Complaint Definitions (P.G. 207-01)

Term Definition Source
Complaint Allegation of an unlawful or improper act or omission, OR other condition requiring investigation to determine if an unlawful act or omission occurred P.G. 207-01
Pick-Up Arrest An arrest for an offense or juvenile delinquency NOT previously reported P.G. 207-01
Continuous Event Offense or multiple offenses committed against a complainant; an ongoing situation occurring in different precincts and/or boroughs P.G. 207-01
Incident Event where one or more criminal offenses were committed against one or more victims by one or more offenders P.G. 207-01
Offense A single criminal act within an incident; up to 10 may be recorded per incident P.G. 207-01

Case Status Definitions (P.G. 207-07)

Term Definition Source
Active Case A case with leads still being pursued or investigative steps remaining P.G. 207-07
Closed Case A case where all reasonable investigative steps have been completed P.G. 207-07
Unfounded Case Investigation determines that no offense actually occurred (report was properly prepared) P.G. 207-07

Report Status Distinction (P.G. 207-03)

Term Definition Source
Voided The report should NOT have been prepared in the first place (procedural error) P.G. 207-03
Unfounded The report was properly prepared, but no offense actually occurred (investigative finding) P.G. 207-03, 207-07

Bias and Discrimination Terms (P.G. 207-10, 207-36)

Term Definition Source
Bias Incident An offense motivated by bias based on race, religion, ethnicity, gender, age, disability, or sexual orientation (including LGBTQIA+) P.G. 207-10
Disability A physical, mental, or medical impairment that substantially limits major life activities P.G. 207-36

Alert System Terms (P.G. 207-31, 207-39)

Term Definition Source
Vulnerable Senior A person 65+ with dementia (Alzheimer’s or similar), missing under circumstances indicating imminent danger P.G. 207-31
Silver Alert Alert system for missing vulnerable seniors; may also apply to under-65 with dementia per Detective Zone CO P.G. 207-31
Blue Alert Alert system activated when a law enforcement officer is killed, seriously injured, or missing in the line of duty and the suspect is at large P.G. 207-39

Corruption and Misconduct Terms (P.G. 207-21, 207-22)

Term Definition Source
Corruption/Other Misconduct Illegal or improper conduct by a member of the service; all members have an absolute duty to report P.G. 207-21
Confidential ID Number Number issued to anonymous reporters of corruption; obtaining it satisfies the reporting duty P.G. 207-21

Civilian Complaint Terms (P.G. 207-27, 207-28)

Term Definition Source
Investigating Supervisor The supervisor who investigates a civilian complaint: Platoon Commander, Special Ops Lt., ICO, or CO/Duty Captain P.G. 207-28
Supervisor Reviewing Civilian Complaint Must be at least one rank higher than the member receiving the complaint P.G. 207-28

Complaint Recording Terms (P.G. 207-01)

Term Definition Source
7 Majors Rule Hierarchy for classifying top charge in multi-crime complaints: Murder, Rape 1, Robbery, Felonious Assault, Burglary, Grand Larceny, GLA P.G. 207-01
NIBRS National Incident Based Reporting System; governs how multiple offenses from a single event are recorded (up to 10, hierarchy-based) P.G. 207-01
Rosario Material Complaint Report Worksheets (scratch copies) kept on file as discoverable material; all associated forms attached to the printed OMNI 61 P.G. 207-34
Jurisdiction Code 00 NYPD Patrol jurisdiction P.G. 207-01
Jurisdiction Code 01 Transit jurisdiction P.G. 207-01
Jurisdiction Code 02 Housing jurisdiction P.G. 207-01

Bias Crime Terms (P.G. 207-10)

Term Definition Source
FADO Force Unnecessary, Abuse of Authority, Discourtesy, Offensive Language – the four categories of CCRB complaints P.G. 207-28
Disability (Bias) Physical, medical, mental, or psychological impairment or history/record of such; includes bodily organ/system impairment and recovering addicts (if currently recovering AND not currently abusing) P.G. 207-10
Non-Bias Log # Obtained by calling Hate Crimes (0800x2400) when CO determines incident is NOT bias P.G. 207-10
Bias Log # Obtained by calling Operations when CO determines incident IS possible bias P.G. 207-10

Corruption Reporting Terms (P.G. 207-21, 207-22)

Term Definition Source
MEO 16 of 1978 Mayor’s Executive Order 16 of 1978; PG 207-21 and 207-22 serve as NYPD’s guidelines for reporting corruption per this order P.G. 207-21/22
Minor Misconduct (Non-NYPD) DWI and Domestic Violence by non-NYPD government agents; documented on 61 with copies to IAB (no 49 needed) P.G. 207-22

Department Locker Terms (P.G. 207-19)

Term Definition Source
Theft from Department Locker Includes actual theft AND attempted break-ins; requires IAB notification and log # P.G. 207-19

Missing Person Terms (P.G. 207-23)

Term Definition Source
Safe Return Program MedicAlert + Alzheimer’s Association program; check for bracelet/necklace; obtain Safe Return ID # P.G. 207-23
Operation Safe Child Program for registered children; obtain info from Safe Child Card when available P.G. 207-23
DAS Messenger Domain Awareness System Messenger application; used by Patrol Supervisor to transmit missing person message with photo and details P.G. 207-23

CCRB Terms (P.G. 207-27, 207-28)

Term Definition Source
CCRB # Number issued by CCRB Intake Unit for FADO complaints P.G. 207-28
COD # Chief of Department number; issued for misconduct complaints that are not FADO (e.g., unwarranted traffic summons) P.G. 207-28
CCRB Statistical Summary Sheet Prepared by receiving MOS; complainant does NOT receive a copy P.G. 207-28
Reviewing Supervisor Must be at least one rank above the RECEIVING member (not the investigating member) P.G. 207-28

Immigration-Related Terms (P.G. 207-37)

Term Definition Source
U Visa A visa for victims of qualifying criminal activity who have suffered substantial abuse and are helpful to law enforcement P.G. 207-37
Qualifying Criminal Activity Specified crimes that make a victim eligible for U Visa certification P.G. 207-37
Certifying Official The Department official authorized to sign U Visa certification forms P.G. 207-37

Forms

Form Name Form Number Purpose Reference
Complaint Report Worksheet PD313-152A Initial recording of complaint information P.G. 207-01
Complaint Report PD313-152 Official complaint record P.G. 207-01
Civilian Complaint Form PD313-154 Recording civilian complaints against members P.G. 207-28
Unusual Occurrence Report PD370-152 Documenting unusual occurrences including bias incidents P.G. 207-10
Complaint Index Report — Indexing and cross-referencing complaints P.G. 207-01
Omniform Complaint Revision — Amending/revising existing complaint reports P.G. 207-01

Units and Abbreviations

Abbreviation Full Name Context
SVU Special Victims Unit All sex crimes, child abuse (under 11), human trafficking
IAB Internal Affairs Bureau Corruption, misconduct, police impersonation
ECT Enhanced Crime Team Responds to bias incidents
Hate Crime Unit Hate Crime Unit (under Detective Bureau) Only unit that determines bias classification
Major Case Unit Major Case Unit Bank robberies/burglaries, truck contents >$100K, commercial burglary >$100K, art theft
JVCTF Joint Violent Crimes Task Force Armed bank robberies
Financial Crimes Task Force Financial Crimes Task Force Skimmer devices, financial information recording
Missing Persons Squad Missing Persons Squad Coordinates missing person investigations (complainants NOT referred directly)
ATF Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives Contraband weapons notification
DB Wheel Detective Bureau Wheel Routing mechanism for detective notifications
ICO Integrity Control Officer First in hierarchy for investigating civilian complaints (patrol commands)
SOL Special Operations Lieutenant Second in hierarchy for investigating civilian complaints; prepares bias 49 if no Crime Prevention Officer
HCU Hate Crime Unit Determines whether incident is or is not bias; under Detective Bureau
MPS Missing Persons Squad Coordinates missing person investigations; if unavailable, contact Real Time Crime Center
DAS Domain Awareness System Messenger application used by Patrol Supervisor to transmit missing person messages
ISS Investigative Support Section Receives narcotics offense reports for controlled substances >2 oz found outside buildings
EEOD Equal Employment Opportunity Division Investigates CCRB complaints containing remarks about race, gender, etc.
IRS Investigative Review Section Receives COD# complaint originals from CO of member’s command
CCRB Civilian Complaint Review Board Investigates FADO complaints against uniformed members (NOT civilian members)
DOI Department of Investigations City agency; Commissioner of Investigations referenced in MEO 16 of 1978

Section 207 — Review Questions

20 multiple-choice questions covering key Sergeant exam topics from Section 207.

Question 1

A burglary occurs on a subway train as it travels between two precincts. At which location should the complaint be recorded?

A. The precinct where the train originated B. The next station where the train stops C. The precinct where the victim resides D. The precinct covering the midpoint of the route

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-01, complaints occurring on a moving train are recorded at the NEXT station where the train stops. This is a Transit Bureau jurisdiction rule.

Question 2

A continuous event begins with a kidnapping in the 75th Precinct, moves through the 77th Precinct, and the victim is murdered in the 84th Precinct. Where is the complaint recorded?

A. The 75th Precinct, where the continuous event began B. The 77th Precinct, where the event passed through C. The 84th Precinct, where the homicide occurred D. The precinct of the victim’s residence

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-01, while continuous events are generally recorded at the precinct of the INITIAL offense, homicide is an exception — it is recorded where the homicide occurred (84th Precinct).

Question 3

A continuous event involves a robbery in the 40th Precinct followed by a Rape 1st Degree in the 42nd Precinct. Where is the complaint recorded?

A. The 40th Precinct, where the initial offense occurred B. The 42nd Precinct, where the rape occurred C. Both precincts prepare separate complaint reports D. The Special Victims Unit records the complaint

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-01, Rape 1st Degree is an exception to the continuous event rule. Like homicide, it is recorded where the rape occurred, regardless of where the continuous event began.

Question 4

A complainant reports losing her wallet. She believes she last had it at a restaurant in the 19th Precinct but first realized it was missing when she arrived home in the 23rd Precinct. Where is the complaint recorded?

A. The 19th Precinct, where she last had the wallet B. The 23rd Precinct, where she first discovered the loss C. Either precinct, at the complainant’s choice D. The precinct nearest to the restaurant

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-01, lost property complaints are recorded at the precinct where the complainant FIRST DISCOVERED the loss, which is the 23rd Precinct.

Question 5

A desk officer reviews a complaint report and determines that an identical report was already filed at the same precinct for the same incident. What is the proper disposition?

A. Mark the report as unfounded B. Void the report C. Forward both reports to the detective squad D. Combine both reports into one

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-03, the Desk Officer should void the report. A duplicate report is one of the grounds for voiding — the report should not have been prepared because it already exists. This is distinct from unfounded, which means investigation found no crime occurred.

Question 6

What is the key distinction between a VOIDED complaint report and an UNFOUNDED complaint report?

A. Voided means the crime was not serious enough; unfounded means no crime occurred B. Voided means the report should not have been prepared; unfounded means investigation found no offense occurred C. Voided requires supervisor approval; unfounded requires detective squad approval D. Voided applies to misdemeanors; unfounded applies to felonies

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-03, VOIDED means the report should NOT have been prepared in the first place (e.g., duplicate, wrong precinct). UNFOUNDED means the report was properly prepared, but subsequent investigation determined that no offense actually occurred.

Question 7

A patrol supervisor responds to a commercial burglary in Brooklyn where the loss is estimated at $7,000. What is the proper action regarding detective squad notification?

A. Do not refer to the detective squad; the loss is below the threshold B. Refer to the detective squad; the loss exceeds $5,000 C. Refer to the Major Case Unit; commercial burglary requires their notification D. Refer to the detective squad only if the loss exceeds $10,000

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-07, a burglary with a loss exceeding $5,000 should be referred to the detective squad. The $10,000 threshold applies only to Manhattan. Since this occurred in Brooklyn, the $5,000 threshold applies.

Question 8

A patrol supervisor responds to a commercial burglary in Manhattan where the estimated loss is $8,000. Should this be referred to the detective squad?

A. Yes, the loss exceeds the $5,000 citywide threshold B. No, in Manhattan the threshold is $10,000 C. Yes, all commercial burglaries are referred regardless of loss amount D. No, commercial burglaries are only referred to the Major Case Unit

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-07, the detective squad referral threshold for burglary in Manhattan is $10,000, not $5,000. Since the loss of $8,000 is below the Manhattan threshold, this does not meet the dollar-amount criterion for referral (though other factors such as person present or unique MO could still warrant referral).

Question 9

Which of the following situations requires the patrol supervisor to notify the Major Case Unit?

A. A robbery where the victim is 65 years old B. A truck hijacking where the contents are valued at $80,000 C. A bank burglary D. A burglary where a person was present in the building

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-07, the Major Case Unit must be notified for bank burglary/robbery, truck contents over $100,000, commercial burglary over $100,000, and art theft. A bank burglary qualifies. The truck hijacking at $80,000 is below the $100,000 threshold. The elderly victim robbery and person-present burglary are detective squad referrals, not Major Case Unit.

Question 10

In a bias motivated incident, who makes the final determination as to whether the incident IS or IS NOT a bias incident?

A. The patrol supervisor who responds to the scene B. The Commanding Officer of the precinct where the incident occurred C. The Commanding Officer of the Hate Crime Unit, after conferral with the Chief of Detectives D. The Operations Unit, after reviewing the Unusual Occurrence Report

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-10, ONLY the Commanding Officer of the Hate Crime Unit determines whether an incident is or is not a bias incident, after conferral with the Chief of Detectives. No other rank or unit makes this determination.

Question 11

Upon notification of a potential bias incident, the Commanding Officer of the command concerned must take what specific action regarding the victims?

A. Assign a detective to interview the victims within 48 hours B. Personally visit and interview the victims C. Ensure a patrol supervisor interviews the victims before end of tour D. Direct the Enhanced Crime Team to conduct victim interviews

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-10, the CO of the command concerned must VISIT and PERSONALLY INTERVIEW the victims. This is a mandatory, non-delegable responsibility.

Question 12

Reports related to bias motivated incidents must be submitted within what time frame?

A. 48 hours B. 5 days C. 10 days D. 30 days

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-10, reports related to bias incidents are due within TEN (10) DAYS of the incident.

Question 13

A member of the service witnesses another member engaging in corrupt activity. The witnessing member fears retaliation and does not want to be identified. What satisfies the member’s reporting obligation?

A. Reporting anonymously to the local precinct desk officer B. Documenting the incident in the member’s personal memo book C. Obtaining a Confidential ID Number from the IAB Command Center D. Reporting to a patrol supervisor who will relay the information

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-21, members can report corruption anonymously to the IAB Command Center (24 hours) and receive a Confidential ID Number. Obtaining this number satisfies the member’s reporting responsibility.

Question 14

A member of the service becomes aware of corruption but decides not to report it. What is the consequence?

A. No consequence unless the member personally participated in the corruption B. The member may face administrative counseling C. Failure to report is itself considered serious misconduct D. The member faces consequences only if the corruption is later discovered

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-21, FAILURE TO REPORT corruption or misconduct is itself serious misconduct. All members have an ABSOLUTE DUTY to report. Additionally, conduct to cover up corruption constitutes obstruction of justice.

Question 15

A civilian makes an allegation of misconduct against an officer. The officer’s supervisor responds. What must the supervisor do BEFORE interviewing the accused member?

A. Notify the Commanding Officer and obtain permission to investigate B. Interview the complainant and confer with IAB C. Obtain a written statement from the accused member D. Review the member’s disciplinary history

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-21, when an allegation is made against a member, the supervisor interviews the complainant and confers with IAB BEFORE interviewing the accused member. The sequence is critical.

Question 16

Who is the first choice as the Investigating Supervisor for a civilian complaint under P.G. 207-28?

A. The Integrity Control Officer (ICO) B. The Special Operations Lieutenant C. The Platoon Commander D. The Commanding Officer

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-28, the hierarchy for Investigating Supervisor is: (1) Platoon Commander, (2) Special Operations Lieutenant, (3) ICO. The CO or Duty Captain acts only if all three are unavailable.

Question 17

A woman arrives at the precinct to report her neighbor missing. The neighbor is not a family member. The desk officer should:

A. Accept the report — the complainant need not be a family member B. Refuse the report — only family members can file missing person reports C. Accept the report but note that it is from a non-family member and may be unreliable D. Direct the woman to contact the Missing Persons Squad directly

Answer A. Per P.G. 207-23, the complainant is NOT limited to family members. A guardian, school representative, hospital administrator, roommate, or any other person may report someone missing. Additionally, under NO circumstances should the complainant be referred directly to the Missing Persons Squad.

Question 18

A family calls the precinct at 10 PM to report their elderly father, who has Alzheimer’s disease, as missing. He left the house two hours ago. The desk officer states they must wait 24 hours before filing a report. Is this correct?

A. Yes, missing person reports require a 24-hour waiting period B. Yes, but only for adults — children can be reported immediately C. No, there are no minimum time limits before accepting a missing person report D. No, but only because the person has a medical condition

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-23, there are NO MINIMUM TIME LIMITS before accepting a missing person report. The report must be taken immediately, regardless of the person’s age or medical condition. The 24-hour waiting period is a myth that has no basis in NYPD procedure.

Question 19

Who has the authority to activate a Silver Alert for a missing vulnerable senior?

A. Any patrol supervisor B. The Commanding Officer of the precinct of residence C. A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau D. The Operations Unit upon request from a precinct commander

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-31, ONLY a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may activate a Silver Alert. No lower rank and no unit outside the Detective Bureau has this authority. The alert must be issued within 24 hours of determination.

Question 20

A sex crime victim presents at a hospital emergency room. How should the complaint be handled?

A. Patrol is dispatched to the hospital to take the complaint report B. The precinct detective squad responds to the hospital C. The 911 dispatcher refers the complaint directly to the Special Victims Unit; patrol does NOT respond D. The hospital contacts the precinct of occurrence, which assigns a patrol unit

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-33, when a sex crime victim presents at a hospital (walk-in), the 911 dispatcher refers the complaint DIRECTLY to the Special Victims Unit. Patrol is NOT directed to respond. SVU handles hospital walk-in sex crime complaints from initial response.

Question 21

At 2130 hours, a male perpetrator on a moving A train displays a gun, robs a 13-year-old victim of a wallet, then at the next stop (83rd Precinct) forces the victim into a vehicle. The two perpetrators drive to a housing development in the 75th Precinct and rape the victim at gunpoint. They then drive to a desolate street in the 101st Precinct and shoot the victim in the head. Where should the complaint be recorded and what is the proper jurisdiction code?

A. 83rd Precinct, Transit Jurisdiction code 01 B. 101st Precinct, Patrol Jurisdiction code 00 C. 83rd Precinct, Patrol Jurisdiction 00 D. 75th Precinct, Housing Jurisdiction code 02

Answer D. Per P.G. 207-01, this is a continuous event with a Rape 1st Degree exception. The WHERE to record rule says Rape 1 is recorded where the rape occurred (75th Precinct, on a housing development). Since it occurred on-development, the jurisdiction code is Housing (02). The attempted murder is classified as Felonious Assault (#4 on 7 Majors list) which is outranked by Rape (#2), but the WHERE and HOW rules are applied independently.

Question 22

In a continuous event involving a Kidnapping in the 83rd Precinct, a Rape 1 in the 75th Precinct, and an Attempted Murder in the 101st Precinct, what is the top charge for classification purposes on the complaint report?

A. Attempted Murder 2nd degree B. Kidnapping 1st degree C. Rape 1st Degree D. Robbery 1st degree

Answer D. Per PG 207-01 (7 Majors Rule): Kidnapping is NOT a 7 Major, so it is eliminated. Attempted Murder is classified as Felonious Assault (#4). Rape is #2 and Robbery is #3. Since the scenario also includes a Robbery from the original crime, and Robbery (#3) is below Rape (#2), the classification is Robbery 1st Degree. Remember: the HOW to classify rule is applied independently from the WHERE to record rule.

Question 23

Controlled substances weighing more than 2 ounces are found outside a building. How should this be documented?

A. Complaint Report at the precinct of occurrence with a Pct 61 number B. Complaint Report at the precinct of occurrence only if found under circumstances requiring investigation C. Reported as a narcotics offense to Investigative Support Section; ISS serial number entered on Complaint Worksheet (no Pct 61 number) D. No documentation required for found property outside a building

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-01/207-08, controlled substances over 2 ounces found outside a building are reported as a narcotics offense to the Investigative Support Section, Real Time Crime Center. No Pct 61 number is assigned; the ISS serial number is entered on the Complaint Worksheet instead.

Question 24

When an offense at a religious institution is investigated as a possible bias incident but the CO/Duty Captain determines it is NOT bias motivated, the precinct detective should forward copies of the 61 and OCR to:

A. C.O., Hate Crimes Unit only B. C.O., Hate Crimes Unit AND Chief of Detectives, through channels C. Chief of Detectives, Hate Crimes Unit, direct D. Operations Unit for a Non-Bias Log number

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-10, for a NON-BIAS determination, the detective forwards copies to C.O., Hate Crime Unit AND Chief of Detectives, through channels. For a POSSIBLE BIAS (confirmed), the detective forwards only to C.O., Hate Crime Unit (direct). The exam often mixes these addressees between bias and non-bias procedures.

Question 25

A Corrections Officer is alleged to have committed domestic violence against his wife. What is the proper documentation?

A. Prepare a Report on Typed Letterhead (49) to the Chief of IAB and notify IAB Command Center B. Record on a Complaint Report (61) with additional copies for IAB; no 49 or IAB notification needed C. Notify IAB Command Center and prepare both a 49 and a Complaint Report D. Refer the allegation to the Corrections Department’s internal affairs unit

Answer B. Per P.G. 207-22, minor misconduct (defined as DWI and Domestic Violence) against a non-NYPD government agent is documented on a Complaint Report (61) with additional copies to IAB. No 49 or separate notification to IAB is required for minor misconduct.

Question 26

Regarding the CCRB investigating supervisor hierarchy for a patrol command, which of the following is NOT in the hierarchy?

A. Integrity Control Officer (ICO) B. Special Operations Lieutenant (SOL) C. Operations Coordinator D. Platoon Commander

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-28, the investigating supervisor hierarchy for patrol commands is: (1) ICO, (2) SOL, (3) Platoon Commander, and if all three lieutenants are unavailable, the CO or Duty Captain. The Operations Coordinator is notably absent from this list.

Question 27

A child 9 years old or younger is reported missing. Who performs the Patrol Supervisor’s duties at the scene?

A. The Patrol Supervisor of the command of occurrence B. The detective assigned to the case C. The CO/XO/Duty Captain of Pct/PSA/TD of occurrence D. The Missing Persons Squad supervisor

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-23, when a missing person is a child 9 years old or younger, the CO/XO/Duty Captain of the Pct/PSA/TD of occurrence performs the Patrol Supervisor’s duties. The Captain effectively takes over the Patrol Supervisor’s role for these cases.

Question 28

The Desk Officer of the missing person’s RESIDENCE command must finalize the Complaint Report within what time frame?

A. End of tour B. 24 hours C. 2 hours of receipt D. 4 hours of receipt

Answer C. Per P.G. 207-23, the Desk Officer of the Residence Command must immediately review the 61 and finalize it within 2 hours of receipt. This is distinct from the D.O. of the command of occurrence, who reviews and signs the reports but does not have the same 2-hour finalization requirement.

Section 208 — Arrests

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 208
  • Source PDF: public-pguide1.pdf
  • Priority: High
  • Status: Completed

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the legal standards and departmental procedures for effecting arrests
  • Know the processing requirements for different categories of arrests (felony, misdemeanor, violation)
  • Identify the Patrol Supervisor’s responsibilities during arrest processing
  • Recognize specialized arrest procedures including warrant arrests and off-duty arrests
  • Master the three levels of search and authorization requirements
  • Know DAT eligibility criteria and the three categories of disqualification
  • Understand family offense/DV definitions, concurrent jurisdiction, and mandatory arrest provisions
  • Know Miranda warning requirements and juvenile interview restrictions
  • Understand lineup/showup procedures and identification requirements
  • Know consular notification obligations for foreign nationals

Procedures

Procedure Title Status
P.G. 208-01 Law of Arrest completed
P.G. 208-02 Arrests — Removal to Facility completed
P.G. 208-03 Arrests — General Processing completed
P.G. 208-04 Arrest by Civilian completed
P.G. 208-05 General Search Guidelines completed
P.G. 208-06 Arrests — Photographs completed
P.G. 208-07 Arrests — Fingerprinting completed
P.G. 208-08 Arrests — DNA Collection completed
P.G. 208-09 Rights of Persons in Custody completed
P.G. 208-10 Prisoner Identification and Processing completed
P.G. 208-11 Arrest Processing — Felony Complaints completed
P.G. 208-12 Arrest Processing — Misdemeanor Complaints completed
P.G. 208-13 Arrest Processing — Violation Complaints completed
P.G. 208-14 Arrests — Off-Duty Members completed
P.G. 208-15 Arrests — Warrant Execution completed
P.G. 208-16 Arrests — Surrender of Wanted Persons completed
P.G. 208-17 Arrests — Pick-Up Arrests completed
P.G. 208-20 Arrest Processing — Intoxicated Drivers completed
P.G. 208-22 Arrest Processing — Desk Officer Responsibilities completed
P.G. 208-23 Arrest Processing — Online Booking System (OLBS) completed
P.G. 208-24 Lineups and Showups completed
P.G. 208-25 Arrest Processing — Multiple Defendants completed
P.G. 208-26 Arrest Processing — Juvenile Offenders completed
P.G. 208-27 DAT — General Procedure completed
P.G. 208-34 Arrests — Organized Crime Offenses completed
P.G. 208-35 Arrests — Prostitution-Related Offenses completed
P.G. 208-36 Family Offenses / Domestic Violence completed
P.G. 208-37 Family Offenses — Orders of Protection completed
P.G. 208-38 Family Offenses — Mandatory Arrest completed
P.G. 208-39 Family Offenses — Additional Procedures completed
P.G. 208-40 Family Offenses — Victim Notification completed
P.G. 208-41 Family Offenses — Reporting Requirements completed
P.G. 208-42 Arrests — Hate Crimes completed
P.G. 208-44 Arrests — Sex Offenses completed
P.G. 208-46 Arrests — School-Related Incidents completed
P.G. 208-47 Arrests — Taxi/Livery Related Offenses completed
P.G. 208-48 Arrests — Identity Theft completed
P.G. 208-49 Arrests — Human Trafficking completed
P.G. 208-50 Arrests — Robbery Patterns completed
P.G. 208-52 Arrests — Strangulation/Criminal Obstruction of Breathing completed
P.G. 208-53 Arrests — Stalking Offenses completed
P.G. 208-54 Arrests — Mental Health Related completed
P.G. 208-55 Arrests — Terrorism/National Security completed
P.G. 208-56 Foreign Nationals completed
P.G. 208-57 Arrests — Military Personnel completed
P.G. 208-58 Arrests — Diplomatic/Consular Immunity completed
P.G. 208-59 Arrests — Federal Employees completed
P.G. 208-60 Arrests — Members of the Service completed
P.G. 208-61 Arrests — Other Law Enforcement Officers completed
P.G. 208-62 Arrests — Attorneys completed
P.G. 208-66 Arrests — Parole/Probation Violators completed
P.G. 208-67 Arrests — Fugitives from Other Jurisdictions completed
P.G. 208-68 Arrests — Unlawful Assembly/Demonstrations completed
P.G. 208-69 Notifications completed
P.G. 208-70 Strangulation Offenses completed
P.G. 208-71 Arrests — Firearms/Weapons Offenses completed
P.G. 208-72 Arrests — Narcotics Offenses completed
P.G. 208-73 Arrests — Graffiti/Vandalism completed
P.G. 208-74 Arrests — Grand Jury Notification completed
P.G. 208-75 Arrests — Sealing of Records completed
P.G. 208-78 Arrests — Transit System Offenses completed
P.G. 208-79 Arrests — NYCHA/Housing Authority completed
P.G. 208-80 Arrests — Bench Warrant Processing completed
P.G. 208-81 DAT — Disqualifying Factors completed
P.G. 208-82 DAT — Conferral with District Attorney completed
P.G. 208-83 Religious Head Covering During Arrest Processing completed
P.G. 208-84 Early Diversion Initiative (Project Reset) completed

Sergeant Focus Areas

Key Supervisor Procedures

  • P.G. 208-01 — Law of Arrest: Warrant scope (statewide vs county), warrantless arrest standards for petty offenses vs crimes, felony vs misdemeanor pursuit into private residences
  • P.G. 208-02 — Removal to Facility: Supervisor verifies arrest at scene BEFORE removal; designates MOS to document all personnel present and BWC activation
  • P.G. 208-03 — General Processing: High risk prisoner = TWO MOS escort at ALL times; Resisting Recidivist = 2+ qualifying arrests in 5 years; desk officer observes ALL prisoners; juvenile parent/guardian must be present for Miranda
  • P.G. 208-05 — Search Guidelines: Three search levels; strip search requires reasonable suspicion + immediate supervisor approval; CO/Duty Captain resolves disagreements; desk officer has FINAL say on religious head covering
  • P.G. 208-09 — Rights in Custody: Six-part Miranda; cease interrogation on request for attorney or silence; attorney must know prisoner’s identity; muster room interview under continuous observation
  • P.G. 208-24 — Lineups/Showups: Minimum 6 persons; suspect selects position; detective supervisor supervises entire procedure; do NOT advise suspect of right to attorney
  • P.G. 208-27 — DAT General: Class E felonies, misdemeanors, violations eligible; Department REQUIRED to accept listed ID forms; desk officer makes FINAL determination on identity; never hold DAT-eligible prisoner solely for debriefing
  • P.G. 208-36 — Family Offenses/DV: FCA vs NYPD expanded definitions; concurrent jurisdiction (18+, FCA relationship, family offense); interview separately; child abuse failure to report = Class A misdemeanor; crime against child = arrest, no DAT
  • P.G. 208-56 — Foreign Nationals: Mandatory vs non-mandatory notification countries; Intelligence Division Operations Unit handles mandatory notifications
  • P.G. 208-69 — Notifications: Operations Unit direct for government employees, MOS, food stamps >$500; Intelligence Division for UN employees, foreign armed forces, youth gangs
  • P.G. 208-81 — DAT Disqualifiers: Three categories: Condition of Defendant (ID, warrant, FTA 2 years), Specific Charges (family offense, sex crime, hate crime), Order of Protection Needed (27 offenses)
  • P.G. 208-82 — DA Conferral: Same day or next tour; if >4 days, reassign; 1st platoon extends into 2nd platoon

Section 208 — Desk Appearance Ticket (DAT) Procedures

Covers P.G. 208-27, 208-81, 208-82

P.G. 208-27: DAT — General Procedure

What Is a DAT?

A Desk Appearance Ticket (DAT) is an appearance ticket issued in lieu of continued detention after arrest processing. It directs the defendant to appear in court at a later date.

Eligible Offenses

Offense Level DAT Eligible?
Class E Felonies Yes
Misdemeanors Yes
Violations Yes
Class A-D Felonies No

Satisfactory Forms of Identification

The Department is REQUIRED to accept the following forms of ID:

ID Type Notes
NYS Driver’s License —
Passport Any country
U.S. Military ID —
Citizenship Papers —
Out-of-State Driver’s License —
Resident Alien Card —
Municipal ID Including Canadian municipal ID
Public Benefit Card —

Exam Alert: The Department is REQUIRED to accept all of the above. The desk officer makes the FINAL determination on identity, but these specific documents MUST be accepted as satisfactory.

Key ID Rules

Rule Detail
Photo ID requirement NO requirement for photo ID if identity is otherwise verifiable (e.g., personal knowledge of the defendant)
Third party contact Allow defendant to contact a third party to bring identification
Processing delay Delay processing for a reasonable time to allow ID to arrive
Investigation Card An investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance
DNA owed DNA owed is NOT a disqualifying factor for DAT

OMNIFORM Monitoring

  • Monitor OMNIFORM DAT Processing screen
  • If no NYSID returned, check back every 30 MINUTES maximum
  • Do not hold prisoner indefinitely waiting for NYSID

Debriefing and DAT

Rule Detail
Desk Officer ensures prisoner is debriefed Standard requirement
DAT-eligible prisoner held solely for debriefing NEVER permitted
Exception Unless there is reasonable suspicion of another crime

Sergeant Focus: A DAT-eligible prisoner should NEVER be held solely for debriefing purposes. The only exception is when there is reasonable suspicion that the prisoner is involved in another crime. Holding a DAT-eligible prisoner without legal basis exposes the Department to liability.

P.G. 208-81: DAT — Disqualifying Factors

Three Categories of Disqualification

Category 1: CONDITION OF DEFENDANT

Factor Detail
Not properly identified Cannot verify identity (ID not up to standards found in PG 208-27)
Open warrant Active warrant exists
Failure to appear Failed to appear in Criminal Court in the last 2 YEARS
Escape-related charges Escape 2nd, Absconding 1st, Absconding from Treatment Facility, Bail Jumping 2nd
Physical/mental condition Prisoner may face harm if released & court evaluation is in their best interest
Under influence of drugs/alcohol To the degree that prisoner may endanger self or others

Exam Alert: Physical or mental condition alone does not automatically disqualify. The desk officer must CONFER WITH LEGAL BUREAU before using this as a basis to deny a DAT. Document the conferral in the Digital Activity Log AND the Command Log.

Exam Alert: The failure to appear factor specifically refers to Criminal Court within the last 2 years. It also includes an open warrant — both relate to the defendant’s reliability to return.

Category 2: SPECIFIC CHARGES

Charge Type Examples
Family offense Against member of the same family/household
ANY sex crime Including all attempts or conspiracy to commit
Hate crime As defined in Penal Law Section 485.05
Weapon on school grounds CPW on school property
License-suspendable crime Vehicular Assault 2nd, Leaving the Scene with any injury or prior leaving the scene, Misd or Felony VTL 1192 DWI
Criminal Possession of a Firearm Automatic disqualifier
Any A Misdemeanor or Felony involving harm to identifiable person/property QUALIFIED — only disqualifies if defendant has pending prosecution for any offense OR has yet to return on a previous DAT for either of the above 2 crimes

Exam Alert: Identifiable person means the crime charged involves a specific victim. Identifiable property includes theft (Grand Larceny, Petit Larceny, GLA) and property damage (including public property). The crime only has to involve harm — it does NOT have to actually cause harm.

Exam Alert: The last two items (CPF and A-Mis/Felony with harm) are QUALIFIED disqualifiers. Before denying a DAT on the basis of a pending prosecution or unreturned DAT, the desk officer must ensure the defendant actually has a requisite pending prosecution or previously issued DAT by checking appropriate Department computer systems.

Exam Alert: VTL 1192-1 DWAI (impaired) is a VIOLATION, not a misdemeanor — it is DAT-eligible. Only misdemeanor or felony DWI charges under VTL 1192 trigger the license-suspension disqualifier.

See Also: PG 208-40 — If a prisoner is hospitalized more than 24 hours for DWI and did NOT cause SPI/DOA, the prisoner can receive a DAT for the DWI.

Category 3: REASONABLE BELIEF ORDER OF PROTECTION NEEDED

  • Strong possibility of violence against complainant AND person should seek an OOP against defendant
  • MAY deny DAT for a number of offenses based on this belief (including Violation of an OOP)
  • WILL deny DAT for the specific crimes listed below

Exam Alert: These crimes do NOT, by themselves, preclude a DAT. They must be coupled with the belief of recurring violence/need for an OOP.

Crimes Against Children, Family, Vulnerable People:
  • Children: Reckless Assault of Child by a Child Day Care Provider; Luring a Child; Substitution of Children; Custodial Interference 1st; Disseminate Indecent Material to Minor 2nd
  • Family: Aggravated Family Offense (do family offense with conviction of one in the last 5 years)
  • Vulnerable Person: Endangering the Welfare of an Incompetent Physically Disabled Person 1st; Endangering Vulnerable Elderly Person, or Incompetent or Physically Disabled Person 2nd
Other Crimes:
  • Assault on a Retail Worker (PL 120.19) — New Law in 2024 (E Felony)
  • Aggravated Harassment 1st (with specifically identified victim [not a group] OR on private property with owner complainant)
  • Criminal Interference with Health Care Services or Religious Worship 1st
  • Stalking 2nd, Menacing 2nd Sub 2 (intend fear of DOA/PI/SPI by repeatedly following, etc.)
  • Menacing 1st (do Menacing 2nd with conviction of such in last 10 years)
  • Coercion 2nd (threaten injury or prop damage, criminal accusation, commit a crime, etc.)
  • Criminal Contempt 1st (Violate OOP), Tampering with a Witness 3rd
Public Servants:
  • Assault 3rd or Attempt; Menacing 2nd or 3rd; Harassment 1st, Aggravated Harassment; Aggravated Harassment of a Judge
  • Reckless Endangerment 2nd against City/State enforcement agent performing official duty
  • Criminal Impersonation 1st (impersonate a P.O. & attempt a felony or a Dr. & call in a script)
  • Threaten, harass, menace: UMOS; City/State/Fed elected official; Employee of ANY (public or private) transit agency/authority/company operating under authorization from NYS or NYC while performing assigned duties
By Landlords:
  • Harassment of Rent Regulated Tenant 1st & 2nd, Unlawful Eviction (Admin Code 26-521)
  • Unlawful Surveillance 2nd, Dissemination of an Unlawful Surveillance Image 1st

Sergeant Focus: The three categories are frequently tested. Remember: Category 1 = who the defendant IS, Category 2 = what the defendant DID, Category 3 = what the defendant MIGHT DO (future danger requiring court protection).

P.G. 208-82: DAT — Conferral with District Attorney

Conferral Timeline

Situation Requirement
Standard Arresting officer MUST confer with DA’s office the SAME DAY or NEXT regularly scheduled tour
Next tour > 4 DAYS away Assign conferral to another officer
1st platoon arrest Arresting officer extends tour into 2nd platoon to confer with DA

Exam Alert: The 4-day rule is critical. If the arresting officer’s next regularly scheduled tour is more than 4 days away, the conferral responsibility must be reassigned. The 1st platoon/2nd platoon extension is also heavily tested — the officer does not wait until a later tour but extends into the next platoon.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must track DAT conferrals to ensure they happen within the required timeframe. A missed conferral can result in case dismissal.

Section 208 — Domestic Violence and Family Offenses

Covers P.G. 208-36, 208-37, 208-38, 208-39, 208-70

P.G. 208-36: Family Offenses / Domestic Violence

Updated: 10/20/25 | R.O. 106 | 17 pages

PURPOSE: To process family offenses and other offenses that occur between family/household members as per the Family/Household - Expanded Definition.

Family/Household Definitions

Family Court Act (FCA) Definition (subdivisions a through f)

A family/household member under the FCA includes persons who: - a. Are legally married to one another - b. Were formerly legally married to one another - c. Are related by marriage (affinity) - d. Are related by blood (consanguinity) - e. Have a child in common regardless of whether such persons have been married or have lived together at any time - f. Are not related by consanguinity (blood) or affinity (marriage) and who are, or have been, in an intimate relationship regardless of whether such persons have lived together at any time

A family/household thus includes: common-law marriages, same sex couples, registered NYC domestic partners, different generations of the same family, siblings, in-laws, persons involved in intimate relationships, and persons who live or have lived together in a family-type relationship.

NYPD Expanded Definition (subdivisions a through h)

The NYPD expands the FCA definition to also include persons who: - g. Are currently living together in a family-type relationship - h. Formerly lived together in a family-type relationship

Exam Alert: The NYPD definition is BROADER than the FCA definition. The FCA covers subdivisions a through f; the NYPD adds g and h (current/former cohabitation in a family-type relationship). Know both definitions and which subdivision letters apply to each.

Complainant/Victim Definition

For purposes of this procedure ONLY, is limited to a person described in subdivisions a through h above (the full NYPD Expanded Definition).

Intimate Relationship Standard

Determined by common sense standard regarding the totality of circumstances, considering:

Factor Detail
Nature or type of the relationship The relationship does NOT have to be sexual to be intimate
Frequency of interaction How often the parties see each other
Duration How long the relationship has lasted
Time spent together In either a work or leisure-related capacity
Shared expenses/finances Joint financial obligations
Extent of interaction with family members Integration into each other’s families

Does NOT qualify: - Casual acquaintance - Ordinary fraternization in a business, educational, or social context

Exam Alert: If unable to determine if the relationship is intimate, the MOS will request the response of the Patrol Supervisor to make the determination.
