Primary Physical Aggressor The person identified as the primary aggressor in a simultaneous misdemeanor vs. misdemeanor family offense; determined by comparative extent of injuries, defensive acts, current/future threats, and prior DV history P.G. 208-36
ICCU Inter City Correspondence Unit — receives forwarded paperwork for DV incidents occurring outside NYC but within NYS P.G. 208-36
Hope Card Wallet-size card or digital version for Final OOPs; UMOS scan QR code with Dept phone to verify (Ops Order 11 s.2025) P.G. 208-36
DVPO Domestic Violence Prevention Officer — opens/manages cases in DVIMS, assists in apprehension, conducts home visits, follows up with victims on photos P.G. 208-37
DVIMS Domestic Violence Incident Management System — used by DVPO for case management (NOT the same as ECMS) P.G. 208-37
ECMS Enterprise Case Management System — used by assigned detective to document DVPO arrest efforts P.G. 208-37
I-Card Investigation Card activated for named DV perpetrator; must be activated no later than end of assigned tour; should include NYSID # P.G. 208-36
Rosario Material DIRs are potential Rosario material; if an arrest is made, A/O must give copy of DIR to ADA P.G. 208-36

Firearm Seizure in DV

Term Definition Source
Mandatory Seizure WILL seize firearms and license/permit if: OOP exists against holder, physical force or threat of involved in incident, or license holder is arrested (regardless of charge) P.G. 208-36
Discretionary Seizure MAY seize if UMOS reasonably believes presence of firearms creates imminent risk of physical injury or higher P.G. 208-36

Forms

Form Name Form Number Purpose Reference
OLBS Worksheet PD244-159 Online Booking System worksheet for arrest processing P.G. 208-03
Arrest Supplement PD244-157 Supplemental arrest information and documentation P.G. 208-03
Desk Appearance Ticket PD260-121 Appearance ticket issued in lieu of detention P.G. 208-27
Investigation Card PD373-163 Record of field investigation; does NOT preclude DAT issuance P.G. 208-27

Units and Abbreviations

Abbreviation Full Name Context
BWC Body-Worn Camera Must be activated during medical/psychological inquiry and at arrest scene
OLBS Online Booking System Primary arrest processing system
NYSID New York State Identification Number Fingerprint-based identification; check every 30 min max during DAT processing
DA District Attorney Conferral required same day or next tour for DAT cases
FCA Family Court Act Defines family/household relationships for concurrent jurisdiction
SVU Special Victims Unit Notified for sex offenses and child abuse
DOCCS Department of Corrections and Community Supervision Notified for parole violator arrests
TBTA Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority Notified for offenses at TBTA facilities
CUNY City University of New York Notified for offenses at CUNY facilities
DOE Department of Education School Safety Division notified for DOE-related offenses
VTL Vehicle and Traffic Law VTL 1192 (DWI) is a DAT disqualifier
ICCU Inter City Correspondence Unit Receives forwarded DV paperwork for incidents outside NYC but within NYS
DVPO Domestic Violence Prevention Officer Opens/manages cases in DVIMS; assists in apprehension within pct/PSA confines
DVIMS Domestic Violence Incident Management System DVPO case management system (NOT ECMS)
ECMS Enterprise Case Management System Assigned detective documents DVPO arrest efforts here
IAB Internal Affairs Bureau Notified for bribery arrests and MOS DV offenses; provides log number
BCS Borough Court Section Where perps are transported; also where ICO obtains post-arraignment info
LLS Language Line Service Certified interpreter service used to verify DV info from non-English speaking victims
FORMS Finest Online Records Management System System for inputting DIRs, photos, and other records for NYC incidents

Critical Numbers

Number What It Represents Source
6 Minimum total persons in a lineup (suspect + 5 others) P.G. 208-24
2 years Failed to appear in court — DAT disqualifier P.G. 208-81
5 years Lookback period for resisting recidivist classification (2+ arrests) P.G. 208-03
30 minutes Maximum interval for NYSID recheck during DAT processing P.G. 208-27
4 days If next tour > 4 days away, reassign DA conferral to another officer P.G. 208-82
$500 Federal food stamp threshold for Operations Unit notification P.G. 208-69
27 Number of specific offenses on the Order of Protection needed list (DAT disqualifier Category 3) P.G. 208-81
7 Copies of 49 (Report on Typed Letterhead) to Chief of Department in a bribery arrest P.G. 208-34
3 Steps in the Attempted Bribery (getting wired up) procedure (PG 208-35) P.G. 208-35
3 Places the IAB Log # must appear when MOS commits DV offense (DIR Results box, 61 Details, FORMS Database) P.G. 208-37
5 Copies of 49 (+1 if guns removed) for MOS DV offense report P.G. 208-37
10 years Lookback period for Menacing 1st (do Menacing 2nd with conviction in last 10 years) — DAT Category 3 disqualifier P.G. 208-81
5 years Lookback period for Aggravated Family Offense (family offense with prior conviction within 5 years) — DAT Category 3 disqualifier P.G. 208-81

Section 208 — Review Questions

25 multiple-choice questions covering key Sergeant exam topics from Section 208.

Question 1

A sergeant receives a warrant issued by a city court in Westchester County. The suspect is located in a neighboring county. Can the warrant be executed there?

A. No, city court warrants can only be executed within the city limits B. Yes, city/town/village court warrants may be executed in the county of issuance or adjoining county C. Yes, all warrants have statewide reach D. No, the warrant must be re-issued by the adjoining county’s court

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-01, warrants issued by a city, town, or village court may be executed within the county of issuance OR an adjoining county. Only warrants from NYC Criminal Court, district court, or superior court have statewide authority.

Question 2

An officer observes a traffic infraction in an adjoining county while off-duty. The officer is employed in Kings County. Can the officer make a warrantless arrest?

A. Yes, any offense in the officer’s presence allows arrest B. Yes, but only if the infraction is also a misdemeanor C. No, petty offenses require the arrest to be within the geographical area of employment AND the county of occurrence or adjoining county D. No, off-duty officers cannot make warrantless arrests

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-01, warrantless arrest for a petty offense (violation or traffic infraction) requires the offense be in the officer’s presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. An officer in an adjoining county outside their area of employment cannot make this arrest.

Question 3

A misdemeanor suspect flees into a private residence. Under what circumstance may an officer enter without a warrant or consent?

A. Whenever the officer has probable cause for the misdemeanor B. Only to prevent imminent injury, destruction of evidence, escape, or under exigent circumstances C. Never — a warrant is always required for a misdemeanor suspect D. Only if the suspect is also wanted on a felony warrant

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-01, for a misdemeanor suspect fleeing into a private residence, officers may enter ONLY to prevent imminent injury, prevent destruction of evidence, prevent escape, or under exigent circumstances. For a felony suspect, officers may pursue directly into the residence without these additional requirements.

Question 4

During a search at a police facility, the arrestee’s gender is not apparent. How should the searching officer’s gender be determined?

A. The desk officer assigns the searching officer based on the arrestee’s biological sex B. A supervisor makes the determination based on visual assessment C. The search is assigned to the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability D. Two officers of different genders conduct the search together

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-05, if the arrestee’s gender is not apparent or the arrestee objects to the assigned gender, the search is conducted by a member of the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability. Officers must NEVER search a person to determine their gender.

Question 5

Who must authorize a strip search?

A. The desk officer on duty B. The arresting officer’s immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor C. The commanding officer or duty captain D. Any supervisor of higher rank than the arresting officer

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-05, a strip search requires the knowledge and approval of the arresting officer’s IMMEDIATE SUPERVISOR or the borough court section supervisor. The CO/Duty Captain becomes involved only when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor.

Question 6

The desk officer and an outside supervisor disagree about whether a strip search should be conducted. Who makes the final decision?

A. The desk officer, as the officer in charge of the command B. The outside supervisor, as the arresting officer’s supervisor C. The CO or Duty Captain, after being notified by the desk officer D. The Legal Bureau, after consultation

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-05, when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor regarding a strip search, the desk officer notifies the CO/Duty Captain, who makes the FINAL decision.

Question 7

How many parts comprise the Miranda warnings as outlined in the Patrol Guide?

A. Four B. Five C. Six D. Seven

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-09, Miranda warnings consist of SIX parts: (1) right to remain silent, (2) anything said may be used against you, (3) right to attorney before and during questioning, (4) attorney provided if cannot afford one, (5) right to remain silent until consulting attorney, (6) willing to answer questions.

Question 8

A juvenile is arrested and the parent is present. The juvenile agrees to answer questions, but the parent objects. What should the officer do?

A. Proceed with questioning since the juvenile consented B. Cease questioning — the parent’s objection overrides the juvenile’s willingness C. Continue but limit questions to basic identifying information D. Request a Legal Bureau consultation before proceeding

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-03, if a parent or guardian objects to questioning, NO questioning is permitted even if the juvenile is willing to answer. The parent’s objection is controlling.

Question 9

During custodial interrogation, the suspect states I think I should talk to a lawyer. What is the proper response?

A. Continue questioning, as the statement was equivocal B. Ask the suspect to clarify whether they want an attorney C. Cease interrogation immediately D. Advise the suspect of Miranda rights again and continue

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-09, interrogation must cease immediately when a subject indicates they want an attorney or wish to remain silent. Officers should err on the side of cessation when any request for counsel is made.

Question 10

A person is arrested for a Class E felony and presents a valid out-of-state driver’s license. Is the person eligible for a DAT?

A. No, DATs are only for misdemeanors and violations B. No, out-of-state identification is not accepted for DAT processing C. Yes, Class E felonies are DAT-eligible, and out-of-state licenses are a satisfactory form of ID D. Yes, but only if the person also has a local address

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-27, DATs may be issued for Class E felonies, misdemeanors, and violations. Out-of-state driver’s licenses are on the list of satisfactory identification that the Department is REQUIRED to accept.

Question 11

A defendant arrested for a misdemeanor has an investigation card on file. Does this preclude issuance of a DAT?

A. Yes, any prior police contact disqualifies DAT issuance B. Yes, an investigation card indicates the person is known to police C. No, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance D. No, but the desk officer must obtain supervisor approval

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-27, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance. Similarly, DNA owed is NOT a disqualifying factor.

Question 12

Which of the following is a DAT disqualifying factor under the Condition of Defendant category?

A. The defendant owes DNA B. The defendant has an investigation card on file C. The defendant failed to appear in court within the last 2 years D. The defendant has a prior misdemeanor conviction

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-81, failure to appear in court within the last 2 YEARS is a disqualifying factor under Category 1 (Condition of Defendant). DNA owed and investigation cards are specifically NOT disqualifying. A prior conviction alone is not a disqualifier.

Question 13

The arresting officer’s next regularly scheduled tour is 5 days away. What must happen regarding DA conferral for a DAT case?

A. The officer confers with the DA upon return in 5 days B. The conferral is assigned to another officer C. The desk officer confers with the DA on the officer’s behalf D. The case is automatically upgraded from DAT to continued custody

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-82, if the arresting officer’s next regularly scheduled tour is more than 4 DAYS away, the DA conferral must be assigned to another officer.

Question 14

Under the Family Court Act, which of the following relationships qualifies as family/household?

A. Current roommates who share an apartment but are not in an intimate relationship B. Former coworkers who dated briefly three years ago C. Persons who have a child in common but were never married D. Business partners who have known each other for ten years

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-36, the FCA definition of family/household includes persons who have a child in common. Casual acquaintances, business relationships, and mere roommates (without a family-type relationship) do not qualify. Note: the NYPD expanded definition would cover persons living together in a family-type relationship, but a simple roommate arrangement is not a family-type relationship.

Question 15

What additional category does the NYPD expanded definition of family/household include beyond the Family Court Act definition?

A. Persons who attend the same school or workplace B. Persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship C. Persons who share a common friend group D. Persons who live in the same apartment building

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36, the NYPD expands the FCA definition to include persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship. The FCA itself covers: legally married, formerly married, related by marriage, related by blood, child in common, and intimate relationship.

Question 16

A 17-year-old is arrested for misdemeanor harassment against their former spouse. Does concurrent jurisdiction exist between Criminal Court and Family Court?

A. Yes, all three conditions are met B. No, because the offender is under 18 and the charge is not a felony C. No, because harassment is not a family offense D. Yes, because the parties were married

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36, concurrent jurisdiction requires: (1) family offense, (2) FCA relationship, and (3) offender 18+. A 16-17 year old qualifies ONLY if charged with a felony-level family offense. Misdemeanor harassment by a 17-year-old goes to Criminal Court only.

Question 17

At a domestic violence scene, no certified interpreter is available and the victim speaks limited English. A family member offers to interpret. What should the officer do?

A. Accept the family member as interpreter for the entire investigation B. Decline — only certified interpreters may be used at any DV scene C. Use the family member temporarily to stabilize the scene, then verify information with a certified member or Language Line D. Postpone the investigation until a certified interpreter arrives

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-36, officers should avoid using family members or bystanders as interpreters. However, they may be used temporarily to stabilize the scene. Information must then be verified through a certified member or Language Line.

Question 18

An officer suspects child abuse but decides not to report it because the evidence is inconclusive. What is the legal consequence of willful failure to report suspected child abuse?

A. No consequence if the evidence is inconclusive B. Administrative reprimand only C. Class A misdemeanor plus civil liability D. Class B misdemeanor

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-36, willful failure to report suspected child abuse constitutes a CLASS A MISDEMEANOR and creates civil liability. The standard is suspected abuse — conclusive evidence is not required before reporting.

Question 19

In a lineup, how many total persons must be present?

A. At least 4 (suspect plus 3 others) B. At least 5 (suspect plus 4 others) C. At least 6 (suspect plus 5 others) D. At least 8 (suspect plus 7 others)

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-24, a lineup must include the suspect plus at least 5 OTHER persons, for a minimum of 6 total participants.

Question 20

During lineup preparation, where should the suspect be placed in the lineup?

A. In the center position B. In a position randomly assigned by the detective C. In the position selected by the suspect D. In the last position

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-24, the suspect must be permitted to SELECT their own position in the lineup.

Question 21

The desk officer determines a prisoner is high risk. What is the required escort protocol?

A. One MOS assigned during transport only B. Two MOS assigned during transport to court C. Two MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times D. One MOS with supervisor check-ins every 30 minutes

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-03, a high risk prisoner requires TWO MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times — not just during transport, but continuously throughout processing and detention.

Question 22

A person has been arrested three times for Resisting Arrest in the past 4 years. What classification applies?

A. No special classification B. High risk prisoner C. Resisting recidivist — enhance processing with detective squad D. Violent felon — transfer to detective squad for processing

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-03, a Resisting Recidivist is a person arrested 2 or more times within the past 5 YEARS for Resisting Arrest, Assault 2nd (sub 3), Assault on PO, or Aggravated Assault on PO. Three arrests for Resisting Arrest in 4 years qualifies. Processing is enhanced with the detective squad.

Question 23

A foreign national from a mandatory notification country is arrested. The prisoner states they do NOT want their consulate notified. What should the officer do?

A. Honor the prisoner’s wishes and do not notify B. Document the refusal and proceed without notification C. Contact the Intelligence Division Operations Unit — mandatory notification is required regardless of the prisoner’s wishes D. Notify the prisoner’s attorney instead

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-56, for mandatory notification countries, consular notification is REQUIRED regardless of the prisoner’s wishes. The Intelligence Division Operations Unit (available 24 hours) handles mandatory consular notifications.

Question 24

For non-mandatory notification countries, what is the officer’s obligation regarding consular notification?

A. Notify the consulate automatically B. Inform the prisoner of their right to have their embassy/consulate notified C. No obligation — consular notification is optional for non-mandatory countries D. Contact the Intelligence Division for guidance

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-56, for non-mandatory notification countries, the officer must INFORM the prisoner of their right to have their embassy or consulate notified. The prisoner may then choose whether to exercise that right.

Question 25

During DAT processing, the OMNIFORM system shows no NYSID has been returned. How frequently should the officer check for results?

A. Every 15 minutes B. Every 30 minutes maximum C. Every 60 minutes D. Only once, then proceed without NYSID

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-27, if no NYSID is returned during DAT processing, the officer should check back every 30 MINUTES maximum. The prisoner should not be held indefinitely waiting for NYSID results.

Question 26

An officer is conducting a frisk of a prisoner in a wheelchair. When frisking the prisoner’s lower body (legs and under buttocks), which direction should the officer’s palms face?

A. Palms facing down, sliding along each leg B. Palms facing up, sliding hands up each leg and under buttocks C. Palms facing inward toward the body D. It does not matter as long as the search is thorough

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-05, when frisking a mobility-disabled prisoner in a wheelchair, the officer frisks the lower body by sliding hands up each leg and under buttocks with palms UP. When searching the device (wheelchair) itself, palms face DOWN. The exam frequently reverses the UP/DOWN direction.

Question 27

A UMOS is offered a bribe by a suspect during a traffic stop. What charge should the suspect face?

A. Attempted Bribery B. Bribery — the mere offer is sufficient; no actual exchange is required C. Corruption of a Public Official D. No charge unless the officer accepts the bribe

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-34 and 208-35, Attempted Bribery is NOT a criminal charge. If a UMOS is offered a bribe, the perp is charged with Bribery. An actual exchange does not have to take place; the mere offer is sufficient. A past exam offered Charge with Attempted Bribery as an answer choice and it was wrong.

Question 28

In a bribery arrest, the ADA refuses to draw up the bribery charge. Who makes the notifications to the C.O./Duty Captain and IAB?

A. The Desk Officer, as in a regular bribery arrest B. The Arresting Officer — this is an exception to the normal notification chain C. The Platoon Commander D. The patrol supervisor on duty

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-34, when the ADA refuses to draw up the bribery charge, the Arresting Officer (not the Desk Officer) notifies the C.O./Duty Captain and IAB Command Center. This differs from a regular bribery arrest where the Desk Officer handles notifications. This breaks the chain of command.

Question 29

Which mnemonic lists the personal crimes that qualify as family offenses?

A. C GIF CUSS B. DRAMAS C. SAFETEA D. CRIMES

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36, DRAMAS covers the personal family offenses: Dis Con, Reckless Endangerment, Aggravated Harassment 2nd/Harassment 1st or 2nd, Menacing 2nd or 3rd, Assault 2nd or 3rd (or Attempts), Strangulation 1st or 2nd/Criminal Obstruction of Breathing/Stalking all degrees. C GIF CUSS covers the property/sexual crimes.

Question 30

A domestic violence incident occurs outside NYC but within New York State. Which of the following is correct regarding documentation?

A. Prepare DIR and enter it into FORMS; prepare Complaint Report and enter into OMNIFORM B. Prepare DIR and Complaint Report Worksheets; do NOT input to FORMS or OMNIFORM; forward to ICCU C. No documentation required since the incident is outside NYPD jurisdiction D. Prepare DIR in FORMS but use the Complaint Report Worksheet instead of OMNIFORM

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36 and 208-70, for DV incidents occurring outside NYC but within NYS, prepare a Complaint Report Worksheet (do NOT input to OMNIFORM), prepare DIR (do NOT input to FORMS), and take photos (do NOT upload to FORMS). Forward all paperwork to ICCU (Inter City Correspondence Unit) by scanning/emailing and sending hard copies via Dept mail.

Question 31

When an MOS commits a domestic violence offense, in how many places must the IAB Log number be documented?

A. One — the DIR only B. Two — the DIR and the Complaint Report C. Three — the DIR Results box, Details of the 61, and FORMS Database D. Four — the DIR, Complaint Report, Digital Activity Log, and FORMS

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-37, when an MOS commits a DV offense, the IAB Log number must appear in THREE places: (1) DIR in the box Results of Investigation and Basis of Action Taken, (2) Complaint Report Details section, and (3) FORMS Database.

Question 32

A Criminal Court Order of Protection is violated. The violation occurred in NYC. What charge is appropriate and where is the arrest processed?

A. Family Offense — processed in Family Court with concurrent jurisdiction B. Criminal Contempt or Aggravated Criminal Contempt — processed in NYC Criminal Court; NOT a Family Offense and NO concurrent jurisdiction C. Violation of Court Order — processed in the court that issued the OOP D. Criminal Contempt — processed in Family Court since the OOP was issued there

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36, Criminal Contempt for violating a Supreme Court, Family Court, or Criminal Court OOP is charged as Criminal Contempt or Aggravated Criminal Contempt. This is NOT a Family Offense, there is NO concurrent jurisdiction, and if the violation occurred in NYC, the arrest goes to Criminal Court.

Section 209 — Summonses

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 209
  • Source PDF: public-pguide1.pdf
  • Priority: Medium
  • Status: Completed

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the conditions under which a summons may be issued instead of an arrest (age, capacity, eligible offenses)
  • Know the VTL and Penal Law exceptions that require arrest rather than summons issuance
  • Identify the correct returnable agency for each summons type (Criminal Court, TVB, OATH, TAB, DOF)
  • Master CJRA civil summons eligibility, OATH Recidivist criteria, and NYPD Codes 1-4
  • Know the 5 mandatory identification elements for parking summonses and the consequences of omission
  • Understand abandoned and derelict vehicle definitions and associated timeframes
  • Recognize the Patrol Supervisor’s responsibilities for Code 3 verification, voided summons review, and summons accountability
  • Know the procedures for suspended/revoked license stops, ignition key removal, and department vehicle summonses
  • Distinguish between Urgent Police Action, Necessary Police Action, and convenience for department vehicle summons defenses
  • Understand TAB return date calculation (28 days) and Criminal Court return date sourcing (FINEST message)

Procedures

Procedure Title Status
P.G. 209-01 Conditions of Service completed
P.G. 209-02 Returnable Agencies completed
P.G. 209-03 CJRA Civil Summons (OATH) completed
P.G. 209-04 Return Date/Time completed
P.G. 209-07 Parking Summonses completed
P.G. 209-08 Parking Summons — Additional Procedures completed
P.G. 209-09 Personal Service of Summonses (TVB/Criminal Court) completed
P.G. 209-10 TVB Summons Procedures completed
P.G. 209-11 Criminal Court Traffic Summons completed
P.G. 209-12 OATH Summonses (Non-CJRA) completed
P.G. 209-15 Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB) Summonses completed
P.G. 209-16 TAB Summons — Additional Procedures completed
P.G. 209-17 E-Bike and E-Scooter Summonses completed
P.G. 209-18 Bicycle Summonses completed
P.G. 209-19 Pedestrian Summonses completed
P.G. 209-20 Environmental Control Board (ECB) Procedures completed
P.G. 209-21 Summons Accountability completed
P.G. 209-22 Summons Records completed
P.G. 209-23 Summons Dispositions completed
P.G. 209-24 Mandatory Driver’s License Checks completed
P.G. 209-26 Suspended/Revoked License completed
P.G. 209-27 Removal of Ignition Key completed
P.G. 209-28 Unlicensed Tow Truck Operators completed
P.G. 209-30 Summons on Department Vehicle completed
P.G. 209-31 Voiding a Summons — General Procedures completed
P.G. 209-32 Voiding a Parking Summons completed
P.G. 209-33 Voiding a TVB Summons completed
P.G. 209-34 Voiding a Criminal Court Summons completed
P.G. 209-35 GPS/Electronic Tracking Devices completed
P.G. 209-37 Speed Detection Devices completed
P.G. 209-39 Red Light Camera Violations completed
P.G. 209-40 Speed Camera Violations completed

Sergeant Focus Areas

Key Supervisor Procedures

  • P.G. 209-01 — Conditions of Service: Verify officers check age (16+), capacity (able to care for self), and offense eligibility before issuing summonses; ensure VTL exceptions (Leaving Scene, Dangerous Articles, False Statements, Stolen Vehicle, DWI) result in arrest, not summons
  • P.G. 209-03 — CJRA Civil Summons: Patrol supervisor MUST respond to verify Code 3 (law enforcement exception); desk officer makes FINAL determination on identity/residence; verify OATH Recidivist screening before civil summons issuance
  • P.G. 209-07 — Parking Summonses: Monitor 5 mandatory ID elements (plate number, type, expiration, make, model) — omission = dismissal; enforce 3-summons max per vehicle per day; ensure no summonses issued to abandoned/derelict vehicles
  • P.G. 209-09 — Personal Service: Verify Vehicle Reports completed for ALL vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stops; ensure one violation per summons; RTR notation when violator records activity
  • P.G. 209-24 — License Checks: Mandatory at traffic collisions, car stops, and investigatory situations; do NOT detain solely for license check; Activity Log entry when DMV system is inoperative
  • P.G. 209-26 — Suspended License: Verify proper confiscation and PD634-152 preparation; accept 14-day correction proof (validated license, temporary license, DMV receipt); park vehicle legally for registered owner
  • P.G. 209-27 — Ignition Key: 3-minute threshold for removal; PD571-121 in duplicate; 48-hour deadline for Property Clerk invoicing
  • P.G. 209-30 — Dept Vehicle Summons: Review Summons Plea Forms within 30 days; scrutinize necessary police action claims — convenience is NOT a valid defense; know the safety violation list
  • P.G. 209-31 to 209-34 — Voided Summonses: Supervisory review required for all voided summonses; monitor voiding patterns for training deficiencies or integrity concerns

Section 209 — Conditions of Service and Summons Types

Covers P.G. 209-01, 209-02, 209-03, 209-04

P.G. 209-01: Conditions of Service

When a Summons Replaces Arrest

A member of the service is authorized to issue a summons or notice of violation INSTEAD OF making an arrest when the violator meets ALL of the following conditions:

Condition Requirement
Age Violator must be 16 years of age or older
Capacity Violator must be able to care for self (not incapacitated by illness, injury, or intoxication)
Offense type Must fall within an eligible statute (see below)

Discretion for CJRA Violations

For any of the 6 CJRA offenses, a member may judiciously warn and admonish the violator instead of issuing a summons, OR issue a summons/arrest for a corresponding crime.

Eligible Statutes for Summons Issuance

Statute Notes
Agriculture & Markets Law All applicable violations
General Business Law All applicable violations
Labor Law All applicable violations
Multiple Dwelling Law All applicable violations
Vehicle and Traffic Law (VTL) With exceptions — see below
NYC Administrative Code All applicable violations
NYC Health Code All applicable violations
Penal Law violations With exceptions — see below

VTL Exceptions — CANNOT Issue Summons, MUST Arrest

VTL Section Offense
Section 600 Leaving the Scene of an Accident
Section 380 Dangerous Articles (Transportation)
Sections 392 / 392-a False Statements
Sections 421 / 422 / 423 / 426 Stolen Vehicle offenses
Section 1192 DWI / DWAI

Exam Alert: The five VTL exception categories are heavily tested. Remember the mnemonic: L-D-F-S-D (Leaving scene, Dangerous articles, False statements, Stolen vehicle, DWI). These offenses ALWAYS require arrest, never a summons.

Penal Law Violation Exceptions — CANNOT Issue Summons

PL Section Offense
PL 100 Criminal Solicitation
PL 215.58 Failure to Respond to Appearance Ticket
PL 240.40 Public Under Influence of Narcotics or a Drug

16-Year-Olds — Special Rules

Summons Type Eligibility
Criminal Court summonses for ALL violations Yes — eligible
Criminal Court summonses for VTL misdemeanors Yes — eligible
Criminal Court summonses for non-VTL misdemeanors No — not eligible

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must verify that officers correctly distinguish between violations (summons-eligible for 16-year-olds) and non-VTL misdemeanors (not summons-eligible for 16-year-olds). This is a common error in the field.

P.G. 209-02: Summonses and Notices of Violation Returnable Agencies

REVISED: Effective 03/27/26, R.O. 26. Updated title and expanded agency routing to include bicyclists, e-bike riders, and e-scooter riders under TVB. Added garages and parking lots routing. Clarified all other summonsable offenses catch-all.

Agency Routing Matrix

Offenses Processed Returnable To
Stopping, Standing and Parking offenses NYC Department of Finance, Parking Violations Operations
Traffic infractions other than Stopping, Standing and Parking (including traffic infractions committed by bicyclists, e-bike riders, and e-scooter riders) NYS Department of Motor Vehicles, Traffic Violations Bureau (TVB)
Pedestrian offenses and traffic misdemeanors NYC Criminal Court
Garages and parking lots NYC Criminal Court
All other summonsable offenses, except violations of OATH and TAB NYC Criminal Court
Food Vendor Regulations, General Vendor Regulations, Health Code Violations, Public Health Law (Canine Waste), Noise Code Provisions, Sanitation Provisions, Air Code Provisions, Graffiti Law Violations Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings (OATH)

Exam Alert: The 03/27/26 revision now explicitly lists bicyclists, e-bike riders, and e-scooter riders under TVB for traffic infractions. Garages and parking lots are separately listed as returnable to Criminal Court. Know that NYC Criminal Court has concurrent jurisdiction over most VTL traffic infractions (except parking, standing, stopping, and civil-penalty-only offenses).

Exam Alert: For OATH summonses, refer to COMMON OFFENSES RETURNABLE TO THE OFFICE OF ADMINISTRATIVE TRIALS AND HEARINGS (OATH) (PD160-013).

P.G. 209-03: Civil Summons Returnable to OATH — Criminal Justice Reform Act (CJRA)

REVISED: Effective 06/26/25, R.O. 53. Now 5 pages. Updated title. Expanded valid forms of ID to include NYS Driver Permit and other government photo ID. Added VEHICLE REPORT requirement for all vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stops. Added Messenger role for SUMMONS ENVELOPE RECEIPT delivery. Clarified desk officer I-Card processing (PG 208-23). Clarified that Code 4 takes precedence when combined with other codes.

Criminal Justice Reform Act of 2016

The CJRA allows certain violations to be issued as civil summonses returnable to OATH instead of criminal summonses returnable to Criminal Court.

The 6 CJRA Violations

# Violation Administrative Code Section
1 Open Container (alcohol) AC 10-125(b)
2 Public Urination AC 16-118(6)
3 Littering AC 16-118(1)(a)
4 Spitting AC 16-118(1)(b)
5 Excessive Noise (used when not removing a sound reproduction device) AC 24-218
6 NYC Park Rules (New York City Park Rules) Title 56 of the Rules of the City of New York

Exam Alert: Memorize all 6 CJRA violations. A common exam trick is listing a violation NOT on this list (e.g., disorderly conduct) and asking if it qualifies for CJRA civil summons. Also note: CJRA does NOT apply in the transit system — use TAB (Transit Adjudication Bureau) instead.

Exam Alert: For all 6 CJRA violations, do NOT use the Health Code — use the NYC Administrative Code. The exam may present a Health Code section for public urination or littering; the correct source of law is always the Administrative Code for these offenses.

Exam Alert: A Civil Summons Returnable to OATH will not be written in the transit system when issuing summonses under the New York City Transit Rules and Regulations.

Definitions (from revised 209-03)

Term Definition
CRIMINAL JUSTICE REFORM ACT OF 2016 A series of laws passed by the NYC Council that allows certain violations of the NYC Administrative Code and NYC Park Rules to be eligible for a Civil Summons Returnable to OATH
OATH An administrative law court responsible for adjudicating civil summonses
OATH RECIDIVIST A person NOT eligible for a Civil Summons Returnable to OATH (see criteria below)

OATH Recidivist — NOT Eligible for Civil Summons

A violator is classified as an OATH Recidivist and must receive a Criminal Court summons (not OATH civil) if ANY of the following apply:

# Condition
1 2 or more felony arrests in the past 2 years
2 3 or more unanswered Civil Summonses Returnable to OATH in the past 8 years
3 Currently on parole or probation

Exam Alert: The OATH Recidivist criteria are a top exam target. Remember: 2 felonies / 2 years, 3 OATH / 8 years, parole/probation. Any one condition disqualifies the person from receiving a civil summons.

Procedure — UMOS Issuing Civil Summons Returnable to OATH

Step Action
1 Inform violator of offense committed (unless physical resistance, flight, or other factors render impractical)
2 Request violator to show proof of identity and residence
3 Establish violator’s identity and mailing address through observation of valid ID documents
4 Request name check through Communications Section and/or mobile device
5 Issue summonses in numerical order per P.G. 209-36
6 Use black or blue ink ballpoint pen; block letters; legibly
7 Enter all available information required by captions on summons, including return date from D.O./counterpart at roll call
8 Record only one violation per summons — separate summons for each additional offense
9 Enter RTR and circle at end of details section if violator recorded police activities
10 When practical, advise violator that instructions and OATH hearing center location are on rear of summons
11 Request patrol supervisor to respond if legitimate law enforcement reason exists for not issuing OATH
13 After patrol supervisor verification for Code 3, issue Criminal Court summons per P.G. 209-09 and check NYPD CODE 3; complete VEHICLE REPORT in FORMS for every vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stop

Name Check Results — Actions

Name Check Result Action
Active warrant or I-Card labeled Perpetrator - Probable Cause to Arrest Remove violator to command
OATH Recidivist identified Issue Criminal Court summons; check NYPD CODE 1
Criminal Contempt — violator committed act on MTA/NYC Transit property in violation of court order banning entry Remove to command; charge Criminal Contempt 2nd Degree as additional charge

When to Issue a C Summons Instead of an OATH

If a member issues a Criminal Court (C) summons instead of an OATH for one of the 6 CJRA violations, they must note the Reason Code in the appropriate space on the summons.

NOTE: If a violator is stopped for multiple offenses and at least one is a non-CJRA violation answerable in Criminal Court, the summonsing officer will document ALL violations on summonses returnable to Criminal Court. Check NYPD CODE 4 on all summonses. If Code 4 and any other code(s) are applicable, select Code 4.

NYPD Codes for CJRA Processing

Code Meaning Notes
Code 1 OATH Recidivist Person meets recidivist criteria — issue Criminal Court summons
Code 2 Active warrant or Probable Cause I-card Person is being returned to issuing court to adjudicate warrant and summons, or being arrested on a separate charge
Code 3 Law enforcement exception Officer articulated a legitimate law enforcement reason to issue Criminal Court summons in lieu of OATH for a CJRA violation. Requires patrol supervisor verification
Code 4 Non-CJRA violation or multiple violations Violation cited is not covered under CJRA, or subject violated multiple rules in which at least one is returnable to Criminal Court (all summonses returnable to Criminal Court). If Code 4 and any other code(s) are applicable, select Code 4

Sergeant Focus: When a Criminal Court summons is issued under Code 3 (law enforcement exception), a patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response — failure to respond is a procedural violation. Ensure officer details reason in digital Activity Log.

Prior Test: A UMOS writes a C summons instead of an OATH for a CJRA violation. A patrol supervisor must respond — true or false? WRONG — the supervisor must respond ONLY for Code 3 (law enforcement exception). If the officer used Code 1, 2, or 4, no supervisor response is required. The exam tests whether you know the supervisor response is specific to Code 3, not to every C summons.

Exam Alert: PG 209-03 Code 2 covers two scenarios: (1) Active warrant — UMOS writes the summons, D.O. directs an officer (does not have to be the arresting officer) to return to issuing court; if court is closed, process as online arrest. (2) I-Card (Probable Cause to Arrest) — per P.G. 208-23, release violator to responding detective after issuance of summons returnable to Criminal Court; ensure summonsing officer checks NYPD CODE 2.

See Also: PG 208-23 — Computerized Investigation Card System procedures.

Generally Accepted Forms of Identification

# ID Type
1 Valid Photo Driver License
2 Valid passport
3 Citizenship or naturalization papers
4 NYS Non-Driver Identification
5 NYS Driver Permit
6 Municipal Identification Card (IDNYC)
7 Other government photo identification

Exam Alert: Members should note these are general guidelines and other forms of identification may be acceptable. If the violator does not possess valid ID, remove to the command.

CJRA Processing — Desk Officer Role

Step Action
14 Question UMOS regarding the circumstances of the incident and make Command Log entry
— Entry includes: (1) Name, address, physical description of violator; (2) Location, time of incident, reason for removal to command; (3) Name, rank, shield # of member who brought violator; (4) Offense(s) charged and serial number(s) of summons(es) served; (5) Time violator entered and departed the command
15 If violator has active arrest warrant: direct arresting officer to issue summons returnable to Criminal Court; direct an officer to return violator to issuing court; ensure NYPD CODE 2 checked; summonsing officer does not have to be the escorting officer; if court not in session, process as online arrest
16 Comply with P.G. 208-23 if an active INVESTIGATION CARD labeled Perpetrator - Probable Cause to Arrest exists: release violator to responding detective after issuance of summons returnable to Criminal Court (unless processed as online arrest); ensure NYPD CODE 2 checked
17 If violator has no proper ID, make every effort to identify (third party contact, Department computers/devices, digital booking photos, DAS, Mobile ID Device); if violator cannot reasonably be identified, process as an online arrest

Exam Alert: If the violator cannot be identified after every effort (third party contact, Dept computer, booking photo, DAS, Mobile ID Device), process as an online arrest.

CJRA Processing — Operations Coordinator

  • (Step 18) Designate an MOS to enter information from Civil Summons Returnable to OATH into the FORMS application
  • Ensure designated MOS reviews the Details of Charge(s) section of summons and enters required information into FORMS

CJRA Processing — Command Clerk

Step Task Details
19 Separate and process Separate Civil Summons Returnable to OATH by date of service
19a WHITE (OATH) copies Insert all copies issued on SAME day into OATH envelope addressed to OATH, 66 John Street, 10th Floor, New York, N.Y. 10038; forward to patrol borough office with A.M. mail
19b WHITE CARDBOARD (Agency) copy File in command by date of service and retain thereat for two years

Exam Alert: The White Cardboard (Agency) copy is retained at the command for 2 years — not 1 year, not 6 months. This retention period applies to both 209-03 and 209-12 OATH summonses.

CJRA Processing — Patrol Borough Office Clerk

Step Task
20 Prepare two copies of SUMMONS ENVELOPE RECEIPT (PD160-011)
21 Direct messenger to deliver envelope containing Civil Summons Returnable to OATH to the Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings

CJRA Processing — Messenger

Step Task
22 Obtain receipt on one copy of SUMMONS ENVELOPE RECEIPT and return to borough office

Additional Data

  • The desk officer will make the final determination as to whether the violator’s identity, residence, and active warrant and INVESTIGATION CARD history have been ascertained and verified
  • Nothing in this procedure precludes officers from judiciously warning and admonishing individuals for violations of NYC Administrative Codes, the Rules of the City of New York, or NYC Park Rules or summonsing/arresting violators when a corresponding crime exists

Code 4 — Multiple Offenses Key Note

Exam Alert: When a traffic incident under PG 209-09 involves multiple offenses, each separate summons goes to its own court (TVB, C Criminal Court, etc.). If Code 4 applies and one or more is a non-CJRA violation returnable to Criminal Court, all summonses are returnable to Criminal Court. If Code 4 and any other code(s) are applicable, select Code 4. Select Code 4 when issuing C for Transit Rules (PG 209-39).

See Also: PG 209-09 — Traffic incident procedures where multiple summonses each route to their respective court.

P.G. 209-04: Return Date and Time

Return Date Matrix

Summons Type Return Date/Time
Criminal Court Per daily FINEST message
Traffic Violations Bureau (TVB) Per instructions printed on the summons
Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB) 28 calendar days from date of issuance
Parking Violations No date/time entered by officer — information is pre-printed on the summons

TAB Return Date — Special Rules

  • If the 28th day falls on a weekend or holiday, the return date is the next business day
  • Count begins from the date of issuance, not the date of service

Exam Alert: The 28-day TAB return date is frequently tested. Remember: 28 calendar days, and if it falls on a weekend/holiday, roll forward to the next business day.

Common Exam Angles

  1. VTL exceptions to summons issuance — Expect questions listing offenses and asking which require arrest vs. allow summons
  2. OATH Recidivist criteria — Expect questions presenting a scenario and asking if the person qualifies for CJRA civil summons
  3. NYPD Code 3 — Supervisor verification requirement is a favorite Sergeant exam topic
  4. 16-year-old summons eligibility — Expect questions testing the VTL misdemeanor exception
  5. Returnable agency routing — Parking vs. traffic infraction vs. pedestrian offense routing
  6. TAB 28-day return date — Calculation questions including weekend/holiday adjustments

Sergeant Study Notes

  • The desk officer is the final authority on identity/residence verification for CJRA processing
  • A patrol supervisor must physically respond when Code 3 (law enforcement exception) is used — the supervisor must respond and verify, and the UMOS details the reason in their Digital Activity Log
  • For Code 3, the circumstances must be limited and the UMOS must articulate the reason
  • Supervisors should verify that officers check for OATH Recidivist status BEFORE issuing a civil summons
  • The CJRA does NOT apply in the transit system — supervisors must ensure officers use TAB procedures instead
  • When a violator cannot care for themselves (illness, injury, intoxication), a summons is NOT appropriate regardless of the offense — the person must be processed differently

Section 209 — Electronic Summons Tracking System (ESTS)

Covers P.G. 209-36

P.G. 209-36: Electronic Summons Tracking System (ESTS)

Commanding Officer/Designee — Requisition and Safeguard

Task Details
Requisition Parkers & Movers/Cs from Dept of Finance; OATHs from OATH court; TABs from Transit
Safeguard and inspect Verify quantity and numeric order
Irregular summonses Return irregular summonses to the above agencies with a UF50
Summons Box Designate a secure receptacle for served summonses under direct control of D.O. (property room, etc.)

Summons Type Codes

Code Summons Type
A Parking Summons
B Moving Summons
C Criminal Court Summons

Exam Alert: A = Parking, B = Moving, C = Criminal Court. The exam may use these letter codes in questions about distribution, scanning, and forwarding procedures.

Number of Summonses in a Pack

Summons Type Quantity Per Pack
TABs and OATHs 25
A, B, and C 20

Exam Alert: Know the pack sizes: TABs and OATHs come in packs of 25; A (Parking), B (Moving), and C (Criminal Court) come in packs of 20. Examiners test these numbers.

Distribution Card Documentation

  • Ensure summonses are recorded on Distribution Card
    • Record first and last number of each packet in the 1st column
  • A, B, C, and OATH recorded separately on same type of Summons Distribution Card
  • TABs recorded on separate TAB/Notice of Violation Distribution Card

Signing Out New Summonses — UMOS

Step Action
1 Obtain summons pack from D.O./Distributing Supervisor
2 Sign the Distribution Card (to receive summonses)
3 Ensure correct number of summonses in pack and consecutively numbered
4 Document on appropriate certification card (A = Certification of Parking Summonses Served; TABs = Certification of TAB/Notice of Violation Served; B, C, OATH = Certification of Moving/Criminal Court Summonses Served)

Returning Finished Summonses — UMOS

  • When summons pack is finished, sign Certification Card
  • Give summonses to clerical staff (NOT to D.O.)
    • Include appropriate copy of served summonses

Exam Alert: When a summons pack is finished, the UMOS gives it to clerical staff, NOT the D.O. This is a frequently tested detail. The Certification Card is the card the MOS carries on patrol.

Two Signatures — Key Distinction

Card When Signed Purpose
Distribution Card When receiving summonses Acknowledges receipt of new summons pack
Certification Card When returning summonses Certifies all summonses are accounted for

Desk Officer/Distributing Supervisor — Distribution

Task Details
Before issuing Review ESTS to see if officer has unused summonses; determine need for more
Distribute In numerical order
C summons packs Ensure Don’t Enforce Loitering 1,3,4&7 sticker is on each C summons pack
Acknowledge receipt Have officer acknowledge receipt on Distribution Card

Desk Officer/Distributing Supervisor — Unserved and Missing

Situation Action
Unserved summonses Scan unserved summonses back into ESTS
MOS cannot produce unused summonses Notify IAB and obtain Log #
Missing summonses Notify IAB and obtain Log #; notify ICO immediately

Exam Alert: If the ICO is not available when summonses are missing, notify the CO or XO (NOT the Duty Captain). This is a specific exam trap.

Damaged Summonses

  • Break the chain (separate from the pack)
  • Notify C.O. without delay

Bar Code Reader/Scanner Inoperable

The PG gives instructions in 5 separate places for what to do when the bar code reader/scanner is inoperable. The steps are identical every time:

  1. Input summonses manually
  2. Notify the Borough
  3. Call in the Telephone Record

PG Conflict: The PG says Patrol or Transit Borough but does not mention Housing. In March 2020, an I.O. changed 1 of these 5 steps to add Housing but neglected to update the other 4. The exam will likely test the PG language (Patrol or Transit Borough) but know that Housing was added by I.O.

ESTS Inoperable

  • If ESTS is inoperable, enter summonses manually
  • Call Patrol or Transit Borough to replace the reader
  • Document notification to Borough in Telephone Record

Exam Alert: NO ONE other than the D.O./Distributing Supervisor scans summonses. Officers do not scan their own summonses into ESTS.

Unused Summonses Returned

UMOS

Situation Action
Temporary assignment If temporarily assigned to enforcement, obtain sufficient # at enforcement command; return there at end of assignment
Transfer or Separation Give unused summonses to D.O./Distributing Supervisor at command where received

Desk Officer/Distributing Supervisor

  • Scan unused returned summonses same as above
  • Forward with Certification Card to C.O./Designee

Missing Summonses

Key Rules

Scenario Who Is Notified
Officer requests more summonses and D.O. determines officer has unused summonses but cannot produce them D.O. calls IAB
Officer is transferred or separated from the Department and summonses are missing D.O. calls IAB AND notifies ICO immediately; if ICO unavailable, notify CO/XO

Missing Summonses Subsequently Found

After IAB was notified and summons purged from ESTS Exception Report: - Desk Officer calls IAB Command Center IMMEDIATELY to determine if summons can be reentered to ESTS - Enter name of IAB person notified in Add Summons Follow-Up Note in ESTS

Summonses Served — Processing

UMOS

  • Deliver remaining copies, intact, to D.O. at EOT on date of issuance
  • NEVER directly in the receptacle

Exam Alert: The UMOS delivers served summons copies to the D.O. at end of tour on the date of issuance. They are NEVER placed directly into the summons receptacle — the D.O. must examine them first.

Desk Officer

Step Action
1 Examine entries on summons for accuracy, completeness, and legibility
2 Scan summons served during tour prior to EOT
3 If ESTS inoperable: enter manually, call Patrol or Transit Borough to replace reader, document in Telephone Record
4 After summonses scanned, place summonses (including Court Copy) in the summons receptacle
5 Improperly prepared summonses — follow PG 209-06 (steps are identical)

First Platoon D.O./Supervisor

Separation and Forwarding

Task Details
Separate summonses By adjudicating agency AND date of service; count the number returnable to each agency
B summonses (movers) Place Complaint/Information copy in pre-printed envelope; forward to TSO to enter in FORMS
Marijuana summonses Ensure paper clipped to Supporting Deposition/Field Test; photocopy and file at command
Separate Complaint/Information (Pt 1) Separate from remainder of summonses

Traffic Safety Officer

  • Daily — enter info of each B summons served in B Summons app in FORMS using Paper option
  • Enter serial # from top left of summons
  • Store Complaint/Information (Pt.1) copies in a secure location at command

Operations Coordinator

  • Supervise B summonses entry into FORMS app
  • Maintenance of copies kept at command

Scanning and Batching

Parameter Limit
Package (groups for scanning) 100 or less
Scan (at network scanner) No more than 50 simultaneously

Exam Alert: Do NOT mix up the packaging and scanning numbers. Package in groups of 100 or less, but scan no more than 50 at a time. Examiners like to invert these numbers on test day.

Cover Sheet

  • Print a Precinct Cover Sheet from Summons Menu in OMNIFORM for each type of summons (A, B, C, etc.)

Exception Report — Missing Summonses

If a summons remains on the Exception Report (Served but Not Batched), it is considered MISSING: - Notify immediately: ICO (or if ICO unavailable, C.O./X.O.) AND IAB (obtain Log #) - Account for missing summons in ESTS Terminate/Void function — enter IAB Log # in notes - Void: comply with PG 209-18 Summonses Served/Prepared in Error - Purge: void purges missing summons from Exception Report; summons # and history remain in ESTS

Forwarding Summonses

Pre-Printed Envelopes

  • Place Scanned Complaint/Information copies in pre-printed envelopes and SEAL them
  • Fill out the captions on envelope
  • Sign on appropriate space on the flap of envelope
  • Enter ESTS generated Tracking # below the flap on envelope

Transmittal Report

  • Print a Transmittal Report from ESTS
  • Attach to pre-printed envelopes
  • Forward Transmittal Sheet and Pre-Printed Envelopes daily, each morning to boro/designated command
    • Forward sealed envelopes daily, regardless of number enclosed

Housing Bureau

  • Messenger takes envelopes from PSA directly to court (PVB, OATH, Criminal, etc.)

Outside Agencies Issued Summonses

  • Do NOT scan outside agency summonses
  • Traffic safety does NOT enter outside agency B summonses into the application in FORMS
  • Place summonses by outside agency in separate envelope marked Outside Agency
  • Forward summonses as indicated above

Exam Alert: Outside agency summonses must be kept completely separate — do not scan them, do not enter them in FORMS. They go in their own envelope marked Outside Agency.

Remaining Copies of Summonses

  • Place in a separate Dept envelope
  • Mark envelope File and leave envelope UNSEALED

Clerical Member

  • File envelope with command file copies of summonses served in command
  • By date of service in command record room
  • Maintain file copies for six months
  • After six months, remove file copies and retain in storage cartons in command record room

Integrity Control Officer (ICO) Responsibilities

ICO Weekly

Report Action
ESTS Exception Report Generate and review; if discrepancies found, notify IAB and obtain Log #
Scanned Summons Analysis Report Monitor in Omniform — Supervisor function of Summons Menu — to ensure summonses are being scanned into network scanner including cover sheet

ICO Monthly

Report Action
ICO Ageing Report Monitor for summonses distributed but not served for 90 days (NOT 30 days)
Self-Inspection Reports ESTS Self Inspection to ensure all summonses accounted for; B Summons Self-Inspection of app in FORMS

Exam Alert: The ICO Ageing Report threshold is 90 days, not 30 days. If any UMOS is listed on the ICO Ageing Report, the ICO must conduct an IMMEDIATE investigation. This is a commonly tested number.

ICO — Warrant and Missing Summons Checks

Task Details
Warrant checks Ensure warrant checks were conducted
Missing summonses Confirm notification to IAB and Log # obtained; ensure 1st Platoon D.O. made entries regarding missing summonses in ESTS including IAB Log #; notify C.O./X.O. of missing summons

C.O./X.O. — Missing Summonses

Task Details
Notification Confirm notification to IAB and IAB Log # was obtained
ESTS Confirm ESTS System was updated with IAB Log #
Void and Document Ensure missing summonses were terminated/voided in ESTS and PG 209-18 was complied with

Common Exam Angles

  1. Summons type codes — A = Parking, B = Moving, C = Criminal Court
  2. Pack sizes — TABs/OATHs = 25; A/B/C = 20
  3. Two signatures — Distribution Card (receive) vs. Certification Card (return)
  4. Finished summonses — Go to clerical staff, NOT the D.O.
  5. Missing summonses — Notify IAB and ICO immediately; if ICO unavailable, notify CO/XO (not Duty Captain)
  6. Bar code reader inoperable — 5 identical steps in PG; Patrol or Transit Borough (Housing added by I.O.)
  7. Package vs. scan limits — Package 100 or less, scan 50 or less (examiners invert these)
  8. ICO Ageing Report — 90 days, not 30; UMOS on report = IMMEDIATE investigation
  9. Outside agency summonses — Do NOT scan, do NOT enter in FORMS, separate envelope
  10. Loitering sticker — Don’t Enforce Loitering 1,3,4&7 sticker on each C summons pack

Sergeant Study Notes

  • Ensure officers sign the Distribution Card when receiving summonses and the Certification Card when returning
  • Review ESTS regularly to verify no officer has unused summonses outstanding for extended periods
  • When distributing summonses, always distribute in numerical order and verify consecutive numbering
  • Monitor the ICO Ageing Report monthly — any UMOS with summonses outstanding 90+ days requires immediate investigation
  • Verify that the Don’t Enforce Loitering 1,3,4&7 sticker is present on all C summons packs before distribution
  • NO ONE other than the D.O./Distributing Supervisor should be scanning summonses into ESTS
  • Damaged summonses: break the chain and notify C.O. without delay

Section 209 — OATH and Environmental Control Board Procedures

Covers P.G. 209-12, 209-20

P.G. 209-12: OATH Summonses (Non-CJRA)

OATH Jurisdiction

The Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings (OATH) handles civil enforcement of various city code violations that do not rise to criminal court level. These are separate from the 6 CJRA violations covered in P.G. 209-03.

OATH Jurisdiction — Source of Law

Non-CJRA OATH summonses are written ONLY for NYC codes: - Administrative Code and - Parks Regs (RCNY — Rules of the City of New York)

Exam Alert: Do NOT issue OATH summonses for State codes such as the Vehicle and Traffic Law (VTL) or the Alcoholic Beverage Control Law (ABC). State code violations go to Criminal Court or TVB, not OATH.

Non-CJRA OATH Offenses

Offense Category Examples
Food/General Vendor violations Unlicensed vending, improper vending location
Health Code violations Sanitary code infractions
Public Health Law — Canine Waste Failure to clean up after a dog
Noise Code violations Commercial or construction noise violations
Sanitation violations Improper waste disposal, recycling violations
Air Code violations Emission and air quality infractions
Graffiti violations Possession of graffiti instruments, vandalism

OATH vs. CJRA Distinction

Feature CJRA Civil Summons (P.G. 209-03) Non-CJRA OATH Summons (P.G. 209-12)
Violations 6 specific CJRA violations only Vendor, health, noise, sanitation, air, graffiti
Recidivist check Required (OATH Recidivist criteria) Not applicable
NYPD Codes Codes 1-4 apply Not applicable
Transit system NOT applicable (use TAB) NOT applicable in transit
Returnable to OATH OATH

Exam Alert: Do not confuse CJRA civil summonses with non-CJRA OATH summonses. Both are returnable to OATH, but CJRA summonses require recidivist screening and NYPD Code assignment. Non-CJRA OATH summonses do not.

Civil Penalty Only

  • If the offense has ONLY a civil penalty (e.g., Cigarette Sale to Minor), the violator cannot be taken to the station house
  • Issue the OATH summons in the field

Exam Alert: An OATH summons for a civil-penalty-only offense cannot result in removal to the command. The violator must be processed in the field. Do not confuse this with CJRA violations where removal is permitted for ID purposes.

Canine Waste Law

  • NEVER write a blind person for a canine waste violation
  • Use discretion regarding elderly and handicapped individuals

Violation NOT Observed by UMOS

When the UMOS did not personally observe the violation: - Probable cause for the OATH summons can be based on a witness statement - In the Details of Charge(s) section, enter: informed by [witness name], whose contact info is known to the NYPD, that he observed… - Complete the Statement of Witness section on the back of the White (OATH) copy - Have the witness sign to indicate they witnessed the offense and the summons is accurate - If the witness refuses to ID self and/or sign, write REFUSED in the witness signature caption - Enter all witness contact information in the Digital Activity Log

PG Conflict: PG 209-03 OATH summonses (CJRA) can NOT be based on informed by — those 6 violations must be personally observed by the UMOS. PG 209-12 OATH summonses (non-CJRA) CAN be informed by a witness. Know both rules exist.

209-12 Key Differences from 209-03

Feature PG 209-03 (CJRA) PG 209-12 (Non-CJRA)
Violations 6 specific CJRA violations only Any NYC Admin Code / RCNY violation
Informed by witness NOT permitted — must be observed Permitted
Recidivist check Required Not applicable
NYPD Codes Codes 1-4 Use Code 4 box for non-CJRA
Parks Regs described as Rules of the City of NY RCNY

UMOS Processing — Non-CJRA OATH

Step Action
1 Inform violator of offense
2 Obtain ID and residence (if refused or in doubt — take to Precinct, no mention of P.S.)
3 If properly IDed, separate OATH for each violation
4 Write in BLOCK letters with blue or black ballpoint pen
5 Confer with Desk Officer regarding arrest if violation could go on OATH or C summons — D.O. authorizes arrest to Criminal Court
6 If violator has recorded police activity, enter RTR and circle at end of details section

Non-CJRA OATH Summons Completion

  • Use OATH summons form — insert 3-digit OATH code, Section of Law, and Mail-in and Maximum penalties
  • Fill in all captions, especially the Affidavit on back
  • Sign in TWO places: bottom front AND the Affidavit
  • Return date: obtained from Desk Officer via FINEST, EXCEPT TEA (Traffic Enforcement Agents) who use their existing schedule
  • For non-CJRA OATH offense, check the Code 4 box

Exam Alert: The officer must sign the OATH summons in TWO places — bottom of the front and the Affidavit (Certificate of Service) on the back. If the Affidavit is not signed, the summons will be dismissed.

Person Less Than 18 Years of Age

When serving an OATH summons on a person under 18: - Serve the parent or guardian either: - In person when the summons is served, OR - By mail

OATH Copy Distribution

Copy Disposition
White Cardboard (Agency Copy) Retained with the Certification Card
White (OATH Copy) Sent to command of occurrence at EOT as directed by C.O.
Yellow Issuing officer keeps
Pink (Violator/Respondent Copy) Given to violator

Exam Alert: For vehicle alarm violations (PG 214-24), the Pink copy goes with the Certification Card instead of the White Cardboard. This is a specific exception to the normal copy distribution.

Processing Requirements

  • Proper identification of the violator
  • Accurate recording of the offense and applicable code section
  • Correct OATH return information
  • Service of summons copy to the violator

P.G. 209-20: Environmental Control Board (ECB) Procedures

Covers enforcement procedures for violations heard by the Environmental Control Board (now part of OATH), including: - Environmental violations within NYC - Building code violations - Zoning violations - Coordination with city inspection agencies - Proper ECB summons completion

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors should ensure officers correctly route OATH-eligible offenses to OATH rather than Criminal Court. Misrouting creates unnecessary court appearances and administrative burden.

Common Exam Angles

  1. CJRA vs. non-CJRA OATH — Both go to OATH, but CJRA requires recidivist screening
  2. OATH offense categories — Know which violations are OATH-returnable
  3. Routing errors — Questions may present a scenario where an officer incorrectly routes an OATH violation to Criminal Court
  4. Transit system exclusion — OATH summonses (both CJRA and non-CJRA) do NOT apply in the transit system

Sergeant Study Notes

  • Train officers on the distinction between CJRA and non-CJRA OATH violations
  • Review summons routing to catch misrouted violations
  • Ensure officers serving OATH summonses include accurate code sections and offense descriptions
  • Verify that non-CJRA OATH summonses are not being subjected to unnecessary recidivist screening

Section 209 — Parking Summonses and Related Procedures

Covers P.G. 209-07, 209-08, 209-27

P.G. 209-07: Parking Summonses

Abandoned and Derelict Vehicle Definitions

Classification Definition
Abandoned Vehicle (1) No plates displayed for more than 6 hours; OR (2) On public street for more than 7 consecutive days; OR (3) On another person’s property for more than 96 hours
Derelict Vehicle Dilapidated, burned, stripped vehicle with no value except as scrap

Exam Alert: Know all three abandoned vehicle timeframes: 6 hours (no plates), 7 days (public street), 96 hours (another’s property). Do NOT issue parking summonses to abandoned or derelict vehicles — they require different processing.

5 Mandatory Identification Elements

Omission of ANY of these elements results in DISMISSAL of the summons:

# Element
1 License plate number
2 Plate type
3 Expiration date
4 Make (manufacturer)
5 Model

Exam Alert: The 5 mandatory ID elements are one of the most frequently tested topics in Section 209. Omission of even ONE element = automatic dismissal. Remember: Number, Type, Expiration, Make, Model.

Summons Limits and Restrictions

Rule Details
One offense per summons Each summons covers only one parking violation
Maximum 3 summonses No more than 3 summonses to the same vehicle at the same location per day
Already has 3+ Do NOT issue additional summonses if vehicle already has 3 or more summonses displayed on windshield

Out-of-State Plates — Plate Cover Violations

  • Do NOT ordinarily issue summonses for local plate cover violations (VTL 402) to vehicles with out-of-state plates
  • Exception: Issue summons if the vehicle’s home state also prohibits plate covers

Stolen Vehicle Check

Query NYSPIN to check if the vehicle is stolen when: - A prior summons is already on the vehicle - Damage is consistent with theft - The vehicle is in an unusual location or observed at an unusual time

Service of Parking Summons

Step Action
1 Place service copy on windshield
2 Include orange self-addressed envelope
3 In inclement weather, fold the summons to protect it

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors should periodically review parking summons issuance to verify all 5 mandatory elements are present. A pattern of dismissals due to missing elements indicates a training deficiency.

P.G. 209-08: Parking Summons — Additional Procedures

Covers supplemental procedures for parking summons issuance including: - Special parking enforcement zones - Meter violations - Alternate side parking rules - Coordination with traffic enforcement agents

P.G. 209-27: Removal of Ignition Key

When to Act

  • Vehicle is unattended with the key in the ignition for more than 3 minutes

Procedure

Step Action
1 Lock the ignition and remove the key
2 Prepare REMOVAL OF IGNITION KEY form (PD571-121) — 2 copies
3 Attach one copy to the steering wheel
4 Attach the other copy to the key

Disposition

Scenario Action
Violator returns Issue summons, return key, destroy the form
Not claimed within 48 hours Desk officer invoices key to Property Clerk

Exam Alert: The 48-hour window before Property Clerk invoicing and the 3-minute threshold for unattended vehicles are commonly tested timeframes.

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer is responsible for tracking unclaimed keys and ensuring timely invoicing to the Property Clerk after 48 hours. Supervisors should include ignition key removals in their review of daily activity.

Common Exam Angles

  1. Abandoned vehicle timeframes — 6 hours (no plates), 7 days (public street), 96 hours (another’s property)
  2. 5 mandatory ID elements — Any omission = dismissal
  3. 3-summons maximum — Same vehicle, same location, per day
  4. Ignition key timeframes — 3 minutes unattended, 48 hours to Property Clerk
  5. Out-of-state plate covers — Generally do not issue unless home state prohibits
  6. NYSPIN check triggers — Prior summons, theft-consistent damage, unusual location/time
  7. Derelict vs. abandoned — Know the distinction; neither gets a parking summons

Sergeant Study Notes

  • Train officers on the 5 mandatory elements — dismissals waste enforcement effort
  • Review the 3-summons limit to prevent excessive issuance to the same vehicle
  • Ensure officers query NYSPIN when indicators of vehicle theft are present
  • Monitor ignition key removal logs to verify the 48-hour invoicing deadline
  • Verify officers are not issuing parking summonses to abandoned or derelict vehicles

Section 209 — Specialized Summons Procedures

Covers P.G. 209-21, 209-22, 209-23, 209-24, 209-26, 209-28, 209-30, 209-31, 209-32, 209-33, 209-34, 209-35, 209-37, 209-39, 209-40

P.G. 209-24: Mandatory Driver’s License Checks

When License Checks Are Mandatory

Situation License Check Required?
Traffic collisions Yes — mandatory
Car stops Yes — mandatory
Other investigatory situations involving a motor vehicle operator Yes — mandatory

Method

  • Use RMP tablet as the primary tool
  • If no tablet available, use department smartphone
  • Do NOT unnecessarily detain a person solely for the purpose of conducting a license check if no other cause for detention exists

Exam Alert: PG 209-24 is a driver’s license check — this is NOT a warrant check (that is the next procedure). Do not confuse the two on the exam.

DMV System Inoperative

  • If the DMV computer system is inoperative and circumstances do not warrant detention, and the operator possesses valid license, registration, and insurance:
    • Make a Digital Activity Log entry with: name, address, phone, DOB, and License #
  • Do NOT delay the stop indefinitely waiting for the system

Exam Alert: License checks are mandatory at traffic collisions, car stops, and investigatory situations — but officers may NOT unnecessarily detain a person solely for a license check when no other cause exists.

Search DMV Records

Method Details
Pedigree tab Search using last name, first name, DOB, and gender
License tab Secondary check of operator’s Client ID
  • Examine DMV files with the same name and address but different dates of birth
  • Examine other documents for other names, addresses, or DOBs
  • Be alert for out-of-state licenses which may have been issued with lax ID standards
  • VOUCHER any items suggesting additional driving records/identifications
  • Review suspect’s criminal history in Dept databases — do Name Search on DAS, NYPSIN, and Accurint

Consolidation of Client ID Request

  • Fill out Consolidation of Client ID Request if applicable
  • Attach pertinent supporting data
  • Give to D.O. for review before distributing as per instructions on form

Exam Alert: The Consolidation of Client ID Request form is given to the D.O. for review before distribution — this follows the general rule. However, in most other contexts, the D.O. distributes the form. The exam may test who distributes it: here in 209-24, it is the UMOS who fills it out and gives it to the D.O., not the D.O. who distributes it.

False Personation

  • If an operator intentionally lies regarding ID to deceive a police officer, they can be charged with False Personation — a Class B Misdemeanor (PL 190.23)

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors should monitor that officers conduct mandatory license checks at every qualifying stop. The Activity Log entry when DMV is inoperative creates the audit trail.

P.G. 209-26: Suspended or Revoked License

Confiscation Procedures

Step Action
1 Confiscate the license
2 Prepare SEIZED DRIVER’S LICENSE RECEIPT/REPORT (PD634-152)
3 Do NOT mark or mutilate the confiscated license
4 Park the violator’s vehicle in a legal area until the registered owner arranges removal

14-Day Correction Claim

If the violator claims the suspension/revocation was corrected within the past 14 days, accept any of the following: - Newly validated license - Temporary license - DMV receipt showing correction

False Personation Warning

  • Be alert for False Personation (PL 190.23) — a violator may provide a false identity to avoid detection of the suspended/revoked status

Exam Alert: The 14-day correction window and the three acceptable forms of proof (validated license, temporary license, DMV receipt) are commonly tested. Also remember: do NOT mark or mutilate the confiscated license.

Sergeant Focus: Verify that officers park the violator’s vehicle in a legal area rather than leaving it in traffic. The vehicle remains until the registered owner (not the violator with the suspended license) arranges removal.

P.G. 209-28: Unlicensed Tow Truck Operators

Enforcement

  • Issue summons for AC 20-496(b) returnable to Criminal Court

Exempt Entities

Exempt Category
Government vehicles
Vehicle dismantlers
Public transit vehicles
Bus companies
Utility companies
TLC-licensed taxi/livery vehicles
School buses
Vehicle rental companies

Out-of-City Tow Trucks — Exemptions

The tow truck licensing requirement does NOT apply to out-of-city tow trucks that are: - Passing through NYC - Picking up a vehicle to transport outside the city - Dropping off a vehicle that originated from outside the city

Exam Alert: Know the three out-of-city exemptions: passing through, picking up for transport outside, dropping off from outside. A tow truck from New Jersey picking up a vehicle in Manhattan to tow it to New Jersey is exempt.

P.G. 209-30: Summons on Department Vehicle

Definitions

Term Definition
Urgent Police Action Response to an emergency, active pursuit, or moving surveillance
Necessary Police Action Less than urgent; enhances safety of an operation; NOT solely for the member’s convenience
Valid Verifiable Defense A legitimate defense to the summons that can be verified through department records

Safety Violations

The following are classified as safety violations — they are NOT eligible for dismissal unless the vehicle was engaged in urgent or necessary police action:

Safety Violation
Double parking
Fire hydrant
Sidewalk
Bus stop
Crosswalk
Obstruction of traffic
No standing
Snow emergency
No parking — authorized vehicles
No stopping
No parking — taxi stand

Processing Timeline

  • Forward SUMMONS PLEA FORM through channels within 30 days of issuance

Exam Alert: The distinction between urgent police action (emergency, pursuit, surveillance) and necessary police action (enhances safety, NOT for convenience) is heavily tested. Safety violations require proof of urgent OR necessary police action — convenience alone is NEVER a valid defense.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must review Summons Plea Forms for accuracy and ensure they are forwarded within the 30-day deadline. Verify that officers claiming necessary police action can articulate how the action enhanced safety — convenience is explicitly excluded.

P.G. 209-31 through 209-34: Voided Summonses

P.G. 209-31: Voiding a Summons — General Procedures

Covers the circumstances under which a summons may be voided, including: - Clerical errors rendering the summons defective - Improper service - Documentation requirements for voided summonses

P.G. 209-32: Voiding a Parking Summons

Covers specific procedures for voiding parking summonses, including: - Supervisor authorization requirements - Required documentation - Notification to Parking Violations Operations

P.G. 209-33: Voiding a TVB Summons

Covers specific procedures for voiding Traffic Violations Bureau summonses, including: - Supervisor review and approval - Notification requirements - Record-keeping obligations

P.G. 209-34: Voiding a Criminal Court Summons

Covers specific procedures for voiding Criminal Court summonses, including: - Authorization chain - Court notification requirements - Documentation and accountability

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors play a critical role in the summons voiding process. Every voided summons requires supervisory review to prevent abuse. Monitor voiding patterns — an officer with an unusually high void rate may indicate a training issue or integrity concern.

P.G. 209-21, 209-22, 209-23: Additional Summons Procedures

P.G. 209-21: Summons Accountability

Covers the tracking and accountability requirements for summons books, including: - Issuance and return of summons books - Serial number tracking - Supervisory review of summons activity

P.G. 209-22: Summons Records

Covers record-keeping obligations for all issued summonses, including: - Activity Log entries - Command log entries - Statistical reporting

P.G. 209-23: Summons Dispositions

Covers procedures for tracking the outcome of issued summonses, including: - Court appearance results - Default judgments - Fine collection notifications

P.G. 209-35 and 209-37: Electronic Monitoring

P.G. 209-35: GPS/Electronic Tracking Devices

Covers the legal requirements and procedures for the use of electronic tracking devices in connection with summons enforcement, including: - Court order requirements - Installation and removal procedures - Privacy protections

P.G. 209-37: Speed Detection Devices

Covers the use of speed detection equipment for traffic enforcement, including: - Radar and LIDAR operation - Calibration and testing requirements - Officer training and certification - Documentation for court proceedings

P.G. 209-39: Red Light Camera Violations

Covers procedures related to automated red light camera enforcement, including: - Camera-generated violation processing - Owner liability (vs. operator liability) - Adjudication through the Department of Finance - No points assessed on driver’s license

P.G. 209-40: Speed Camera Violations
