Covers procedures related to automated speed camera enforcement, including: - School zone speed camera operations - Camera-generated violation processing - Owner liability provisions - Adjudication through the Department of Finance - Fine structure and penalties

Exam Alert: Both red light camera and speed camera violations are civil penalties assessed against the vehicle owner, not the operator. No points are added to the driver’s license. These are processed through the Department of Finance, not Criminal Court or TVB.

Common Exam Angles

  1. Mandatory license checks — Know the three situations (collision, car stop, investigatory)
  2. Suspended license — 14-day correction — Three acceptable forms of proof
  3. Department vehicle summons — Urgent vs. necessary police action vs. convenience
  4. Safety violations list — Must have urgent or necessary police action to defend
  5. 30-day Summons Plea Form deadline — Forwarded through channels
  6. Tow truck exemptions — 8 exempt categories plus 3 out-of-city exemptions
  7. Voided summonses — Supervisor authorization required for all types
  8. Red light/speed camera — Owner liability, no points, Dept of Finance adjudication
  9. False Personation — Alert when processing suspended/revoked license stops

Sergeant Study Notes

  • Review officers’ mandatory license check compliance at every car stop and collision
  • Ensure Summons Plea Forms are submitted within 30 days — late submissions may result in default judgments against MOS
  • Monitor voided summons patterns for training deficiencies or integrity concerns
  • Verify that officers conducting suspended license stops park the vehicle legally and prepare PD634-152
  • When reviewing department vehicle summons defenses, scrutinize necessary police action claims — the member must show the action enhanced safety, not just convenience
  • Ensure officers understand that camera-generated violations are owner liability, civil penalties with no license points

Section 209 — Traffic Summonses and Personal Service

Covers P.G. 209-09, 209-10, 209-11, 209-15, 209-16, 209-17, 209-18, 209-19

P.G. 209-09: Personal Service of Summonses (TVB/Criminal Court)

Required Steps for Summons Issuance

Step Action
1 Inform the violator of the offense committed (unless resistance or flight makes this impractical)
2 Request proof of identity and residence
3 If no valid ID → remove to command for identification
4 If violator has an active warrant → process warrant arrest
5 One violation per summons — issue separate summons for each offense

Vehicle Report Requirement

  • Complete a VEHICLE REPORT in FORMS for every vehicle stop (car, e-bike, e-scooter, motor scooter, bicycle, etc.)
  • This applies whether enforcement action is taken or not

Exam Alert: The Vehicle Report requirement applies to ALL vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stops — not just those resulting in a summons. RTR notation is required when the violator exercises their right to record.

Recording by Violator

  • If the violator records police activities during the stop, note RTR (Right to Record) on the summons
  • Circle at the end of the details section

Exam Alert: RTR stands for Right to Record — it must be entered on the summons AND circled at the end of the details section. This applies to both traffic stops (209-09) and OATH encounters (209-03, 209-12). Per I.O. 2s.21, if the violator has recorded police activity, enter RTR.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must verify that Vehicle Reports are completed for every stop. Missing reports indicate either unreported stops or incomplete documentation — both require corrective action.

P.G. 209-10: TVB Summons Procedures

Covers specific procedures for issuing summonses returnable to the Traffic Violations Bureau (TVB), including: - Proper completion of TVB summons forms - Offense code selection - Supporting deposition requirements - Officer’s obligation to appear at TVB hearings

P.G. 209-11: Criminal Court Traffic Summons

Covers procedures for issuing traffic-related summonses returnable to Criminal Court, including: - Traffic misdemeanor processing - Pedestrian violations - Coordination with Criminal Court scheduling - Return date assignment per FINEST message

P.G. 209-15: Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB) Summonses

TAB Jurisdiction

  • Covers violations occurring within the transit system
  • CJRA civil summonses do NOT apply in the transit system — use TAB instead

Return Date

  • 28 calendar days from date of issuance
  • If the 28th day falls on a weekend or holiday → next business day

Exam Alert: Remember that CJRA/OATH civil summonses are NOT used in the transit system. Transit violations go through TAB with the 28-day return date.

P.G. 209-16: TAB Summons — Additional Procedures

Covers supplemental TAB processing, including: - Fare evasion enforcement - Transit rules of conduct violations - Coordination with Transit Bureau

P.G. 209-17: E-Bike and E-Scooter Summonses

Covers enforcement procedures specific to electric bicycles and electric scooters, including: - Classification of e-bikes and e-scooters - Applicable violations - Summons routing (TVB, Criminal Court, or TAB depending on location) - Vehicle Report requirements for all e-bike/e-scooter stops

P.G. 209-18: Bicycle Summonses

Covers enforcement procedures for bicycle-related violations, including: - Applicable VTL sections for bicycles - Sidewalk riding enforcement - Helmet requirements for commercial cyclists - Vehicle Report requirements

P.G. 209-19: Pedestrian Summonses

Covers enforcement procedures for pedestrian violations, including: - Jaywalking and crosswalk violations - Returnable to NYC Criminal Court (not TVB) - Personal service requirements - Identity verification procedures

Common Exam Angles

  1. Personal service requirements — Inform of offense, request ID, one violation per summons
  2. Vehicle Report — Required for ALL vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stops, not just summons issuance
  3. RTR notation — Required when violator records police activities
  4. No valid ID — Remove to command; do NOT release without identification
  5. Active warrant during stop — Process the warrant arrest
  6. TAB vs. CJRA — Transit system uses TAB, not CJRA/OATH civil summons
  7. 28-day TAB return — Calendar days, weekend/holiday rolls to next business day

Sergeant Study Notes

  • Audit Vehicle Report completion rates to ensure all stops are documented
  • Verify officers understand that RTR notation is required, not optional
  • When reviewing summons activity, check that each summons contains only one violation
  • Ensure officers know the difference between TVB-returnable and Criminal Court-returnable offenses
  • Monitor that officers remove violators to the command when no valid ID is presented, rather than releasing them

Section 209 — Key Terms, Forms, and Abbreviations

Core Summons Definitions

Term Definition Source
Summons A written directive ordering a person to appear before a court or administrative tribunal at a specified date and time to answer for a violation or offense P.G. 209-01
Civil Summons (OATH) A summons issued under the CJRA for one of 6 specified violations, returnable to OATH instead of Criminal Court P.G. 209-03
Personal Service Direct hand-delivery of a summons to the violator, with verbal notification of the offense charged P.G. 209-09

CJRA and OATH Terms

Term Definition Source
CJRA (Criminal Justice Reform Act of 2016) Legislation allowing 6 specified violations to be processed as civil summonses returnable to OATH rather than criminal summonses returnable to Criminal Court P.G. 209-03
OATH (Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings) NYC administrative tribunal that adjudicates civil violations including CJRA offenses, vendor violations, health code violations, noise complaints, and environmental offenses P.G. 209-03, 209-12
OATH Recidivist A person ineligible for CJRA civil summons due to: (1) 2+ felony arrests in past 2 years, (2) 3+ unanswered OATH summonses in past 8 years, OR (3) currently on parole or probation P.G. 209-03
ECB (Environmental Control Board) Now part of OATH; adjudicates environmental, building, and zoning violations P.G. 209-20

CJRA Violations — The 6 Qualifying Offenses

# Violation Code Section Source
1 Open Container (alcohol) AC 10-125(b) P.G. 209-03
2 Public Urination AC 16-118(6) P.G. 209-03
3 Littering AC 16-118(1)(a) P.G. 209-03
4 Spitting AC 16-118(1)(b) P.G. 209-03
5 Excessive Noise AC 24-218 P.G. 209-03
6 NYC Park Rules Title 56 P.G. 209-03

NYPD Codes (CJRA Processing)

Code Meaning Supervisor Involvement Source
Code 1 OATH Recidivist — issue Criminal Court summons None required P.G. 209-03
Code 2 Active warrant or I-card — issue Criminal Court summons None required P.G. 209-03
Code 3 Law enforcement exception — issue Criminal Court summons Patrol supervisor MUST respond to verify P.G. 209-03
Code 4 Non-CJRA violation or multiple violations None required P.G. 209-03

Vehicle Definitions

Term Definition Source
Abandoned Vehicle A vehicle with: (1) no plates for 6+ hours, (2) on public street for 7+ consecutive days, OR (3) on another’s property for 96+ hours P.G. 209-07
Derelict Vehicle A vehicle that is dilapidated, burned, or stripped, with no value except as scrap metal P.G. 209-07

Department Vehicle Summons Terms

Term Definition Source
Urgent Police Action Response to an emergency, active pursuit, or moving surveillance P.G. 209-30
Necessary Police Action Less than urgent; enhances safety of an operation; NOT solely for the member’s convenience P.G. 209-30
Valid Verifiable Defense A legitimate, verifiable defense to a summons; safety violations are NOT eligible unless urgent or necessary police action occurred P.G. 209-30
Safety Violation Specific parking violations (double parking, fire hydrant, sidewalk, bus stop, crosswalk, obstruction of traffic, no standing, snow emergency, no parking-authorized vehicles, no stopping, no parking-taxi stand) requiring urgent/necessary police action defense P.G. 209-30

License-Related Terms

Term Definition Source
Suspended License A driver’s license that has been temporarily withdrawn by DMV; operator is prohibited from driving P.G. 209-26
Revoked License A driver’s license that has been permanently withdrawn by DMV; operator must reapply P.G. 209-26
14-Day Correction Window Period within which a violator may claim suspension/revocation was corrected; accept validated license, temporary license, or DMV receipt P.G. 209-26
False Personation PL 190.23; providing a false identity, particularly relevant during suspended/revoked license stops P.G. 209-26

Returnable Agencies

Agency Abbreviation Handles Source
NYC Department of Finance — Parking Violations Operations DOF/PVO Parking summonses P.G. 209-02
NYS DMV Traffic Violations Bureau TVB Non-parking traffic infractions P.G. 209-02
NYC Criminal Court — Traffic misdemeanors, pedestrian offenses, Criminal Court summonses P.G. 209-02
Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings OATH CJRA civil summonses, vendor/health/noise/sanitation/air/graffiti violations P.G. 209-02, 209-03
Transit Adjudication Bureau TAB Transit system violations (28-day return) P.G. 209-04, 209-15

DAT vs. Summons Distinction

Feature DAT (Desk Appearance Ticket) Summons
Issued after Custodial arrest and processing Field encounter (no arrest)
Requires arrest Yes — person is arrested, processed, then released with DAT No — issued in lieu of arrest
Fingerprinted Yes No
Eligible offenses Class E felonies, misdemeanors, violations Violations, infractions, certain misdemeanors
Reference P.G. 208-27 P.G. 209-01

Forms

Form Name Form Number Purpose Reference
Parking Summons — Issued for parking violations; returnable to DOF/PVO P.G. 209-07
TVB Summons — Issued for non-parking traffic infractions; returnable to TVB P.G. 209-10
Criminal Court Summons — Issued for traffic misdemeanors, pedestrian offenses, other Criminal Court matters P.G. 209-11
OATH Civil Summons — Issued for CJRA violations and other OATH-eligible offenses P.G. 209-03, 209-12
TAB Summons — Issued for transit system violations; 28-day return P.G. 209-15
REMOVAL OF IGNITION KEY PD571-121 Prepared in duplicate when key removed from unattended vehicle; one copy on steering wheel, one attached to key P.G. 209-27
SEIZED DRIVER’S LICENSE RECEIPT/REPORT PD634-152 Prepared when confiscating a suspended/revoked license P.G. 209-26
SUMMONS PLEA FORM — Used to contest a summons issued to a department vehicle; must be forwarded within 30 days P.G. 209-30
VEHICLE REPORT — Completed in FORMS for every vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stop P.G. 209-09

Abbreviations

Abbreviation Full Name Context
AC Administrative Code (NYC) Source of CJRA violations and other city offenses
BWC Body-Worn Camera Activated during enforcement encounters
CJRA Criminal Justice Reform Act (2016) Allows 6 violations as civil summonses to OATH
DMV Department of Motor Vehicles (NYS) Operates the TVB; license status checks
DOF Department of Finance (NYC) Adjudicates parking violations, red light/speed camera violations
ECB Environmental Control Board Now part of OATH; environmental/building/zoning violations
NYSPIN New York Statewide Police Information Network Used to check stolen vehicle status
OATH Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings Adjudicates civil summonses
PL Penal Law (NYS) Source of criminal violations
PVO Parking Violations Operations Division within DOF handling parking summonses
RTR Right to Record Notation on summons when violator records police activity
TAB Transit Adjudication Bureau Adjudicates transit system violations; 28-day return
TVB Traffic Violations Bureau Adjudicates non-parking traffic infractions
VTL Vehicle and Traffic Law (NYS) Source of traffic offenses

Critical Numbers

Number What It Represents Source
3 minutes Threshold for unattended vehicle with key in ignition before removal P.G. 209-27
6 hours No plates displayed = abandoned vehicle P.G. 209-07
7 days On public street = abandoned vehicle P.G. 209-07
96 hours On another’s property = abandoned vehicle P.G. 209-07
48 hours Unclaimed ignition key invoiced to Property Clerk P.G. 209-27
14 days Correction window for suspended/revoked license claims P.G. 209-26
28 days TAB summons return date (calendar days from issuance) P.G. 209-04
30 days Deadline to forward Summons Plea Form for dept vehicle summons P.G. 209-30
2 years Lookback period for felony arrests (OATH Recidivist — 2+ arrests) P.G. 209-03
8 years Lookback period for unanswered OATH summonses (Recidivist — 3+ unanswered) P.G. 209-03
3 Maximum parking summonses to same vehicle at same location per day P.G. 209-07
5 Mandatory ID elements on parking summons (omission = dismissal) P.G. 209-07
6 Number of CJRA-qualifying violations P.G. 209-03
16 Minimum age to receive a summons P.G. 209-01

Section 209 — Review Questions

15 multiple-choice questions covering key Sergeant exam topics from Section 209.

Question 1

An officer stops a 16-year-old who committed a non-VTL misdemeanor. Can the officer issue a Criminal Court summons?

A. Yes, 16-year-olds may receive summonses for all offenses B. Yes, but only with supervisor approval C. No, 16-year-olds may receive Criminal Court summonses for violations and VTL misdemeanors only D. No, 16-year-olds are never eligible for summonses

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-01, 16-year-olds may receive Criminal Court summonses for ALL violations and for VTL misdemeanors. Non-VTL misdemeanors are NOT eligible for summons issuance to 16-year-olds.

Question 2

Which of the following VTL offenses may be handled by summons rather than arrest?

A. VTL 1192 — Driving While Intoxicated B. VTL 600 — Leaving the Scene of an Accident C. VTL 511 — Aggravated Unlicensed Operation D. VTL 392 — False Statements regarding vehicle registration

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-01, VTL 511 (Aggravated Unlicensed Operation) is NOT among the VTL exceptions. The five VTL exception categories requiring arrest are: Section 600 (Leaving Scene), Section 380 (Dangerous Articles), Sections 392/392-a (False Statements), Sections 421/422/423/426 (Stolen Vehicle), and Section 1192 (DWI/DWAI).

Question 3

A person is stopped for public urination and is found to have 2 felony arrests in the past 18 months and 1 unanswered OATH summons. What type of summons should be issued?

A. OATH civil summons — the person does not meet recidivist criteria B. Criminal Court summons — the person is an OATH Recidivist C. No summons — the person must be arrested D. TAB summons — public urination falls under transit jurisdiction

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-03, an OATH Recidivist is defined as someone with 2 or more felony arrests in the past 2 years, 3 or more unanswered OATH summonses in the past 8 years, OR currently on parole/probation. This person has 2 felony arrests in 18 months (within 2 years), meeting the first criterion. A Criminal Court summons must be issued instead of a CJRA civil summons.

Question 4

An officer issues a Criminal Court summons under NYPD Code 3 (law enforcement exception) for an open container violation. What supervisory action is required?

A. No supervisory action is required for Code 3 B. The desk officer must approve the Code 3 designation C. A patrol supervisor must respond to the scene to verify the exception D. The commanding officer must be notified within 24 hours

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-03, when a Criminal Court summons is issued under Code 3 (law enforcement exception), a patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response.

Question 5

An officer is preparing a parking summons and records the license plate number, plate type, make, and model, but forgets to include the expiration date. What is the likely outcome?

A. The summons will be processed normally with a minor notation B. The summons will be DISMISSED due to the missing mandatory element C. The summons will be returned for correction within 30 days D. The court will obtain the expiration date from DMV records

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-07, there are 5 mandatory identification elements on a parking summons: license plate number, plate type, expiration date, make, and model. Omission of ANY one of these elements results in automatic DISMISSAL of the summons.

Question 6

A vehicle has been parked on a public street without moving for 8 consecutive days. Under the Patrol Guide, this vehicle is classified as:

A. A derelict vehicle B. An abandoned vehicle C. A nuisance vehicle requiring towing D. A legally parked vehicle unless in a restricted zone

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-07, a vehicle on a public street for more than 7 consecutive days is classified as an abandoned vehicle. A derelict vehicle is one that is dilapidated, burned, or stripped with no value except as scrap. Parking summonses should NOT be issued to abandoned or derelict vehicles.

Question 7

Where are pedestrian violation summonses returnable?

A. NYS DMV Traffic Violations Bureau B. NYC Department of Finance C. NYC Criminal Court D. Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings (OATH)

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-02, pedestrian offenses are returnable to NYC Criminal Court. The TVB handles non-parking traffic infractions. The Department of Finance handles parking violations. OATH handles CJRA civil summonses and other administrative violations.

Question 8

What is the return date for a summons issued through the Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB)?

A. 14 calendar days from date of issuance B. 21 calendar days from date of issuance C. 28 calendar days from date of issuance, with weekend/holiday adjustment D. Per the daily FINEST message

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-04, TAB summonses have a return date of 28 calendar days from the date of issuance. If the 28th day falls on a weekend or holiday, the return date moves to the next business day. Criminal Court summons dates are set per the daily FINEST message.

Question 9

During a car stop, the officer discovers the driver’s license has been suspended. The driver claims the suspension was corrected 10 days ago and shows a DMV receipt. What should the officer do?

A. Confiscate the license and issue a summons — only a validated license is acceptable B. Accept the DMV receipt as proof of correction within the 14-day window C. Radio dispatch to verify the DMV receipt before accepting it D. Arrest the driver for operating with a suspended license

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-26, if a violator claims the suspension/revocation was corrected within the past 14 days, officers should accept a newly validated license, a temporary license, OR a DMV receipt showing correction. A DMV receipt within 10 days falls within the 14-day window.

Question 10

An officer removes the ignition key from an unattended vehicle. The owner does not return. After what period does the desk officer invoice the key to the Property Clerk?

A. 24 hours B. 48 hours C. 72 hours D. 7 days

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-27, if the ignition key is not claimed within 48 hours, the desk officer invoices it to the Property Clerk. The ignition key is removed only after the vehicle has been unattended with the key in the ignition for more than 3 minutes.

Question 11

A member parked the department vehicle at a fire hydrant while responding to an emergency call. A parking summons was issued. What type of defense applies?

A. Valid Verifiable Defense — fire hydrant is not a safety violation B. No defense — department vehicles are not exempt from parking rules C. Urgent Police Action — response to an emergency qualifies D. Necessary Police Action — the parking enhanced safety of the operation

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-30, response to an emergency constitutes Urgent Police Action (along with active pursuit and moving surveillance). Fire hydrant parking is a safety violation, which requires proof of urgent or necessary police action. Since the member was responding to an emergency, Urgent Police Action applies.

Question 12

A member parks on the sidewalk to pick up lunch from a restaurant. A summons is issued. Is Necessary Police Action a valid defense?

A. Yes, because the member was in uniform and on duty B. Yes, because sidewalk parking is not classified as a safety violation C. No, Necessary Police Action specifically excludes actions taken solely for the member’s convenience D. No, only Urgent Police Action can be used for sidewalk violations

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-30, Necessary Police Action is defined as less than urgent but enhancing safety of an operation — and specifically excludes actions taken solely for the member’s convenience. Picking up lunch is a personal convenience, not a police operation.

Question 13

Which of the following is NOT one of the 6 CJRA violations eligible for OATH civil summons?

A. Open Container — AC 10-125(b) B. Disorderly Conduct — PL 240.20 C. Public Urination — AC 16-118(6) D. Excessive Noise — AC 24-218

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-03, Disorderly Conduct (PL 240.20) is NOT one of the 6 CJRA violations. The 6 CJRA violations are: Open Container, Public Urination, Littering, Spitting, Excessive Noise, and NYC Park Rules (Title 56).

Question 14

A tow truck registered in New Jersey enters NYC to pick up a broken-down vehicle and tow it back to a repair shop in New Jersey. The tow truck does not have an NYC tow truck license. Should the officer issue a summons?

A. Yes, all tow trucks operating in NYC must be licensed under AC 20-496(b) B. No, out-of-city tow trucks picking up a vehicle for transport outside the city are exempt C. Yes, but only a warning summons since it is a first offense D. No, New Jersey tow trucks are covered under interstate commerce exemptions

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-28, the NYC tow truck licensing requirement does NOT apply to out-of-city tow trucks that are: (1) passing through NYC, (2) picking up a vehicle to transport outside the city, or (3) dropping off a vehicle that originated from outside the city. This tow truck from NJ picking up a vehicle to tow back to NJ falls under exemption (2).

Question 15

A person stopped for littering has no valid ID. The officer confirms the violation is CJRA-eligible. What is the correct course of action?

A. Issue the OATH civil summons using the person’s verbal identification B. Release the person with a warning since no ID is available C. Remove the person to the command for identification D. Issue a Criminal Court summons instead, since OATH requires valid ID

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-03, if a violator has no valid ID, the officer must remove the person to the command for identification. The desk officer makes the FINAL determination on identity and residence verification. The summons type (OATH civil or Criminal Court) is determined after identity is established.

Question 16

A UMOS writes a C summons instead of an OATH for an open container violation and selects Code 1 (OATH Recidivist). Must a patrol supervisor respond to the scene?

A. Yes, a supervisor must respond any time a C is issued instead of an OATH B. Yes, the desk officer must verify all C summons codes C. No, a patrol supervisor must respond to the scene ONLY for Code 3 (law enforcement exception) D. No, but the commanding officer must be notified within 24 hours

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-03, a patrol supervisor must respond to the scene and verify the exception ONLY when Code 3 (law enforcement exception) is used. Codes 1, 2, and 4 do not require a supervisory response. This is a frequently tested distinction.

Question 17

When a UMOS finishes a summons pack, the served summonses should be given to:

A. The Desk Officer for review and filing B. The Operations Coordinator for data entry C. Clerical staff, with the appropriate copy of served summonses D. The Integrity Control Officer for accountability review

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-36, when the summons pack is finished, the UMOS signs the Certification Card and gives the summonses to clerical staff (not the D.O.), along with the appropriate copy of served summonses.

Question 18

TABs and OATHs come in packs of how many summonses? And A, B, and C summonses?

A. TABs/OATHs = 20; A/B/C = 25 B. TABs/OATHs = 25; A/B/C = 20 C. TABs/OATHs = 25; A/B/C = 25 D. TABs/OATHs = 50; A/B/C = 100

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-36, TABs and OATHs come in packs of 25, while A (Parking), B (Moving), and C (Criminal Court) summonses come in packs of 20.

Question 19

The ICO Ageing Report monitors summonses that have been distributed but not served for how many days?

A. 30 days B. 60 days C. 90 days D. 120 days

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-36, the ICO Ageing Report monitors summonses distributed but not served for 90 days. If any UMOS appears on the ICO Ageing Report, the ICO must conduct an IMMEDIATE investigation. The exam commonly tests 30 days as a distractor.

Question 20

An OATH summons is written for a non-CJRA violation based on a witness statement. The witness refuses to sign the Statement of Witness section. What should the officer write in the witness signature caption?

A. Unable to obtain B. N/A C. REFUSED D. Leave the caption blank

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-12, if the witness refuses to ID themselves and/or sign the Statement of Witness, the officer writes REFUSED in the witness signature caption. Note: CJRA violations (209-03) must be personally observed and cannot be based on witness statements — only non-CJRA OATH summonses (209-12) can use informed by.

Question 21

Missing summonses are discovered when an officer is being transferred from the Department. Who must the Desk Officer notify?

A. IAB only B. IAB and the Commanding Officer C. IAB and the ICO immediately; if ICO unavailable, the CO or XO D. IAB and the Duty Captain

Answer C. Per P.G. 209-36, when an officer is transferred or separated from the Department and summonses are missing, the D.O. must call IAB AND notify the ICO immediately. If the ICO is unavailable, notify the CO or XO — NOT the Duty Captain.

Question 22

During a mandatory driver’s license check under PG 209-24, the operator intentionally provides a false identity. What charge applies?

A. Criminal Impersonation — Class A Misdemeanor B. False Personation — Class B Misdemeanor C. Obstructing Governmental Administration — Class A Misdemeanor D. Identity Theft — Class E Felony

Answer B. Per P.G. 209-24, when an operator intentionally lies regarding their identification to deceive a police officer, they can be charged with False Personation (PL 190.23), which is a Class B Misdemeanor.

Score: _____ / 22

Section 210 — Prisoners

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 210
  • Source PDF: public-pguide2.pdf
  • Priority: High
  • Status: Completed

Learning Objectives

  • Understand general procedures for safeguarding prisoners in police custody
  • Know the Desk Officer’s responsibilities for prisoner intake, detention, meals, and release
  • Identify proper prisoner search, transport, and monitoring protocols
  • Recognize holdover prisoner procedures and borough court section coordination
  • Master transport rules including rear cuffing, transport chain thresholds, and leg restraint requirements
  • Know high risk prisoner factors and the additional precautions they trigger
  • Understand pregnant prisoner restraint procedures and the sergeant’s assessment role
  • Know attorney interview and parent/guardian visit rules (location, duration, supervision)
  • Understand bedside arraignment procedures, MLD requirements, and bail posting rules
  • Know prisoner meal rules ($1.80 max, three meal periods, no hot liquids)
  • Master holding pen guidelines (30-minute inspections, 3-hour max, separation rules, item removal)
  • Distinguish between voided arrests and decline prosecutions — procedures, documentation, and authority
  • Understand warrant check requirements before release and record sealing for voided arrests
  • Know inter-agency procedures for hospitalized prisoners and release conflict resolution

Procedures

Procedure Title Status
P.G. 210-01 Prisoners General Procedure completed
P.G. 210-02 Hospitalized Prisoners completed
P.G. 210-03 Hospitalized Prisoners — Arrests by Other Agencies completed
P.G. 210-04 Medical Treatment of Prisoners / Psychiatric Treatment completed
P.G. 210-05 Bedside Arraignment completed
P.G. 210-06 Prisoner Meals completed
P.G. 210-07 Unusual Occurrences Involving Prisoners completed
P.G. 210-08 Guidelines for Prisoner Holding Pens completed
P.G. 210-13 Release of Prisoners completed
P.G. 210-15 Release of Prisoners Arrested by Other Agencies completed
P.G. 210-16 Release at Complaint Room by Direction of ADA completed
P.G. 210-17 Special Category Prisoners completed

Key Terms

  • Holdover Prisoner — A prisoner who has entered the court process but has not yet been arraigned and has been returned to police custody for overnight detention
  • Voided Arrest — Release when reasonable cause to believe prisoner did not commit the offense, or no offense was committed
  • Decline Prosecution — DA exercises discretion not to prosecute; requires Decline Prosecution Letter from ADA
  • High Risk Prisoner — Assessed based on 7 factors: seriousness of offense, refusal to identify, forcible resistance, threats, violence history, weapons, escape history
  • Prisoner Security Guidelines — All prisoners searched on entry/exit from any police, detention, DA, or court facility; searched before transport and upon arrival
  • Mobile Livescan Device (MLD) — Portable fingerprint scanner for hospitalized prisoners; requires 2 MOS present; prohibited for other purposes
  • Transport Chains — Required for more than 2 prisoners; cuffs must be double locked

Sergeant Focus Areas

Key Supervisor Procedures

  • P.G. 210-01 — General Procedure: Desk officer must step from behind desk to verify prisoner security before transport; immediate supervisor determines pregnant prisoner restraint method; authorize and monitor attorney/parent visits
  • P.G. 210-04 — Medical Treatment: Patrol supervisor must be notified when prisoner requires medical treatment; verify documentation and guard assignment
  • P.G. 210-05 — Bedside Arraignment: Monitor 6-day timeline for borough court section notification; verify 2 MOS present for MLD fingerprinting
  • P.G. 210-06 — Meals: Desk officer authorizes additional meals for medical/unusual conditions; expense report due by 15th of month
  • P.G. 210-08 — Holding Pens: Sergeant must personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording condition and position; verify 30-minute MOS inspections; enforce 3-hour maximum with Command Log entries for extensions
  • P.G. 210-13 — Release: Patrol supervisor is primary authority for consent to release; verify voided arrest documentation (5 elements on OLBS Arrest Worksheet); ensure BCS intake supervisor seals fingerprint records
  • P.G. 210-15 — Other Agency Release: Escalate inter-agency conflicts to precinct commander/duty captain
  • P.G. 210-16 — ADA Release: Verify warrant check before release; process on warrant only if active warrant found

Section 210 — General Procedure, Hospitalized Prisoners, and Other Agency Arrests

Covers P.G. 210-01, 210-02, 210-03

P.G. 210-01: Prisoners General Procedure

Purpose

Safeguard prisoners in police custody at all stages — from intake through transport, detention, and release.

Holdover Prisoner

Term Definition
Holdover Prisoner A prisoner who has entered the court process, has NOT yet been arraigned, and has been returned to police custody for overnight detention

Prisoner Security Guidelines

Exam Alert: All prisoners will be searched on entry to and exit from ANY police facility, detention facility, DA facility, or court facility. Searches are also required before transport departure and upon arrival at destination.

Search Point Required?
Entry to any police/detention/DA/court facility Yes
Exit from any police/detention/DA/court facility Yes
Before transport departure Yes
Upon arrival at destination Yes

Desk Officer Duties — Prisoner Intake and Detention

Duty Details
Cell space assignment Request borough court section for cell space assignment
Police attendant Assign police attendant to cellblock
Isolated prisoner If prisoner placed alone (threats/fights/disruption), ensure MOS continues to monitor closely
Custody changes Record ANY change of custody in Command Log

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer is the central authority for prisoner management at the command level. Sergeants must ensure desk officers are actively managing cellblock assignments, custody records, and attendant assignments — not delegating these responsibilities.

Permitted Prisoner Interviews

The desk officer will permit prisoner interviews by the following persons:

Authorized Person Notes
Supervisory officer —
Detective Bureau member —
District Attorney —
Chief Medical Examiner —
Legal representative (attorney) See attorney interview rules below
City department official Only if prisoner is an employee of that department
Federal law enforcement officer —
Clergy Only on prisoner’s request
NYS DOCCS representative —
Foreign Consulate representative —

Attorney Interview Rules

Location Interview Conducted In
Precinct Muster room ONLY
Borough court Location designated by supervisor

Exam Alert: Attorney interviews at the precinct are conducted in the muster room ONLY — not the cellblock, not the detective squad room, not the desk area. This is a frequently tested detail.

Documentation: Record the following on the ARREST REPORT SUPPLEMENT: - Name, address, and phone number of attorney - Who retained the attorney - Whether prisoner was interviewed - Time attorney arrived and departed

Parent/Guardian Visits (Prisoner Age 18-21)

Rule Detail
Maximum duration 15 minutes
Location Muster room
Eligibility trigger Prisoner detained more than 4 hours
Supervision In the presence of desk officer or BCS supervisor

Exam Alert: Parent/guardian visits apply to prisoners aged 18-21 (not juveniles under 18, who have separate procedures). The visit is limited to 15 minutes, in the muster room, only after 4 hours of detention, and supervised by the desk officer or BCS supervisor.

Female Prisoners — Special Rules

  • Do NOT confine a female prisoner with a nursing baby in a cell
  • Assign a female attendant for female prisoners

Transport Rules

Handcuffing and Restraint

Rule Requirement
Standard restraint Rear cuff ALL prisoners OR place on transport chains
Transport chain cuffs Double lock transport chain cuffs
More than 2 prisoners Transport chains MUST be used

Exam Alert: The threshold for mandatory transport chains is more than 2 prisoners. Two prisoners may be rear cuffed; three or more require transport chains.

Desk Officer Transport Verification

Sergeant Focus: Before ANY prisoner is removed from the command, the desk officer must step from behind the desk and personally check that prisoners are properly secured. This is a physical, hands-on duty — not a visual check from behind the desk.

Additional Escort Requirements

An additional escort is required when:

Condition Additional Escort?
More than 2 prisoners without chains Yes
More than 9 prisoners on chains Yes
Several stops required Yes
Multiple destinations Yes
High risk prisoner Yes

High Risk Prisoner Factors

A prisoner is considered high risk based on ANY of the following:

Factor Description
Seriousness of offense Violent felonies, serious crimes
Refusal to identify Unwillingness to identify self
Forcible resistance Physical resistance to arrest or custody
Threats Threats of violence or escape
Violence history History of violent behavior
Weapons Possession of weapons at time of arrest
Escape history Prior escape or attempted escape

Exam Alert: Know ALL 7 high risk factors. Exam questions may present a scenario and ask whether the prisoner qualifies as high risk. Any single factor is sufficient.

Leg Restraints

Rule Detail
MUST use When prisoner refused fingerprinting
MUST use For ALL hospital transports
May NOT use For punitive purposes
Does not replace Rear handcuffing — leg restraints are used IN ADDITION to rear cuffs
If NOT used for hospital Desk Officer must make a detailed Command Log entry explaining why

Exam Alert: The two mandatory triggers for leg restraints are (1) refusal to be fingerprinted and (2) ALL hospital transports. Leg restraints are NEVER used as punishment and do NOT replace rear handcuffing.

Exam Alert: If leg restraints are NOT used for a hospital transport, the Desk Officer must document the reason in a detailed Command Log entry. The PG does not mention psych specifically — it says ALL prisoners going to the hospital for medical treatment should have leg restraints.

Pregnant Prisoners

Step Action
Initial restraint Rear cuff, absent exceptional circumstances
Assessment Immediate supervisor determines if less restrictive method is appropriate
Less restrictive options Front cuff, or one hand cuffed to a stationary post
Prohibition Leg restraints shall NOT be employed on pregnant prisoners
Notification Guarding officer notifies immediate supervisor of restraint method used

Factors for Supervisor Assessment

Factor
Safety of officers and others
Likelihood of escape
Circumstances of the crime
Demeanor of the prisoner
Late-stage pregnancy / fall risk
Active labor
Newborn has been discharged from the hospital
Attending physician’s recommendations regarding restraints

Sergeant Focus: The immediate supervisor (sergeant) makes the determination on whether to use less restrictive restraints for pregnant prisoners. This is a direct supervisory decision — not a desk officer or commanding officer decision. Document the assessment and rationale.

Pregnant Prisoner at Medical Facility — Special Rules

Stage Rule
Pregnant (general) Least restrictive manner — front cuffing, one hand to stationary post
In labor NO CUFFS, NO RESTRAINT of any manner
During delivery Stay outside delivery room
Post-delivery Only cuff/restrain in exceptional circumstances determined by immediate supervisor case by case

Exam Alert: A prisoner in active labor receives NO cuffs and NO restraints of any kind. During delivery, the officer stays outside the delivery room. Post-delivery restraint requires exceptional circumstances — determined by the immediate supervisor on a case-by-case basis.

UMOS Documentation — Pregnant Prisoner Restraint

Requirement
Digital Activity Log entry indicating whether exceptional circumstances exist
Facts which supported the determination
If applicable, document the type of restraints authorized

Prisoner Meals

Detail Rule
Maximum cost $1.80 per meal per prisoner
Meal periods 0500-0600, 1200-1300, 1800-1900
Prohibited NO hot liquids (coffee, tea, hot chocolate)

(See P.G. 210-06 for full meal procedures.)

Prisoner Security Survey

Detail Rule
Frequency Annually
Timing Last week of September
Report due September 30th

Exam Alert: The Prisoner Security Survey is conducted annually during the last week of September, with the report due by September 30th.

Additional Policies

Policy Rule
Sexual assault/harassment Zero tolerance for sexual assault or harassment of prisoners
Communicable disease info CONFIDENTIAL — release ONLY with written consent of Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters

P.G. 210-02: Hospitalized Prisoners

Prisoner Admitted — Arresting/Guarding Officer

When a prisoner is admitted to the hospital:

Step Action
Notify two desk officers (1) Desk Officer, Precinct of Arrest and (2) Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization
Request relief Request relief from Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization
Remain with prisoner Stay with prisoner until relieved by guarding officer
Once relieved Arresting officer returns to process the arrest (10-2)

Exam Alert: The arresting/guarding officer calls two different desk officers — the Pct of Arrest and the Pct of Hospitalization. Many exam questions test which desk officer handles which duty. The Pct of Arrest handles the arrest report, notifications, and court coordination. The Pct of Hospitalization handles reliefs, guarding logistics, and hospital-level decisions.

Search of Prisoner at Hospital (by A/O if direct from scene)

Rule Detail
Gender appropriate Search must be gender appropriate
If no gender-appropriate officer available Hospital personnel search; witnesses are mandated
Clothing Removed by hospital personnel, then officer searches clothing
Receipt Give hospital staff receipt for property received from them
Property removed Remove usual property AND deliver to D.O. Pct of Arrest OR designated arrest processing facility
Property kept by hospital Give D/O a list of property kept by hospital, EXCEPT clothing

See Also: PG 208-02 — Directs listing property in digital Activity Log.

Property Removed from Prisoner

Property is removed when it is:

Category Examples
Unlawfully carried —
Required as evidence —
Lawfully carried but dangerous Dangerous to life, could facilitate escape, used to attempt suicide, could assault another (glass, sharp edges, belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, etc.)
Used to deface or damage property —

Press Card Handling

Item Action
Press Card Issued by Mayor’s Office of Media/Entertainment — invoice as appropriate (safekeeping, evidence, etc.)

NYPD Auxiliary Police Property

Item Action
Shield or I.D. card Forward to Auxiliary Police Section with report of facts
Voucher Do NOT voucher

Pistol License

Item Action
Pistol license Forward to Commanding Officer, License Division with report of facts
Voucher Do NOT voucher

Desk Officer, Precinct of Arrest — Hospitalized Prisoner Duties

Duty Details
Arrest Report Direct OLBS be prepared; generate arrest number in OMNIFORM
OLBS & Print Cards Direct UMOS to deliver copies to Desk Officer, Pct of Hospitalization (not direct to hospital)
Borough Court Section Notify BCS supervisor
Telephone Record Enter notification including: prisoner’s name, arrest #, date/time went 97H, admission #, whether printed or not
DAT or Bedside Arraignment Have guarding officer issue D.A.T. if eligible; if not eligible, bedside arraignment

Exam Alert: OLBS and print card copies go to the Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization — NOT directly to the hospital. The exam may present a scenario where the desk officer sends documents directly to the hospital; this is incorrect.

D.O. Precinct of Arrest — MLD Procedures

When a hospitalized prisoner requires fingerprinting:

Step Action
Verify MLD availability Notify Patrol Borough concerned
Designate UMOS A UMOS trained in MLD use, other than the A/O, retrieves the MLD
Safety requirement At least 2 UMOS must be present during MLD fingerprinting
Command Log entry Name, rank, tax number of designated UMOS and location where MLD is to be utilized

Exam Alert: The MLD may NOT be used for any purpose other than hospitalized prisoner fingerprints. If the MLD is in a location with minimal or no cell connection, captured prints are stored until a proper network connection is established, then transmitted.

Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization — Duties

Duty Details
Dept of Corrections Ask if hospital has a DOC Prison Ward; make Telephone Record of inquiry
DOC has room Follow P.G. 210-05
Continuous effort Make continuous effort to have DOC assume custody
Reliefs Ensure appropriate reliefs for guarding officer
OLBS & Prints Send duplicates of OLBS and prints (finger AND palmprints) to guarding officer
Staffing UMOS from Pct of Hospitalization guard prisoners

Exam Alert: Asking DOC to take the prisoner is the duty of the D/O Precinct of Arrest under P.G. 210-04 when the prisoner comes from your command as a new arrest. Do not confuse with the Pct of Hospitalization’s role, which is hospital-level logistics.

Prior Test: If the precinct of hospitalization is short-staffed and officers are sent from outside commands, who performs patrol and who guards? — The outside officers perform patrol, and officers from the precinct of hospitalization guard prisoners.

Patrol Supervisor — Hospitalized Prisoner

When notified of a prisoner’s admission:

Step Action
Respond to hospital Evaluate escape potential — examine room and officer attentiveness
Instruct guarding member Security, visits, handcuffing, constant vigilance
Hospital room inadequate Request a move to a more secure room
Lack of cooperation Report to Commanding Officer (breaking the chain!)
No room change available Take additional steps (another guard, etc.) and document in Digital Activity Log
ICU admission Evaluate again; assess escape potential; if escape potential exists, use Plastic Flex Cuffs (additional data)

Exam Alert: When hospital security is inadequate, the escorting officer requests a room change — NOT the Patrol Supervisor. The PG specifically states the escorting officer requests room change if security inadequate. The patrol supervisor’s role is to evaluate and instruct, and to report lack of cooperation to the C.O.

Who Guards the Prisoner?

Rule Detail
Gender Guard can be gender neutral
Juvenile Plainclothes officer guards juveniles

Exam Alert: Unlike searches (which must be gender appropriate), guarding a hospitalized prisoner can be gender neutral. However, juvenile prisoners must be guarded by a plainclothes officer.

Guarding Member — Security Measures

Duty Details
Constant observation Even though prisoner is cuffed
No passing items Do not let anyone pass items to prisoner
Periodic area checks Especially after visits, treatments, and tour changes
Phone use No phone for prisoner UNLESS Desk Officer or Patrol Supervisor approves
Phone approval documentation Activity Log entry: supervisor approving, who prisoner called, name/relationship/phone number
Keep D.O. informed Update Desk Officer of Hospital on prisoner’s condition

UMOS Visiting Hospital (Plainclothes)

Rule Detail
Firearms and weapons Keep all firearms and weapons concealed
Visibility Not visible to hospital personnel or visitors
Accessibility Not accessible to prisoners or unauthorized persons

Housing and Transit — Guarding Rules

Rule Detail
Who guards Housing and Transit guard their own prisoners
Arresting officer Call PSA/TD covering location of hospital when perp admitted; request relief from PSA/TD D.O.
Multiple arrests If A/O has other arrests to process, D.O. Precinct of Hospitalization might guard prisoner — but only for 1 tour

See Also: PG 210-03 — Other agencies: if A/O has other perps, Patrol will guard but make 2nd call for relief in 1 hour.

Interviewers and Visitors — 210-01 vs. 210-02 Comparison

Exam Alert: 210-01 has 10 Interviewers and 1 Visitor (Parent/Guardian). 210-02 has a similar but different list of 10 Interviewers and 3 Visitors. The exam frequently tests the differences between these two lists.

Interviewers at Hospital (210-02):

Authorized Interviewer Notes
Ranking officer, this Department (210-01 says Supervisory officer)
Detective (210-01 includes Detective Bureau or Detective Squad — broader)
District Attorney or representative Same as 210-01
Chief Medical Examiner or representative Same as 210-01
Official of city dept if prisoner is employee Same concept as 210-01
Federal law enforcement officer Same as 210-01
Member of clergy (upon prisoner request) Same as 210-01
Parole or probation officer Not in 210-01 interviewer list
Hospital personnel (assigned to treat) Not in 210-01 interviewer list
Social worker Not in 210-01 interviewer list

Visitors at Hospital (210-02):

Authorized Visitor Requirements
Telegram recipient Hospital Superintendent states perp is seriously ill
Family member Issued a UF-50 (form 49 to an outsider) from D.O. Pct of Arrest authorizing visit
LAWYER If requested — drops down from Interviewer list to Visitor list

Prior Test: Where does a lawyer fall in 210-02? — A lawyer is a Visitor, NOT an Interviewer at the hospital. In 210-01 (at the precinct/BCS), the lawyer is an Interviewer. This is a major distinction the exam tests.

PG Conflict: In 210-01, a lawyer (prisoner’s legal representative) is listed as an Interviewer. In 210-02, the lawyer drops to the Visitor list (if requested). Know both lists exist — the exam may present a hospital scenario where lawyer appears as an interviewer option.

Documentation Differences — 210-01 vs. 210-02

Location Documentation Type Notes
210-01 (Pct or BCS) Arrest Report Supplement (ARS) Lawyer = interview; Parent/Guardian = visit
210-02 (Hospital) Digital Activity Log Guarding MOS logs visitors only — no mention of interviewers documentation

Exam Alert: At the hospital, visitors sign the Digital Activity Log — there is no mention of documenting interviewers in the Activity Log. The guarding MOS updates the D.O. Pct of Arrest, who then lists visitors on an ARS.

UF-50 — Authorized Family Visits

Detail Rule
What is UF-50 A memo with the Official Seal of NYC preprinted in the upper left corner
Purpose For communications outside the Department
Where to apply In person at the Precinct of Arrest (not the Pct of Hospitalization)

Exam Alert: Family members apply for visit authorization at the Precinct of Arrest, NOT the Precinct of Hospitalization. The exam may try to trick you into selecting the hospital precinct.

Who Is Authorized Family?

Category Includes
Married individuals Includes common-law marriages
Same-sex couples —
Registered NYC domestic partners —
Persons in intimate relationships —
People in a family-type relationship —
Family members Children at least 16 years old, siblings, different generations (includes in-laws)

Exam Alert: If a potential visitor is likely to interfere with an ongoing investigation, consult with the investigative supervisor before issuing authorization. Watch for the exam presenting an assigned detective as the correct answer — that is wrong; it must be the investigative supervisor.

Guarding Officer — Visitor Procedures

Step Action
Activity Log entry Make entry AND have visitor SIGN Activity Log
Before EOT Notify D.O. Pct of Arrest of all visitors during tour
D.O. Pct of Arrest Make Command Log entry from guarding officer notification; make ARS with visitor info
Prisoner under 18 Notify parent/guardian of impending release — make Command Log entry (NOT Telephone Record)

Exam Alert: When a prisoner under 18 is about to be released from the hospital, the D.O. Pct of Arrest notifies the parent/guardian and makes a Command Log entry — NOT a Telephone Record. This is a specific distinction the exam tests.

Who Pays for Hospitalization?

Rule Detail
Non-NYC HHC hospital D.O. Pct of Arrest sends report in sealed envelope to NYC HHC Fiscal Affairs, General Accounting Dept with all pertinent details

Prisoner’s Condition — Arraignment Readiness

Condition Who Acts Action
Condition permits arraignment Guarding Officer Notify D/O Pct of ARREST
Near death or totally incapacitated D.O. Pct of HOSPITALIZATION Notify Commanding Officer

Restraint Types at Hospital

Leg Shackles

When Used Detail
Arm or wrist injuries —
Refused prints —
Hospital transport UNLESS D/O decides not due to extenuating circumstances
Escape potential No discretion — mandatory

Long Chain Cuffs

When Used
Comatose prisoner
Paralyzed prisoner
Last stage of pregnancy

No Cuffs Decision

Role Action
Patrol Supervisor Confers with doctor and makes recommendation to D.O. Pct of Hospitalization
D.O. Pct of Hospitalization Decides whether cuffs off if impractical or inexpedient (e.g., comatose)

Exam Alert: The decision to remove cuffs entirely rests with the Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization — NOT the Patrol Supervisor. The PS confers with the doctor and makes a recommendation, but the D.O. decides. Do not confuse with the pregnant prisoner restraint decision (which IS the immediate supervisor’s call).

High Escape Potential — Two Sets of Cuffs

Who Directs Detail
Desk Officer or Patrol Supervisor Direct all guarding UMOS to have 2 sets of cuffs available
Purpose Switch cuffed hands during treatment/transfer with relieving officer — keep one hand cuffed during process
Prohibition Do NOT cuff both hands unnecessarily

Cuffed to Bed or Gurney

Rule Detail
Double lock Always double lock handcuffs
Observation Even if cuffed, maintain constant observation
Before removing for treatment Use a second pair to cuff free hand BEFORE removing original cuff

Commanding Officer, Pct of Hospitalization — Prisoner Near Death/Incapacitated

Duty Details
Investigation Direct investigation of prisoner’s condition, history, and arrest situation
Guard removed If guard removed: contact hospital daily, direct PS to visit, weekly telephone message to BCS each Wednesday
Guard required Assign UMOS when condition warrants
Prisoner dies Notify BCS with date/time of death and doctor issuing death certificate; have guarding MOS obtain copy of death certificate; direct D.O. to forward to Chief Clerk’s Office, borough Criminal Court

Exam Alert: When a guard is removed from a hospitalized prisoner, the weekly status update to BCS is made each Wednesday — not any other day. This specific day is a testable detail.

P.G. 210-03: Hospitalized Prisoners — Arrests by Other Agencies

Other Police Agencies Defined

Agency
NYC Department of Correction (DOC)
NYS Department of Corrections and Community Supervision (DOCCS)
Court Officers
Port Authority Police
MTA Police
Amtrak Police
Sea Gate Police
FDNY Fire Marshals
DEP Police
Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority (TBTA) Police

Guarding Responsibility

Rule Detail
Primary responsibility Arresting officer from other agency must guard prisoner
Desk officer action Notify the agency to provide 24-hour guards
Follow-up If relief not provided within 1 hour, make follow-up notification

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer must actively track whether the arresting agency has provided relief guards. If no relief arrives within 1 hour, a follow-up notification is required. Do not allow NYPD resources to be used as indefinite guard replacements for other agencies.

Common Exam Angles

  1. Attorney interview location — Muster room ONLY at precinct; supervisor-designated location at borough court
  2. Transport chain threshold — More than 2 prisoners requires chains
  3. Desk officer transport check — Must step from behind desk to personally verify
  4. Leg restraint triggers — Refused fingerprinting AND all hospital transports; if not used for hospital, D.O. must document in Command Log
  5. Pregnant prisoner restraint — Immediate supervisor (sergeant) determines less restrictive method; NO restraints during labor; stay outside delivery room
  6. Parent/guardian visit — Age 18-21, more than 4 hours, 15 minutes max, muster room, desk officer/BCS supervisor present
  7. High risk factors — All 7 factors; any single factor qualifies
  8. Security survey — Last week of September, due September 30th
  9. Other agency hospitals — 1-hour follow-up if no relief provided
  10. $1.80 meal cap and NO hot liquids
  11. Two desk officers for hospitalized prisoners — Pct of Arrest (arrest report, court, notifications) vs. Pct of Hospitalization (reliefs, guarding, hospital logistics)
  12. Interviewers vs. Visitors (210-01 vs. 210-02) — Lawyer is an Interviewer at precinct (210-01) but drops to Visitor at hospital (210-02)
  13. Family visit authorization — Applied for at Precinct of Arrest, NOT Pct of Hospitalization; UF-50 required
  14. Guard gender — Gender neutral for guarding, but plainclothes for juveniles
  15. Room change request — Made by escorting officer, NOT Patrol Supervisor
  16. No cuffs decision — D.O. Pct of Hospitalization decides (not PS); PS confers with doctor and recommends
  17. Under 18 release notification — Command Log entry, NOT Telephone Record
  18. Weekly BCS update — Each Wednesday when guard removed from hospitalized prisoner
  19. 2 sets of cuffs — High escape potential; keep one hand cuffed during transfer/treatment
  20. Housing/Transit — Guard own prisoners; Pct of Hospitalization may guard for only 1 tour if A/O has other arrests

Sergeant Study Notes

  • The desk officer is the central figure in prisoner management: cell assignments, attendant assignments, custody log entries, transport verification, and authorized visitor access
  • Sergeants make the restraint determination for pregnant prisoners — know the assessment factors including physician recommendations
  • The desk officer must physically step from behind the desk to verify prisoner security before transport — this is a hands-on check
  • High risk prisoner designation triggers additional escort requirements during transport
  • Attorney visits at the precinct are in the muster room ONLY — document everything on the Arrest Report Supplement
  • The 1-hour follow-up rule for other agency hospitalized prisoners prevents NYPD from absorbing another agency’s guarding responsibilities indefinitely
  • Zero tolerance policy on prisoner sexual assault/harassment — any allegation must be reported and investigated
  • Communicable disease information about prisoners is confidential and cannot be released without written consent from the Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters — isolate prisoner if isolated cell is available
  • Know the two-desk-officer split for hospitalized prisoners and which duties belong to which D.O.
  • The escorting officer requests room changes at the hospital when security is inadequate — the Patrol Supervisor evaluates and instructs but does not make the request
  • When guard is removed from a near-death prisoner, the Wednesday weekly update to BCS is a testable detail

Section 210 — Unusual Occurrences and Holding Pen Guidelines

Covers P.G. 210-07, 210-08

P.G. 210-07: Unusual Occurrences Involving Prisoners

Prisoner Unusual — Defined

An unusual occurrence is any of the following involving an unarraigned adult prisoner OR a juvenile under 18 in NYPD custody or Corrections:

Occurrence Action
Assaults MOS Notify desk officer and patrol supervisor
Prisoner sexually assaulted/harassed (or alleges such) Notify desk officer and patrol supervisor
Escapes or attempts escape Notify desk officer and patrol supervisor; if escape/attempt, notify IAB Command Center
Other unusual (catchall) Notify desk officer and patrol supervisor

Exam Alert: The definition covers any prisoner, any age — both unarraigned adults and juveniles under 18 in custody or Corrections. The four categories are: assaults MOS, sexual assault/harassment (or allegation), escape/attempt, and other unusual.

Desk Officer — Notifications

Notify Details
C.O./Duty Captain AND Operations Unit Provide prisoner’s name, sex, and charge
If escape or attempt Also notify IAB Command Center

Commanding Officer/Duty Captain — Investigation and Report

Duty Details
Conduct investigation —
Prepare report Typed letterhead (form 49)
Forward to Chief of Department Number of copies varies (see below)
One additional copy to each Chief of Patrol, Chief of IAB, Chief of Training, C.O. Criminal Justice Bureau (CJB), C.O. Performance Monitoring Unit, each intermediate command, C.O. of UMOS if different

Copies to Chief of Department

Situation Number of Copies
Adult prisoner 3 copies
Child in custody is DOA 2 copies
Child in custody (not DOA) 1 copy

Exam Alert: The number of copies of the typed letterhead (49) report forwarded to the Chief of Department depends on the prisoner: 3 for adult, 2 for child DOA, 1 for child in custody. This is a frequently tested numerical detail.

See Also: PG 221-03 — Suicide/injury of prisoner. PG 221-05 — DOA SILD.

Sergeant Focus: The patrol supervisor must respond to ALL unusual occurrences involving prisoners. Document the incident thoroughly in the Command Log, prepare required reports, and notify the commanding officer for serious incidents (death, escape, serious injury).

P.G. 210-08: Guidelines for Prisoner Holding Pens

Prisoner Holding Pen Roster

Maintain PRISONER HOLDING PEN ROSTER (PD244-1410) for all prisoners placed in holding pens.

Inspection Requirements

Requirement Detail
Designated MOS Designate a MOS to inspect prisoners every 30 minutes
Unattended prohibition Do NOT leave prisoners unattended at any time
Supervisor inspection Assign supervisor to personally and periodically inspect prisoners
Supervisor documentation Record prisoner condition (sleeping, alert) and position (sitting, lying down)

Exam Alert: The 30-minute inspection interval is one of the most tested numbers in Section 210. A designated MOS must check prisoners every 30 minutes, and a supervisor must also personally and periodically inspect, documenting condition and position.

Sergeant Focus: Sergeants have a dual responsibility: (1) ensure the designated MOS is conducting 30-minute inspections, and (2) personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording their condition (sleeping, alert) and physical position (sitting, lying down). This documentation can be critical in liability situations.

Maximum Detention Time

Rule Detail
Standard maximum 3 hours in a holding pen
Extension authority Desk officer or detective squad supervisor may approve extension
Documentation Extension must be recorded in Command Log

Exam Alert: The 3-hour maximum for holding pen detention is frequently tested. Know that extensions require desk officer or detective squad supervisor approval AND a Command Log entry.

Prisoners NOT Permitted in Holding Pens

Condition Action
Ill Remove to hospital (see P.G. 210-04)
Intoxicated Remove to hospital (see P.G. 210-04)
Emotionally disturbed Remove to hospital (see P.G. 210-04)

Exam Alert: Ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners must NEVER be placed in holding pens. They must be removed to a hospital under P.G. 210-04.

Separation Requirements

Rule Requirement
Male and female NEVER in the same holding pen
Adult and juvenile NEVER in the same holding pen
Juveniles Must be placed in a designated juvenile area

Exam Alert: The separation rules are absolute — there are no exceptions. Male/female separation and adult/juvenile separation are mandatory. Juveniles must be placed in a designated juvenile area, not simply a separate cell in the adult holding area.

Suicide and Assault Prevention — Item Removal

The following items must be removed from prisoners upon placement in a holding pen:

Item Reason
Belts Suicide/assault prevention
Neckties Suicide/assault prevention
Shoelaces Suicide/assault prevention
Drawstrings Suicide/assault prevention
Jackets Suicide/assault prevention
Overcoats Suicide/assault prevention

Exam Alert: Know all 6 items that must be removed: belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, jackets, and overcoats. A common exam question lists items and asks which must be removed. Remember the mnemonic: B-N-S-D-J-O (Belts, Neckties, Shoelaces, Drawstrings, Jackets, Overcoats).

Menstrual Hygiene

Rule Detail
Products Issue menstrual hygiene products on request
Restroom access Assign a female MOS to escort prisoner to restroom

Sergeant Focus: Menstrual hygiene products must be provided upon request — do not wait for the prisoner to ask repeatedly. A female MOS must be assigned to escort the prisoner to the restroom. Failure to provide these basic accommodations is a supervisory failure.

Common Exam Angles

  1. 30-minute inspections — Designated MOS checks every 30 minutes; supervisor also inspects personally
  2. 3-hour maximum — Standard max detention in holding pen; extension requires desk officer/detective squad supervisor + Command Log
  3. Separation rules — Male/female NEVER together; adult/juvenile NEVER together; juveniles in designated area
  4. 6 items removed — Belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, jackets, overcoats
  5. No ill/intoxicated/EDP — These prisoners cannot be placed in holding pens
  6. Supervisor documentation — Record condition (sleeping, alert) AND position (sitting, lying down)
  7. Menstrual hygiene — Products on request; female MOS escort to restroom
  8. PD244-1410 — Prisoner Holding Pen Roster must be maintained
  9. Unusual occurrence definition — Unarraigned adult or juvenile under 18: assaults MOS, sexual assault/harassment, escape/attempt, other unusual
  10. Typed letterhead (49) copies — 3 for adult prisoner, 2 for child DOA, 1 for child in custody
  11. Escape/attempt — Notify IAB Command Center in addition to standard notifications
  12. Prisoner DOA or seriously injured by police action — UMOS: request PS response, safeguard scene, obtain medical attention; PS comply with PG 221-05

Prisoner DOA or Seriously Injured by Police Action / Police Firearms Discharge

Role Duty
UMOS Immediately request response of Patrol Supervisor; safeguard crime scene; obtain medical attention
Patrol Supervisor Respond and comply with PG 221-05

Sergeant Study Notes

  • The sergeant must personally inspect prisoners in holding pens — this is not delegable to a police officer
  • Document prisoner condition (sleeping, alert) and position (sitting, lying down) during each inspection
  • The 3-hour maximum is the standard; extensions are the exception and require both supervisory approval AND Command Log documentation
  • Ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners are categorically excluded from holding pens — immediate hospital removal is required
  • The 6 items removed for suicide/assault prevention are frequently the subject of exam questions — know the complete list
  • Juveniles must be placed in a designated juvenile area — mere separation from adults in the same holding area is insufficient
  • Prisoner holding pen management is a high-liability area — thorough documentation of inspections protects both the prisoner and the Department
  • Any unusual occurrence (injury, illness, escape, death, altercation) requires immediate notification of the desk officer and patrol supervisor

Section 210 — Medical/Psychiatric Treatment, Bedside Arraignment, Prisoner Meals, and Unusual Occurrences

Covers P.G. 210-04, 210-05, 210-06, 210-07

P.G. 210-04: Prisoners Requiring Medical/Psychiatric Treatment

Updated: 01/14/26 | R.O. 8 | 10 pages

When to Remove a Prisoner to the Hospital

A prisoner must be removed to the hospital when any of the following conditions exist:

Condition Action
Prisoner is ill Remove to hospital
Prisoner is injured Remove to hospital
Prisoner is intoxicated Remove to hospital
Prisoner is emotionally disturbed Remove to hospital
Prisoner requests medical attention Remove to hospital

Exam Alert: Ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners are NOT to be placed in holding pens (see P.G. 210-08). They must be removed to a hospital for evaluation and treatment.

Which Hospital?

Situation Hospital
Non-emergency Local hospital
Life-threatening Nearest hospital
Narcotics/dangerous substance ingestion Go DIRECTLY to nearest hospital (NEVER to stationhouse)

Exam Alert: A perp who ingests narcotics or a dangerous substance goes directly to the nearest hospital — NEVER to the stationhouse first. The exam may present a scenario asking whether to process the arrest first; the answer is always go directly to the hospital.

Narcotics Ingestion — Triple Notification

When a prisoner ingests narcotics or a dangerous substance, notify Patrol Supervisor AND Communications Section three times:

Notification When
1st Upon observing or suspecting ingestion
2nd When transport to hospital is initiated
3rd When 84 (arrived) at hospital

Additionally, inform EMS and hospital personnel of the type and quantity of drugs ingested.

UMOS — Initial Medical Treatment Duties

Duty Details
Request ambulance Accompany prisoner to hospital from place of arrest if necessary
Notify Desk Officer
Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form Prepare when prisoner: receives treatment, refuses treatment after claiming injury/illness, has apparent need for treatment, has previously treated injuries, has prescribed medication, has communicable disease status

Exam Alert: A prisoner who volunteers their positive communicable disease status triggers MTP preparation only if not already on the Prisoner Movement Slip.

Spit Hood Procedures (NEW in R.O. 8)

Role Duty
ESU Member Apply spit hood if prisoner is spitting at or threatening to spit at any person
ESU Member Monitor and remain with subject wearing spit hood until relieved by a medical professional with minimum EMT certification, unless hood is removed
ESU Member If disagreement between EMS and ESU about application/removal, request ESU Supervisor to respond
ESU Supervisor Confer with EMS supervisor to resolve disagreement about spit hood application or removal

Exam Alert: Only ESU personnel may apply a spit hood — NOT regular MOS. If there is a disagreement between EMS and ESU about applying or removing the spit hood, the ESU Supervisor resolves it by conferring with the EMS supervisor.

MTP Triggers — When to Prepare

Prepare MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150) for each prisoner who:

Trigger
a. Receives medical/psychiatric treatment
b. Refuses treatment after claiming injury or illness
c. Is in apparent need of treatment
d. May require prescribed medication
e. Volunteers communicable disease status (if not on Prisoner Movement Slip)
f. Already has previously treated injuries
g. Has a spit hood placed on them (NEW)

Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form (MTP) — Key Details

Detail Rule
Reserve function Enter info from Section I of MTP into MTP app in FORMS by selecting the Reserve function; number generated by FORMS entered on the MTP
Copy of MTP MUST accompany prisoner until no longer in NYPD custody
HHC entitled to copy Allow hospital personnel to make a copy of the MTP upon completion of treatment
Signing If perp, doctor, or hospital staff refuses to sign, note on MTP and in Digital Activity Log
MTP copies to DOC Forward three copies (white, blue, pink) with prisoner to DOC
Buff copy retained BCS retains fourth (buff) copy at Borough Court Section facility

Section I of MTP — Transport Numbers

Transport Method Number Recorded
Taken by EMS (ambulance) ACR # (Ambulance Call Report)
Taken by other means (RMP, etc.) PCR # (Patient Care Report)

Memory Aid — ACR vs PCR: A = Ambulance (ACR), P = Police/other (PCR). The ACR number comes from the ambulance attendant. The PCR number also comes from the ambulance attendant (received from them).

Remarks Section of MTP

Content Notes
Specific treatment instructions from EMS —
Whether EMS took perp to hospital room for further treatment —
RMA Check-box for RMA; does NOT go in remarks

Supervisor at Scene

Duty Details
If perp removed from scene direct to hospital Ensure MTP is prepared per steps above and ADDITIONAL DATA (Preparation of MTP)
Review and sign Section I of MTP

Desk Officer — Prisoner Needs Medical Treatment During Arrest Process

Duty Details
Medical/drug/psych treatment needed Call ambulance and have prisoner taken to hospital
Command Log entry ACR or PCR # AND if Medic Alert Emblem worn, name of whom notified
2nd Command Log entry When prisoner leaves the stationhouse
MTP Ensure prepared; review and sign Section I
Escort Assign UMOS to escort prisoner; direct A/O to continue arrest process when possible
Relief Ensure appropriate relief for guarding officer

See Also: PG 210-01 — Release of confidential status information (communicable disease): Only DCLM (Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters) can authorize release. Isolate prisoner if isolated cell is available.

Arresting/Escorting Officer — Transport to Hospital

Step Duty
15 Rear cuff AND place leg restraints before transporting to hospital
15a Handcuffs and leg restraints will be DOUBLE LOCKED
16 Remain with prisoner at all times in hospital
17 Notify D.O. of prisoner status prior to transport from local hospital to Bellevue/Elmhurst
18 Ride inside the ambulance when accompanying prisoner to Bellevue or Elmhurst
19 Make digital Activity Log entry: (a) Medic Alert Emblem if worn, (b) PIN (Patient Identification Number) — in place of ACR/PCR
20 Notify D.O. precinct of arrest/BCS re: Medical Alert Emblem and PIN

Prisoners Requiring Psychiatric Admission

Step Action
Notify D.O. of psych status Before leaving local hospital for Bellevue/Elmhurst
Transport Ride in (private) ambulance with prisoner to Bellevue/Elmhurst
Digital Activity Log Enter PIN # (Patient Identification Number) obtained from hospital intake

Exam Alert: The PIN (Patient Identification Number) is different from the ACR or PCR number. The PIN is obtained from hospital intake people — NOT from EMS. The exam tests: ACR = initial EMS transport, PCR = initial RMP/other transport, PIN = secondary transport to Bellevue/Elmhurst.

Psych Admission — Where Transported?

Gender Hospital
Men Bellevue
Women Elmhurst General

Exam Alert: Generally, men go to Bellevue and women go to Elmhurst for psychiatric admission. Transport is done by hospital via private contract — NOT FDNY EMS.

Psych Admission — D.O. Pct of Arrest/Boro Court Section

Duty Details
Confer with medical staff Medical staff determines appropriate transfer method and hospital
Determine additional resources D.O. determines if additional resources needed for the transfer
Additional resources decision Patrol Supervisor decides if extra personnel or equipment (like ESU) are needed

Desk Officer — Psych Treatment Documentation

Duty Details
Command Log entry PIN # (NOT ACR or PCR), Medic Alert information, new location of prisoner

Exam Alert: For psych transfers, the Desk Officer logs the PIN number in the Command Log — NOT the ACR or PCR. The exam may present choices mixing up these numbers.

Escorting Officer at Hospital

Step Duty
23 Request room change if security is inadequate (Escorting officer does this — NOT Patrol Supervisor!)
24 Do NOT remove handcuffs or leg restraints unless requested by attending physician
24a If physician requests removal, inform physician of circumstances of arrest
24b If physician still requests removal, request response of patrol supervisor to evaluate need for additional personnel and equipment (including ESU) to ensure safety during treatment prior to removal
25 If asked to leave exam room: remain immediately outside room, attempt to maintain visual contact
26 Make digital Activity Log entries; request attending physician to sign entries
26a If physician refuses to sign, indicate refusal on Activity Log
27 Complete entries on MTP form
28 Notify D.O. precinct of arrest (who notifies BCS immediately) if prisoner admitted to any hospital

Prior Test: Doctor asks the guarding officer to remove cuffs — what is the correct response? Explain circumstances of arrest. If doctor still wants them off, request Patrol Supervisor to respond. Do NOT remove without PS present and authorizing. BUT if doctor asks you to leave the room, you do NOT need the PS — just step outside and maintain visual contact.

Exam Alert: There is a critical distinction between a doctor asking to remove restraints (requires PS response and authorization) versus asking the officer to leave the examination room (officer complies, steps outside, maintains visual contact — no PS needed). The exam frequently tests this difference.

DOC Taking Custody — Psych Admission

Step Role Duty
29 D.O. Command of Arrest Inquire with DOC prison ward whether prisoner can be lodged; request DOC take custody
29a D.O. Make entry in Telephone Record
29b D.O. Notify escorting officer of results of inquiry with DOC
30 Escorting Officer Search prisoner per P.G. 208-05 for weapons, evidence, contraband regardless of prior search
30a Escorting Officer Make digital Activity Log entry indicating results of search
31 Escorting Officer Forward copy of OLBS ARREST WORKSHEET (PD244-159), MTP, Prisoner Movement Slip, and physical copy of active warrant(s) to DOC Supervisor
31 (warrant only) Escorting Officer If prisoner has active warrant only and no arrest number, forward MTP and physical copy of active warrant(s) only
31a Escorting Officer If DOC refuses custody, notify D.O. command of arrest

Exam Alert: If DOC refuses to take custody of prisoner, the D.O. command of arrest should consult with a supervisor from the appropriate Borough Court Section. The BCS supervisor will resolve custody issues with the DOC Custody Management Unit.

When Prisoner Released Back from DOC

Step Role Duty
32 BCS Immediately notify D.O. precinct of arrest when DOC releases prisoner to police custody
33 D.O. Command of Arrest Make Telephone Record entry (identity, location, arrest/warrant info); arrange EO to respond within 4 hours
34 D.O. Command of Arrest Arrange for EO to return to DOC prison ward within 4 hours of receiving notification from BCS

Exam Alert: The command MUST take custody within 4 hours from notification by DOC. If unable, a notification must be made to the Borough Court Section supervisor, who will coordinate with DOC Custody Management Unit.

Escorting Officer — Prisoner Released from DOC

Step Duty
35 Transport prisoner: (a) Return to command of arrest if processing not completed; (b) Remove to BCS if processing completed
35b(1) Confer with BCS Supervisor before transporting wheelchair/mobility-impaired prisoner
36 Deliver completed MTP to D.O., Borough Court Section facility

D.O. Borough Court Section — MTP Distribution

Step Duty
37 Forward three copies of MTP (white, blue, pink) with prisoner to DOC
37a Retain fourth (buff) copy at BCS facility
38 Direct A/O-EO to obtain receipt for prisoner on pink copy and return to BCS
38a-d Complete entries on buff copy; return buff to A/O-EO; file pink at BCS; forward duplicate MTP to A/O command of arrest if different

D.O. Command of Arrest — After DOC Release

Step Duty
39 Escorting Officer delivers buff copy MTP to D.O. command of arrest
40 Ensure all data from buff MTP entered into FORMS Medical Treatment of Prisoner application
41 Finalize ALL pending submissions within FORMS

Operations Coordinator, Command of Arrest

Step Duty
42 Ensure all MTPs finalized within FORMS
43 FILE the buff copy Medical Treatment of Prisoner Form

Prescription Drugs — Medication at Time of Arrest

Duty Details
Invoice Medication invoiced per P.G. 208-03, Arrests - General Processing
