  • The Department attorney’s advice overrides all other advice — including from supervisors and ADAs; sergeants cannot override this
  • If a sergeant receives contrary advice from an ADA after a Department attorney conferral, the sergeant must inform the ADA of the prior conferral and follow the Department attorney’s advice
  • The Command Log entry for conferral with a Department attorney must include all 7 required elements — this documentation protects both the member and the supervisor
  • For civil lawsuits, the REQUEST FOR LEGAL ASSISTANCE must be forwarded immediately — timeliness is critical to avoid default judgments
  • The CO must contact both IAB Records Unit and Department Advocate’s Office before completing Part B — this is a mandatory step, not discretionary
  • The WHITE copy goes DIRECT to Legal Bureau, Room 1406, One Police Plaza — NOT through channels
  • Members must follow up with Legal Bureau after 10 business days — if the REQUEST has not been received, notify the commanding officer
  • A Notice of Claim served on the NYPD is improper — do NOT accept it; direct the process server to the Office of the Comptroller
  • A REQUEST FOR LEGAL ASSISTANCE should NOT be prepared for a subpoena — subpoenas are handled under P.G. 211-15 or 211-16
  • Pro se complaints have specific filing deadlines that vary by member status (60/90/30 days) — the Legal Bureau handles identification and filing
  • If Corporation Counsel declines representation, the member must obtain private counsel or union representation — the sergeant should ensure the member is aware of this obligation

Reference: P.G. 211-20, 211-21

Section 211-09 — Appearances Before Governmental Agencies or Private Organizations

Purpose

To notify the Police Commissioner when a member intends to testify or make an official statement before a governmental or private organization about Department operations or policies.

Scope

This procedure applies to:

  • Official capacity testimony
  • Character/opinion/expert testimony
  • Appearances that are volunteered, invited, or subpoenaed

Does NOT Apply To

Exam Alert: Know which appearances are excluded from this procedure.

Excluded Appearances Reason
Criminal/civil courts Normal course of duty
Grand Jury Normal course of duty
Corporation Counsel Normal course of duty
Trial Room Normal course of duty
DOCCS hearings Normal course of duty
Traffic Violations Bureau (TVB) Normal course of duty
Parking Violations Bureau (PVB) Normal course of duty
OATH Normal course of duty
Non-official capacity speech that is not confidential Private speech

Written Request Requirements

Written request prepared in TRIPLICATE on Typed Letterhead addressed to the Police Commissioner.

Required Information

  • Rank, Name, Tax Registry Number, Command
  • Agency name and address
  • Date and location of appearance
  • Type of proceeding
  • Date of receipt of request/subpoena
  • For character testimony: include name, rank, TRN, and command of the person concerned

Forwarding Timeline and Distribution

Exam Alert: The 5 business day rule and triplicate distribution are high-priority exam targets.

Forward no later than 5 BUSINESS DAYS before testimony:

Copy Routing
Original Police Commissioner — DIRECT
Copy Police Commissioner — THROUGH CHANNELS
Copy Deputy Commissioner, Public Information — DIRECT

Exam Alert: Note the difference: one copy goes DIRECT to the PC, one goes THROUGH CHANNELS. A third copy goes DIRECT to DCPI.

Voluntary vs. Subpoenaed Appearances

Type Requirement
Voluntary Requires PRIOR WRITTEN APPROVAL from Police Commissioner
Subpoenaed Member is required to appear by law, but written notification to PC is still required

Exam Alert: Even subpoenaed appearances require written notification to the PC. The difference is that voluntary appearances need PRIOR APPROVAL, while subpoenaed appearances need notification (the member must still appear regardless).

Unable to Meet 5-Day Requirement

If unable to meet the 5 business day requirement:

  • Immediately notify the Office of the Police Commissioner or Legal Bureau by telephone

Conflict with Department Attorney Advice

If a conflict arises regarding testimony:

  • Member MUST FOLLOW Department attorney advice

Non-Official Capacity Appearances

When speaking in a non-official capacity:

Rule Requirement
Statement Must clearly state speaking on own behalf, not the Department’s
Timing Must be during OFF DUTY time

NY State Attorney General’s Law Enforcement Misconduct Investigative Office

Exam Alert: This is a newer provision with strict compliance requirements.

Members must:

  • Fully cooperate
  • Answer all questions fully and truthfully
  • Honor all requests for documents and evidence

Failure to comply = disciplinary action

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors should ensure members understand the mandatory nature of cooperation with the AG’s Misconduct Investigative Office. There is no discretion here — full cooperation is required.

Reference: P.G. 211-09

Section 211-07 — Prevention of Court Appearance on Scheduled Day Off

Purpose

To prevent members from being required to appear in court on their scheduled days off, and to establish procedures when such conflicts arise.

Member’s Responsibility — At Court

When a judge sets an adjournment date that conflicts with the member’s scheduled time off:

  1. Inform the judge of the conflict
  2. Request another date

If the rescheduled date still conflicts:

  1. Make a digital Activity Log entry with:
    • Judge name
    • ADA name
    • Appearance date
    • Adjournment date
    • Court and part
  2. Inform the ACU supervisor, who requests a new date per the member’s work schedule

Exam Alert: The member must first attempt to resolve the conflict directly with the judge before involving ACU.

ACU Supervisor Actions

If the ACU supervisor is unable to obtain a new date:

  • Signs the member’s digital Activity Log
  • Enters the information into the CPR System

Command-Level Responsibilities

Roll Call Clerk

Sergeant Focus: When a member’s court appearance is scheduled on an RDO:

  1. Make a diary entry
  2. Inform the Operations Coordinator

Operations Coordinator

  • Contacts the ADA to ascertain necessity of appearance
  • Attempts to reschedule if possible

Integrity Control Officer

  • Checks records relating to court appearances on RDOs

Sergeant Focus: Sergeants should be aware that the Integrity Control Officer monitors RDO court appearances for patterns that may indicate integrity issues.

Scheduling Members Appearing on RDO

Element Requirement
Start time 0900 hours or as appropriate
Tour length Consistent with normal chart

Excusal After Court

  • Member returning from court may be excused upon request if exigencies permit
  • Member enters Requested Excusal on the OVERTIME REPORT (PD138-064)

Exam Alert: Excusal is discretionary (may be excused… if exigencies permit), not automatic. This is a may vs. shall distinction.

Cancellation — Critical Rule

Exam Alert: This is one of the most important rules in Section 211.

A voicemail, email, or message left attempting to cancel is NOT sufficient.

The requesting agency must EXPLICITLY CONCUR with the cancellation.

If the agency does not concur, the member MUST appear as scheduled.

Method Sufficient?
Voicemail left with agency NO
Email sent to agency NO
Message left with agency NO
Agency explicitly agrees to cancel YES

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure members understand that unilateral cancellation attempts do not relieve them of the obligation to appear. Only explicit concurrence from the requesting agency cancels the appearance.

Reference: P.G. 211-07

Section 211-15 through 211-18 — Subpoena Processing and Records Requests

P.G. 211-15 — Processing Subpoenas for Police Department Records and Testimony

Updated: 12/31/24 | R.O. 78 | 6 pages

Purpose

To ensure proper compliance by the Police Department and its members with lawful court mandates and subpoenas requiring attendance at court or production of Department records.

Scope

The unit that is the repository of a given Department record has the responsibility to respond to a subpoena demanding the record’s production. A command will NOT forward a subpoena to the Legal Bureau, Central Records Division, or other units within the Department when the requested record(s) is/are present in the subpoenaed command.

Key Definitions

Term Definition
Subpoena Generic legal term for any device compelling appearance at a specific time/place for testimony or production of documents
Subpoena ad testificandum Compels a person to appear and give testimony
Subpoena duces tecum Compels the production of documents or evidence
Command Liaison Officer (CLO) Every commanding officer/counterpart — designated as the official liaison for implementation/compliance with this procedure; may delegate tasks but remains responsible
Subpoena Record Book Permanent bound record book maintained at each command with 12 captions (see below)
Command Subpoena File Chronological file of photocopies of subpoenas and ADA/ACC records requests

Subpoena Record Book — 12 Captions

Caption Content
a. Number (serialized, beginning with 001 each year)
b. Date and time received
c. Type of subpoena (duces tecum or ad testificandum)
d. Name of member concerned
e. Brief description of records requested
f. Return date
g. Issuing court
h. Title of case (e.g., Smith vs. Jones)
i. Party issuing subpoena (plaintiff, defendant, DA, or judge)
j. Fee receipt issued (indicate No fee or receipt number)
k. Date records forwarded
l. Date court receipt received

Exam Alert: The Subpoena Record Book has 12 captions (a through l). Know especially caption j (fee receipt) and caption l (date court receipt received) — these track compliance documentation.

Critical Rule — No Forwarding Without Permission

Under NO circumstances will a subpoena or subpoena duces tecum served on or received at a command be forwarded to the Subpoena Litigation Unit without the express permission of the Supervisor, Subpoena Litigation Unit.

Exam Alert: This is one of the most important rules in 211-15. Commands do NOT forward subpoenas to the Subpoena Litigation Unit without permission. This applies to both the subpoena itself AND original records.

Procedure — Subpoena for Personal Appearance Only (Ad Testificandum)

Upon receiving a subpoena that only requires personal appearance/testimony:

Step Role Action
1 CLO Record receipt of subpoena in Subpoena Record Book; ensure all captions completed
2 CLO Issue FEE RECEIPT (PD122-017) per P.G. 212-82 if fee is tendered
3 CLO Photocopy subpoena; deliver ORIGINAL to member concerned
3a Note Command Serial Number, Fee Receipt number, date/time of delivery on reverse of photocopy; member signs photocopy
4 CLO Direct member to comply with subpoena
5 CLO File photocopy in Command Subpoena File
6 CLO Notify roll call clerk; ensure Command Diary entry is made
6a Comply with notification procedures to Appearance Control Unit
7 CLO Ensure member has appeared in court as required

So-Ordered Requirement — Subpoena Duces Tecum

Exam Alert: This is a CRITICAL distinction frequently tested. A subpoena duces tecum directed to the Police Department MUST be so-ordered by a judge. If not so-ordered, Department records will NOT be produced.

Situation Action
Subpoena duces tecum is so-ordered Comply — produce the requested documents
Subpoena duces tecum is NOT so-ordered Member complies with personal appearance only; Department records will NOT be produced
If issuing attorney’s contact info is available CLO contacts attorney and advises that unless so-ordered, records will not be produced; no further action required

Exam Alert: So-ordered means the attorney who desires the documents has obtained judicial approval for the subpoena duces tecum. The judge indicates this by signing or initialing at a caption entitled so-ordered.

Procedure — Subpoena Duces Tecum (Documents Only, No Personal Appearance)

Step Role Action
8 CLO Ensure subpoena duces tecum has been endorsed by judge as so-ordered
8a If NOT so-ordered: make Subpoena Record Book entries, indicate Date of Compliance that subpoena was not so-ordered, file copy in rear of Command Subpoena File
8b If attorney contact available and not so-ordered: advise that records will not be produced unless so-ordered; no further action
9 CLO Complete all applicable captions in Subpoena Record Book
10 CLO Gather photocopies of all documents requested in subpoena maintained at command (including Activity Log entries, if applicable)
11 CLO Prepare and sign Certification on OFFICIAL LETTERHEAD (PD158-151)
11a If photocopies contain redactions: use Certification letter Number 1 (Appendix A)
11b If photocopies contain NO redactions: use Certification letter Number 2 (Appendix B)
11c If records DO NOT EXIST at command or any other command/unit: use Certification letter Number 3 (Appendix C)
12 CLO Prepare RECEIPT FOR SUBPOENAED DEPARTMENT RECORDS on OFFICIAL LETTERHEAD (PD158-151) (Appendix D)
13 CLO Forward copies of records, copy of subpoena, certification, and receipt to clerk of court via U.S. Mail or Department mail
13a Indicate on outside of envelope that it contains records requested via subpoena
13b Include index, calendar, docket or indictment number as available
14 CLO Upon receiving court receipt: attach to filed subpoena copy; note in Subpoena Record Book under Date Court Receipt Received

Exam Alert: There are 3 certification letter variants: (1) with redactions, (2) without redactions, (3) records do not exist. The CLO may delegate the authority to prepare and sign the certification to properly trained members within the command.

Redaction Rules

Rule Detail
When to redact When subpoenaed records contain information unrelated to the facts of the case
Method Block out or delete so unrelated information cannot be discerned
NO redactions to Legal Bureau, Corporation Counsel, or DA Redaction rules do NOT apply to material forwarded to these entities
Before forwarding to anyone outside PD (other than ADA/ACC/law enforcement) Redact name, address, telephone number, and any data identifying a victim or witness

Exam Alert: The redaction rule has two layers: (1) redact unrelated information from all subpoenaed records, and (2) additionally redact victim/witness identifying information before forwarding to parties outside the PD (other than ADA/ACC/law enforcement). NO redactions apply to material sent to Legal Bureau, Corporation Counsel, or DA.

Non-Trial Testimony — Consultation Rule

Members receiving a subpoena ad testificandum requiring testimony at other than a trial proceeding are advised: - Do NOT answer any question without first consulting with the ADA/ACC assigned to the case - If no ADA/ACC is assigned or the member is unsure, consult the Subpoena Litigation Unit

Notifications to Subpoena Litigation Unit — Immediate Telephone Call Required

Make immediate telephone notification to the Subpoena Litigation Unit if any command receives a subpoena for:

Category Description
a. Confidential records (personnel folders, documents relating to confidential investigations)
b. Records relating to cases of notoriety or publicity
c. Records relating to sensitive or unusual cases and corruption cases

Also notify if there is any doubt about whether subpoenaed records should be released. Record notification in Telephone Record. Instructions from Subpoena Litigation Unit will be strictly complied with.

Notifications in Unusual Cases

Situation Action
Member is witness for defendant in criminal case, or for plaintiff where City of New York is defendant Notify ADA or ACC of this fact prior to start of hearing or trial
Subpoena to appear at parole revocation hearing Notify DA’s Office of the appropriate county; note notification in Command Diary

Records Forwarding Rules

Rule Detail
Original records Under NO circumstances will original records be removed from a Department facility without prior approval of a Department attorney or the commanding officer
Photocopies ONLY Only photocopies will be forwarded
Records not at receiving command Forward subpoena to command having custody of the record
Multiple-item subpoena Receiving command complies with items it has; forwards photocopy of subpoena to command with remaining items
Records to attorneys (non-ADA/ACC) NEVER forward records directly to an attorney without prior consultation with Subpoena Litigation Unit

ADA/ACC Requests — No Subpoena Required

ADAs and ACCs are NOT required to issue a subpoena for production of Police Department records. No member will require or request a subpoena for records from ADAs/ACCs. Such document requests are handled per P.G. 211-18.

Transferred Members

Situation Action
Member transferred from command Process server advised of new command; directed to serve there
Member retired Process server informed; service will not be accepted
Subpoena received by non-personal delivery for transferred member Forward subpoena to member’s current command
Non-personal delivery for retired member Contact issuing attorney to inform of retirement

Subpoenas Concerning Personal Matters

A subpoena served on a member in their capacity as a private individual (e.g., civil litigation, witness to off-duty events) may be served at command. If accepted by someone other than the named party (e.g., desk officer), that person must ensure the subpoena is actually received by the named member. Fee tendered goes to the member. Court appearance is on the member’s off-duty time. Questions go to Subpoena Litigation Unit.

P.G. 211-16 — Subpoenas Issued by Courts Outside New York City

Purpose

To determine whether an out-of-NYC subpoena requires a response and the nature of that response.

NYC Court Subpoenas

  • Valid — must comply per applicable procedures

Fee Receipt

  • Fee Receipt (PD122-017) issued per P.G. 212-79

New York State Court Subpoenas (Outside NYC)

Scenario Action
No personal appearance required Comply per P.G. 211-15
Personal appearance required Consult Legal Bureau Subpoena Litigation Unit + comply with P.G. 212-83 (Leaving City on Police Business)

Federal Court Subpoenas

Criminal Cases

Exam Alert: Federal criminal subpoenas have broader reach than civil subpoenas.

  • Valid from any Federal Court in the United States
Requirement Action
Documents only Comply per P.G. 211-15
Personal appearance Notify Subpoena Litigation Unit + report to Police Commissioner

Report to PC format: - On Typed Letterhead, in duplicate - Include: court name/location, type of proceeding, person concerned, date of receipt - If PC not notified prior: include reason - Original — direct to PC - Duplicate — through channels

Civil Cases

Exam Alert: Federal civil subpoenas have limited validity compared to criminal subpoenas. This is a frequently tested distinction.

A Federal civil subpoena is valid ONLY when:

Condition Detail
1 Served within the court’s district
2 Served within 100 miles of the hearing place
3 Issued by a Federal Court in NYS and served in NYS
4 Endorsed by a judge for extraordinary service

Out-of-State Subpoenas (Other States)

Exam Alert: This is a critical distinction for the exam.

Criminal or civil subpoenas from other states are NOT VALID unless presented to and endorsed by a New York State judge.

However: - The Department should make every reasonable effort to assist other jurisdictions - Permission to appear obtained through channels - Comply with leaving city procedures

Exam Alert: Not valid does not mean ignore. The Department still cooperates — the member just needs proper authorization through channels.

When in Doubt

Consult the Legal Bureau Subpoena Litigation Unit.

P.G. 211-17 — Processing Legal Bureau Requests for Department Records Including FOIL

Purpose

To ensure prompt response to lawful subpoenas and FOIL requests.

FOIL — Freedom of Information Law

Exam Alert: FOIL provisions are heavily tested. Know the presumption, timeline, and fee.

Element Detail
Statute NYS Public Officers Law Sections 84-89
Presumption All government records are PRESUMED available for public inspection
Exemptions Narrowly drawn — burden on agency to justify denial

Key Personnel

Role Description
Subpoena Litigation Unit One Police Plaza, Room 110C
FOIL Records Access Officer Supervisor in charge of FOIL Unit within Subpoena Litigation Unit
FOIL Appeals Officer Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters
Command Liaison Officer Every CO — designated official liaison for tracking records requests

FOIL Response Timeline

Exam Alert: The 5 business day deadline is a critical number.

Within 5 BUSINESS DAYS, the Department must:

Option Action
1 Make record available
2 Deny in writing
3 Acknowledge receipt with approximate date of availability

Key Rules for Records Handling

Rule Detail
Subpoenas served at commands NOT forwarded to Subpoena Litigation Unit without permission
Photocopies Must be complete, clear, legible, without alteration
Confidentiality concerns Forward report on Typed Letterhead to Supervisor, Subpoena Litigation Unit
Original records Under NO circumstances forward to Subpoena Litigation Unit without permission

Exam Alert: Two things require permission: (1) forwarding subpoenas served at commands, and (2) forwarding original records. Never forward originals without permission.

FOIL Denial Appeals

Element Detail
Time limit 30 days to appeal
Appeals to Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters

FOIL Photocopy Fee

Exam Alert: A commonly tested number.

25 cents per page

P.G. 211-18 — Processing Requests for Department Documents from ADAs and Assistant Corporation Counsels

Purpose

To ensure prompt compliance with ADA/ACC document requests.

Brady Obligation

Exam Alert: This is one of the most critical legal concepts in this section.

Failure to turn over reports results in REVERSAL OF CONVICTION — even if the prosecutor was unaware of the report’s existence.

Courts have held: Possession by NYPD = possession by the prosecutor.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must understand that ANY Department document relevant to a case is considered to be in the prosecutor’s possession. Failure to produce it is a Brady violation that can overturn a conviction.

Request Requirements

Element Detail
Subpoena required? NO — ADA/ACC request does NOT require an accompanying subpoena
Format Request on DA/Corp Counsel letterhead

Required Information on Request

  • Case name
  • Attorney name and phone number
  • Arrest date
  • Precinct of arrest
  • Arresting officer name
  • Docket number

Recording

  • Record in Subpoena Record Book under:
    • D.A. Request or
    • Corporation Counsel Request

Doubt About Propriety

  • Consult Legal Bureau Subpoena Litigation Unit

Summary Arrests — Special Requirement

Sergeant Focus: For summary arrests, the arresting/assigned officer shall:

  1. Secure photocopies of ALL Department forms
  2. Secure photocopies of ALL reports
  3. Secure photocopies of ALL personal notes
  4. Secure photocopies of ALL scrap-paper notes
  5. Make all photocopies available to ADA/ACC at the complaint room

Exam Alert: Note that scrap-paper notes are specifically included. ALL notes, regardless of format, must be produced. This reinforces the Brady obligation.

Common Exam Angles

  1. So-ordered requirement — Subpoena duces tecum directed to the Police Department MUST be so-ordered by a judge; if not, Department records will NOT be produced
  2. Subpoena Record Book — 12 captions — Know all 12 (number, date/time received, type, member name, description, return date, issuing court, case title, party issuing, fee receipt, date forwarded, date court receipt received)
  3. No forwarding without permission — Subpoenas and original records NEVER forwarded to Subpoena Litigation Unit without express permission
  4. 3 certification letters — (1) with redactions, (2) without redactions, (3) records do not exist
  5. Redaction rules — Redact unrelated info; additionally redact victim/witness identifiers before forwarding outside PD; NO redactions to Legal Bureau/Corporation Counsel/DA
  6. Non-trial testimony — Do NOT answer questions without first consulting ADA/ACC or Subpoena Litigation Unit
  7. Immediate SLU notification — Required for confidential records, notoriety/publicity cases, sensitive/unusual/corruption cases, or any doubt
  8. CLO is the commanding officer — Every CO is designated as Command Liaison Officer; may delegate but retains responsibility
  9. Original records — NEVER removed from Department facility without approval of Department attorney or commanding officer; photocopies ONLY
  10. ADA/ACC requests — no subpoena needed — ADAs and ACCs do NOT need a subpoena for Department records (P.G. 211-18)
  11. Brady obligation — NYPD possession = prosecutor’s possession; failure to produce = reversal of conviction
  12. FOIL — 5 business days — Must make record available, deny in writing, or acknowledge receipt
  13. FOIL appeal — 30 days to appeal to DC Legal Matters
  14. FOIL photocopy fee — 25 cents per page
  15. Summary arrest notes — ALL notes including scrap-paper notes must be photocopied and made available to ADA/ACC
  16. Federal criminal subpoenas — Valid from any Federal Court in US; if personal appearance required, report to PC on Typed Letterhead in duplicate
  17. Federal civil subpoenas — Valid ONLY when: within court’s district, within 100 miles, issued by NYS Federal Court and served in NYS, or endorsed for extraordinary service
  18. Out-of-state subpoenas — NOT valid unless endorsed by a NYS judge; Department still cooperates through channels
  19. Subpoena concerning personal matters — Member appears on off-duty time; fee goes to the member
  20. Transferred/retired members — Transferred: subpoena forwarded to new command; Retired: service not accepted

Sergeant Study Notes

  • The Command Liaison Officer is the commanding officer — sergeants should understand that the CO bears ultimate responsibility for subpoena compliance, though tasks may be delegated
  • The so-ordered requirement for subpoena duces tecum is the most critical rule in 211-15 — if not so-ordered, documents are not produced regardless of what the attorney demands
  • Subpoena Record Book must be maintained as a permanent bound record with all 12 captions — sergeants should verify entries are complete
  • NEVER forward original records outside the facility without Department attorney or CO approval — photocopies ONLY
  • Redaction has two layers: (1) unrelated information is redacted from all subpoenaed records, (2) victim/witness identifying data is additionally redacted before forwarding outside PD — but NO redaction applies to material going to Legal Bureau, Corporation Counsel, or DA
  • For non-trial testimony, members must consult with the assigned ADA/ACC before answering any questions — if unsure who is assigned, contact the Subpoena Litigation Unit
  • The Subpoena Litigation Unit must be immediately notified by telephone for confidential records, high-profile cases, and sensitive/corruption matters — record notification in the Telephone Record
  • Brady obligation means every Department document relevant to a case is considered in the prosecutor’s possession — even scrap-paper notes
  • FOIL requests require response within 5 business days — appeals go to the Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters within 30 days
  • ADA/ACC requests do NOT require a subpoena — the Department will not request or require ADAs/ACCs to serve subpoenas for records
  • For unusual cases: if a member is testifying for the defendant or the City is a defendant, notify the ADA/ACC before the hearing or trial starts
  • The 3 certification letter variants (with redactions, without redactions, records do not exist) ensure proper documentation of compliance — the CLO may delegate signing authority to trained members

Reference: P.G. 211-15, 211-16, 211-17, 211-18

Section 211-11 — Traffic Violations Bureau Appearances / Computerized Time Slips

Purpose

To record and monitor TVB hearing appearances.

Member’s Procedure at TVB

  1. Bring Officer’s Notes copy of the summons
  2. Bring Activity Log entry/printout for each summons
  3. Report immediately to the Police Sign-In Room supervisor

TVB Supervisor Responsibility

Sergeant Focus: Have the member logged into the computer system IMMEDIATELY.

Exam Alert: If the member’s presence is not entered in the system, judges may dismiss cases for non-appearance. Prompt computer entry is critical.

After the Hearing

  1. Return immediately to the Police Sign-In Room
  2. Receive the Officer Time Slip (computerized)
  3. Notify command desk officer by telephone immediately upon dismissal

Exam Alert: The Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) is NOT used for TVB appearances. The Officer Time Slip replaces it.

Form Used at TVB?
Officer Time Slip (computerized) YES
Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) NO

Command-Level Processing

Desk Officer

Sergeant Focus:

  • Reviews time slips for accuracy
  • Notes time officer returns to command

Clerical Member

  • Files time slips chronologically in a separate folder

Integrity Control Officer

  • Reviews time slips frequently for integrity concerns

Training Sergeant — Monthly Review

Sergeant Focus: This is a direct sergeant responsibility and a likely exam topic.

The Training Sergeant reviews the Disposition and Code columns for negative patterns.

Monthly Selection Process

Step Action
Frequency Monthly
Selection 10 members with the highest percentage of dismissals
Criteria Failure to establish prima facie case OR Not Guilty due to unclear evidence
Action Members retrained and/or subject to corrective action

Exam Alert: The number is 10 members selected monthly based on highest percentage of dismissals — not a fixed number of dismissals, but the highest percentage.

TVB Disposition Codes

Hearing Dispositions

Code Meaning
N Not Guilty
G Guilty
W Guilty with Appearance (plea, no officer effect)
D Dismissing Case
P Paid Online

Dismissal Reason Codes

Exam Alert: Know the difference between dismissal codes (why the case was dismissed) and disposition codes (hearing outcome).

Code Meaning (Not Guilty) Meaning (Dismissals)
1 Evidence not clear/convincing Insured
2 Justification Licensed
3 — Registered
4 — Equipment docs presented
5 — Officer No-Show
6 — No Prima Facie case
7 — Cannot identify motorist
8 — No recollection
9 — Non-Owner / No Knowledge

Sergeant Focus: Codes 5 (Officer No-Show), 6 (No Prima Facie case), 7 (Cannot identify motorist), and 8 (No recollection) are the codes most likely to trigger training sergeant review and corrective action. These reflect officer performance issues.

Reference: P.G. 211-11

Key Terms — Section 211: Court & Agency Appearances

Term Definition
CPR System Centralized Personnel Resource System — monitors and controls all court and agency appearances
Short Date Notification Notification to appear the next day (or Friday for Monday) — time-sensitive, triggers email notifications to member, roll call, operations coordinator, and integrity control officer
Telephone Alert System to prevent unnecessary court appearances; member assigned to 2nd platoon, no meal before 1300 hours; if not called by 1300, alert NOT activated
Appearance Control Unit (ACU) Processes all court appearance notifications, mediates conflicts between appearances, monitors member attendance
Officer Time Slip Computerized slip from TVB showing hearing disposition; replaces Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) for TVB appearances
FOIL Freedom of Information Law — NYS Public Officers Law Sections 84-89; all government records presumed available for public inspection; 5 business day response required
Records Access Officer Supervisor of the FOIL Unit within the Legal Bureau’s Subpoena Litigation Unit
Command Liaison Officer Every commanding officer — designated official liaison for tracking records requests from the Subpoena Litigation Unit
Subpoena Litigation Unit Legal Bureau sub-unit located at One Police Plaza, Room 110C; processes all subpoenas and FOIL requests
4th Platoon Rule Members on 4th platoon (1800x0200) with next-day court are reassigned to 3rd platoon (1600x2400); members not notified in advance are excused at 2400 (no OT for 2400-0200)
5 Business Days (1) Time before testimony to notify Police Commissioner of governmental/private organization appearances; (2) FOIL response deadline
5 Days Maximum Temporary DA assignment limit without CO endorsement to Chief of Department
1300 Hours Telephone alert cutoff — if member on alert is not called to court by 1300, the alert is NOT activated that day
25 Cents Per Page FOIL photocopy fee
30 Days Time limit to appeal a FOIL denial to the Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters
0700-2300 Mon-Fri Hours to contact a Department attorney for legal advice; outside these hours, contact CO/duty captain
Brady Obligation Prosecution’s duty to disclose exculpatory evidence; NYPD possession of records = prosecutor possession; failure to produce = potential conviction reversal
Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) Standard court attendance form — notably NOT used for TVB appearances (Officer Time Slip used instead)
Fee Receipt (PD122-017) Issued per P.G. 212-79 for out-of-NYC subpoenas
Subpoena Record Book Command-level log for tracking all subpoenas and DA/Corporation Counsel requests
Typed Letterhead Required format for written notifications to the Police Commissioner, reports to Subpoena Litigation Unit, and Federal criminal subpoena reports

Critical Numbers at a Glance

Number Context
3 Times daily roll call clerk must access CPR System
5 business days FOIL response deadline AND PC notification deadline for governmental appearances
5 days Maximum temporary DA assignment without CO endorsement
6-30 days DA assignment extension requiring CO endorsement to Chief of Department
10 Members selected monthly by Training Sergeant for TVB dismissal review
25 cents FOIL photocopy fee per page
30 days FOIL denial appeal period
1300 hours Telephone alert cutoff time
0700-2300 Department attorney contact hours (Mon-Fri)
Triplicate Copies required for PC notification of governmental appearances

Reference: P.G. 211-05, 211-06, 211-07, 211-09, 211-11, 211-15, 211-16, 211-17, 211-18, 211-20

Review Questions — Section 211: Court & Agency Appearances

Question 1

According to P.G. 211-05, how many times daily must the roll call clerk access the CPR System to check for court appearance notifications?

    1. Once, at the beginning of the business day
    1. Twice — beginning and end of the business day
    1. Three times — beginning, midpoint, and 30 minutes before close
    1. Four times — at regular intervals throughout the day
Answer Correct Answer: C) The roll call clerk must access the CPR System 3 times daily: (1) at the beginning of the business day, (2) at the midpoint of roll call office hours, and (3) 30 minutes before the close of roll call office hours. Reference: P.G. 211-05

Question 2

Which of the following is a valid reason to cancel a court appearance under P.G. 211-05?

    1. Member has a personal appointment
    1. Member is sick, after conferral with the district surgeon
    1. Member left a voicemail with the DA’s office requesting cancellation
    1. Member’s supervisor determines the appearance is unnecessary
Answer Correct Answer: B) Cancellation is accepted ONLY if the member is: sick (after conferral with district surgeon), on annual vacation selection, military leave, bereavement leave, or terminal leave. A voicemail is never sufficient — the requesting agency must explicitly concur. Reference: P.G. 211-05

Question 3

A short date notification under P.G. 211-05 is defined as:

    1. A notification for a member to appear within 72 hours
    1. A notification for a member to appear the next day, or a notification delivered on Friday for a Monday appearance
    1. A notification received less than one week before the appearance date
    1. Any notification that bypasses the normal CPR System process
Answer Correct Answer: B) A short date notification is a notification for a member to appear the next day, or a notification delivered on Friday for a Monday appearance. It is time-sensitive and triggers immediate email notifications to the member, roll call personnel, operations coordinator, and integrity control officer. Reference: P.G. 211-05

Question 4

Under the 4th Platoon Rule in P.G. 211-05, a member normally assigned to the 4th platoon (1800x0200) who is subpoenaed to appear in court the next day shall be:

    1. Excused from the court appearance
    1. Placed on overtime for the court appearance
    1. Reassigned to the 3rd platoon (1600x2400)
    1. Reassigned to the 1st platoon (0800x1600)
Answer Correct Answer: C) Members normally assigned to 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed or directed to appear the next day are reassigned to the 3rd platoon (1600x2400). Members not notified in advance who are already on the 4th platoon are excused at 2400 hours, with no overtime authorized for the 2400-0200 period. Reference: P.G. 211-05

Question 5

Under the criminal court telephone alert system (P.G. 211-06), a member on alert shall not be given a meal period before what time?

    1. 1100 hours
    1. 1200 hours
    1. 1300 hours
    1. 1400 hours
Answer Correct Answer: C) Under the criminal court telephone alert, NO meal period is authorized before 1300 hours. The member must remain available for a call to court. Reference: P.G. 211-06

Question 6

If a member on criminal court telephone alert is not directed to report to court by 1300 hours, what happens?

    1. The member is placed on standby until end of tour
    1. The alert is NOT activated for that day
    1. The member is assigned to administrative duties
    1. The member is directed to contact ACU for further instructions
Answer Correct Answer: B) If a member on telephone alert is not directed to court by 1300 hours, the alert is NOT activated for that day. The 1300 hours cutoff is absolute. Reference: P.G. 211-06

Question 7

Under P.G. 211-06, the ACU supervisor must notify the commanding officer IN WRITING when:

    1. A member requests a schedule change due to court
    1. A member fails to appear when called OR appears in court without being called
    1. A member’s court case is adjourned
    1. A member is placed on telephone alert for three consecutive days
Answer Correct Answer: B) The ACU supervisor must notify the CO in writing when: (a) a member fails to appear when called to court, OR (b) a member appears in court without being called. Both scenarios trigger mandatory written notification. Reference: P.G. 211-06

Question 8

When a member intends to testify before a governmental agency about Department operations (P.G. 211-09), written notification must be forwarded to the Police Commissioner no later than:

    1. 3 business days before the appearance
    1. 5 business days before the appearance
    1. 7 calendar days before the appearance
    1. 10 business days before the appearance
Answer Correct Answer: B) Written notification must be forwarded no later than 5 business days before the testimony. If unable to meet this deadline, the member must immediately notify the Office of the Police Commissioner or Legal Bureau by telephone. Reference: P.G. 211-09

Question 9

Under P.G. 211-09, the written request to the Police Commissioner for appearances before governmental agencies is prepared in how many copies?

    1. Duplicate (2 copies)
    1. Triplicate (3 copies)
    1. Quadruplicate (4 copies)
    1. A single original only
Answer Correct Answer: B) The written request is prepared in triplicate: the original goes direct to the Police Commissioner, one copy goes through channels to the Police Commissioner, and one copy goes direct to the Deputy Commissioner, Public Information. Reference: P.G. 211-09

Question 10

A member who wishes to voluntarily testify before a private organization about Department policies must obtain:

    1. Verbal approval from their commanding officer
    1. Prior written approval from the Police Commissioner
    1. Approval from the Legal Bureau only
    1. No prior approval, but must file a report afterward
Answer Correct Answer: B) Voluntary appearances require PRIOR WRITTEN APPROVAL from the Police Commissioner. This is distinct from subpoenaed appearances, which require notification but not prior approval (since the member is legally compelled to appear). Reference: P.G. 211-09

Question 11

At the Traffic Violations Bureau, which form is NOT used to record officer court attendance?

    1. Officer Time Slip
    1. Activity Log
    1. Court Attendance Record (PD468-141)
    1. Officer’s Notes copy of summons
Answer Correct Answer: C) The Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) is NOT used for TVB appearances. Instead, the computerized Officer Time Slip is used to record TVB hearing attendance and dispositions. Reference: P.G. 211-11

Question 12

Under P.G. 211-11, the training sergeant’s monthly review of TVB dispositions involves selecting how many members for potential retraining?

    1. 5 members with the most dismissals
    1. 10 members with the highest percentage of dismissals
    1. All members with any dismissals
    1. 15 members with the most court appearances
Answer Correct Answer: B) The training sergeant monthly selects 10 members with the highest percentage of dismissals for failure to establish prima facie case or Not Guilty due to unclear evidence. These members are retrained and/or subject to corrective action. Reference: P.G. 211-11

Question 13

Under the Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), the Department must respond to a records request within:

    1. 3 business days
    1. 5 business days
    1. 10 business days
    1. 30 calendar days
Answer Correct Answer: B) FOIL requires a response within 5 business days. The response must either: (1) make the record available, (2) deny the request in writing, or (3) acknowledge receipt with an approximate date of availability. Reference: P.G. 211-17

Question 14

A subpoena issued by a court in another state (not New York) is:

    1. Always valid and must be complied with immediately
    1. Valid only if served within New York City
    1. NOT valid unless presented to and endorsed by a New York State judge
    1. Valid only for criminal matters, not civil
Answer Correct Answer: C) Out-of-state subpoenas (criminal or civil) are NOT valid unless presented to and endorsed by a New York State judge. However, the Department should make every reasonable effort to assist other jurisdictions, with permission obtained through channels. Reference: P.G. 211-16

Question 15

Under P.G. 211-20, if a member receives advice from a supervisor or ADA that contradicts the Department attorney’s prior advice, the member MUST:

    1. Follow the supervisor’s or ADA’s advice as the most recent guidance
    1. Use personal judgment to determine the best course of action
    1. Follow the Department attorney’s advice and inform the other party of the prior conferral
    1. Request a meeting between the Department attorney and the supervisor/ADA before taking action
Answer Correct Answer: C) The member MUST inform the individual giving contrary advice of the prior conferral with the Department attorney. If there is a suggestion to disregard the Department attorney’s advice, the member or supervisor MUST notify the Department attorney and MUST FOLLOW THE DEPARTMENT ATTORNEY’S ADVICE. Disregarding Department attorney advice may result in disciplinary action and civil liability. Reference: P.G. 211-20

Total: 15 Questions | Covers: P.G. 211-05, 211-06, 211-07, 211-09, 211-11, 211-15, 211-16, 211-17, 211-18, 211-20

Section 212 — Command Operations

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 212
  • Source PDF: public-pguide2.pdf
  • Priority: High

Overview

Section 212 is the largest section in the Patrol Guide, covering 56 procedures (212-03 through 212-129) that govern day-to-day operational procedures for command-level activities. This chapter spans tour management, transit and housing operations, special incidents, media relations, firearms licensing, election duties, technology systems, and interaction procedures.

Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

  1. Explain tour expiration procedures, Activity Log requirements, and the 4th platoon rule
  2. Describe transit evacuation types (reach train vs. rescue train) and power removal criteria
  3. Apply proper procedures for critical transit situations and subway power removal
  4. Identify rules governing service animals, photography, and radio malfunction on transit
  5. Conduct proper inspections of NYCHA facilities and transit stations
  6. Handle incidents involving former/retired MOS, diplomats, and off-duty law enforcement
  7. Apply media access rules including MOME press card privileges and First Amendment protections
  8. Verify pistol licenses and process rifle/shotgun permit surrenders
  9. Evaluate and initiate Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPOs)
  10. Execute election-day duties before, during, and after polls
  11. Activate and cancel AMBER Alerts with proper authority
  12. Understand facial recognition limitations and VGTOF handling codes
  13. Ensure effective communication with persons who are deaf or hard of hearing
  14. Manage auxiliary police deployments and coordinator requirements

Chapter Files

Operational Procedures

File Procedures Covered Topic
section-212-tour-operations 212-03, 212-08, 212-10 Tour expiration, Activity Logs, Interrupted Patrol Log
section-212-crime-scene-encounters 212-04, 212-11 Crime scene procedures, investigative encounters (Stop, Question & Frisk), three levels, RTKBC, Stop Report review
section-212-transit-operations 212-16, 212-17, 212-19, 212-21, 212-22, 212-23, 212-24 Train evacuations, critical situations, animals, photography, radio malfunction, station inspection, power removal
section-212-housing-operations 212-26, 212-28, 212-60 NYCHA facility inspection, disabled elevators, interior patrol
section-212-special-incidents 212-36, 212-43, 212-45, 212-47, 212-54, 212-55, 212-56 Former/retired MOS, vacate orders, asylum, counterfeit money, jumpers, fireworks injuries, diplomatic incidents
section-212-hazmat-bombs-fire 212-37, 212-40, 212-58 Hazardous materials (SECTAR, IACC), bombs/explosive devices, fire procedures, two alarm fires, CIMS
section-212-media-information 212-49, 212-76, 212-77 Press access, MOME credentials, official information release, release of info to news media, RATCOP
section-212-firearms-licensing 212-44, 212-46, 212-84, 212-85, 212-118, 212-128 Firearms dealers, unlawful possession, pistol license verification, rifle/shotgun permits, ERPO

section-212-miscellaneous 212-06, 212-41, 212-48, 212-50, 212-51, 212-52, 212-65, 212-66, 212-69, 212-70, 212-73, 212-74, 212-81, 212-82, 212-79, 212-91, 212-100 Reserve surgeon, alarms, labor disputes, bicycle patrol, immigration policy, civilian commendations, subpoena fees, auxiliary police

Study Materials

File Contents

Note: This is the largest section (56 procedures). Study sessions are organized into 10 logical sub-groups above.

Sergeant Focus Areas

  • Daily command operations and supervision (tour expiration, Activity Logs, Interrupted Patrol Log)
  • Transit emergency management (evacuations, power removal, critical situations)
  • Housing facility inspections and interior patrols
  • Media relations and press access rules
  • Firearms licensing verification and ERPO evaluation
  • Election-day duties and poll security
  • Technology system limitations (facial recognition, AMBER Alert authority)
  • Auxiliary police coordinator requirements
  • ADA compliance for deaf/hard of hearing interactions

Section 212 — Crime Scene and Investigative Encounters

P.G. 212-04 — Crime Scene

Police Line vs. Crime Scene Tape

Tape Type Color / Letters Purpose
Police Line Do Not Cross White tape with blue letters Establish a secure area for police operations (close streets, secure transit facilities, etc.)
Crime Scene Do Not Cross Yellow tape with black letters Safeguard a specific area where objects may be evidence; may be within the Police Line
  • Both tapes also display: NYPD Crime Stoppers 1-800-577-TIPS (8744)
  • Crime Scene tape: Only authorized, trained, equipped, actively involved personnel whose presence is absolutely required will be permitted — absent exigents involving life or safety
  • Deployment of Crime Scene tape for any other reason is strictly prohibited

Exam Alert: Know the color distinction: Police Line = white tape/blue letters (secure area). Crime Scene = yellow tape/black letters (evidence area). Crime Scene tape may exist within the Police Line perimeter.

Crime Scene Evidence Cone

  • 5-inch-tall orange cone with NYPD in black letters
  • Placed next to small evidence to prevent disturbance (shell casings, bullet fragments, etc.)

Exam Alert: Per PG 219-01, the sergeant’s vehicle should contain 5 Crime Scene Evidence Cones (Five by Five).

Barrier Tape Duty — Key Distinction

PG Conflict: In PG 212-04 (Crime Scene), it is the UMOS duty to utilize barrier tape as appropriate. In PG 212-05 (Transit Crime Scene), it is the Patrol Supervisor who utilizes barrier tape. Know which role applies to each procedure.

UMOS Responsibilities

  • Request through radio dispatcher:
    • Patrol Supervisor
    • PDS / Specialty Unit required
  • Detain witnesses and persons with pertinent information
  • Secure Crime Scene:
    • Remove unauthorized persons
    • Do not disturb evidence
    • Initially secure a LARGE area when uncertain
    • Utilize barrier tape and Crime Scene Evidence Cones as necessary
  • Advise Patrol Supervisor and Detectives of witnesses detained and other case info
  • Document: Digital Activity Log entries (observations, suspect/witness info, statements)

Patrol Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Assess the scene and determine if CSU services are required
  • Call CSU direct on landline or if no landline available, through radio dispatcher

Exam Alert: On test day, the correct answer for how the Patrol Supervisor contacts CSU is via landline (direct call). Do NOT choose department-issued cell phone — the PG specifies landline first, radio dispatcher only if landline unavailable.

  • Provide specific details: cross streets, apt #, time/date, crime, weapon, # of victims, hospital
  • If Patrol Supervisor cannot respond to scene: detective on scene MAY request CSU and other required notifications

Exam Alert: The procedure says the detective may request CSU — it does NOT say the detective requests Shots Fired. Watch for that distinction on the exam.

  • Confer with Crime Scene Unit upon arrival
  • Notify Desk Officer of details and request additional assistance if required
  • Secure Crime Scene — have Crime Scene signs posted

When to Request Crime Scene (CSU) — The HARBOR Mnemonic

Memory Aid — HARBOR: When Crime Scene Must Respond - Homicide - Aggravated assault with dangerous instrument AND victim likely to die - Rape (forcible) - Burglary involving forced safe OR circumvented alarm - Other crime in which CSU services will assist the investigation - Robbery OR hijacking with injury caused by firearm

Exam Alert: For burglary, CSU responds when the safe is forced OR the alarm is circumvented. Compare this carefully with the Refer to Squad criteria in PG 207-07 where the safe merely needs to be involved and the gun merely involved.

Refer to Squad (207-07) vs. Request Crime Scene (212-04)

Crime Refer to Squad (207-07) Request Crime Scene (212-04)
Assault SPI Likely & D.I.
Robbery Firearm or D.I. Used; Victim 60+ Firearm Caused Injury
Burglary Safe Involved; Gun Involved; >$5,000 stolen (Manhattan >$10,000); Person Present Safe Forced OR Circumvented Alarm

Prior Test: Past exams have mixed up the Squad referral rules from 207-07 with the CSU request rules from 212-04. The key difference: Squad referrals use broader criteria (involved/used), while CSU requests use narrower criteria (forced/caused injury).

DOA Cross-Reference

See Also: PG 216-04 — In a DOA case, the detective notifies CSU, not the patrol supervisor.

P.G. 212-11 — Investigative Encounters (Stop, Question & Frisk)

(Updated R.O. 53, effective 06/26/25 — 16 pages)

BWC Requirement

Exam Alert: Per PG 212-11, always activate BWC if assigned one, no matter what level the stop is. This applies to Level 1, 2, and 3 encounters.

Three Levels of Encounter

Element Level 1 — Request for Information Level 2 — Common Law Right of Inquiry Level 3 — Terry Stop
Based on Objective credible reason Founded suspicion Reasonable suspicion of Felony or PL Misdemeanor
ID Self Verbally & display shield if practicable Verbally: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield, absent exigent circumstances Verbally: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield, absent exigent circumstances
Ask Only related questions May ask accusatory questions May ask accusatory questions
Detain/Force May NOT detain or use force May NOT detain or use force May detain or use force
Search May NOT even ask to search May ask to search May ask to search
Paperwork BWC categorization CLRI - Consent Search Report (PD541-161), RTKBC Stop Report (PD383-151), RTKBC, Digital Activity Log entry

Exam Alert: The critical distinction is that at Level 1, you may NOT even request consent to search. At Level 2, you MAY ask. At Level 3, you MAY ask and document the request on a Stop Report (not a CLRI).

Definitions (Updated)

Term Definition
Objective Credible Reason Based on more than a hunch or a whim; may relate to public safety/service OR law enforcement, but need NOT be based on any indication of criminality
Founded Suspicion Some present indication of criminality based on observable conduct or reliable hearsay information
Reasonable Suspicion Information known to the member would make an ordinarily prudent and cautious police officer believe a felony or PL misdemeanor has been, is being, or is about to be committed
Frisk A carefully limited running of the hands over the outside of a person’s clothing for a deadly weapon or instrument readily capable of causing serious physical injury
Search After Frisk When the officer places hands inside a pocket or other interior portions of clothing to remove an object that the member felt during a frisk and reasonably suspects is a weapon or dangerous instrument
Protective Measures Tactics short of a frisk (e.g., ordering hands out of pockets, stepping away from objects that could be used as weapons, grabbing hands if circumstances suggest weapon)

False or Inconsistent Information

  • At any level of encounter, false or inconsistent information may escalate the encounter

DeBour vs. Terry — Legal Framework

Case Court Levels
People v. DeBour NYS Court of Appeals Articulates 4 levels: (1) Request for info — no suspicion; (2) CLRI — founded suspicion; (3) Forcible stop — reasonable suspicion; (4) Arrest — probable cause
Terry v. Ohio Federal (Supreme Court) Provides authority for Level 3: forcibly stop and possibly frisk, based on reasonable suspicion

Exam Alert: DeBour is the state case (Court of Appeals) and provides the 4-level framework. Terry is the federal case (Supreme Court) and provides Level 3 stop-and-frisk authority. Know which court decided each.

Level 1 Steps — Request for Information

  1. Activate BWC, if assigned, for encounters where activation is required per P.G. 212-123.
  2. Approach the person if there is an objective credible reason to do so.
  3. ID self: Verbally AND display shield conspicuously, if practicable.
  4. Do NOT detain the person, use or threaten the use of force.
  5. Do NOT request consent to search.
  6. Ask general, non-threatening questions related to the reason for the approach.
    • Pointed and accusatory questions are NOT permitted.
    • Person may refuse to answer and is free to leave.
  7. May inform person free to leave, but NOT required to UNLESS person specifically asks.
  8. Should provide an explanation for the encounter, UNLESS it would impair a criminal investigation.
  9. Protective measures: In rare cases, may engage protective measures for safety (because of the nature of the approach or the individual’s behavior).

Level 1 — RTKBC and ID Requirements

  • Offer RIGHT TO KNOW BUSINESS CARD (PD142-012) or RIGHT TO KNOW BUSINESS CARD - GENERAL (PD142-013), as appropriate, per A.G. 304-11.

Exam Alert (Updated): At Level 1, there are specific situations where you are NOT required to proactively identify yourself, explain the reason, or offer an RTKBC: - Engaged in undercover activity or operations - Exigent circumstances require your immediate action - Reasonably expect danger of physical injury, imminent property damage, imminent escape, imminent destruction of evidence - Conducting a security search of person entering a public building/facility/event/gathering (including MTA random checks, UNLESS requested by subject) - Verifying the identity of a person seeking entrance to a restricted area due to public health, safety, or security concern

Level 2 Steps — Common Law Right of Inquiry

  1. Activate BWC, if assigned, per P.G. 212-123.
  2. Approach the person if you have a founded suspicion of criminality.
  3. ID self: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield conspicuously, absent exigent circumstances.
  4. Do NOT detain or use force.
  5. May ask pointed and accusatory questions.
  6. Person may refuse and walk away. If person runs, UMOS may pursue.
  7. Request consent to search (voluntary; elicit clear yes or no):
    1. Ask in a manner that elicits a clear yes or no response. Example: I can only search you, if you consent. Do you understand. May I search you?
    2. If a person does not consent, you cannot conduct a search.
    3. If seeking consent, you must video record the request and the person’s response if you have a BWC.
    4. Document request, consent, refusal, and search (if performed) on the COMMON LAW RIGHT OF INQUIRY - CONSENT SEARCH REPORT (PD541-161).
    5. Offer RTKBC in all cases; provide info on how to obtain a copy of the video record.
  8. May inform person free to leave (not required unless person specifically asks).
  9. May engage protective measures when you have a reasonable concern for your safety.
  10. Provide individual with an explanation for the encounter, unless it would impair a criminal investigation.
  11. Do NOT offer RTKBC if encounter ends in arrest or summons, unless requested.

Exam Alert (Updated): At Level 2, the CLRI - Consent Search Report is only prepared for Level 2 requests to search. If you seek consent during a Level 3 Terry Stop, document that request on your Stop Report, not a CLRI. The CLRI is NOT prepared when conducting security searches, during exigent circumstances, or when danger of physical injury/imminent escape/evidence destruction exists.

Level 2 Note — Creating a Situation

During a Level 1 or Level 2 encounter, an officer may not create a situation (either by words or actions) where a reasonable person would not feel free to walk away. A person may be detained only if a properly conducted Level 1 or Level 2 encounter yields information to support a reasonable suspicion that the person committed, was committing, or was about to commit a felony or Penal Law misdemeanor.

Level 3 Steps — Terry Stop

  1. Activate BWC, if assigned, per P.G. 212-123.
  2. Upon reasonable suspicion that the person has committed, is committing, or is about to commit a felony or PL misdemeanor, stop and detain the person.
  3. Notify the radio dispatcher: location, number of persons being stopped, and whether additional units are needed.
  4. ID self: Rank, Name, Shield, Command; display shield conspicuously, absent exigent circumstances.
  5. Question the suspect to determine whether there is probable cause to make an arrest.
  6. May ask pointed and accusatory questions. Refusal to answer or produce identification does NOT establish probable cause.
    1. Offer RTKBC as appropriate.
  7. May request consent to search (same rules as Level 2 — elicit clear yes or no, video record if BWC assigned, document on STOP REPORT (PD383-151)).
  8. Reasonable force may be used to stop a person.
  9. May engage protective measures for safety.
  10. May frisk if at any point during the encounter you have RS that the person is armed and dangerous.
  11. Detention: only as long as necessary to confirm or dispel suspicion. Authority to detain ends when tasks tied to the stop are completed or reasonably should have been completed.
  12. Provide individual with an explanation for the encounter, unless it would impair a criminal investigation.
  13. Obtain suspect’s name, address, and additional information for your digital Activity Log entry regarding the stop.
  14. Do NOT transport or move suspect from location UNLESS:
    • Voluntary consent, or
    • Exigency (hostile crowd, threat to safety, hospital show-up, etc.)
  15. Release the person immediately after completing the investigation if probable cause to arrest does not exist.
  16. Do NOT offer RTKBC if encounter ends in arrest or summons, unless requested.

Level 3 — When RTKBC/ID Not Required

  1. Same exceptions as Level 1 (undercover, exigent circumstances, security search, restricted area verification, danger of physical injury/imminent escape/evidence destruction).

Frisk Rules (Updated)

  1. If a police officer develops reasonable suspicion that a person is armed and dangerous, the officer may frisk for:
    • A deadly weapon or any instrument, article, or substance readily capable of causing serious physical injury
    • Of a sort not ordinarily carried in public places by law-abiding persons
    RS that a person is armed and dangerous may arise from:
    1. RS that the suspect has committed, is committing, or is about to commit a violent crime (assault with deadly weapon, burglary, rape, robbery, etc.)
    2. Observation of something on the person the officer reasonably suspects is a weapon
    3. A statement by the suspect that she/he is armed
    4. Information known that the suspect may be carrying a weapon (victim/witness statements)
  2. Purpose of the frisk: ensure officer safety — NOT to locate evidence of a crime (such as drugs).
  3. No requirement to question suspect before conducting a lawful frisk.
  4. Conduct the frisk by carefully running your hands down the outside of the person’s clothing.
  5. If the frisk reveals an object that the member reasonably suspects may be a weapon: search only those interior portions of the stopped person’s clothing to remove the weapon.
  6. Bag/personal property — need RS person is armed and dangerous AND bag could contain weapon AND within person’s reach:
    • Soft bag: run hands down outside; open ONLY if you feel the contours of what she/he believes is a weapon
    • Rigid and unlocked: open to ensure no weapon
    • Rigid and locked: need search warrant or consent to search

Exam Alert: Mass transit checkpoint inspections of backpacks/containers for explosive devices follow different rules and are NOT governed by the frisk rules above.

Requesting Identification Documents

At any level, an officer may ask an individual to verbally identify herself/himself or present an identification document. During Level 1 or 2 encounters, the officer must not create a situation where the person does not feel free to leave. Other than the operator of a motor vehicle/motorcycle, members of the public are NOT required to possess or present identification documents. Refusal or inability to produce identification alone will NOT elevate the level of the encounter. Absent probable cause, a person may not be arrested or removed to a Department facility merely because she/he refused to produce identification.

Vehicle Report (New)

  1. Complete VEHICLE REPORT in FORMS for every vehicle, bicycle, motorized scooter, e-scooter, or e-bike stop, regardless of whether enforcement action is taken or not.

Exam Alert (Updated): A VEHICLE REPORT must be completed in FORMS for every vehicle/bicycle/scooter/e-bike stop. This is a new requirement in the R.O. 53 revision.

Stop Report Documentation (Updated)

  1. For all Terry Stops/Level 3 encounters, prepare a STOP REPORT using FORMS for EACH person stopped.
    1. Complete all applicable captions and follow the directions for each section prior to end of tour.
    2. Check REFUSED if the person stopped refused to identify herself/himself.
        1. Request the patrol supervisor to respond to verify refusal.
        1. Do not detain the person, however, if the investigation is complete and there is no probable cause.
    3. Select all relevant factors that led to the stop if more than one descriptive term applies.
    4. Describe in plain language (rather than numeric Penal Law section) the specific felony or PL misdemeanor you suspected.
    5. Describe in your own words, under the Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Stop) caption, all of the facts and information relied upon to conclude RS existed.
    6. If frisked/searched, describe under the Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Frisk and/or Search) caption all facts relied upon, the specific area searched, and whether a weapon or other contraband was recovered.
  2. When prompted to identify Reviewing Supervisor in FORMS, enter the name or tax ID of a supervisor currently performing duty with the platoon/unit and select that supervisor from the dropdown.
    1. Select immediate squad/unit supervisor if assigned and available.
    2. If not available, enter another supervisor performing duty with the squad/unit (patrol supervisor, detail supervisor, etc.).
  3. Notify the reviewing supervisor that the STOP REPORT was prepared and submitted to the supervisor’s electronic INBOX folder in FORMS for review.

Stop Report — Key Notes

The STOP REPORT is NOT prepared for Level 1 and Level 2 encounters, unless the encounter escalates to a Level 3 Terry Stop. The STOP REPORT is NOT prepared when an officer makes a summary arrest for an observed offense/crime or issues a Criminal Court summons or civil summons returnable to OATH for an observed violation, UNLESS the suspect was initially detained for investigation in a Level 3 Terry Stop. The STOP REPORT is NOT prepared for traffic stops based on violations of Vehicle and Traffic Law, unless the suspect is frisked.

Exam Alert: FORMS will time out after 55 minutes. UMOS should periodically click Save to ensure data is not lost.

  1. Record details in digital Activity Log entry:
    1. Date, time and location of stop
    2. Pedigree information (name, DOB, address, telephone number), unless refused, and detailed description
    3. Document refusal to provide pedigree information, if applicable
    4. ICAD number, if applicable
  2. Prior to end of tour, submit the STOP REPORT and digital Activity Log printout to the patrol supervisor/unit supervisor for review.
    1. Reviewing supervisor must be at least 1 rank higher than member submitting the STOP REPORT.

Exam Alert (Updated): Pedigree information is NOT captured electronically and will ONLY be recorded in the member’s digital Activity Log. Do NOT put pedigree information into the STOP REPORT narratives.

Patrol Supervisor/Unit Supervisor Review Duties (Updated)

  1. Respond to the scene of stops when feasible.
  2. Discuss the circumstances of the stop with the member of the service and review the STOP REPORT in FORMS by selecting Signoff.
    1. Determine whether all captions are completed and all relevant check boxes are checked.
    2. Confirm narrative states a specific suspected felony or PL misdemeanor in plain language (not Penal Law section numbers).
    3. Determine whether the Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Stop) includes the facts and circumstances relied upon to conclude RS existed.
    4. If person was frisked, determine whether the Narrative (Describe the Circumstances That Led to the Frisk and/or Search) includes facts relied upon to conclude RS that the person was armed and dangerous, and if searched, the basis for the search, the area searched, and whether a weapon or contraband was recovered.
    5. Complete the Supervisory Action (Must Complete) caption. Consider the facts and determine whether:
        1. The stop was based upon RS of a felony or PL misdemeanor
        1. If frisked, whether the frisk was supported by RS the person was armed and dangerous
        1. If searched, whether there was a sufficient basis for the search
    6. If appropriate, instruct/refer for additional training and/or other remedial action, including disciplinary action.
  3. Complete all captions in the Reviewing Supervisor section.
  4. If the STOP REPORT requires additional information:
    1. Check No following Report Accurate and Complete?
    2. Enter instruction to the reporting member in the Note section in FORMS.
    3. Select the Check icon to return the STOP REPORT for correction.
  5. If accurate and complete but stop was NOT proper:
    1. Select the action taken from the Follow-Up Action (If appropriate) section.
    2. Approve the STOP REPORT.
  6. Approve and Finalize the STOP REPORT following the prompts in FORMS.
  7. Review the member’s digital Activity Log entry (detailed description and pedigree info).
  8. If force was used, determine whether the use of force was reasonable under the circumstances.
  9. Prior to end of tour, electronically sign-off on STOP REPORT and return digital Activity Log back to the member.

Exam Alert: In all scenarios — whether the stop was proper, improper, or the report was prepared in error — the Stop Report is approved — never voided. Even when the stop was improper, the supervisor approves and documents the issue. If prepared in error (was Level 1 or 2, or arrest/summons not the result of a Level 3), note in Comments section.

UMOS — Upon Rejection of Stop Report

  1. Access the report from FORMS Inbox, go to Actions tab, select Edit and make the directed corrections.
    1. Select the Check icon to resubmit to supervisor for review and approval.
  2. Prior to end of tour, print the digital Activity Log entry detailing the Terry Stop and submit to the desk officer/designee.

Desk Officer/Designee Duties

  1. Log into FORMS and select SEARCH. A search must be conducted of all STOP REPORTS prepared during the tour.
  2. If a STOP REPORT was prepared, print out the approved STOP REPORT from the queue and attach it to the printed digital Activity Log entry.
    1. Place STOP REPORT and photocopy of printed digital Activity Log entry into the command’s STOP REPORT binder maintained at the desk in sequential order.

Exam Alert: The Stop Report binder is maintained at the desk in sequential order — not chronological. This means filed by report number order, not by date/time.

ICO Duties (Updated)

  1. Personally conduct, in conformance with the Quality Assurance Division’s self-inspection program, the command self-inspection of STOP REPORTS.
  2. Ensure the patrol supervisor/unit supervisor reviews STOP REPORTS and digital Activity Log and that appropriate actions are taken.
    1. In assessing the patrol/unit supervisor’s review: determine whether the supervisor appropriately reviewed the stop and, if conducted, the frisk and search, and any force used.
    2. Take appropriate remedial action if warranted, including discipline.
    3. Inform commanding officer and training sergeant of any matters of importance, including deficiencies or patterns in stop/frisk conduct or STOP REPORT preparation.

See Also: PG 202-30 — The XO has the exact same duty as the ICO regarding stop report review.

Executive Officer

  1. Personally conduct the Quality Assurance Division’s command self-inspection of POLICE INITIATED ENFORCEMENT.

Commanding Officer Duties (Updated)

  1. Assume responsibility for the integrity of the administration of this procedure.
  2. Consult with the XO, ICO, platoon commanders, special operations lieutenant, training sergeant, patrol supervisors/unit supervisors to ensure the constitutionality and effectiveness of stops.
    1. Identify training needs and necessary remedial or disciplinary actions required.
    2. Prepare a report on Typed Letterhead addressed to CO, Legal Bureau requesting remedial training for any members identified as having a deficient understanding of the law pertaining to street encounters.

Training Sergeant Duties (Updated)

  1. Conduct command level training to help ensure compliance with the Department’s policy regarding investigative encounters.
    1. Periodically review and identify command-wide and individual training needs and necessary remedial actions.
    2. Record training sessions in the Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integrated Collection System (TACTICS) to assist with future review and analysis of command’s compliance and training.
    3. Identify members who have been referred for training in STOP REPORTS and ensure that the training is conducted.
        1. Track, record and report such training to the commanding officer on a quarterly basis.

Furtive Movements and High Crime Area

  • Furtive movements or mere presence in high crime area alone are insufficient for a stop or frisk
  • Even when used in combination with other stop factors, the stopping officer must be able to specifically describe the suspicious nature of the furtive movements observed
  • Must not define high crime area too broadly (e.g., an entire precinct or borough)
  • A person may not be stopped merely because he or she matches a generalized description of a crime suspect (such as an 18-25 year old male of a particular race)
  • If a physical description is the only factor relied on, it must be more specific than race, age, and gender
  • Individuals may not be targeted for stops and frisks because they are members of a racial or ethnic group that appears more frequently in local crime suspect data

Exam Alert: Race may only be considered when the stop is based on a specific and reliable suspect description that includes not just race, age, and gender, but other identifying characteristics and information. When a police officer carries out a stop based on reasonable suspicion that a person fits such a description, the officer may consider the race of the suspect, just as the officer may consider height or hair color.

Additional Data — Binder Maintenance

  • Commands other than patrol precincts, PSAs, and transit districts will designate a supervisor to perform desk officer duties regarding STOP REPORTS.
  • Photocopies of STOP REPORTS will be sent via Department mail to the precinct of occurrence daily. The precinct places them in their STOP REPORT command binder in sequential order.
  • Desk officers/designees in commands other than patrol precincts, PSAs, or transit districts will maintain a standardized STOP REPORT command binder. A corresponding Stop Report Index will be printed out daily and maintained in the binder.
  • Commanding officers will ensure photocopied STOP REPORTS maintained in the command binder are removed and filed in the command by year of occurrence every January 1st and quarterly thereafter (April 1st, July 1st, and October 1st).

Exam Alert (Updated): STOP REPORT binders are purged quarterly (Jan 1, Apr 1, Jul 1, Oct 1) and filed by year of occurrence. The Stop Report Index is printed daily.

Forms and Reports Referenced

Form Number
Right to Know Business Card PD142-012
Right to Know Business Card - General PD142-013
Common Law Right of Inquiry - Consent Search Report PD541-161
Stop Report PD383-151
Investigative Encounters PD383-090
Supplemental Instructions for Preparation of Stop Report PD383-151A

Section 212 — Elections

P.G. 212-86 — Prior to Opening of Polls

Arrival Time

  • Arrive at the poll site at least 1 hour prior to opening.

Duties Before Polls Open

  1. Deliver election supplies and obtain receipt from Board of Elections members.
  2. Inspect ALL scanner machine public counters.
  3. Confirm Board of Elections posted no-electioneering signs within 100 feet of entrance.
  4. Check for suspicious packages and Election Law violations.

Scanner Machine Inspection

Counter Type Expected Reading Action if Not Expected
Public counter Must be ZERO (0) Notify desk officer/supervisor immediately. Scanner NOT used if counter is not zero.
Protective counter Will likely NOT be zero This is normal — do NOT confuse with public counter

Exam Alert: The public counter must read zero. The protective counter will likely NOT be zero — do not mistake one for the other. If the public counter is not zero, the scanner shall NOT be used and the supervisor must be notified immediately.

P.G. 212-87 — While Polls Are Open

Election District Staffing

Requirement Details
Full staffing 4 members — 2 from each of the top 2 political parties
Minimum staffing 2 members from different parties

Officer Duties

  • Remain in poll site unless required for other duty or meal.
  • Remain outside the guardrail except to perform a specific duty.
  • Do NOT take part in the election process.

Persons Permitted Within the Guardrail

  • Election inspectors
  • Voters
  • Authorized Board of Elections representatives
  • Attorney General
  • Authorized poll watchers
  • Persons admitted for the purpose of maintaining order

Poll Watcher Rules

Rule Detail
Maximum per candidate per ED 3 poll watchers
Inside guardrail per candidate per ED Only 1
Entry time May enter before opening (0500)
Departure May remain after closing
Prohibited actions Electioneering, tampering, interfering, accompanying voter inside booth

Close of Polls

Exam Alert: Polls close at 2100 hours. Persons already on line at 2100 may vote. NO new persons may join the line after 2100.

Person Denied Right to Vote

  • Refer to the Supreme Court of the county concerned.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure officers understand the guardrail boundaries, do not participate in the election process, and properly handle disputes about voting rights by referring to the county Supreme Court.

P.G. 212-88 — After Close of Polls

Post-Closing Duties

  • Secure election materials according to Board of Elections procedures.
  • Inspect scanner machines after closing.
  • Ensure orderly conclusion of voting process.
  • Maintain security of poll site until materials are properly transferred.

Sergeant Focus: Ensure all election materials are accounted for and properly secured. Verify that the transition from active voting to post-closing procedures occurs smoothly and without interference.

Section 212 — Firearms and Licensing

P.G. 212-44 — Response to Firearms Dealers Locations

  • Follow established response procedures for incidents occurring at licensed firearms dealer locations.
  • Ensure proper coordination with License Division and investigative units.

P.G. 212-46 — Unlawful Possession of Rifles/Shotguns

  • Follow procedures for handling illegal rifle and shotgun possession.
  • Ensure proper vouching and notification of investigative units.

P.G. 212-84 — Verification of Pistol License

Verification Steps

  1. Request documentary ID in addition to the pistol license.
  2. Check for valid license features.

Valid License Features

Feature Detail
Seal Embossed Police Department seal
Validity period 2 years
Expiration On licensee’s birth date

Verification Resources

Resource Availability
License Division (telephone) 0900-1700, Monday-Friday
ALPS (Automated License and Permit System) Electronic verification

If License Division Closed / ALPS Unavailable

  • Desk officer contacts License Division at 0900 next business day for verification.

Questionable Validity

  1. Retain the license and firearms.
