  2. Have a Complaint Report prepared.
  3. Forward Typed Letterhead to CO, License Division.

Exam Alert: Pistol licenses are valid for 2 years and expire on the licensee’s birth date. Two features to remember.

P.G. 212-85 — Rifle/Shotgun Permit Suspended/Revoked/Disapproved

Assignment

  • CO assigns MOS (preferably a sergeant) for immediate investigation.

Sergeant Focus: Sergeants are the preferred rank for conducting rifle/shotgun permit surrender investigations.

Procedure at Licensee Address

  1. Respond to the licensee’s address.
  2. Request surrender of firearms, permit, and registration.
  3. Prepare 3 copies of Surrender Notice:
Copy Destination
Original Rifle/Shotgun Section
Copy Precinct file
Copy Receipt for permittee

Investigation Deadline

Exam Alert: Investigations must be completed and returned within 60 days.

Refusal to Surrender

  • If permit holder refuses or fails to surrender: serve summons for Admin Code section 10-303 (Criminal Court).
  • Do NOT serve summons for unrenewed permits (administrative matter).

Priority Cases

  • Serious crime conviction
  • Mental incompetence
  • Consult Legal Bureau regarding search warrant for priority cases.

P.G. 212-118 — Incidents Involving Holders of Handgun Licenses or Rifle/Shotgun Permits

  • Follow established procedures for incidents involving licensed firearm holders.
  • Ensure proper documentation and notification of License Division.

P.G. 212-128 — Extreme Risk Protection Order (ERPO)

Definition

A court-issued Order of Protection prohibiting the purchasing or possessing of any firearm.

Triggering Factors

  • Threat, violence, or stalking
  • Order of Protection violation
  • Pending weapons charge
  • Reckless firearm display
  • History of ERPO violations
  • Substance or alcohol use
  • Recent firearm acquisition combined with one of the above factors (a-f)

Evaluation Process

Step Responsibility
Initial evaluation and confirmation Immediate supervisor
Record in Activity Log Immediate supervisor
Determine probable cause (self-harm/harm to others) Immediate supervisor
Final determination on filing Legal Bureau ERPO Unit

Legal Bureau ERPO Unit Availability

Time Contact
Monday-Friday, 0900-1700 ERPO Unit
1700-2300 and weekends Duty attorney

Service and Execution

  • Temporary ERPO served by NYC Sheriff’s Office — NOT NYPD.
  • Intelligence Division assists if a search provision is granted.
  • Department attorney appears with MOS at all hearings.

Exam Alert: Temporary ERPOs are served by the NYC Sheriff’s Office, not NYPD. This is a commonly tested distinction.

License Division Role

  • Conducts background check for final hearing:
    • Prior convictions and pending charges
    • Parole/probation status
    • Registered firearms
    • Any Orders of Protection

Firearm Retention

Exam Alert: Firearms seized under ERPO are retained for a minimum of 2 years. No release without written authorization from CO, License Division (plus CO, Medical Division if the subject is a MOS).

ERPO Renewal

  • ERPO Unit reviews 30 days before expiration to determine if renewal is warranted.

District Attorney ERPOs

  • DA Offices can file ERPOs for arrests/open complaints independently of NYPD.

Exigent Circumstances Exception

  • ERPO is NOT required when:
    • Imminent harm exists, AND
    • Firearm is on person or readily accessible

MOS as Subject of ERPO

  • Notify CO without delay.
  • Modified Assignment per A.G. 318-08.

Sergeant Focus: When a subordinate MOS becomes the subject of an ERPO, the supervisor must ensure immediate CO notification and initiation of the Modified Assignment process.

Section 212 — Hazardous Materials, Bombs, and Fire

P.G. 212-37 — Hazardous Materials

On-Scene Coordinator

Exam Alert: The On-Scene Coordinator for a hazmat incident is the highest ranking uniformed supervisor at the scene — this could be a Patrol Supervisor.

Inter-Agency Command Center (IACC)

  • Separate and distinct from the police temporary HQ (Command Post)
  • IACC Log maintained by assigned UMOS

Trigger for Response

  • Involving suspected hazmat OR where legality of hazmat shipment is questionable
  • If legality is questionable, Motor Carrier Safety Unit (MCSU) member will clarify

Minimal Outdoor Distances

Distance Condition
300 feet Any spill; any fire EXCEPT military shipment or tanker truck
1,000 feet Any explosion OR suspected device; fire involving military shipment or tanker truck

Exam Alert: Memorize the two outdoor distances: 300 feet for spills/fires, 1,000 feet for explosions/suspected devices/military/tanker fires. These distances may be expanded as needed.

Indoor Distances

Condition Distance
Spill Just that room
Fire or Explosion All rooms/areas where person may be exposed, including floors above and below

Radiation

  • Evacuate to the distance where the radiation pager alarm shuts off (per PG 212-102)

UMOS Duties

  • Notify Desk Officer AND Communications Section
  • Establish minimum frozen areas
  • Request response of Patrol Supervisor, ESU Supervisor/Personnel, AND Highway District MCSU

Patrol Supervisor Duties

  • Evaluate and confer with ESU Supervisor/member OR MCSU member
  • If radiation: notify NYC Dept. of Health
  • Notifications and request for Bomb Squad: request Communications to notify Operations Unit and have Bomb Squad respond

PG Conflict: In HazMat (212-37), the Sergeant calls the Bomb Squad through Communications Unit and Operations. In PG 218-37 (Exploding Currency), the UMOS requests the Bomb Squad if bank personnel confirm they passed a dye pack. Know who makes the request in each procedure.

  • Maintain frozen area security until hazmat is secured/neutralized
  • Establish Command Post
  • Advise Operations by phone with all info (location, time, source, danger, materials, markings, other agencies, action taken)
  • Highway Units: if vehicle involved, detain for safety check; notify Highway Supervisor for escort duties

Frozen Area Rules

Exam Alert: Exclude vehicle and pedestrian traffic from the frozen area. Restrict entry to those properly trained and equipped for hazmat — EXCEPT when absolutely necessary to save life or prevent serious injury.

When Incident Involves SECTAR

  • Significant Fire, Explosion, Chemical Burn OR Toxic Air Release / Radiation Hazard to personnel/public
  • This elevates from suspected/questionable to an actual hazmat incident

On-Scene Coordinator Expanded Duties

  • Assume control; establish IACC (as close as possible to incident, separate from Command Post)
  • Ensure communications link between Operations and IACC
  • Assign UMOS to maintain IACC Log
  • Notify Operations immediately with all material details (spell out chemical names carefully)
  • Request Mobile Decontamination Vehicle (MDV) through Operations
    • If on-scene coordinator unavailable: FDNY Deputy Chief+, EMS Director+, or DEP Director/Asst Commissioner+ may also request (through Ops)
  • Establish police lines; evacuate to sufficient distance
  • Parking: designate on high ground and/or upwind to avoid contamination
  • Traffic Plan C (PG 213-13): consider implementing
  • Detain exposed persons (except those needing medical treatment); obtain names/addresses for Command Post Log
  • Coordinate with FD, DEP, Sanitation to prevent hazmat from entering sewer system
  • After Action Critique: critique with agency reps and advise OEM of results

Entries in Logs

Entry Command Post Log IACC Log
Medical treatment (names/addresses of exposed not needing emergency care) Yes Yes (same)
Significant info, personnel arrival/departure Yes Yes (same)
Radiation (ESU reports name/shield of exposed MOS) Yes Not specifically required
Safety Officer ID Yes Not specifically required

Substances Other Than Liquids Rendered Safe

  • ESU Supervisor advises Patrol Supervisor to have Dept. of Sanitation notified to clean up and dispose of substances other than liquids that have been rendered safe

C.O. Precinct of Occurrence

  • Document on Report on Typed Letterhead (49)
  • Have transcript of all Command Post Log entries put on 49
  • Forward to C.O. Police Lab

Exam Alert: The CO does NOT prepare the 49 — the CO has it prepared. The unusual recipient is the C.O. Police Lab, not Operations or Borough.

P.G. 212-40 — Bombs

Key Notification Rules

Exam Alert: For bomb incidents (212-40): No Desk Officer notification, No Borough notification, No Bureau notification. Only Communications Unit and Operations.

Key Numbers

Exam Alert: Stop search 20 minutes before threatened explosion time. Do NOT resume search for at least 1 hour after threatened time. Evacuate to at least 1,000 feet.

MOS Receiving Threat

  • Obtain all relevant info and details
  • Immediately notify Communications Unit (C.U.) and give details
  • Provide caller’s location and telephone number if available

Responding UMOS

  • Respond to location; ID and interview caller
  • Ascertain caller’s location/phone; obtain nature of threat (time, location, device type)
  • Request response of Patrol Supervisor and ESU

Patrol Supervisor/Ranking Officer on Scene

  • Review actions taken
  • Determine if additional personnel or equipment needed
  • Confer with premise owner/representative (not the caller)
  • Search: ensure preliminary search/evacuation based on type of premise, location, nature of business, prior history

Threatening Letter/Package by Mail/Delivery

  • UMOS: request Patrol Supervisor response
  • Safeguard letter/package in suitable envelope/bag to minimize handling
    • Mark For Latent Fingerprint Processing
    • Do NOT copy contents of letter/package
  • Make list of ALL who handled it (include Tax & Shield #s)
  • Request Laboratory Examination; immediately forward with Request for Laboratory Examination form

See Also: PG 218-09 — Questioned documents may be secured in PSE using cardboard inserts. Do not write on envelope once document is inside.

Unattended Article

  • Do NOT move or disturb — ONLY examined/disturbed by UMOS in the Bomb Squad
  • Assess threat to public safety based on: prior history, sensitive location, physical indications (exposed wires, oil stains, odor), recent political events, high-traffic area
  • If owner identified: have owner retrieve property
  • If no owner and/or possible danger to public safety: treat as Suspected Explosive Device

Suspected/Reported Explosive Device

  • UMOS: evacuate if necessary
  • Notify C.U. via landline (if available)
    • If landline unavailable: use radio or cellphone from position of hard cover, as far as possible from item, after everyone cleared from immediate area

Exam Alert: Do NOT use cellphone or radio near a suspected explosive device. The proper method is landline first. Only use radio/cellphone from hard cover at maximum distance.

  • Request: Operations notified, Patrol Supervisor, ESU, and Duty Captain

Search Teams

  • Use stairs, NOT elevators (exception: elevators inaccessible to public)
  • Do NOT use radio or cellphone while anyone is in direct proximity to discovered item
  • Do NOT touch or disturb a suspected explosive device under any circumstances
  • Discontinue search 20 minutes prior to threatened explosion time, regardless of evacuation status
  • Do NOT resume search for at least 1 hour after threatened time

Evacuation Distance

  • At least 1,000 feet from unattended article or suspected device
  • May increase or decrease based on circumstances, location, ESU/Bomb Squad recommendation

ESU Safeguards Device

  • ESU safeguards device pending arrival of Bomb Squad
  • Absent an ESU Supervisor, Senior ESU member on scene determines necessary preliminary steps

Post-Explosion Bomb Scene

  • Ensure Bomb Squad notified and responding
  • Search for deceased, injured, trapped persons immediately
  • Instruct searchers to be cautious of secondary devices
  • Limit search teams to minimum number of UMOS
  • Once search/evacuation done: no one allowed in (including FD) unless a fire exists
  • Do NOT correct dangerous conditions UNTIL Bomb Squad declares area free of secondary devices AND crime scene search is completed
  • Direct ESU to remove injured to safe location for medical treatment (not EMS)

Exam Alert: At a post-explosion scene, direct ESU to remove injured persons — NOT EMS. Watch for exam answers that say direct EMS to remove — the correct answer is ESU.

  • Safeguard but do NOT move bodies of deceased persons
  • Have EMS administer medical treatment at safe locations
  • All UMOS evacuate to safe distance (1,000+ feet) and await Bomb Squad after initial search
  • Document: Complaint Report for bomb threat, suspected device, or post-explosion incident

P.G. 212-58 — Fire

Radio Dispatcher Duties

  • Immediately assign available unit to respond
  • Notify Patrol Supervisor
  • Broadcast new information received via ICAD or from scene
  • Notify Patrol Supervisor and any other responding supervisors of all available information

UMOS Duties

  • Park vehicles to prevent interference with firefighting
  • Ensure area in front of building secured for FDNY; fire hydrants free of vehicles
  • Notify dispatcher: give 10-84; ensure FDNY responding; provide pertinent details
  • Direct responsible person to remain in front to direct fire apparatus if fire not in view
  • Warn/assist evacuated persons; prevent persons from reentering building; secure lobby

Patrol Supervisor Duties

  • Respond to ALL building/structure fires in command

See Also: PG 202-19 — Patrol Supervisor responds to: Weapons, Emergency, Burglary, Serious Crime.

  • Confer with FDNY supervisor (not just any firefighter)
  • Tactical Retreat: if smoke and heat suddenly present, advise MOS to evacuate/tactically retreat to predetermined exit
  • If fire is suspicious: notify Desk Officer AND request precinct detective squad
  • Ensure access for emergency vehicles; confirm fire hydrants accessible; maintain clear path for ambulances

Persons Permitted Through Fire Lines

  • The Mayor and vehicle
  • Ambulance, Police, and FDNY vehicles
  • Members of govt. agencies and vehicles, in performance of duty
  • Employees of public service corporations and vehicles, in performance of emergency duty
  • Red Cross personnel in performance of duty (NO vehicles)
  • Persons holding valid unexpired press credentials (NO vehicle)

Exam Alert: Red Cross personnel and press credential holders are allowed through fire lines on foot only — NO vehicles. Public service corporation employees must be performing emergency duty specifically.

UMOS Documentation

  • Digital Activity Log: location, time, persons displaced/injured, cause, extent of damage, suspicion of arson, dangerous conditions
  • If fire is suspicious: Complaint Report
  • Aided Report for each injured person
  • Secure premises once firefighting concluded

Desk Officer — FRUITS Notification

Memory Aid — FRUITS: When Desk Officer notifies Operations AND Borough: - Forced entry by F.D. - Relocation of tenants - Unusual fire - Injury/death - Three alarms or more - Suspicious fire

Two Alarm Fires

  • When fire reaches 2nd alarm: FD notifies NYPD Operations Unit, who directs duty captain to scene

Exam Alert: At 2 alarms, FD notifies Operations. At 3 alarms, the Desk Officer notifies Operations. Know who makes the notification at each alarm level.

First NYPD Supervisor at Scene

  • Proceed immediately to FDNY Incident Command Post
  • Remain until relieved or Command Post demobilized
  • Advise FDNY Incident Commander of MOS inside affected building (number, unit, last known location)

CIMS Primary Agency Matrix — Fire Suppression

  • Incident Commander (IC) is the ranking FDNY officer
  • NYPD co-locates its command post with FDNY’s
  • Highest-ranking NYPD uniformed supervisor: remain with IC, work and coordinate with Primary Agency
  • PD responsibilities: site management; accountable for inner perimeter, crowd/traffic control

MOS Entering Building on Fire — Last Resort

  • NYPD not trained to fight fires; should AVOID entering
  • Notify dispatcher of all observations PRIOR to entry
  • Inform FDNY on scene how many MOS entered, including last known location
  • Use stairs IN TEAMS OF TWO, NOT elevator
  • Inspect floor below fire floor; note other stairways, exits, areas of refuge
  • Stairwell door: place back of hand to detect heat
    • Unusually warm door = immediately dangerous to life and health (requires PPE)
    • Do NOT open — drawing smoke
  • Absent exigents, MOS should NOT operate on any floor ABOVE the fire
  • Do NOT hold elevator on any floor — FDNY will recall elevators

Exam Alert: When approaching a fire floor, MOS should inspect each floor below the fire floor. They should NOT operate above the fire. Use prone position/crawl, stay close to wall, use wall as guide in smoke conditions.

Section 212 — Housing Operations

P.G. 212-26 — Inspection of NYCHA Facilities

Covered Facilities

  • Community centers
  • Child care centers
  • Maintenance shops
  • Health centers
  • Senior citizen centers
  • Laundry rooms
  • Boiler rooms

Inspection Schedule

  • Check each facility on post during the first AND last hour of each tour, if possible.
  • Frequently inspect security during the tour.
  • Give special attention to Post Condition facilities.

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisors shall inspect Activity Logs to ensure NYCHA facilities are being inspected by members on post. Supervisors should recommend Departmental Recognition for burglary arrests at NYCHA facilities.

Exam Alert: Inspections are required during both the first and last hour of each tour — not just one or the other.

P.G. 212-28 — Disabled Housing Authority Elevator

Key Points

  • Procedure governs handling of passengers trapped in NYCHA elevators.
  • Respond promptly to reports of disabled elevators with trapped occupants.
  • Notify appropriate NYCHA maintenance personnel.
  • Ensure safety of trapped individuals until professional assistance arrives.

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisor should respond to ensure proper coordination between NYPD and NYCHA maintenance staff, particularly when occupants include elderly or medically vulnerable individuals.

P.G. 212-60 — Interior Patrol of Housing Authority Buildings

Purpose

Covers systematic interior patrols of NYCHA buildings to maintain safety and deter criminal activity.

Key Procedures

  • Conduct thorough interior patrols of NYCHA buildings on post.
  • Check stairwells, hallways, rooftops, and common areas.
  • Document patrol activity in Activity Log.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors shall review Activity Logs to verify interior patrols are being conducted and documented. Ensure members are varying patrol times and routes to maximize deterrent effect.

Section 212 — Interaction Procedures

P.G. 212-104 — Interaction with Persons Who Are Deaf or Hard of Hearing

Legal Foundation

  • Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)
  • State and local law
  • Department policy

All require effective communication with persons who are deaf or hard of hearing.

Primary Consideration

  • The type of auxiliary aid requested by the individual is the primary consideration.

Exam Alert: The individual’s own preference for the type of auxiliary aid is the primary consideration. The Department does not unilaterally decide the accommodation.

Cost

  • Must NOT charge the person for auxiliary aids or services.

Qualified Interpreter Requirements

Requirement Detail
Must interpret Effectively, accurately, and impartially
Family member/child/friend May NOT be qualified — especially in domestic disputes

Exam Alert: A family member, child, or friend may not serve as a qualified interpreter, particularly in domestic dispute situations where impartiality is compromised.

Communication Resources

Resource Details
TRS (Telecommunications Relay Service) Dial 711
TTY number (212) 504-4115 for 311 system
Sign language interpreter Contact Operations Unit

Arrest Procedures

  • Arrest proceeds normally with probable cause.
  • Auxiliary aids used for communication regarding charges, interrogation, etc.
  • Arrestee permitted to use TRS for phone calls.
  • Qualified interpreter provided for communication during processing.

Pre-Planned Events

  • CO prepares a Typed Letterhead request to the Chief of Personnel when a sign language interpreter is needed for a pre-planned event.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure that communication accommodations are provided promptly and that the individual’s preferred method of communication is the primary consideration. For pre-planned events, arrange interpreter services in advance through the Chief of Personnel.

Section 212 — Media and Information

P.G. 212-49 — Incidents Involving Media Representatives

(Updated R.O. 93, effective 10/03/25)

Purpose

To cooperate with members of the press by not interfering or allowing others to interfere with media personnel acting in their news gathering capacity.

Definitions

Term Definition
Member of the Press An individual who gathers, reports, or licenses news or content for publishing, broadcasting, or cablecasting (radio, TV, newspapers, magazines, wire, books, Internet). Includes employees of news-gathering organizations, independent contractors, freelancers, and self-employed persons.
MOME Press Credential City of New York Press Card issued by the Mayor’s Office of Media and Entertainment (MOME) Press Credentials Office allowing bearer to pass NYPD/city official lines and attend official city-government sponsored events open to the press.

MOME Press Card Holders

Right Details
Cross police/fire lines Access WILL NOT be denied
Interior crime scenes Access NOT included
Security-restricted areas Access NOT included
Proximity As close as possible with clear line of sight and hearing range (unaided by electronic device)

Exam Alert: Only MOME-issued Press Card holders are entitled to cross police and fire lines. However, this does not include access to interior crime scenes or areas designated off-limits for security reasons.

Press Access Rules (UMOS)

  1. Adhere to the following guidelines unless safety or police duties require otherwise:
    1. Press given access as close as possible with clear line of sight and hearing range (unaided by electronic device)
    2. Press access to demonstrations on private property shall not be impeded
    3. Press shall NOT be arrested for criminal trespass on private property unless owner expressly indicates the press is not to enter or remain
    4. If ranking officer determines press access must be restricted, they retain the discretion to do so
  2. Determine if any threat to safety of press exists; take appropriate action.
  3. Request response of patrol supervisor.
  4. Take enforcement action as necessary.

First Amendment Activity Rules

Exam Alert: During First Amendment Activity, MOME press are NOT required to leave the area even if NYPD orders persons to leave. They shall move to a safe location instead. At the discretion of the incident commander, press may be directed to a different location where they may still see and hear ongoing newsworthy events and will not be subject to arrest for documenting police activity or for not leaving the general area. See P.G. 213-21.

Patrol Supervisor Duties

  1. Cooperate with and assist members of the press; provide safe access to the scene.
  2. Conduct immediate investigation if a member of the press is assaulted, harassed, or their vehicle/equipment is vandalized/damaged.
  3. Attempt to obtain third party witnesses if confrontation with MOS or others.
  4. Request all parties, including witnesses, to report to precinct of occurrence for further investigation when necessary.
  5. Notify without delay:
    1. Office of the Deputy Commissioner, Public Information (DCPI)
    2. Desk officer
    3. Lieutenant platoon commander

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisor must investigate immediately when a press member is assaulted or harassed. Notification to DCPI must be made without delay. DCPI is available via phone 24/7.

Desk Officer

  1. Notify:
    1. CO/XO/duty captain
    2. Operations Unit

Lieutenant Platoon Commander

  1. Respond to command (if in the field) and under supervision of CO/XO/duty captain, conduct investigation.
  2. Prepare report on Typed Letterhead containing details of incident and results of investigation. Forward to:
    1. Chief of Patrol/bureau chief concerned
    2. Borough commander concerned
    3. CO, Public Information Division
    4. Command file

NOTE: The CO/XO/duty captain will perform the duties of the lieutenant platoon commander if the LPC is unavailable.

Commanding Officer/Duty Captain

  1. Forward supplementary report, if necessary.
  2. Respond to command and supervise the investigation and preparation of the report.

Recording and Photography Rules

  • Members WILL NOT interfere with videotaping or photographing in public places.
  • Blocking or obstructing cameras or harassing the photographer constitutes censorship.
  • Members may NOT set up police lines or frozen zones to prevent press from viewing or recording the scene from a public place.

Credential Seizure

Exam Alert: Members may NO LONGER seize press credentials under any circumstances, except when seized in the normal course of an arrest and held for safekeeping like other types of non-evidentiary, non-investigatory personal property.

Prohibited Retaliatory Actions

  • May NOT take police action in retaliation for:
    • Expressing a viewpoint
    • Engaging in First Amendment Activities
    • Exercising the right to witness, observe, record, comment on, or protest police activity

Red Light Offense Arrest of Press

  • If arrested for a Red Light offense (per P.G. 213-21) and presents a MOME-issued credential or official government-issued press credential: processing shall be approved by the incident commander or ranking member from DCPI.
  • Presumption: summons issued on scene, if eligible.
  • Press member may be removed to arrest processing facility only if incident commander determines issuing summons on scene would create a health/safety risk.
  • Applies to all Red Light arrests of press regardless of whether it occurs at a First Amendment Activity event or not.

Government-Issued Press Credentials from Other Jurisdictions

  • Shall be verified to the extent possible at the time of encounter.
  • If unable to verify on scene, officers shall consider the following indicia of validity:
    • Name matches another form of ID
    • Contains a photograph
    • Details the issuing city or agency
    • Includes the associated media outlet
    • Indicates whether the individual is independent
    • Includes an expiration date, serial, or ID number
  • Members should confer with DCPI if necessary.

Press Card Age Requirements

Type Age Requirement
Standard/Reserve Press Card At least 18 years old
Single Event Press Card Under 18 may apply
Under 16 with Single Event card Must be accompanied by an adult (1 adult per child in restricted area)

DCPI Notification

  • Notify DCPI immediately for ANY incident involving the press, regardless of outcome.
  • DCPI available 24/7.
  • DCPI will maintain a central repository of all reports received of incidents involving members of the press.

MOME Verification

  • MOME is available to confirm validity of press credentials.
  • Contact the Operations Unit for the MOME phone number and contact information.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure members understand that press access cannot be restricted through police lines or frozen zones designed to block recording. Any incident involving the press requires immediate DCPI notification. The patrol supervisor must conduct an immediate investigation if press members are assaulted or harassed.

P.G. 212-77 — Release of Information to News Media

General Rule

  • Media inquiries should be directed to DCPI
  • MOS provide assistance ONLY when:
    • It does not pose a threat to personal safety of MOS, the public, or the news media
    • It does not interfere with police operations, crime scene investigations, or other emergency service providers
    • It does not infringe on the rights of the accused or privacy/security of crime victims
  • Even if other agencies are involved, ALL media requests go through DCPI
  • Unauthorized communication with news media without conferral with DCPI is PROHIBITED

Exam Alert: MOS are required to immediately notify DCPI of any interaction with the media. This is an absolute requirement.

Highest Ranking UMOS Addressing Media

  • Prior to addressing media, confer with DCPI regarding info to be released
  • Briefly describe incident: how called to scene, type of job, location, # of aideds, etc.

Crime Scenes or Frozen Areas

Exam Alert: Media access to an incident does NOT extend to interior crime scenes or frozen areas. This applies to all media, including MOME press card holders (per PG 212-49).

Information NOT to Be Released

  • Juvenile under 18 taken into custody — EXCEPT Juvenile Offender or Adolescent Offender (can be released)
    • If case drops from Criminal Court to Family Court: offender becomes Juvenile Delinquent and ID can NOT be released from that point forward
  • Abused child — neglected or abused child or child complainant under 18
  • Sex crime victim ID or location of sex assault if victim’s residence or perp’s residence (if not apprehended)
  • Communicable disease info indicating a person has one
  • Arrest/booking photo unless it would serve a law enforcement purpose
  • Witness or confidential informant information
  • MOS, complainant, or witness — address or phone # of any of these three; info that may hinder prosecution or jeopardize safety

Civil Rights Law — Sex Crime Victim ID

  • Allows victims to bring private action if their ID is disclosed
  • MOS releasing records must ensure sex offense victim identity is NOT divulged
  • Proper release of sex crime victim ID is limited to:
    • Defendant and their counsel or guardian
    • Public officers/employees investigating, prosecuting, and keeping records relating to offense

Communicable Disease — Confidentiality

  • Prisoner or victim status regarding communicable disease (HIV, AIDS, etc.) is confidential
  • Generally do NOT release to public, media, family, friends, or other prisoners
  • Any release requires written consent of the DCLM (Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters)

Prisoner Volunteers Medical Status

  • If prisoner volunteers positive medical status or is treated medically: use isolation cells if available
  • Document on Prisoner Movement Slip or Medical Treatment of Prisoner (either form)

Releasable Information Post Arrest

  • Pedigree: name, age, residence, employment, marital status, similar background (after ID confirmed)
  • Charge: substance or text of charge
  • Complainant info: only when appropriate, ID of complainant
  • Agency involved: identity of investigating/arresting agency and length of investigation
  • Arrest information: circumstances surrounding arrest (time, place, resistance, pursuit, possession/use of weapons, description of items seized)

Release ONLY After Conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives — A RAT COP

Memory Aid — RATCOP: Information released only after conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives: - Reputation — statements concerning reputation/character of accused or witness - Anticipated testimony of prospective witness - Test — performance of tests, results of tests, OR refusal by accused to take a test - Credibility of prospective witness - Opinion/speculation concerning evidence or argument in the case - Photos, films, videotape and/or mug shot

Exam Alert: RATCOP items require conferral with both DCPI and Chief of Detectives before release. This is a commonly tested mnemonic.

P.G. 212-76 — Information Concerning Official Business of Department

General Principles

  • Treat official business as CONFIDENTIAL.
  • Understand the public’s right to be informed.
  • Exercise discretion when divulging authorized information.

Authorized Releases

  • Specific agencies may receive specific categories of information per Department guidelines.

Requests for Statistics/Surveys

  • Forward to Office of Management Analysis and Planning.

Medical Records

Requestor Requirements Fee
MOS (own records) Written request to CO, Medical Division No fee
Outside parties Written request with notarized authorization $5 fee

Grants and Equipment Offers

Exam Alert: Members may NOT accept offers of grants, equipment, or funding from elected officials, agencies, or private entities. All such offers must be directed to the Police Commissioner in writing.

Sensitive Information from Elected Officials

  • Submit the request in writing to the Police Commissioner.

Sergeant Focus: Ensure subordinates understand that all grant/equipment/funding offers must be forwarded to the Police Commissioner — members have no authority to accept directly.

Section 212 — Miscellaneous Procedures

P.G. 212-14 — Traffic Intelligence Report

(Updated R.O. 108, effective 10/24/25)

Purpose

To report traffic conditions that need to be addressed by the NYPD or other outside agencies (i.e., collision-prone locations, chronic traffic congestion-prone locations, double parking, requests for signs or signal lights, pavement markings, construction projects, etc.).

When Prepared

Whenever a uniformed member observes a traffic condition that is not immediately correctable and further assistance is required, OR when a collision-prone location is identified by the Traffic Accident Management System (TAMS).

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Prepare TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORT (PD178-150) when a traffic condition is observed and is not immediately correctable by available resources.
  2. Indicate on the report:
    1. Specific type of condition(s)
    2. Times
    3. Place(s) of occurrence
    4. Any corrective measures taken or recommended
  3. Utilize reverse side for detailed description, diagram(s), and additional comments.
  4. Submit completed report to the administrative QOL officer, command of occurrence, before end of tour.

NOTE: If the condition was observed on a limited access highway, submit the completed report to the highway unit concerned.

Administrative Quality of Life Officer

  1. Review Traffic Intelligence Report(s) daily and indicate corrective action taken and recommendations.
  2. Maintain a Precinct/Highway Unit Traffic Intelligence Report Log captioned:
    • Precinct Serial #, Date of Report, Location, Condition, Corrective Action Taken, Recommendation, Date

NOTE: Reports concerning the same condition at the same location during the same week (Mon-Sun) will be grouped together and given one precinct serial number.

When a Collision-Prone Location Has Been Identified by TAMS

  1. Conduct a survey of each collision-prone location to determine contributing factors.
  2. Prepare TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORT for each collision-prone location.
  3. Confer with executive officer regarding conditions correctable at the precinct level.
  4. Establish liaison with outside agencies (NYC DOT, Parks, DEP, Buildings, etc.) to address conditions that cannot be corrected by precinct personnel.

NOTE: If the report concerns an engineering recommendation, a copy is forwarded to the DOT borough commissioner’s office. A telephone notification is also made.

  1. Forward copies of TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORTS to the borough traffic safety coordinator WEEKLY and indicate:
    1. Corrective actions taken and/or recommendations for further measures
    2. File copies for precinct/highway unit records

NOTE: Conditions requiring immediate attention (potholes, missing traffic signs, inoperable signal lights, etc.) will NOT be forwarded to the borough traffic safety coordinator. Corrective action must be initiated at the scene, including notification to the precinct telephone switchboard operator for entry on the HIGHWAY CONDITION RECORD (PD311-151).

Operations Coordinator

  1. Ensure each Monday the WEEKLY STREET CONDITIONS SURVEY (PD311-150) is reviewed and appropriate agencies/members notified.

Borough Traffic Safety Coordinator

  1. Maintain a borough Traffic Intelligence Report Log with additional column for borough serial number.
  2. Confer with borough executive officer regarding conditions correctable at borough level.
  3. Forward copies to Traffic Management Center ONLY where precinct or borough personnel cannot correct the condition AND it is not reportable on the Weekly Street Conditions Survey or Highway Condition Record.

Traffic Management Center Traffic Safety Officer

  1. Review and analyze TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORTS and:
    1. Assist precinct or patrol borough in correcting condition(s)
    2. Confer with outside agencies, if applicable
    3. Report findings to the commanding officer

NOTE: A TRAFFIC INTELLIGENCE REPORT will be prepared for all incidents involving a construction site (refer to P.G. 214-16). Include all permit numbers, indicate if a MOSAICS computer check was made and results.

Exam Alert: The Administrative QOL Officer reviews Traffic Intelligence Reports daily. Reports are forwarded to the borough traffic safety coordinator weekly. Conditions requiring immediate attention (potholes, missing signs) are NOT forwarded – they require immediate action at the scene.

Sergeant Focus: Preparation of a Traffic Intelligence Report does not relieve precinct personnel from taking other corrective action to expedite traffic flow and ensure pedestrian safety.

P.G. 212-06 — RMP Transport Reserve Surgeon

  • Highway District dispatches RMP for transport.
  • Reserve surgeon located outside the city: dispatched to 50th or 105th Precinct.

P.G. 212-41 — Alarms — DOE Facilities

Condition Response
Forced entry detected Patrol supervisor responds; assigns member to guard premises
No forced entry Code 10-90N (Unnecessary Alarm)

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisor must respond personally when forced entry is detected at a DOE facility and assign a member to guard until the premises are secured.

P.G. 212-48 — Motor Vehicles Repossessed/Parking Scofflaw

  • Follow established procedures for vehicles towed due to repossession or parking scofflaw status.

P.G. 212-50 — Aircraft Violations

  • Handle aircraft-related incidents per Department guidelines.
  • Notify appropriate federal agencies as required.

P.G. 212-51 — Response to Work Disruptions at City Construction Sites

  • Respond to disruptions at city construction sites.
  • Maintain order and ensure worker safety.

P.G. 212-52 — Labor Dispute

UMOS

  • Preserve peace and prevent destruction of property
  • Request Patrol Supervisor
  • Notify Desk Officer

Patrol Supervisor

  • Respond to the scene
  • Interview management and union representatives separately
  • Advise both sides of the following rules:
    • No sticks/poles that may be injurious; no professional agitators/offensive language
    • Precinct C.O. determines the number of pickets allowed
    • Force/violence not tolerated; right of public to use streets/sidewalk protected
    • Law enforced impartially
  • Obtain information for Strike Report
  • Notify: telephone D/O for immediate assignment of personnel, if required
  • Document: deliver Strike Report to D/O
  • Command Post: set up if required (NOT in facilities of Management or Labor)

Exam Alert: The Command Post for a labor dispute must NOT be set up in the facilities of either management or labor. This ensures the appearance of neutrality.

Desk Officer

  • Confer with C.O./Duty Captain regarding personnel assignments
  • Make assignments as directed; obtain personnel from Pct. if available, or request from Borough
  • Notify Operations
  • Document: have Strike Report prepared and forwarded; sign above Reviewed By caption

Harassment and Labor Disputes

Exam Alert: Certain Penal Law sections of harassment will NOT be enforced at labor disputes: - Harassment 1st degree (PL 240.25): Prohibits intentionally and repeatedly harassing by following in public place; engaging in conduct placing person in reasonable fear of physical injury - Harassment 2nd degree (PL 240.20): Course of conduct that alarms/seriously annoys with no legal purpose

However, any violence or physical harassment WILL be enforced — if they strike/shove/kick or attempt/threaten physical contact.

  • Picketing, leafleting, and demonstrating are exempt from harassment arrest
  • May refer victim to Court for injunction or NLRB (National Labor Relations Board)

P.G. 212-65 — Bicycle Patrol

  • Covers bicycle patrol operations and procedures.
  • Members assigned to bicycle patrol follow specific equipment and safety guidelines.

P.G. 212-66 — City Policy Concerning Identifying Information

Exam Alert: City policy ensures access to city services regardless of immigration status. Members shall not inquire about immigration status when providing city services.

P.G. 212-67 — Police/Clergy Program

(Updated R.O. 86, effective 09/23/25)

Purpose

To formalize police/clergy cooperation; provide instruction and direction to both police and clergy; and to aid the Department in its effort to advance the concept of Community Policing.

Definitions

Term Definition
Police/Clergy Liaisons Designated members available to assist the Department in: (1) Maintaining calm, (2) Seeking community cooperation during and after emergencies (bias incidents, hostage cases, EDPs), (3) Acting as unofficial recruiters, (4) Attending appropriate events (Precinct Community Council meetings, Youth Councils, etc.)
Police/Clergy Volunteers Selected by precinct commanders based on precinct needs. Function within precinct boundaries as recruiters, advisers, and a bridge between community and precinct.

Precinct Commander — When a Vacancy Occurs

  1. Notify Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau, in writing, as to cause of vacancy.
  2. Nominate a member of the clergy to fill vacancy using the vacancy nomination form.
  3. Forward nomination to Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau, through channels.

NOTE: Nominations should not exceed more than five per precinct. Deputy commissioners and bureau chiefs may also make nominations direct to the Deputy Commissioner, Community Affairs Bureau.

  1. Direct maintenance of a log listing all recruited and selected clergy (liaisons and volunteers) captioned:
    • Title, Field Name/Address, Telephone Numbers, Religious Institutions, Times Available, Languages Spoken, Other Special Qualifications

Police/Clergy Review Committee

  1. Review nominations.
  2. Forward recommendations to the Police Commissioner.

Exam Alert: Maximum of five clergy nominations per precinct. The Police/Clergy Review Committee reviews nominations and forwards recommendations to the PC.

P.G. 212-69 — Involuntary Protection Service

  • Procedures for handling persons requiring involuntary protective services.

P.G. 212-70 — Civilian Commendations

Application

  • Use form PD127-016.

Requirements

  • Facts must be accurate and not embellished.
  • Applications are potential Rosario material.

Restrictions

  • Endorsements from outside the Department are NOT considered.
  • Final approval by Chief, Community Affairs.

Exam Alert: Civilian commendation facts must not be embellished — they are Rosario material. Outside endorsements are not considered.

P.G. 212-73 — Bicycle Registration Program

  • Procedures for bicycle registration services.

P.G. 212-74 — Community Notification Protocol

  • Follow established community notification procedures for relevant incidents.

P.G. 212-81 — Unauthorized Display of Badges/Plates/Cards

  • Handle incidents of unauthorized display of Department badges, plates, or identification cards.

P.G. 212-82 — Forwarding of Fees to Audits and Accounts Unit

  • Forward all collected fees to the Audits and Accounts Unit per established procedures.

P.G. 212-79 — Subpoena Fees

Type of Subpoena Fee
Civil/State $15
Civil/Federal $40
Family Court (JD) No fee
Criminal No fee

Exam Alert: Know the subpoena fee schedule: State civil = $15, Federal civil = $40, Family Court and Criminal = no fee.

P.G. 212-91 — Department Requests for NYC Transit Buses

  • Follow procedures for requesting NYC Transit buses for Department operations.

P.G. 212-100 — Auxiliary Police

Coordinator Requirement

  • A coordinator is required for ALL patrol duties.

Age Restrictions

Age Permitted Activities
17 Administrative functions only
Under 18 NO patrol duties, NO undercover QOL assignments
18+ Full auxiliary duties with coordinator

If Coordinator Unavailable

  • Auxiliary members assigned to command facility ONLY.
  • NEVER patrol without a coordinator.

Exam Alert: Auxiliary police shall NEVER patrol without a coordinator. If the coordinator is unavailable, auxiliaries are assigned to the command facility only. Members under 18 may not patrol or perform undercover QOL — age 17 is limited to administrative functions.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must verify coordinator availability before any auxiliary patrol deployment. No exceptions to the coordinator requirement.

Section 212 — Special Incidents

P.G. 212-36 — Incidents Involving Former/Retired MOS or Off-Duty Law Enforcement

Initial Response

  1. Ascertain identity of the individual.
  2. Determine if the individual is armed.
  3. Request personal ID and pistol license.
  4. Request to see NYPD ID Card (PD416-091) or other official law enforcement ID.

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisor shall respond and approve resolution (arrest, summons, court referral, or no action).

Exam Alert: These individuals shall NOT receive preferential treatment based on former or present law enforcement status. This is a critical principle tested on exams.

Notifications

  • Desk officer notifies Operations Unit for incidents involving agents from other agencies.

P.G. 212-43 — Vacate Orders for Residential Buildings

Authority

  • Issuing agency (Buildings, Health, Fire, HPD) serves the order.
  • NYPD assists — does not serve the order.

Issuing Agencies

  • Department of Buildings
  • Department of Health
  • Fire Department
  • HPD (Housing Preservation and Development)

Procedure

  1. Verify credentials and authority of agency representative.
  2. NYPD assists in execution of the vacate order.

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisor shall consult the duty captain when: - Doubt exists about the authority of the agency representative - Large-scale relocations are involved - Occupants refuse to vacate - Potential for violence exists

Key Timelines

Responsibility Deadline
HPD seals building Within 72 hours
Investigation and Review Section contacted 24 hours in advance

Exam Alert: HPD is responsible for sealing the building within 72 hours. Investigation and Review Section must be contacted 24 hours in advance.

P.G. 212-45 — Foreign National Requesting Asylum

  • Handle asylum seekers in accordance with Department policy.
  • Ensure proper notifications and referrals to appropriate agencies.

P.G. 212-47 — Counterfeit Money

When Counterfeit Money Is Detected

  • Determined that the passer is an innocent victim, OR
  • No indication who passed it

Investigating Officer

  • Document: have person last in possession of the bill:
    • Write name/date across the face of the bill or scratch initials on a coin
  • Sign: rank, name, shield, date on bill or scratch initials on coin

Desk Officer

  • Document: prepare 4 copies of report on Typed Letterhead (49x4) to Chief of Patrol
  • Deliver:
    • Have messenger sign itemized receipt in Property Receipt Book
    • Deliver 49x4 and counterfeit bill to Patrol Borough office
    • Messenger brings back 1 copy of the 49 as a receipt from Borough
  • Receipt: attach receipted copy of 49 to the entry in the Property Receipt Book

Exam Alert: No voucher is prepared for counterfeit money. The bill goes to the Patrol Borough office with the 49x4, not to the Property Clerk.

Cross-References for Counterfeiting

See Also: PG 208-47 — Arrests for counterfeiting: D/O or counterpart notifies Dept. of Treasury, US Secret Service by phone direct.

See Also: PG 207-06 — Complaints of counterfeiting: D/O notifies Operations; Operations notifies US Treasury Dept., Secret Service Bureau.

P.G. 212-54 — Person Threatening to Jump from Structure

Immediate Actions

  1. Notify radio dispatcher.
  2. Request patrol supervisor, ESU, and ambulance.
  3. Request Harbor Unit if location is over navigable waters.

Intervention Techniques

  • Attempt to persuade or prevent person from jumping.
  • Seek assistance of relatives, friends, or clergy.
  • Confine person to the side facing the street, if possible.
  • Rope off area below.

Cross-Reference

  • Follow P.G. 221-13 (Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons) as appropriate.

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisor shall take command of the scene, coordinate with ESU, and ensure the area below is secured. Consider requesting additional resources (negotiators, mental health professionals) early.

P.G. 212-55 — Investigation of Aided Cases — Illegal Fireworks Injuries

Procedure

  1. Request patrol supervisor and precinct detective squad.
  2. Issue summons or arrest for illegal fireworks possession.

Aided Under 18 Years Old

  • Determine extent of adult involvement.
  • Arrest for Endangering Welfare of Child if probable cause exists.

Database and Notifications

  • Intelligence Division maintains database of all fireworks injury incidents.
  • Detective Bureau Wheel notified 24/7 for ongoing investigations.

Exam Alert: When the aided person is under 18, determine adult involvement and consider charges of Endangering Welfare of Child.

P.G. 212-57 — Service of Family Court/Supreme Court Orders of Protection

(Updated R.O. 104, effective 10/20/25)

Purpose

To assist in the service of all orders of protection and any associated papers issued by Family Court or Supreme Court.

Desk Officer — When Order of Protection Is Presented at a Department Facility

  1. Accept the order of protection from the member of the public or Court.

NOTE: Courts and advocacy organizations encourage petitioners to contact the NYC Sheriff in the borough of issuance so that the NYC Sheriff may serve the order. However, if a member of the public appears at a Department facility seeking assistance, the member of the service must accept the order.

  1. Have the petitioner complete the NYC Dept. of Finance, Office of the Sheriff, Service of Process Intake (SH-0609) form.

Monday Through Thursday (0300-2300) and Friday (0500-2300)

  1. Fax a copy of the order and the SH-0609 form directly to the NYC Sheriff’s Operations Desk.
  2. Confirm receipt via telephone.
  3. Make an entry in the Order of Protection Log: petitioner’s name, respondent’s name, name of court, docket number, and confirmation of NYC Sheriff’s receipt.

Order of Protection Log Format

Left Page Right Page
Pct. Serial #, Petitioner’s Name, Respondent’s Name, Docket #, 1st Tour UMOS Assigned/D.O., Time/Date of Attempt(s) Date & Time of Attempt(s), (Through), 6th Tour UMOS Assigned/D.O., Date & Time of Attempt(s), Final Disposition, Time/Date NYC Sheriff Office Received, Time/Date DVP Officer Entered In Registry

All Other Times

  1. Make appropriate entries in the Order of Protection Log.
  2. Assign a uniformed member to serve the order of protection.
    1. Indicate name under caption 1st TOUR UMOS.

If Order of Protection Is Served

  1. (UMOS) Attempt to serve the order.
  2. Prepare STATEMENT OF PERSONAL SERVICE (PD260-152) and a photocopy.
    1. Examine and account for all documents served; ensure appropriate box is checked.
  3. Make a digital Activity Log entry.
  4. Deliver form to desk officer.
  5. Forward photocopy of the Statement of Personal Service to domestic violence prevention officer.

NOTE: When preparing Statement of Personal Service forms for orders issued by Supreme Court, strike out Family Court and write in Supreme Court.

Desk Officer

  1. Enter into the Order of Protection Log that the order was served.

Domestic Violence Investigator

  1. Coordinate all tasks relating to service and recording of orders of protection (filing, forwarding, etc.).
  2. Enter applicable information for any order of protection served by the Department into the State Order of Protection Registry, via the FINEST system, upon receipt of each photocopy of a Statement of Personal Service.
  3. Enter the date and time entry into the Registry was made under the appropriate caption in the Order of Protection Log.
    1. Place initials inside same caption.
  4. Forward Statement of Personal Service to Family Court or Supreme Court clerk for any order of protection served by the Department.

If Unable to Serve Order of Protection

  1. (UMOS) Make a digital Activity Log entry of each attempt to serve the order.
  2. Return the order and related forms at end of tour to the desk officer.

Desk Officer

  1. Repeat steps 6 and 7 at least once each consecutive tour until the NYC Sheriff’s Office reopens or the order is served.
    1. Indicate name under caption 2nd Tour UMOS Assigned/Desk Officer.
  2. Repeat steps 3 and 4 for further processing when assigned member is unable to serve and the NYC Sheriff’s Office has reopened.
  3. Enter into the Order of Protection Log that the order was forwarded to NYC Sheriff.

Key Rules

  • Each attempt to serve shall be documented in the member’s digital Activity Log (respondent’s name, docket number, pertinent information).
  • Service must be attempted at least once each tour until effected or forwarded to the NYC Sheriff.
  • Assigned UMOS will inquire of neighbors, superintendent, etc. as to respondent’s whereabouts.
  • If respondent has moved, attempt to obtain new address; if within precinct, attempt service at new location.
  • If new address is outside the precinct, indicate in digital Activity Log and return order to desk officer for forwarding to appropriate command.

Exclusionary Order of Protection

Exam Alert: When an exclusionary order has been served (prohibits contact and/or excludes respondent from a location), uniformed members may NOT assist respondents who wish to remove clothing/property (clothes jobs). The respondent should be advised to return to court to have the order amended. However, this does NOT prevent a member from accompanying the PETITIONER if the petitioner has safety concerns about returning to a shared location to retrieve clothing/personal property.

Sergeant Focus: Ensure desk officers are maintaining the Order of Protection Log properly and that service is attempted at least once per tour. The Domestic Violence Investigator is responsible for entering service information into the State Order of Protection Registry via FINEST.

P.G. 212-56 — Diplomatic Incidents

Diplomatic Immunity — Core Rules

Action Permitted?
Arrest NO — shall not be arrested
Issue summons YES — may be issued summons
Enter UN HQ NO — not without Secretary General consent
Detain YES — pending verification, if danger to self/public

Exam Alert: Diplomats with immunity shall NOT be arrested but MAY be issued a summons. This is a frequently tested distinction.

Notifications (Immediate, by Telephone)

  1. Operations Unit
  2. Intelligence Division

Verification Process

  • Intelligence Division verifies diplomatic status through:
    • Mayor’s Office for International Affairs
    • US Mission to the United Nations
    • US Department of State

DWI and Diplomatic Immunity

Element Rule
Sobriety test/breathalyzer May ASK — diplomat entitled to REFUSE
Documentation Document request and response in Activity Log and Unusual Occurrence Report

Exam Alert: MOS may ask a diplomat to take a breathalyzer test, but the diplomat is entitled to refuse. Always document the request and response.

Even with Full Immunity

  • Prepare ALL normally required reports.

Parking Summons

  • Issuing a parking summons on diplomat/consul plates for a safety hazard is NOT a diplomatic incident.

Emergency Child Removal

  • With diplomatic immunity: directly supervised by CO or duty captain.

Sergeant Focus: Do NOT detain a properly identified and verified diplomat. All reports must still be prepared even when immunity applies.

P.G. 212-61 — Burglary/Robbery Apprehension Module (BRAM)

(Updated R.O. 27, effective 05/01/25)

Purpose

To reduce the occurrence of burglaries/robberies by identifying and apprehending those individuals committing burglaries/robberies.

Scope

The Precinct Detective Squad/Burglary Robbery Apprehension Module (PDS/BRAM) will maintain a dedicated focus on burglary/robbery investigations and shall be under the direct organizational control and supervision of the Precinct Detective Squad Commander.

Uniformed Member of the Service — Upon Receiving a Burglary or Robbery Complaint

  1. Determine whether a burglary or robbery has in fact been committed by conducting preliminary investigation.
  2. Broadcast description of perpetrator(s) and conduct search, if appropriate.
  3. Safeguard evidence; follow crime scene procedures per P.G. 212-04.
  4. Interview complainant and initiate investigation.
    1. If perpetrator(s) can be identified, transport complainant to station house to view photos.
  5. Prepare COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A) for robbery.
    1. Prepare for burglary if Burglary Apprehension Team member is not working.
    2. Comply with P.G. 207-01 (Complaint Reporting System).
  6. Notify Detective Borough Evidence Collection Team (ECT) to respond to all burglary incidents, including situations where an arrest is made at the scene.
  7. Notify Precinct Detective Squad who will immediately notify the designated PDS/BRAM investigator, when available.
    1. Provide all available information necessary to continue investigation.
    2. Notify Detective Borough Night Watch if PDS/BRAM office is closed (0100-0800 hours).

NOTE: Copies of robbery/burglary complaints recorded on COMPLAINT REPORTS in the precinct on late tours will be picked up at 0800 hours by a PDS/BRAM member.

Desk Officer

  1. Review and finalize the completed COMPLAINT REPORT in OMNIFORM System.
  2. Have a copy forwarded to the Precinct Detective Squad/BRAM.
  3. Ensure additional copies are sent to other units/agencies, as required.

PDS/BRAM Investigator

  1. Review burglary and robbery complaint reports and assist in identifying patterns or trends.
  2. Confer with the Precinct Field Intelligence Officer, when assigned, prior to and following the issuance of a pattern.
  3. Compile a pattern case folder for all identified patterns, documenting all investigative steps taken.
  4. Maintain photo books in accordance with established Legal Bureau guidelines.
  5. Confer with precinct and borough patrol and investigative units and disseminate all information regarding burglary and robbery trends, patterns, and wanted perpetrators.
  6. Update and maintain the Precinct Crime Information Center with active INVESTIGATION CARD (PD373-163), Field Information posters, serial burglar lists, and photos.
  7. Conduct an investigation when the subject of a Home Visit cannot be located by the Precinct Burglary Apprehension Team (BAT).
  8. Cultivate and maintain sources of information within command (confidential informants, building superintendents, landlords, block watchers, etc.).
  9. Debrief all suspects arrested for burglary/robbery to:
    1. Identify accomplices
    2. Identify fencing locations
    3. Identify other victims
  10. Be responsible for reviewing the list of persons selling items to pawnshops or secondhand dealers.
    1. Conduct INVESTIGATION CARD check, warrant investigation, criminal record check.
  11. Assist the Burglary Apprehension Team with Fencing Sting Operations.
  12. Take major case prints for every person arrested for the crime of burglary.

PDS/BRAM Supervisor

  1. Be responsible for the review of all burglary/robbery complaints received at the office to determine case status.
  2. Review all COMPLAINT REPORTS (PD313-152), Omniform Complaint Revisions, and associated Enterprise Case Management (ECMS) entries to determine whether cases should remain ACTIVE or CLOSED, and to ensure all necessary investigative steps have been taken.
  3. Assign robbery and burglary cases amongst all detectives in the unit.
  4. Ensure the crime information centers are updated accordingly.
  5. Ensure pawnshop/secondhand dealer inspections are properly investigated.
  6. Verify major case prints are taken for every person arrested for burglary.

Key Elements of BRAM Program

Element Detail
Specialized BRAM unit Created within every Precinct Detective Squad
Command structure Under direct control of PDS Commander
Personnel allocation Commensurate with reported burglary/robbery cases
Liaison One PDS/BRAM member designated liaison with Borough Robbery Squad and Intelligence Division
Pattern identification Detective Bureau notifies Patrol Borough Pattern ID Modules on all alerts
Photo books Required — contain photos of persons previously arrested for burglary, robbery, and grand larceny

Exam Alert: The ECT (Evidence Collection Team) shall be notified to respond to ALL burglary incidents, including those where an arrest is already made at the scene. This is a frequently tested point – ECT responds even when an arrest has occurred.

Sergeant Focus: The PDS/BRAM Supervisor is responsible for reviewing all burglary/robbery complaints to determine case status (ACTIVE or CLOSED) and ensuring all investigative steps have been taken. Major case prints must be taken for every burglary arrest.

Section 212 — Technology and Systems

P.G. 212-12 — Citywide Intelligence Reporting System

  • General intelligence reporting system for sharing and tracking intelligence information across commands.
  • Follow established procedures for submitting and accessing intelligence reports.

P.G. 212-78 — Utilization of Real Time Crime Center (RTCC)

  • RTCC provides intelligence and investigative support to field personnel.
  • Utilize RTCC resources for real-time information during investigations and critical incidents.

P.G. 212-99 — AMBER Alert System

Activation Criteria

  • Child must be 17 or younger.
  • Police must believe the child is in imminent danger of serious bodily harm or death.
  • May be activated for abduction by vehicle, foot, or any other means.

Who May Request Activation

  • Only a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may request activation through the NYS Police Communications Section.

Exam Alert: Only a Captain or above from Detective Bureau may request AMBER Alert activation. Not a patrol captain — specifically Detective Bureau.

Activation Process

Step Responsibility
Notify NYS Police Detective Bureau Duty Captain
Notify Operations Unit Detective Bureau Duty Captain
Broadcast description over radio divisions Communications Section
NCIC File 6 completed Investigative unit
NYSPIN File 11A (Abduction Message) Generated automatically
Major Case Unit notified Detective Bureau

Vehicle Involvement

  • If a vehicle is involved: special radio message regarding License Plate Reader System — manually enter the plate number.
  • If vehicle description AND plate available: fax to Traffic Management Center for Variable Message Signs.

Cancellation

Timeframe Method
Within 8 hours Fax to NYS Police
After 8 hours Via DCPI media notification

Exam Alert: AMBER Alert cancellation within 8 hours is by fax to NYS Police. After 8 hours, cancellation is through DCPI media notification. The Detective Bureau Duty Captain ensures proper cancellation.

Non-Eligible Cases

  • DCPI available 24/7 for cases not eligible for AMBER Alert activation.

P.G. 212-108 — NCIC/VGTOF Notifications

VGTOF — Violent Gang and Terrorist Organization File

Handling Codes

Code Meaning Action
Handling Code 1 ARREST Warrant exists — approach with caution
Handling Code 2 DETAIN Reasonable suspicion — active investigation
Handling Code 3 INVESTIGATION Do NOT alert individual — possible terrorism ties
Handling Code 4 INFORMATION GATHERING Do NOT alert individual

Exam Alert: Memorize all four VGTOF Handling Codes. Code 1 = Arrest (warrant), Code 2 = Detain (reasonable suspicion), Code 3 = Investigation (do not alert, terrorism), Code 4 = Information gathering (do not alert).

No Hit but Terrorism Suspected

  • Contact Intelligence Division Criminal Intelligence Section.

FBI Unable to Confirm

  • Do NOT arrest unless probable cause exists for a federal, state, or local law violation.

P.G. 212-123 — Body Worn Cameras (BWC)

Who Must Wear BWC

Exam Alert: Desk Officer, Telephone Switchboard Operator, and Station House Security are required to wear BWC while performing duty. These are positions you might not expect to need a BWC.

Platoon Commander Duties (If No P/C: D.O. or Unit Sup)

  • At start of tour: account for all BWCs assigned to command and enter details in the Command Log
  • Once per tour: review BWC video footage; log in to ID videos not properly categorized; instruct users to categorize as appropriate; document in Command Log Entry
  • Prohibited recording: notify C.O./Duty Captain
  • At EOT: account for all BWCs; ensure all returned to docking station for upload and recharging

UMOS Rules

  • Prior to roll call/start of tour: obtain BWC from docking station; turn on and inspect
  • Position: attach to outermost garment near breast pocket area for optimal recording field of view (use mounting hardware provided)
  • Use: ONLY if personally issued AND authorized by Dept. to record official activity while on-duty
  • Use of non-Department device is strictly prohibited

Exam Alert: Non-Department recording devices are strictly prohibited. The BWC must be personally issued to the member using it.

Supervisor Conducting Roll Call

  • After start of tour but prior to roll call: provide time for UMOS to obtain BWC from docking station
  • Inspect: ensure BWC properly affixed to uniform/outermost garment, turned on, and working

BWC Damaged/Unaccounted For

  • Platoon Commander (or D.O./Unit Sup) immediately investigates
  • Comply with PG 217-10 (Accidents — Dept Property) or PG 219-20 (Loss or Theft of Dept Property)
  • Document in Command Log; notify ITB Service Desk for replacement

Recording Rules — Police Action

Police Action Defined: Police service, law enforcement, or investigative activity conducted in furtherance of official duties, including: - Responding to calls for service - Addressing QOL conditions - Handling pick-up assignments - Any self-initiated investigative or enforcement actions (witness canvass, car stop, investigative encounters)

NOT considered Police Action: - Routine consensual conversations with the public as part of community engagement - Admin duty or non-enforcement function - Routine activities in Dept facilities - Dept meetings or training

Exam Alert: Even though meetings/training are NOT police action, the D.O., Telephone Switchboard Operator, and Station House Security ARE required to wear BWC while performing duty.

Timing

  • Begin recording prior to arrival at incident location OR prior to any self-initiated police action
  • Exceptions:
    • Vehicle operator may activate immediately after arrival if necessary for safe vehicle operation
    • Activate as soon as feasible after taking necessary action for human health/safety during unanticipated or exigent occurrences

Mandatory Activation

  • Police action (as defined above)
  • Any interaction that escalates and becomes adversarial with potential evidentiary value
  • Person under arrest or otherwise in custody
    • Immediately inquire if med/psych treatment necessary (ensure BWC activated when asking)

Discretionary Activation

  • Non-mandatory recording activities where officer judges it would be beneficial
  • May discontinue recording if victim or witness requests anonymity during interview

Prohibited Activation

  • Off-duty employment (including paid detail)
  • Sex crime victim interview (as soon as it becomes apparent)
  • Strip searches
  • Inside medical facility UNLESS necessary for police action (arrest, EDP, etc.)
  • Certain briefings (tactical discussions, resource capabilities, after-action debriefs involving ESU)
  • Confidential or undercover involved (CI/UC interviews or when CIs/UCs are present)

Exam Alert — Mandatory vs. Prohibited: Mandatory takes precedence over prohibited. Example: effecting an arrest where undercovers and informants are involved on scene — activate BWC because the arrest is mandatory.

If UC or CI Is Recorded

  • Enter note in comments section of video management system that a UC is visible
  • Detective Bureau supervisor prepares Report on Typed Letterhead to DCLM with description of officer and clothing

Deactivation Rules

  • Verbally document: state aloud on camera the reason prior to deactivation
  • Crime scene established: may deactivate after arrival at active scene
  • Subject NOT present: may deactivate after attempted apprehension/warrant execution when subject is not present

Political Activity

  • Handschu Guidelines: prohibit indiscriminate recording of political activity
  • TARU documents protests, demos, political events by photo/video
  • UMOS activate BWC at protest/demo/political event only when:
    • Enforcement action being taken
    • Unlawful activity occurring (whether or not enforcement action taken)
    • Directed by the incident commander

IDTU Responds to Hospital/Medical Facility

  • Tech and A/O both activate BWCs
  • Record questions on Interrogation Warnings to Persons in Police Custody
  • Record blood draw (whether by consent or court order)
  • If medical personnel request stop recording: capture request if possible, turn off BWC, record request in Digital A/L

BWC Documentation — Video Management System

  • Select all applicable categories from dropdown list
  • Arrests: enter complete arrest # beginning with borough letter in ID field
  • Investigative encounters NOT ending in arrest/summons: select Investigative Encounter and select final level in Add Metadata
  • All Level 2 encounters not escalating beyond Level 2: document apparent race, gender, more than one individual in Add Metadata
  • Categorize all BWC videos within the next two scheduled tours

End of Tour

  • Insert BWC into docking station at station house to upload video and recharge battery

Arrest Process

  • Arresting/assigned officer: identify all BWC videos associated with arrest plus any pertinent video from previous incidents
  • Prepare two checklists: District Attorney Body-Worn Camera Checklist AND Arrest Checklist
  • Transmit video to DA via Case Share
  • Desk Officer: ensure A/O shared video with DA and DA BWC Checklist prepared

Viewing Recordings — GO 15 (A.G. 318-11)

  • Member may view own BWC video as subject or witness in official Dept investigation (GO 15 statement) prior to making statement at time/place deemed appropriate by supervisor in charge

Failed to Record

  • Patrol/Unit Supervisor: investigate when notified member failed to record mandatory encounter
  • Document in uniformed member’s Digital A/L (not supervisor’s)
  • Prepare Report on Typed Letterhead (49) to Professional Standards Bureau through channels

Prohibited Recording

  • Patrol/Unit Supervisor notifies Desk Officer (or Unit Commander if no D.O.)
  • Commanding Officer: investigate; prepare Report on Typed Letterhead (49) to Chief of Department through channels, with copies to DC Information Technology and Professional Standards Bureau

Typed Letterhead Comparison

Fail to Record (Mandatory Event) Record a Prohibited Incident
Patrol/Unit Supervisor prepares 49 to Professional Standards Bureau through channels Commanding Officer prepares 49 to Chief of Department through channels (copies to DC IT and PSB)

Exam Alert: For failure to record, the Patrol Supervisor writes the 49 (to PSB). For a prohibited recording, the Commanding Officer writes the 49 (to Chief of Dept). Know who writes and who receives in each scenario.

BWC — Additional Data

  • Access Code: cover the lens when inputting access code to keypad at Dept facility
  • BWC is Department property (hardware, software, video, audio, captured data)
  • Do NOT share with anyone except MOS for official purposes, prosecutors as described, or as authorized by PC
  • Audit trail: video management system maintains audit trail for all transactions
  • Video deleted after 39 months (certain categories retained longer: arrest, homicide)
  • CO may request longer retention through channels to DC Information Technology
  • Disclosure: ONLY share with DA, US Attorney, or Special Prosecutor
    • Do NOT share with CCRB, ACS, NYC Law Dept, Corp Counsel (except Family Court prosecutions), or other police jurisdictions
    • Refer all requests to Legal Bureau’s BWC Unit
  • Show-ups: do NOT use still shot of video for a show-up
  • Photo arrays: MAY use (print and paste) for photo arrays
  • Witnesses/victims/civilians: cannot view recordings; refer to prosecutor or Legal Bureau Subpoena Litigation Unit
  • FOIL requests: FOIL@NYPD.ORG or a860-openrecords.nyc.gov/new

Exam Alert: BWC video is automatically deleted after 39 months. Do NOT use BWC video stills for show-ups, but MAY use for photo arrays.

P.G. 212-127 — Gunshot Detection

UMOS

  • Respond: canvass for perps, victims, evidence, witnesses; ensure BWC activated
  • Ascertain whether alert is confirmed or unconfirmed
  • Confirmed activations include:
    • Person shot or evidence of such (blood, blood trail)
    • Ballistics or firearms evidence
    • Property damage from gunfire
    • 1 or more 911 calls for shots fired
    • Eye/earwitness to the shooting (if necessary, establish crime scene)
  • Whether confirmed or unconfirmed: notify Patrol Supervisor AND Communications
  • Safeguard potential evidence of possible shooting
  • Request TARU to retrieve video evidence
  • Confirmed or unconfirmed — document:
    • Prepare 61 for ALL alerts; leave OPEN to Precinct Detective Squad
    • Note in narrative that incident originated as a gunshot detection alert
    • Ensure Shots Fired caption is checked regardless of classification

Crime Classification for Complaint Report

Condition Classification PD Code
No other offense; no ballistics evidence Investigate Shots Fired 083
No other offense; ballistics evidence found Investigate Shot Fired, Ballistics Recovered 088
Shotspotter alert; no ballistics Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert 080
Shotspotter alert with ballistics Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert, Ballistics Recovered 089

Exam Alert: Know the four PD codes for gunshot detection classification. The key distinction is whether it is a Shotspotter alert (080/089) vs. general shots fired (083/088), and whether ballistics evidence was recovered.

Patrol Supervisor

  • Respond to confirmed alerts
  • Notify and request response of PDS, C.O./X.O./Duty Captain, CSU or ECT as appropriate
  • Notify Desk Officer with pertinent details

Exam Alert: Operations and Borough are NOT specifically notified by anyone in the initial gunshot detection response — they receive notifications directly through the system.

Desk Officer

  • Confirmed alerts: update Operations, Patrol Borough, and Pct/PSA/TD C.O./X.O./Duty Captain

C.O./X.O./Duty Captain

  • Respond to all confirmed alerts
  • Supervise response: ensure thorough canvass for evidence and victims

1st Platoon Desk Officer

  • Review all 61s for previous 24 hours; ensure all gunshot alerts are open to the squad
  • If 61 says no evidence recovered: review alert and determine if additional 911 calls exist
  • Determine if daylight canvass necessary (review ICAD narratives, Complaint Report, audio)

Neighborhood Coordination Officer

  • Open companion case for confirmed alerts in Enterprise Case Management System (ECMS)
  • Conduct daylight canvass for cameras, ballistics, witnesses, etc.

Commanding Officer

  • Review alert reports; ID problematic locations requiring additional resource deployment
  • Chronic false alerts: document and notify Information Technology Bureau (ITB)

P.G. 212-121 — Unauthorized Radio Transmission

  • Follow established procedures for handling unauthorized use of police radio frequencies.
  • Notify appropriate investigative and technical units.

P.G. 212-129 — Facial Recognition Technology

Fundamental Principle

Exam Alert: Facial recognition does NOT establish probable cause by itself. It generates investigative leads only.

Authorized Uses (ONLY)

  1. Basis to believe person committed, is committing, or is about to commit a crime
  2. Missing person, victim, or witness
  3. Deceased person identification
  4. Person incapacitated or unable to self-identify
  5. Arrestee without valid ID
  6. Imminent threat to health or public safety

Key Definitions

Term Definition
Probe Image Image of an unidentified person from witnesses, victims, or reliable sources
Photo Repository ONLY arrest and parole photographs — the approved database
Possible Match Candidate An investigative lead ONLY — does NOT constitute probable cause

Exam Alert: The photo repository contains only arrest and parole photographs. No other image sources are in the approved database.

Process Flow

  1. Investigator submits probe image to RTCC-FIS (Facial Identification Section).
  2. FIS assigns a tracking number.
  3. Investigator confirms authorized use.
  4. FIS runs the query against the photo repository.
  5. Visual comparison performed.
  6. Background check conducted.
  7. Peer review of results.
  8. Supervisor final review.
  9. FIS Possible Match Report issued.

Report Requirements

  • Must include notification that a match alone does NOT constitute probable cause.

Prohibited Uses

  • Use of images outside the photo repository is PROHIBITED unless approved by:
    • Chief of Detectives
    • Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau
    • Chief of Intelligence

Privacy

  • All records are subject to privacy and confidentiality restrictions.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors conduct the final review in the facial recognition process. Ensure all subordinates understand that a possible match is an investigative lead only and must never be presented as establishing probable cause.

Section 212 — Tour Operations

P.G. 212-01 — Roll Call Formations

(Updated R.O. 109, effective 10/24/25)

Purpose

To inspect, instruct and conduct roll call of uniformed members of the service.

Timing

  • Five minutes after the start of tour of duty.

Patrol Supervisor Duties

  1. Assemble platoon in ranks in the sitting or muster room.
  2. Make assignments.
  3. Inspect the platoon:
    1. Allow uniformed members to reasonably demonstrate that a protective vest is being worn.
    2. List members deficient in uniform, equipment or personal appearance.
      • Pay special attention to seasonal/optional uniform items (AG 305-03, AG 305-04).
    3. Reprimand and instruct deficient members privately.
    4. Follow up to determine if corrections have been made; take disciplinary action if necessary.
  4. Ensure QOL officers and administrative QOL officers are not utilized as backfill for other precinct assignments (shooting posts, prisoner transports, hospital posts, patrol supervisor, desk officer, etc.) without authorization of the commanding officer.
  5. Ensure QOL officers, administrative QOL officers, and QOL sergeants are not assigned to out-of-precinct details during their regularly scheduled tours without permission of the Bureau Chief concerned, the Chief of Department, or the Police Commissioner.
  6. Ensure members are familiar with integrity concerns.
  7. Ensure members are aware of active Paid Detail locations and times of assignments at these locations within the precinct.
