  8. Conduct a platoon briefing informing uniformed members of:
    1. Information compiled by training sergeant,
    2. Incident critiques,
    3. Updates regarding crime patterns, crime trends, and wanted persons, and
    4. Precinct conditions and tactics for addressing the conditions, etc.
  9. Report personally to ranking officer, members absent and reason, if known.
  10. Instruct platoon not to congregate in groups around the stationhouse.
  11. Direct members of platoon to leave stationhouse for post or assignments at selected intervals.

Exam Alert: The platoon briefing is a Patrol Supervisor responsibility. It covers training sergeant info, incident critiques, crime patterns/trends/wanted persons, and precinct conditions/tactics.

Protective Vest Inspection

Exam Alert: Upon notification that a protective vest inspection will be conducted, the uniformed member must take appropriate actions to demonstrate the vest is being worn: - Raising, removing, unbuttoning top garment to expose the vest to visual inspection, OR - Patting on the front and rear of the vest to produce a sound, OR - Any other manner indicating to the patrol supervisor that the vest is being worn

Sergeant Focus: Only authorized/approved protective vests will be worn.

Lieutenant-Platoon Commander

  1. Supervise roll call.
    1. Personally inspect the outgoing platoon to ensure personal appearance meets Department standards.

Special Operations Lieutenant

  1. Ensure QOL officers and admin QOL officers are not utilized as backfill without authorization of the Commanding Officer.
  2. Ensure QOL officers, admin QOL officers, and QOL sergeants are not assigned to out-of-precinct details without permission of the Bureau Chief, Chief of Department, or Police Commissioner.

Travel Time Note

Sergeants, police officers and detectives required to report in uniform to a location within the geographic boundaries of the precinct to which assigned at the beginning of their tour, shall be given sufficient travel time within the tour.

P.G. 212-02 — Meal Period

(Updated R.O. 6, effective 01/07/26)

Purpose

To provide for meal periods, obtain authorization for out of command meal, obtain approval from competent authority for meal period, and report meal location to radio dispatcher.

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Take meal in either a police facility, bonafide restaurant, or Department vehicle.
  2. Notify radio dispatcher at beginning of meal, of location where meal is to be taken.
    1. If meal is to be taken in a Department facility, notify the desk officer.

Members shall commence and end their meal period on assigned post. Travel time to and from a meal location is not authorized.

Foot/Train Patrol

  1. Obtain meal on assigned post, if possible, and in a place maintained for eating purposes.
  2. Make digital Activity Log entry prior to leaving post including meal location address, and again upon returning to post.
  3. Inform radio dispatcher upon return to post and make digital Activity Log entry.

RMP Assignment

  1. Notify radio dispatcher, by appropriate code signal, prior to commencing assigned meal period and upon return to patrol and provide:
    1. Address when meal taken in restaurant
    2. Nearest intersection when meal taken in RMP
    3. Stationhouse when meal taken in command facility
  2. Remain in area designated by commanding officer, if meal is taken within command facility.
    1. Be available for assignment by desk officer.
  3. Acknowledge calls directed to unit and:
    1. Remain within assigned sector, when practical
    2. Do not park more than one Department vehicle in the same place at the same time
    3. Comply with any request for police service and make digital Activity Log entry.
  4. Notify desk officer when deprived of scheduled meal due to police service.

Desk Officer

  1. Take meal, when appropriate, providing another supervisor is assigned to cover desk officer responsibilities and is able to provide continuous police service within the command.
  2. Make accommodations, if possible, so that members are not deprived of meal, if their originally scheduled meal is delayed due to police services rendered.
    1. Assign a police officer for relief of RMP operator or recorder, when necessary.
    2. Assign a police officer to provide meal relief for member assigned to fixed post, if available.

Assigned Meal Relief Officer

  1. Remain in view on post at time of relief when assigned to relieve radio motor patrol unit for meal.
    1. Assume all duties of recorder during relief period.

RMP Operator

  1. Transport relieved member directly to meal location.

Uniformed Member on Meal

  1. Notify desk officer if radio motor patrol fails to return within fifteen minutes after end of meal period.

Exam Alert: If RMP fails to return within 15 minutes after end of meal period, the member on meal must notify the desk officer. This is a specific time limit frequently tested.

Patrol Supervisor

  1. Notify radio dispatcher, via landline, from meal location when commencing meal period.
    1. Make digital Activity Log entry at beginning of meal (include address of meal location) and upon conclusion of meal period.

Duty in Civilian Clothes

  1. Request permission from immediate supervisor prior to taking meal.
  2. Notify immediate supervisor of intended meal location.
    1. If immediate supervisor is unavailable, notify bureau/borough operations desk/Field Operations Desk (FOD) of intended meal location.
  3. Document name of supervisor notified and meal location in appropriate Department record (digital Activity Log, movement log, etc.).

Additional Data — Key Rules

Rule Detail
Intoxicants All on-duty members are strictly prohibited from consuming intoxicants in any amount during meal period
Civilian clothes exception Members in civilian clothes may be granted permission by bureau chief/counterpart
Meal eligibility Must be scheduled for 8 consecutive hours of duty
8/9-hour tour One hour meal period
12-hour tour Two, non-consecutive, 45-minute meal periods
Not during first/last Members are not to be assigned meal periods during the first hour or last hour and one-half of their tour, except in emergency
3-hour excusal rule If a police officer requests excusal of 3 hours or less, they are eligible for a meal period provided the request for partial excusal is made prior to start of the scheduled tour

Exam Alert: Know the meal period rules for different tour lengths: 8/9-hour tour = 1 hour; 12-hour tour = two non-consecutive 45-minute periods. Meals are NOT assigned during the first hour or last 1.5 hours of the tour.

Extended Tour Overtime Meal Rules

  • Meal period on extended tour overtime may be granted by a supervisor only if assigned meal period was denied during the regularly scheduled tour.
  • Such meal period may not be granted if it interferes with arrest processing or needs of the service.
  • Under no circumstances will a rescheduled meal on extended tour overtime be assigned during the last hour of overtime.
  • Once the event necessitating the overtime ends, a member who has not yet taken a meal period is precluded from taking any meal thereafter.

Outside Details — Police Officers

  • Police officers assigned to outside details (parades, events, etc.) should be granted a one-hour meal period absent exigent circumstances.
  • Police officers denied a meal period are entitled to one hour compensatory time at straight time.
  • This credit is not available if meal was denied due to exigent circumstances.
  • Detail supervisors shall assign meal periods prior to posting the detail.
  • If exigent circumstances prevent granting meals, commanding officers of outside details must submit a written communication to the Commanding Officer, Office of Labor Relations detailing the actual exigent circumstances. The Police Commissioner makes the final determination.

Meal in Adjoining Precinct

  • CO of a precinct may designate a portion of an adjoining precinct where members may obtain meals during hours when a suitable eating facility is not available within the precinct.
  • Such designation forwarded on Typed Letterhead to the appropriate borough commander.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure meal periods are properly scheduled and documented. Key responsibilities include: assigning meal periods prior to posting details, ensuring members are not deprived of meals, and properly documenting any exigent circumstances that prevent granting meals.

P.G. 212-03 — Expiration of Tour

Foot Patrol

  • Leave post and proceed directly to stationhouse at end of tour.
  • Contact desk officer 1 hour prior to end of tour when on assignment requiring relief.

RMP Crew

  • Proceed to stationhouse, park in relief area.
  • Remain until relieved by incoming crew.
  • Inform relieving operator of any incomplete assignments or vehicle defects.
  • If RMP will not be used next tour: operator secures auto and delivers keys to desk officer.

Sergeant Focus: Desk officer shall check CPR System Roll Call to account for all members from previous tour. Conduct immediate investigation if any member failed to scan out.

4th Platoon Rule

Element Detail
Platoon 4th (1800x0200)
Trigger Subpoenaed for next-day court (2nd platoon)
Reassignment 3rd platoon (1600x2400)
Not notified in advance Excused at 2400 hours
Overtime NO OT for 2400-0200 period
Working beyond 0200 Entitled to OT compensation

Exam Alert: The 4th platoon rule reassigns members to the 3rd platoon (1600x2400), not the 2nd platoon. Members are excused at 2400 if not notified in advance, and there is no overtime for the 2400-0200 gap.

P.G. 212-08 — Activity Logs

Who Uses Digital vs. Paper

Format Who
Digital Activity Log (Department smartphone) All uniformed MOS below captain rank (except permanent admin/clerical)
Paper Activity Log (PD112-145) Undercovers — shall NOT use digital

Exam Alert: Undercovers use the paper Activity Log (PD112-145), never the digital version.

Required Entries

  • Day, date, and tour
  • Assignment
  • Meal time
  • Operator and recorder name
  • Vehicle inspection (condition, odometer, gas level)
  • Chronological activities

RMP Activity Log Rules

  • Only the recorder makes chronological entries.
  • Operator may note See Recorder’s Activity Log.
  • BOTH operator and recorder must record their own enforcement actions.

Digital Activity Log Features

  • Can capture non-MOS signatures (physician, DA, etc.)

Rosario Material Warning

Exam Alert: Activity Logs are potential Rosario material — they may be reviewed by the Department, outside agencies, DAs, and defense attorneys.

Restrictions

  • DO NOT upload official Department arrestee photos to the digital Activity Log (sealed upon termination of criminal action).
  • Disclosure or sale of Activity Logs is prohibited under NY City Charter sections 2604(b)(4) and 2604(d)(5) — violation carries financial penalties and possible misdemeanor charges.

P.G. 212-10 — Interrupted Patrol Log

Purpose

Controls time spent in the stationhouse by platoon members during their tour.

Procedure

  1. Member reports to desk officer with reason for presence in stationhouse.
  2. Desk officer makes entries in own handwriting:
Column Content
Time In When member arrived
Rank Member’s rank
Name Member’s name
Assignment Current assignment
Reason Why member is in stationhouse
Time Out When member departed
Total Time Duration in stationhouse
Desk Officer Desk officer initials/signature

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer shall maintain the Interrupted Patrol Log under direct control at all times and review it frequently during the tour to ensure member presence is being expedited.

Exam Alert: Entries are made in the desk officer’s own handwriting — not the member’s. Commanding officers shall frequently review this log to determine if stops are necessary and being expedited.

Section 212 — Transit Operations

P.G. 212-16 — Evacuations of NYC Transit Trains

Key Definitions

Term Definition
Reach Train Passengers walk through stalled train to reach a train positioned in front or behind
Rescue Train Brought alongside; passengers cross via emergency evacuation devices
Emergency Evacuation Devices Located at south end of southbound platform near blue light

Power Removal Rules

Evacuation Type Power Removal Required?
Reach train NO
Rescue train NO
Evacuation to benchwall/roadbed YES — MUST ensure power is removed first

Exam Alert: Power must be removed only when evacuating to benchwall or roadbed. Reach trains and rescue trains do not require power removal.

Notifications and Responsibilities

  • MOS: Notify patrol supervisor/duty captain; report who ordered evacuation and why.
  • Patrol supervisor: Establish command post; keep dispatcher and duty captain informed.
  • Duty captain: Contact RTOCC (Rapid Transit Operations Command Center); maintain constant communication.
  • Typed Letterhead report submitted to Chief, Transit Bureau for all incidents.

P.G. 212-17 — Critical Situations on NYC Transit

Types of Critical Situations

  • Fires, floods, collisions, derailments
  • Persons under trains
  • Major service disruptions

Key Procedures

  • Remove power when necessary.
  • Post a member in the street to direct emergency units.
  • DO NOT permit removal of sick/injured firefighter without fire officer consent.
  • Remove persons from under trains EXCEPT when deceased and possibly a crime victim.

Train Crew Signal for Police

  • Horn/whistle pattern: LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT

Exam Alert: The train crew signal for police is LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT. Remember this distinctive pattern.

Required Reporting Details

Report to duty captain/incident commander: - Train operator information - Conductor information - Line and direction of travel - Car numbers - Full incident details

P.G. 212-19 — Animals on NYC Public Transportation

General Rule

  • Section 1050.9(h)(1) NYCRR: No animals unless enclosed in a container.

Exemptions

Category Details
Law enforcement dogs Permitted without container
Service animals Seeing eye, hearing, physical disability, non-apparent disabilities (epilepsy, heart, lung disease)
Animals in training Permitted

Exam Alert: Members CANNOT require proof of disability or documentation for a service animal. May only inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance.

Stray Dogs on Roadbed

  • Request patrol supervisor and ESU.
  • DO NOT enter roadbed without supervisor permission.
  • DO NOT chase after the dog (causes it to run).
  • Restraining pole shall not be waved as a weapon.

Dead Animal

  • Notify radio dispatcher.
  • Contact Sanitation Department for removal.

P.G. 212-20 — Ejection from New York City Transit Property

(Updated R.O. 90, effective 10/02/25)

Purpose

To provide procedures for uniformed members having occasion to eject a passenger from New York City Transit property.

Scope

Any person who violates any portion of NYCRR Part 1050, regardless of whether or not that person has also been issued a TAB summons/NOV or Criminal Court Summons for that violation, is subject to ejection from NYC Transit facilities. Issuance of a Notice of Violation does not preclude a member from ejecting a passenger from the transit system, if the member concerned deems it appropriate.

Procedure — When Necessary to Eject a Passenger

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Advise passenger of ejection and reason.
  2. Permit passenger who voluntarily leaves or shows willingness to do so, to leave without interference.
  3. Use no more force than is necessary to effect the ejection, if passenger refuses to leave.
    1. Generally, grasping the upper arm and forearm to escort the person off the system is considered using reasonable force necessary to effect the ejection.

NOTE: If the passenger’s conduct requires more force than is ordinarily needed to assist and guide the passenger out of the transit system, an arrest may be appropriate.

  1. Make digital Activity Log entries and prepare a TRANSIT SYSTEM EJECTION REPORT using Finest Online Records Management System (FORMS) each time an ejection is effected.
    1. Submit completed report prior to completion of tour.
    2. Use radio code disposition 10-93E to finalize assignment anytime an individual is ejected from the NYC Transit System.

Transit District Supervisor

  1. Review TRANSIT SYSTEM EJECTION REPORT using FORMS and ensure all required captions are complete and accurate.
    1. If unable to verify accuracy and completeness, return to submitting member for necessary attention.
    2. Approve TRANSIT SYSTEM EJECTION REPORT using FORMS, after verifying accuracy and completeness.

Exam Alert: Radio code 10-93E is used to finalize the assignment when an individual is ejected from transit. The Transit District Supervisor reviews and approves the ejection report via FORMS.

Sergeant Focus: The supervisor shall review every Transit System Ejection Report for accuracy and completeness before approving it in FORMS. If the report is deficient, it must be returned to the member for correction.

P.G. 212-21 — Photography on NYC Transit System

General Rule

  • Photography, filming, and video recording are PERMITTED on the transit system.
  • Ancillary equipment (lights, reflectors, tripods) may NOT be used.

Exception

  • Press with valid MOME (Mayor’s Office of Media and Entertainment) ID may use ancillary equipment.

When to Take Action

Situation Action
Camera alone, no hazard No action
Ancillary equipment without press ID Take action
Creating a hazard Take action

Exam Alert: Camera alone = no action needed. Only act if ancillary equipment is used without a MOME press ID or if a hazard is being created.

P.G. 212-22 — Malfunction of Transit Bureau Radio Network

Dispatch Procedures

  • Dispatch re-routes Transit calls to Patrol Services Bureau.
  • If a call is not finalized in 30 minutes, dispatch another unit.
  • Prioritize 911 calls from field MOS.

Command Responsibilities

Sergeant Focus: During radio malfunction: - Radio check of strategic areas at 30-minute intervals - Assign officers to paired patrols with UHF radios - Suspend assignments in civilian clothes - Members make hourly signals to command - Strategic Response Group placed on Alert for Transit use if required

Critical Limitation

  • NYPD radios (other than Transit Bureau) DO NOT work underground, except in Manhattan stations equipped with repeaters.

P.G. 212-23 — Inspection of Rapid Transit Stations on Post

Radio Code Signals

Code Meaning
10-75T Transit patrol by non-Transit member
10-75S Station inspection by Transit member
10-75O TOMS
10-75M Mobile order maintenance

Inspection Requirements

  • Confer with railroad clerk at each station about conditions.
  • Inspect concessions, toilets, stairways, mezzanines, platforms, and all token booths (open and closed).
  • Visit each station at least once per tour.

Opposite Sex Toilet Inspection

  • Knock and announce Police Inspection.
  • WILL NOT enter until departure of occupants.
  • UNLESS informed of criminal activity inside.

Exam Alert: The opposite-sex toilet rule requires knock and announce first. Entry is delayed until occupants depart, unless criminal activity is reported.

RMP Recorder

  • Shall inspect each uncovered station in the sector.

P.G. 212-24 — Removal of Power in Subway

Authorization

  • ONLY in extreme emergencies involving life-threatening situations.
  • Patrol supervisor determines necessity.

Factors to Consider

  • Time of day (rush vs. non-rush)
  • Weather (extreme heat)
  • Station type (elevated vs. below ground)
  • Train locations in vicinity
  • Extent of injuries
  • Risk to public

Emergency Alarm Box

Feature Detail
Spacing 400-600 feet apart
Location Beneath blue lights
Operation Pull lever down as far as possible, then release
Effect Removes power on ALL tracks in vicinity

Exam Alert: Emergency alarm boxes are spaced 400-600 feet apart under blue lights. They remove power on ALL tracks in the vicinity, not just one track.

Critical Warnings

  • NYC Transit will automatically restore power if not notified immediately via the telephone at the alarm box.
  • Officers shall NOT personally shut off power UNLESS grave risk of imminent danger and no other safe alternative exists.

Notification

  • Notify Operations Unit of time of request; notify again when power is restored.

Alternative to Power Removal

  • Ride in lead cars through the area to search for suspects.

Section 212 — Key Terms and Numbers

Critical Terms

Term Definition
CPR System Centralized Personnel Resource — attendance and roll call tracking system
Digital Activity Log Primary activity recording tool on Department smartphone; required for all uniformed MOS below captain rank
Interrupted Patrol Log Desk officer log tracking time members spend in the stationhouse during tour
Rosario Material Documents, notes, or logs potentially discoverable in criminal proceedings
Reach Train Train brought in front of or behind a stalled train so passengers can walk through
Rescue Train Train brought alongside for cross-platform transfer via emergency evacuation devices
RTOCC Rapid Transit Operations Command Center — primary transit coordination hub
Emergency Alarm Box Located 400-600 feet apart under blue lights; removes power on ALL tracks in vicinity
MOME Mayor’s Office of Media and Entertainment — issues press credentials
DCPI Deputy Commissioner, Public Information — notified for all press incidents (24/7)
ERPO Extreme Risk Protection Order — court order prohibiting firearm purchase/possession
VGTOF Violent Gang and Terrorist Organization File within NCIC
RTCC-FIS Real Time Crime Center — Facial Identification Section
Probe Image Image of unidentified person submitted for facial recognition comparison
Photo Repository Approved facial recognition database containing ONLY arrest and parole photographs
Possible Match Candidate Investigative lead ONLY — does NOT establish probable cause
AMBER Alert Child abduction alert for victims 17 or under in imminent danger of serious harm/death
ALPS Automated License and Permit System — electronic pistol license verification
Public Counter Scanner machine counter that MUST read zero before polls open
Protective Counter Scanner counter that is NOT expected to be zero — do not confuse with public counter
TRS Telecommunications Relay Service — dial 711 for deaf/hard of hearing communication
4th Platoon Rule Members on 1800x0200 subpoenaed for next-day court reassigned to 3rd platoon (1600x2400)

Investigative Encounter Terms

Term Definition
Level 1 Encounter Request for Information — based on objective credible reason; no detention, no search request
Level 2 Encounter Common Law Right of Inquiry — based on founded suspicion; may ask accusatory questions and request consent to search
Level 3 Encounter Terry Stop — based on reasonable suspicion of felony or PL misdemeanor; may detain, use force, frisk
DeBour NYS Court of Appeals case articulating the 4-level encounter framework (request for info, CLRI, forcible stop, arrest)
Terry v. Ohio Federal Supreme Court case providing authority for Level 3 forcible stop and possible frisk
RTKBC Right to Know Business Card — pre-printed card given to persons at Level 2 and 3 encounters
RTKBC-G Right to Know Business Card — General — blank version used when regular RTKBCs are exhausted
CLRI Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report; prepared when consent to search is requested at Level 2
Stop Report Documentation prepared for each Level 3 (Terry) stop; submitted through FORMS; reviewed by supervisor
TACTICS Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integration Collection System — records training sessions for investigative encounter compliance
Protective Measures Tactics short of a frisk (order hands from pockets, instruct person to put down object) used when officer safety is at risk
Frisk Carefully running hand down outside of person’s clothing to check for weapons — NOT to locate evidence

Crime Scene and Incident Terms

Term Definition
Police Line Do Not Cross White tape with blue letters; establishes secure area for police operations
Crime Scene Do Not Cross Yellow tape with black letters; safeguards area where evidence may be present
Crime Scene Evidence Cone 5-inch orange cone marked NYPD in black; placed next to small evidence items
HARBOR Mnemonic for mandatory CSU response: Homicide, Aggravated assault (likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe/circumvented alarm), Other (CSU assists), Robbery/hijacking (firearm caused injury)
IACC Inter-Agency Command Center — separate from police Command Post; established at hazmat/SECTAR incidents
SECTAR Significant fire, Explosion, Chemical burn, Toxic Air Release, Radiation hazard — triggers full hazmat response
MDV Mobile Decontamination Vehicle — requested through Operations by on-scene coordinator
FRUITS Mnemonic for when D.O. notifies Ops AND Borough for fires: Forced entry by FD, Relocation of tenants, Unusual fire, Injury/death, Three alarms+, Suspicious fire
RATCOP Mnemonic for info released only after conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives: Reputation, Anticipated testimony, Test results, Credibility, Opinion/speculation, Photos/films/mug shot

BWC and Technology Terms

Term Definition
BWC Body Worn Camera — Dept property; must be personally issued; activate for all police action
Police Action Police service, law enforcement, or investigative activity in furtherance of official duties — triggers mandatory BWC activation
GO 15 (A.G. 318-11) Rule allowing member to view own BWC video as subject/witness in Dept investigation prior to making statement
Gunshot Detection Alert system (Shotspotter) that detects potential gunfire; generates confirmed or unconfirmed alerts
ECMS Enterprise Case Management System — NCO opens companion case for confirmed gunshot alerts

Critical Numbers

Number Significance
1 hour Contact desk officer before end of tour (foot patrol needing relief); arrive at poll site before opening
30 minutes Dispatch another unit if Transit call not finalized; radio check intervals during malfunction
100 feet No-electioneering zone around poll site entrance
400-600 feet Spacing of emergency alarm boxes (under blue lights) in subway
2100 hours Poll closing time — persons on line may vote, no new entries
72 hours HPD deadline to seal vacated premises
60 days Rifle/shotgun surrender investigation completion deadline
2 years Pistol license validity period; minimum firearm retention under ERPO
8 hours Window to cancel AMBER Alert via fax to NYS Police
30 days ERPO Unit reviews before expiration for renewal determination
$15 Subpoena fee — Civil/State
$40 Subpoena fee — Civil/Federal
$5 Medical records fee for outside parties
711 TRS dial code for deaf/hard of hearing relay service
(212) 504-4115 TTY number for 311 system
0900-1700 License Division hours (Mon-Fri); Legal Bureau ERPO Unit hours (Mon-Fri)
300 feet Minimum outdoor hazmat distance for spills and most fires
1,000 feet Minimum outdoor hazmat distance for explosions/suspected devices/military/tanker fires; bomb evacuation distance
20 minutes Discontinue bomb search before threatened explosion time
1 hour Do not resume bomb search after threatened explosion time
39 months BWC video automatic deletion period
PD Code 080 Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert (Shotspotter, no ballistics)
PD Code 083 Investigate Shots Fired (no ballistics)
PD Code 088 Investigate Shot Fired, Ballistics Recovered
PD Code 089 Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert, Ballistics Recovered

Handling Codes (VGTOF)

Code Action Key Detail
1 ARREST Warrant exists — approach with caution
2 DETAIN Reasonable suspicion — active investigation
3 INVESTIGATION Do not alert — possible terrorism ties
4 INFORMATION GATHERING Do not alert individual

Transit Radio Codes

Code Meaning
10-75T Transit patrol by non-Transit member
10-75S Station inspection by Transit member
10-75O TOMS
10-75M Mobile order maintenance
10-90N Unnecessary Alarm (DOE facilities, no forced entry)

Train Crew Signal

Signal Meaning
LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT Train crew requesting police assistance

Section 212 — Review Questions

Test your knowledge of Command Operations procedures. Click each answer to reveal the correct response.

1. Who is required to use a paper Activity Log (PD112-145) instead of the digital Activity Log?

    1. Members on light duty
    1. Auxiliary police officers
    1. Undercover officers
    1. Members above the rank of captain
Answer

C) Undercover officers. Per P.G. 212-08, undercovers use the paper Activity Log (PD112-145) and shall NOT use the digital version.

2. Entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log are made in whose handwriting?

    1. The member reporting to the stationhouse
    1. The desk officer’s own handwriting
    1. The patrol supervisor on duty
    1. The platoon commander
Answer

B) The desk officer’s own handwriting. Per P.G. 212-10, the desk officer makes all entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log in their own handwriting and maintains the log under direct control.

3. Activity Logs are considered potential what type of material?

    1. Brady material
    1. Rosario material
    1. Classified material
    1. Privileged material
Answer

B) Rosario material. Per P.G. 212-08, Activity Logs are potential Rosario material and may be reviewed by the Department, outside agencies, DAs, and defense attorneys.

4. Under the 4th platoon rule, members subpoenaed for next-day court are reassigned to which platoon?

    1. 1st platoon
    1. 2nd platoon
    1. 3rd platoon (1600x2400)
    1. They remain on the 4th platoon
Answer

C) 3rd platoon (1600x2400). Per P.G. 212-03, members on the 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed for next-day court (2nd platoon) are reassigned to the 3rd platoon. If not notified in advance, they are excused at 2400 hours with no OT for the 2400-0200 period.

5. Before evacuating transit passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, what must happen first?

    1. A rescue train must be positioned
    1. Power must be removed
    1. The duty captain must approve
    1. ESU must arrive on scene
Answer

B) Power must be removed. Per P.G. 212-16, when evacuating passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, power MUST be removed first. Power is NOT removed for reach trains or rescue trains.

6. What distinguishes a reach train from a rescue train?

    1. Reach trains use emergency devices; rescue trains do not
    1. Reach trains involve walking through the stalled train; rescue trains involve cross-platform transfer via emergency devices
    1. Reach trains require power removal; rescue trains do not
    1. There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
Answer

B) Reach trains involve walking through the stalled train to a train positioned in front or behind; rescue trains are brought alongside for cross-platform transfer using emergency evacuation devices. Per P.G. 212-16, neither requires power removal.

7. The transit radio code 10-75T indicates what?

    1. Transit station inspection by Transit member
    1. Transit patrol by a non-Transit member
    1. Transit emergency evacuation
    1. Mobile order maintenance
Answer

B) Transit patrol by a non-Transit member. Per P.G. 212-23, 10-75T is for transit patrol by non-Transit personnel. 10-75S is station inspection by Transit, and 10-75M is mobile order maintenance.

8. Emergency alarm boxes in the subway are spaced how far apart and located where?

    1. 200-400 feet apart, under red lights
    1. 400-600 feet apart, under blue lights
    1. 600-800 feet apart, under yellow lights
    1. 100-200 feet apart, under green lights
Answer

B) 400-600 feet apart, under blue lights. Per P.G. 212-24, emergency alarm boxes are located 400-600 feet apart beneath blue lights. Pulling the lever removes power on ALL tracks in the vicinity.

9. Can members require proof of disability or documentation for a service animal on public transportation?

    1. Yes, written documentation is required
    1. Yes, but only a verbal confirmation
    1. No, but members may inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance
    1. No, members may not ask any questions
Answer

C) No, but members may inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance. Per P.G. 212-19, members CANNOT require proof of disability or documentation. They may only ask if the animal is providing disability assistance.

10. What photography/recording is permitted on the transit system without a press ID?

    1. No photography of any kind
    1. Camera alone with no ancillary equipment
    1. Camera with a tripod only
    1. Camera with lights and reflectors
Answer

B) Camera alone with no ancillary equipment. Per P.G. 212-21, photography and recording are permitted, but ancillary equipment (lights, reflectors, tripods) may NOT be used without a valid MOME press ID.

11. MOME Press Card holders are entitled to what access?

    1. Access to interior crime scenes
    1. Access to cross police and fire lines
    1. Access to security-restricted areas
    1. Access to all areas without restriction
Answer

B) Access to cross police and fire lines. Per P.G. 212-49, MOME press card holders are entitled to cross police and fire lines. This does NOT include interior crime scenes or security-restricted areas.

12. During First Amendment Activity, if NYPD orders persons to leave an area, what happens to MOME press?

    1. They must leave with everyone else
    1. They are arrested if they refuse to leave
    1. They are NOT required to leave but should move to a safe location
    1. They may stay only with incident commander approval
Answer

C) They are NOT required to leave but should move to a safe location. Per P.G. 212-49, during First Amendment Activity, MOME press are not required to leave even if NYPD orders persons to leave — they should be directed to a safe location.

13. Before polls open, the scanner machine public counter must read what?

    1. Any number below 100
    1. The same as the protective counter
    1. Zero (0)
    1. The number of registered voters
Answer

C) Zero (0). Per P.G. 212-86, ALL scanner machine public counters must read zero before polls open. If not zero, the desk officer/supervisor must be notified immediately and the scanner shall NOT be used. The protective counter will likely NOT be zero — do not confuse the two.

14. No-electioneering signs must be posted within how many feet of the poll site entrance?

    1. 50 feet
    1. 75 feet
    1. 100 feet
    1. 200 feet
Answer

C) 100 feet. Per P.G. 212-86, the Board of Elections must post no-electioneering signs within 100 feet of the poll site entrance.

15. Polls close at what time, and what is the rule for persons on line?

    1. 2000 hours; all persons must leave immediately
    1. 2100 hours; persons on line may vote, no new entries after 2100
    1. 2200 hours; persons on line may vote
    1. 2100 hours; no one may vote after 2100
Answer

B) 2100 hours; persons on line may vote, no new entries after 2100. Per P.G. 212-87, polls close at 2100 hours. Persons already on line at closing may vote, but NO new persons may join the line after 2100.

16. Temporary Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPOs) are served by whom?

    1. NYPD patrol officers
    1. The precinct detective squad
    1. NYC Sheriff’s Office
    1. US Marshals Service
Answer

C) NYC Sheriff’s Office. Per P.G. 212-128, temporary ERPOs are served by the NYC Sheriff’s Office, NOT by NYPD.

17. Firearms seized under an ERPO are retained for a minimum of how long?

    1. 6 months
    1. 1 year
    1. 2 years
    1. 5 years
Answer

C) 2 years. Per P.G. 212-128, firearms are retained for a minimum of 2 years. No release is permitted without written authorization from CO, License Division (and CO, Medical Division if the subject is a MOS).

18. What is the maximum age for an AMBER Alert subject?

    1. 12 or younger
    1. 15 or younger
    1. 17 or younger
    1. 18 or younger
Answer

C) 17 or younger. Per P.G. 212-99, the child must be 17 or younger and police must believe the child is in imminent danger of serious bodily harm or death.

19. Who may request AMBER Alert activation?

    1. Any sergeant or above
    1. The precinct commanding officer
    1. A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau
    1. The Operations Unit duty officer
Answer

C) A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau. Per P.G. 212-99, only a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may request activation through the NYS Police Communications Section.

20. Within what timeframe can an AMBER Alert be cancelled by fax to NYS Police?

    1. 4 hours
    1. 6 hours
    1. 8 hours
    1. 12 hours
Answer

C) 8 hours. Per P.G. 212-99, cancellation within 8 hours is by fax to NYS Police. After 8 hours, cancellation is through DCPI media notification.

21. Facial recognition technology establishes what level of evidence?

    1. Probable cause for arrest
    1. Reasonable suspicion for a stop
    1. Investigative lead only — NOT probable cause
    1. Proof beyond reasonable doubt
Answer

C) Investigative lead only — NOT probable cause. Per P.G. 212-129, facial recognition does not establish probable cause by itself. A possible match candidate is an investigative lead only.

22. The facial recognition photo repository contains only what type of images?

    1. Driver’s license photos
    1. Social media photos
    1. Arrest and parole photographs
    1. Surveillance camera captures
Answer

C) Arrest and parole photographs. Per P.G. 212-129, the photo repository contains ONLY arrest and parole photographs. Use of images outside this repository is prohibited unless approved by the Chief of Detectives, Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau, or Chief of Intelligence.

23. VGTOF Handling Code 1 means what?

    1. Detain — reasonable suspicion
    1. Arrest — warrant exists, approach with caution
    1. Investigation — do not alert individual
    1. Information gathering only
Answer

B) Arrest — warrant exists, approach with caution. Per P.G. 212-108, Handling Code 1 = Arrest (warrant exists), Code 2 = Detain, Code 3 = Investigation (do not alert), Code 4 = Information gathering (do not alert).

24. Can a diplomat with full immunity be arrested?

    1. Yes, for any felony
    1. Yes, but only for violent felonies
    1. No, but may be issued a summons
    1. No, and may not be issued a summons
Answer

C) No, but may be issued a summons. Per P.G. 212-56, diplomats with immunity shall NOT be arrested but MAY be issued a summons. Pending verification, they may be detained if a danger to self or public.

25. In a Level 2 investigative encounter, the UMOS requests consent to search. What document must be prepared?

    1. Stop Report
    1. Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report (CLRI)
    1. Level 2 Encounter Report only
    1. Arrest Report
Answer

B) Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report (CLRI). Per P.G. 212-11, when consent to search is requested at Level 2, the UMOS prepares a CLRI. At Level 3, the request is documented on the Stop Report instead.

26. What does the HARBOR mnemonic stand for regarding when Crime Scene Unit must respond?

    1. Homicide, Assault (any), Robbery, Burglary (any), Officer-involved, Rape
    1. Homicide, Aggravated assault (likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe/circumvented alarm), Other (CSU will assist), Robbery/hijacking (firearm caused injury)
    1. Homicide, Arson, Robbery, Burglary, Obstruction, Rape
    1. Homicide, Assault (SPI), Robbery (any), Burglary (safe involved), Other crimes, Rape
Answer

B) Homicide, Aggravated assault (dangerous instrument AND likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe OR circumvented alarm), Other crime (CSU will assist investigation), Robbery OR hijacking (firearm caused injury). Per P.G. 212-04, these are the specific criteria for mandatory CSU response.

27. At a hazmat scene, what is the minimum outdoor evacuation distance for an explosion or suspected device?

    1. 300 feet
    1. 500 feet
    1. 1,000 feet
    1. 1,500 feet
Answer

C) 1,000 feet. Per P.G. 212-37, the minimum outdoor distance for any explosion or suspected device is 1,000 feet. For spills and most fires, the distance is 300 feet. The 1,000-foot distance also applies to fires involving military shipments or tanker trucks.

28. During a bomb threat search, teams must discontinue searching how long before the threatened explosion time?

    1. 10 minutes
    1. 15 minutes
    1. 20 minutes
    1. 30 minutes
Answer

C) 20 minutes. Per P.G. 212-40, search teams must discontinue 20 minutes prior to the threatened explosion time, regardless of whether the premise is evacuated or occupied. Search must NOT resume for at least 1 hour after the threatened time.

29. At a post-explosion bomb scene, who removes injured persons to safe locations for medical treatment?

    1. EMS
    1. FDNY
    1. ESU
    1. Responding UMOS
Answer

C) ESU. Per P.G. 212-40, the patrol supervisor/ranking officer directs ESU to remove injured to safe locations — not EMS. EMS administers medical treatment at the safe locations, but the physical removal is an ESU responsibility.

30. The RATCOP mnemonic identifies information that may only be released after conferral with DCPI AND which other official?

    1. Borough Commander
    1. Chief of Patrol
    1. Chief of Detectives
    1. Police Commissioner
Answer

C) Chief of Detectives. Per P.G. 212-77, RATCOP items (Reputation, Anticipated testimony, Test results/refusal, Credibility, Opinion/speculation, Photos/films/mug shots) require conferral with both DCPI and Chief of Detectives before release.

31. Under PG 212-123, who prepares the Typed Letterhead (49) when a member fails to record a mandatory BWC event vs. when a member records a prohibited incident?

    1. Patrol Supervisor writes both
    1. Commanding Officer writes both
    1. Fail to record: Patrol Supervisor; Prohibited recording: Commanding Officer
    1. Fail to record: Commanding Officer; Prohibited recording: Patrol Supervisor
Answer

C) Fail to record a mandatory event: Patrol/Unit Supervisor prepares the 49 (to Professional Standards Bureau). Record a prohibited incident: Commanding Officer prepares the 49 (to Chief of Department, with copies to DC IT and PSB). Per P.G. 212-123, know who writes and who receives in each scenario.

32. BWC video is automatically deleted after how many months?

    1. 12 months
    1. 18 months
    1. 24 months
    1. 39 months
Answer

D) 39 months. Per P.G. 212-123, BWC video is automatically deleted after 39 months. Certain categories (arrest, homicide) may be retained longer. The C.O. may request extended retention through channels to DC Information Technology.

33. At what alarm level does FDNY notify NYPD Operations, and at what alarm level does the Desk Officer notify Operations?

    1. FD notifies at 2 alarms; D.O. notifies at 3 alarms
    1. FD notifies at 3 alarms; D.O. notifies at 2 alarms
    1. Both notify at 2 alarms
    1. Both notify at 3 alarms
Answer

A) FD notifies NYPD Operations at 2 alarms; the Desk Officer notifies Operations at 3 alarms. Per P.G. 212-58, this is a commonly tested distinction about who initiates the Operations notification at each alarm level.

34. In PG 212-52 (Labor Disputes), certain harassment charges will NOT be enforced. Which type of harassment WILL be enforced?

    1. Harassment 1st degree (PL 240.25)
    1. Harassment 2nd degree (PL 240.20)
    1. Any violence or physical harassment (strike/shove/kick)
    1. Following a person in a public place
Answer

C) Any violence or physical harassment. Per P.G. 212-52, Harassment 1st degree (PL 240.25) and Harassment 2nd degree (PL 240.20) will NOT be enforced at labor disputes. However, any actual violence or physical harassment — striking, shoving, kicking, or attempting/threatening same — WILL be enforced.

35. Can an MOS request a breathalyzer test from a diplomat suspected of DWI?

    1. No, this would violate diplomatic immunity
    1. Yes, and the diplomat must comply
    1. Yes, the MOS may ask, but the diplomat may refuse
    1. Only with approval from the Intelligence Division
Answer

C) Yes, the MOS may ask, but the diplomat may refuse. Per P.G. 212-56, MOS may ASK a diplomat to take a sobriety test (breathalyzer), but the diplomat is entitled to REFUSE. The request and response must be documented in the Activity Log and Unusual Occurrence Report.

Section 213 — Mobilization/Emergency Incidents

Overview

Section 213 of the NYPD Patrol Guide covers mobilization, emergency response, special events, and First Amendment activities. This chapter is HIGH PRIORITY for the Sergeant Promotional Exam, particularly the procedures governing First Amendment activities (213-06, 213-20) and special events management (213-15). Sergeants must know authorization hierarchies, specific numerical thresholds, processing time limits, and the rights of demonstrators and prisoners.

This study guide covers 17 procedures from Section 213. Six procedures have full extracted text; the remaining 11 are summarized from cross-references.

Priority Topics for Sergeant Exam

  1. Mass arrest authorization – Only Senior Executive (deputy chief+) or designee (captain+) can order mass arrests at First Amendment events (213-20)
  2. MAPC activation and prisoner limits – Chief of Department activates; 20 per wagon, 5 per officer, 4 officers per supervisor (213-06)
  3. 7 hours 30 minutes processing time limit for First Amendment violations/misdemeanors (213-06)
  4. Incident Commander hierarchy – Precinct CO / Patrol Borough Commander / Chief of Patrol (213-15)
  5. Legal observer identification – NLG = green hats, NYCLU = blue hats/vests; free access through police lines (213-20)
  6. Barrier exclusion distances – 100 feet on through streets, 25 feet from corners (213-15)
  7. Security alert levels – GREEN/BLUE/YELLOW/ORANGE/RED; NYC at ORANGE; changes via FINEST Message (213-18)
  8. Body-worn cameras NOT activated at demonstrations unless per P.G. 212-123 (213-20)
  9. Handcuff checks at three points – entering van, exiting van, regularly at processing (213-20)
  10. De-escalation required before enforcement action at First Amendment activities (213-20)

Duties at a Disorder — P.G. 213-05

Overview

P.G. 213-05 details the duties and responsibilities of each rank when responding to an unusual disorder. This is one of the most heavily tested procedures in the 213 series. Sergeants must understand the role hierarchy, the Command Post structure, deployment planning, prohibited tactics, team assignments, and the distinction between mobilization levels.

Pct CO / XO / Duty Inspector / Duty Captain or Above

Enroute

  • Contact Command Post (CP) by radio OR phone for assessment

Upon Arrival

  • Assume duties of Incident Commander
    • Designate the Mobilization Point

Exam Alert: The CO/XO, Duty Captain need NOT be present to raise the Mobilization Level, but they MUST confer with one of the above before raising it.

Designate/Deploy

  • Mobilization Point
  • Traffic and Security Posts
  • Rooftop and overhead security

Additional Personnel

  • After briefing by Patrol Supervisor, Boro Wheel, or Operations Unit
    • Authorize mobilization of additional personnel if necessary

Debrief

  • Highest ranking supervisor on scene conducts the debrief

Command Post (CP)

Establish and Staff

  • Ensure properly staffed and operating Command Post has been established
  • Assign Command Post Staff:
    • Operations, Intelligence, Personnel/Admin & Logistics

Location

  • ID location for CP

Disorder Plan

  • Obtain Disorder Plan from Pct/PSA/TD

Review Disorder Area

  • Personally, or if unable, by map

Exam Alert: Only Levels 3 & 4 involve Precincts. If Boro SRG goes, the Duty Captain responds with a Command Post Staff. On ANY mobilization, the Duty Captain is going.

Communications

  • Confer with Communications regarding appropriate citywide frequency
  • Captain and above have special or additional frequency
  • Cell phones at CP to improve communications
  • RMPs with MDTs — Direct Personnel Officer to deploy RMPs with MDTs where needed (Mobilization Point, Borough, CP)
  • Implement Dual Dispatch System

Dual Dispatch System

Priority Dispatched To
High priority Detail officers
Low priority Precinct officers

Exam Alert: In the Dual Dispatch System, HIGH priority jobs go to DETAIL officers and LOW priority jobs go to PRECINCT officers. Do NOT let the exam trick you by inverting high and low. This is a classic exam trap.

Deployment Plan — Seven Things to Estimate

The CO/IC creates a plan by estimating seven things about the disorder:

  1. Leadership — Identify leadership; if a smaller group, identify specific individuals
  2. Size and Movement — Number of participants, geographic boundary of area involved
  3. Motivation and Intent — Whether majority of participants are disorderly; use Community Affairs and Detectives to identify future locations the crowd may target
  4. Duration and Intensity — Whether the disorder has escalated to violence or property damage

Sectors

  • Divide area into sectors for mobile patrol by SRG, preferably in vans

Deployment

  • Deploy 2/3 of available enforcement personnel
  • Hold 1/3 in reserve
  • When sufficient mobile forces are deployed in sectors, assign additional personnel if needed:
    • Foot posts — Sufficient size: 1 Sgt & 8 P.O.s
    • Perimeter security posts and 1 disorder control squad (1 & 8)

Exam Alert: The deployment ratio is 2/3 deployed, 1/3 held in reserve. Foot posts are staffed at 1 Sergeant and 8 officers. These numbers are frequently tested.

SRG Tactics

  • Use mobile tactics of speed, surprise & deception
  • Deploy as a unit, when possible, to mobile or foot posts within designated sectors

Tactical Actions

  • Contain/Exclude disorderlies from specific areas
    • Contain & isolate by sectoring, perimeter security, or other appropriate manner
  • Disperse — Order them to disperse
  • Arrests — Direct that arrests be made, when appropriate
  • Intelligence — Begin rumor control using Community Affairs and Community Leaders

Perimeter Security Post Duties

  • Prevent disorderlies from:
    • Entering particular areas
    • Creating dangerous crowd conditions
    • Impeding traffic
  • Reroute public or private transportation vehicles around the area as necessary
  • Curiosity seekers and unauthorized persons
    • Keep them out of the area when dangerous conditions exist

Exam Alert: The rule about keeping curiosity seekers out is a QUALIFIED rule (added in 2021). You keep them out only when dangerous conditions exist — not automatically. The exam may test this qualifier.

Rooftops, Alleys, Rear Yards

  • Provide rooftop and overhead security
  • Do NOT allow a Sergeant to assign a UMOS alone to Rooftops, Alleys, or Rear Yards (this is a directive to the Lieutenant)
  • Only uniformed officers in Rooftops, Alleys, and Rear Yards
  • Do NOT permit a Sergeant to assign fewer than 2 officers in uniform to these locations

Exam Alert: A sergeant cannot assign a single officer alone to rooftops, alleys, or rear yards. The minimum is 2 officers, and they must be in uniform. This is a safety directive aimed at the Lieutenant level but the Sergeant must also know it.

Captain’s Instructions (to ALL Supervisors)

  • Nature, mission, objectives — Specific objectives for specialized units
    • Use minimum force
    • Specialized equipment & tactics
  • Unity of Assignment — No independent/unauthorized actions (act like a TEAM)
    • Captain commands SRG or a Borough’s worth of Precinct cops and maintains unity
  • Post Boundaries
    • Boundaries of their post/sector
    • Arrest process area
    • Command Post (CP) & phone number at CP
  • Communication
    • Radio frequencies & call signals
    • Immediate reporting of unusual/important incidents
    • Ring schedule:
      • Every 30 minutes for enforcement units
      • Every 60 minutes for other units
  • Provide Borough & Operations with regular updates on progress & need for additional resources

Exam Alert: The ring schedule is 30 minutes for enforcement units and 60 minutes for other units. Do not confuse these intervals.

Prepare for Relief

  • Confer with new Incident Commander
  • Outgoing and Incoming IC should debrief all field commanders on:
    • Changing patterns of disorder
    • Strength of disorderly groups
    • Any police-related incidents occurring during tour
    • Any recommendations for changes in overall strategy

Operation of Command Post

  • Assist Incident Commander in operation of CP if directed
    • Operations, Personnel, Intelligence, Logistics

Lieutenant Duties

Respond

  • Quickly & safely to the stationhouse

Mobilization Point

  • Ascertain mobilization point & respond promptly by RMP

Assignment

  • Mobile Field Force (MFF) Commander
    • 3 to 5 squads of 1 & 8, each from Precincts

Parking

  • Direct parking of department vehicles in designated spaces only at mobilization point
  • Have Sergeants give vehicle keys to security detail if required

Personnel Documentation

  • Collect ROSTERS and turn Rosters into the Command Post

Communications

  • Instruct Sergeants regarding RADIO:
    • Ensure all are on the proper frequency
    • Maintain radio discipline
    • Warn against unauthorized transmissions

Inspect

  • Inspect Sergeant’s proper uniform and equipment

Instructions

  • Follow instructions of Captain
  • Instruct Sergeants, and ensure Sergeants and subordinates receive the following:
    • TEAM CONCEPT
      • Importance of TEAM CONCEPT in disorder control
      • Mission of the team
      • No independent action; follow direction and adhere to team concept
    • Tactics
      • Maintain formation of 1 arm’s length between adjoining MOS
      • Be in onguard stance when in line or wedge formation
    • Locations
      • Post/Sector boundaries
      • Nature of disorder
      • Location & phone # of CP & arrest process area
    • Ranking Officers
      • ID of Incident Commander
      • MFF Lt & other key ranking officers
    • Communications
      • Equipment information
      • Proper radio frequency & their MFF call sign
    • Use of Force
      • Use minimum force
      • Don’t PUNISH, rather be PROFESSIONAL
      • Importance of de-escalation tactics and crowd psychology
      • Tolerate verbal abuse by crowd attempting to incite
      • Maintain firearms discipline
    • Arrest Team
      • Supervisor should NOT wade into crowd
      • Ensure A/O observes perpetrator’s actions
    • Rooftops, Alleys, Rear Yards
      • Be aware of dangers (rock/bottle throwers, snipers, etc.)
      • Do NOT permit Sergeant to assign fewer than 2 officers in uniform

Prohibited Tactics

  • Complacency
  • Breaking formation
  • Unnecessary conversation
  • Congregating

Exam Alert: The four prohibited tactics are: complacency, breaking formation, unnecessary conversation, and congregating. If you see these as answer choices, they are all prohibited.

Proper Uniform & Equipment

Lt, Sgt & Cop — ALL must have: - Helmet - Baton - Flashlight - Disorder Guidelines available in RMP

For Lt & Sgt — Add: - ROSTERS to the above list

Exam Alert: Disorder Guidelines must be available in the RMP — not carried on person. Rosters are required for Lieutenants and Sergeants only, not for police officers.

Inspections (When Preparing to Respond)

  • Desk Officer (except Transit, per PG 213-03)
    • Stages & inspects for proper equipment
  • Sergeant
    • Ensures proper equipment (213-03)
    • Upon arrival at mobilization point — Conduct thorough inspection of members assigned (213-05)
  • Lieutenant
    • After obtaining rosters (213-05), warn against unauthorized transmissions

After Getting Instructions at Mobilization Point — Enroute to Staging Area or Post

  • Sergeant ensures proper equipment (213-03)

Sergeant Duties

Respond

  • Respond to Station House (10-02)
    • Form squad of 8 officers
    • 1st platoon squad becomes 2 officers
    • Ensure members are properly equipped
    • Report to mobilization point in a van

Roster

  • While enroute, prepare Roster of members
  • Upon arrival, submit roster to Lieutenant

Communication (While Enroute)

  • Have members turn radios to the correct frequency BUT
    • Only Sgt and the van operator will have radios turned on while enroute

Exam Alert: While enroute to the mobilization point, only the Sergeant and the van operator have their radios turned on. All other members have their radios tuned to the correct frequency but turned OFF. This prevents radio confusion during transit.

Assignment

  • May assist Incident Commander as CP Staff (Ops, Personnel, Intel, Logistics)

Instruct

  • Relay instructions from Lieutenant to your squad

Parking

  • Direct parking only in designated areas
  • Have keys given to security detail, if required

UMOS Duties

Reporting

  • Respond to Station House (10-02) OR
  • Report direct to the Mobilization Point, as directed
    • Do NOT drive through disorder to get to mobilization point

Communications

  • When directed, turn radio to proper channel

Instructions — RECORD the Important Ones:

  • Sector/Post Boundaries
  • Nature of disorder
  • Mission of TEAM, Equipment info
  • Location & phone # of Command Post and Arrest Process Area
  • Ranking supervisors
  • Radio Frequency & call signs

Mobilization Levels — Two Types

Exam Alert: There are TWO DIFFERENT TYPES of mobilizations. Levels 1 & 2 are SRG mobilizations. Levels 3 & 4 are PCT/TD/PSA mobilizations. The exam will test whether you know which type corresponds to which level.

Levels 1 & 2 Levels 3 & 4
Type SRG Mobilizations PCT/TD/PSA Mobilizations
Level Description
Level 1 SRG from affected Patrol Borough + 1 Patrol Supervisor from affected TD/PSA
Level 2 SRGs Citywide + 1 Patrol Supervisor from affected TD/PSA
Level 3 PCTs/TD/PSAs from affected Patrol Borough — 1 & 8 from each PCT/TD/PSA in the Borough
Level 4 PCTs/TD/PSAs Citywide — 1 & 8 from each PCT/TD/PSA Citywide

Level 3 & 4 — Arrest Platoon

  • 1st Platoon — 1 Sergeant from 3 commands picked by Operations, plus 2 P.O.s from each command

Team Assignments

Crowd Dispersal

  • Give warnings to disorderly crowds, including instructions to step out of roadways or disperse from the area
  • Take action against unlawful and disorderly groups
  • In line or wedge formations, direct team to disperse a disorderly crowd

Mobile Response

  • Create patrol sectors in disorder area for mobile response units
  • Create a mobile reserve for emergency response and reliefs

General Patrol

  • Select appropriate posts for patrol
  • Foot patrol — Provide foot patrol teams to create a sense of police presence in area

Perimeter Duty

  • ID borders of disorder and provide perimeter security on appropriate roadways:
    • Detour traffic around the disorder area
    • ID alternate routes for detoured traffic
    • Prevent unauthorized entry into the disorder area
    • Prevent disorderly groups from entering specific disorder area

Escort Duty

  • Liaison with other agencies and utilities
  • Staging location — Provide staging locations where the system of escorts can be administered

Arrest Duty

  • Implement appropriate arrest tactics under the supervision of sergeants and lieutenants

Protecting Vulnerable, Sensitive, or Critical Locations

  • Responsible for correctly identifying locations and providing security for designated areas

First Amendment Activities and Mass Arrest Processing

Overview

This section is among the most exam-critical in the entire Patrol Guide. It covers the Department’s response to First Amendment activities (protests, demonstrations, marches), the tiered response framework, and the mass arrest processing procedure. Sergeants must thoroughly understand the legal protections, de-escalation requirements, authorization hierarchy for enforcement actions, arrest processing rules, and the specific rights of prisoners and legal observers.

P.G. 213-20 — Response to First Amendment Activities

Key Definitions

Term Definition
First Amendment Activity Exercise of rights protected by the 1st Amendment: speech, assembly, petition, press
Senior Executive Deputy chief or higher rank
Designee Captain or above designated by a Senior Executive
De-escalation Reducing intensity of conflict or potential conflict
Incident Commander MOS in charge of the police response
Nonviolent Participant Person engaged in lawful First Amendment activity
Violent Behavior Conduct involving force causing or intended to cause physical injury or property damage
Protest Liaison Community Affairs Bureau (CAB) member assigned to communicate with demonstrators

Key Points

Tiered Response Structure

  • Tier One Response: A sergeant may serve as Incident Commander
  • Escalation to higher tiers involves higher-ranking Incident Commanders

Protest Liaisons (Community Affairs Bureau)

  • CAB members are assigned as protest liaisons to communicate with demonstrators
  • Liaisons must wear uniform clearly displaying rank, name, shield, and command
  • Liaisons may NOT wear civilian attire
  • CAB personnel are NOT assigned to patrol or enforcement duties except in exceptional circumstances

Body-Worn Cameras

  • Body-worn cameras are NOT activated at demonstrations unless per P.G. 212-123
  • This is a specific restriction – cameras are not turned on simply because a protest is occurring

Handschu Consent Decree

  • Political activity investigations require written approval from the Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau
  • This is a legal constraint that limits investigative authority at First Amendment events

Legal Observers

  • National Lawyers Guild (NLG): Identified by bright green hats
  • NYCLU: Identified by blue hats/vests
  • Legal observers are permitted free access through police lines

Arrests at First Amendment Activities

  • Only a Senior Executive (deputy chief or higher) or designee (captain or above) can order mass arrests
  • Individual arrests for criminal conduct are still permitted by any supervisor
  • De-escalation attempts are required before enforcement action
  • Warnings must be given before enforcement (when feasible)
  • Sufficient resources must be assembled before taking enforcement action

Handcuff Checks — Three Required Points

  1. Entering transport van
  2. Exiting transport van
  3. Regularly at arrest processing

Transfer of Command

  • Transfer of command between boroughs must be documented by radio transmission via Communications Division

Notifications

  • Citywide Events Planning is available 1000-1800 hours, Monday through Friday
  • Outside those hours, contact the Operations Unit

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • At Tier One events, the sergeant serves as Incident Commander
  • Ensure de-escalation is attempted before any enforcement action
  • Do NOT order mass arrests – only a Senior Executive or designee has that authority
  • Ensure individual arrests for criminal conduct are properly documented
  • Verify protest liaisons are in proper uniform with visible identification
  • Confirm body-worn cameras are NOT activated unless P.G. 212-123 applies
  • Perform handcuff checks at the three required points
  • Document any transfer of command via radio through Communications Division
  • Know when to contact Citywide Events Planning vs. Operations Unit

Exam Alert: Only a Senior Executive (deputy chief or higher) or designee (captain or above) can order mass arrests at First Amendment activities. A sergeant CANNOT order mass arrests.

Exam Alert: NLG legal observers wear bright green hats. NYCLU legal observers wear blue hats/vests. Both must be permitted free access through police lines.

Exam Alert: Body-worn cameras are NOT activated at demonstrations unless per P.G. 212-123.

Exam Alert: Handcuff checks are required at three points: (1) entering transport van, (2) exiting transport van, (3) regularly at arrest processing.

Exam Alert: Citywide Events Planning hours: 1000-1800, Mon-Fri. Outside those hours, contact the Operations Unit.

Exam Alert: The Handschu Consent Decree requires written approval from the Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau for political activity investigations.

Sergeant Focus: A sergeant can serve as Incident Commander at a Tier One response. However, any escalation beyond Tier One requires a higher-ranking IC. De-escalation is mandatory before enforcement – this is not optional. Warnings must be given when feasible, and sufficient resources must be assembled before acting.

Sergeant Focus: CAB protest liaisons may NOT wear civilian attire and may NOT be assigned to enforcement duties except in exceptional circumstances. If you see a CAB member in plainclothes at a demonstration, this is a procedure violation.

P.G. 213-06 — Mass Arrest Processing Procedure

Key Points

Activation

  • Mass Arrest Processing Center (MAPC) is activated by the Chief of Department
  • At First Amendment events, only the First Amendment Activity Senior Executive or designee can authorize MAPC activation

Prisoner Group Limits

  • Groups of no more than 20 prisoners per wagon
  • Arresting officers process no more than 5 prisoners each
  • Arrest supervisors assigned no more than 4 arresting officers

Exam Alert: The norm is 4 A/O’s per Sergeant, 5 collars per cop = 1 Sergeant for 20 perps. When expecting MORE than 20 arrests, the Large Scale Arrest Processing procedure (PG 213-06) kicks in.

Processing Time Limit

  • First Amendment Activity arrest processing: 7 hours 30 minutes maximum for violations/misdemeanors

MAPC Required Provisions

The MAPC must provide: - Clean water - Food (if held more than 2 hours) - Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) - Telephone access - Medical care - Restrooms

Prisoner Handling

  • All prisoners must be searched before transport
  • Female prisoners searched by female MOS
  • Juvenile prisoners separated from adults
  • Each prisoner photographed at MAPC
  • Property clerk invoices completed for all property
  • Desk Appearance Tickets (DATs) issued when appropriate per existing guidelines

Documentation

  • Command log maintained at MAPC
  • Arresting officer stays with prisoner group through processing

Incident Commander Duties (Large Scale — PG 213-06)

  • Arrest teams: Have arrests made in groups of no more than 20 perps with their arresting/assigned officers (4 cops & 1 Sergeant)
  • ONE Patrol Wagon to designated Precinct stationhouse
  • Ensure arresting/assigned officers know how to articulate the arrests (do not want Declined Prosecutions)
  • Notify the Desk Officer of how many collars to expect

Arrest Supervisor Duties (Large Scale — PG 213-06)

  • Photograph — Before placing in wagon, have each perp photographed with arresting/assigned officers
  • Document — Ensure MASS ARREST PEDIGREE LABEL affixed to back of photo with captions filled
  • Property — If perp has property, ensure MASS ARREST PEDIGREE LABELS get put on each receptacle
  • Warrant Checks — Ensure that city, state, and federal warrant checks are performed
  • 10-84 at SH with prisoners — Inform Incident Commander/Command Post when 10-84 at stationhouse
    • To verify if prisoners will be D.A.T.’d, summonsed, or processed as online arrest
  • LiveScan — Assign other than A.P.O. to do LiveScan
  • Review & Sign — ALL arrest paperwork

Exam Alert: The Sergeant needs approval from the Incident Commander to D.A.T. (Desk Appearance Ticket) or process under PG 96. The IC also arranges rides to court for keepers (prisoners not eligible for DAT). Photographs are taken BEFORE placing perps in the wagon.

Precinct APO (Arrest Processing Officer)

  • Primary function: Return arresting/assigned officers to patrol
  • Assist with computer functions and ensure paperwork is properly prepared

Precinct Commanding Officer

  • Ensure command has adequate supply of arrest paperwork for arrest processing
  • If anticipating a large-scale arrest situation, send a TELEPHONE MESSAGE to the Borough Court Section Supervisor with all pertinent details including the number of arrestees anticipated
  • Borough Court Section notifies the D.A. and Office of Court Administration to expedite
  • Borough Court Section may set aside a centralized processing area

Exam Alert: For large-scale arrests, the Precinct CO sends a TELEPHONE MESSAGE (not a phone call, not an email) to the Borough Court Section Supervisor. The BCS then notifies the D.A. and may establish a centralized processing area.

Supervisor Responsibilities (Arrest Processing Supervisor)

  • Ensure prisoners are fed (food required if held more than 2 hours)
  • Ensure medical attention is provided when needed
  • Ensure restroom access is available
  • Ensure telephone calls are permitted
  • Verify female prisoners are searched by female MOS
  • Verify juvenile prisoners are separated from adults
  • Monitor processing time – must not exceed 7 hours 30 minutes for First Amendment violations/misdemeanors
  • Ensure command log is maintained
  • Confirm each prisoner is photographed
  • Ensure property clerk invoices are completed for all property

Exam Alert: MAPC is activated by the Chief of Department. At First Amendment events, only the Senior Executive or designee can authorize MAPC.

Exam Alert: Maximum prisoners per wagon: 20. Maximum prisoners per arresting officer: 5. Maximum arresting officers per arrest supervisor: 4.

Exam Alert: First Amendment arrest processing time limit: 7 hours 30 minutes for violations/misdemeanors.

Exam Alert: Food must be provided if a prisoner is held more than 2 hours.

Exam Alert: Female prisoners must be searched by female MOS. Juvenile prisoners must be separated from adults.

Sergeant Focus: The arrest processing supervisor has direct responsibility for prisoner welfare: food, medical care, restrooms, and telephone access. Failure to provide these is a procedure violation. Track the clock – 7 hours 30 minutes is the hard ceiling for First Amendment violation/misdemeanor processing.

P.G. 213-21 — Tiered Response to Policing First Amendment Activities (Source Unavailable)

Note: This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

Known Information

  • Establishes the detailed tiered response framework for First Amendment activities
  • P.G. 213-20 references Tier One Response where a sergeant may serve as Incident Commander
  • The tiered system likely defines escalating levels of response (Tier One, Tier Two, etc.) with corresponding:
    • Incident Commander rank requirements
    • Resource deployment levels
    • Authorization thresholds for enforcement actions
  • Complements P.G. 213-20 by providing the structural framework for scaling the police response

Sergeant Focus: While the full text is unavailable, know that at Tier One a sergeant can be the Incident Commander. Higher tiers require higher-ranking ICs. This tiered approach ensures proportional response to First Amendment events.

Section Summary — First Amendment and Mass Arrest

Procedure Title Status Key Takeaway
213-06 Mass Arrest Processing Full content MAPC activation, prisoner limits, processing time
213-20 Response to First Amendment Activities Full content Definitions, legal observers, arrest authority, de-escalation
213-21 Tiered Response to First Amendment Activities Cross-reference only Tier structure for scaling police response

Mobilization Readiness and Disorder Preparedness

Overview

This section covers the foundational procedures for mobilization, emergency response, and preparing for unusual disorders. Sergeants must understand command post operations, rapid mobilization protocols, readiness levels, and the supplies and plans required to handle large-scale incidents. P.G. 213-09 (Unusual Disorder Kits) is the fully extracted procedure here; the remaining procedures are summarized from cross-references.

P.G. 213-01 — Command Post Operations (Source Unavailable)

Note: This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

Known Information

  • Establishes protocols for setting up and operating a command post during mobilization and emergency incidents
  • Referenced by P.G. 213-09 (Unusual Disorder Kits) as part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series
  • Related to P.G. 213-14 (Command Post Duties), which likely details specific roles within the command post
  • P.G. 213-15 (Special Events) requires a command post established at or near an event, suggesting 213-01 provides the general framework

P.G. 213-02 — Emergency Incidents (Source Unavailable)

Note: This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

Known Information

  • Covers the Department’s response framework for emergency incidents
  • Part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series referenced by P.G. 213-09
  • Likely defines what constitutes an emergency incident and the initial response protocols

P.G. 213-03 — Rapid Mobilization

Note: Full exam-focused content for this procedure is covered in the Rapid Mobilization section. The summary below highlights the most critical points.

Known Information

  • Establishes procedures for rapidly assembling personnel during emergencies
  • Part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series referenced by P.G. 213-09
  • Related to P.G. 213-04 (Mobilization Readiness Levels), which likely defines the escalation tiers that trigger rapid mobilization
  • Patrol Supervisor calls Level 1 through radio; for missing persons, must consult CO/Duty Captain PRIOR
  • Platoon Commander serves as Incident Commander until CO/Duty Captain arrives, then becomes Operations Officer at Field Command Post
  • Supplementary Restraint Kits contain 300 flex cuffs, 40 flex cuff cutters, 40 Cold Fire extinguishers

PG Conflict: PG 213-03 says Lt/Sgt should go direct to the mobilization point. PG 213-05 says Lt/Sgt should 10-02 FIRST, then go. On test day, if the question references 213-05 (Duties at a Disorder), the answer is 10-02 First.

See Also: Rapid Mobilization section — Full procedure with role-by-role duties, bureau exceptions, and supplementary restraint kit details.

P.G. 213-04 — Mobilization Readiness Levels (Source Unavailable)

Note: This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

Known Information

  • Defines the various readiness levels used during mobilization
  • Part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series referenced by P.G. 213-09
  • Complements P.G. 213-03 (Rapid Mobilization) by establishing the tiered alert system

P.G. 213-05 — Duties at a Disorder

Note: Full exam-focused content for this procedure is covered in the Duties at a Disorder section. The summary below highlights the most critical points.

Known Information

  • Outlines specific duties of members of service responding to unusual disorders
  • Part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series referenced by P.G. 213-09
  • Supported by P.G. 213-07 (Unusual Disorder Maps), P.G. 213-08 (Unusual Disorder Plan), and P.G. 213-09 (Unusual Disorder Kits)
  • Deployment ratio: 2/3 deployed, 1/3 in reserve; foot posts at 1 Sgt & 8 P.O.s
  • Dual Dispatch System: HIGH priority to detail officers, LOW priority to precinct officers
  • Ring schedule: every 30 minutes for enforcement units, every 60 minutes for other units
  • Two types of mobilizations: Levels 1 & 2 = SRG; Levels 3 & 4 = PCT/TD/PSA
  • Prohibited tactics: complacency, breaking formation, unnecessary conversation, congregating

Exam Alert: Sergeants respond to the stationhouse (10-02) first under PG 213-05, form a squad of 8 officers, and report to the mobilization point in a van. While enroute, only the Sergeant and van operator have radios turned on.

See Also: Duties at a Disorder section — Full procedure with deployment plans, team assignments, lieutenant/sergeant duties, and mobilization level details.

P.G. 213-07 — Unusual Disorder Maps (Source Unavailable)

Note: This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

Known Information

  • Covers the preparation and maintenance of maps used during unusual disorder responses
  • Part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series referenced by P.G. 213-09
  • P.G. 213-09 requires 75 sector maps in each Unusual Disorder Kit, suggesting these maps follow a standardized format defined in 213-07

P.G. 213-08 — Unusual Disorder Plan (Source Unavailable)

Note: This procedure could not be cleanly extracted from the source PDF. The title and related information below are derived from cross-references in other procedures.

Known Information

  • Establishes the overall plan for responding to unusual disorders at the command level
  • Part of the 213-01 through 213-08 series referenced by P.G. 213-09
  • Likely integrates P.G. 213-05 (Duties), P.G. 213-07 (Maps), and P.G. 213-09 (Kits) into a cohesive response framework

P.G. 213-09 — Unusual Disorder Kits

Key Points

  • Each command’s Commanding Officer is responsible for maintaining an Unusual Disorder Kit
  • Kit must contain the following supplies:
    • 75 sector maps
    • 200 index cards
    • 200 Complaint Report Worksheets
    • 200 Aided Report Worksheets
    • 200 Detail Roster sheets
    • 300 tags
    • Staplers, tape, markers, batteries, flashlights
  • Kit must be stored in an accessible location known to all supervisors
  • References procedures P.G. 213-01 through 213-08

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Ensure kit location is known to all supervisors in the command
  • Verify kit contents during semiannual inspections
  • Confirm kit is readily accessible and not locked away in an unknown location
  • Be prepared to deploy kit contents during mobilization or unusual disorder

Exam Alert: Semiannual inspection of the Unusual Disorder Kit is required in January and July.

Exam Alert: The kit must contain 75 sector maps, 200 each of index cards/Complaint Report Worksheets/Aided Report Worksheets/Detail Roster sheets, and 300 tags.

Sergeant Focus: All supervisors must know the kit’s location. If you are newly assigned to a command, learn the kit location immediately. Ensure inspections occur on schedule in January and July.

Section Summary — Mobilization Readiness

Procedure Title Status Key Takeaway
213-01 Command Post Operations Cross-reference only Framework for command post setup
213-02 Emergency Incidents Cross-reference only Emergency response protocols
213-03 Rapid Mobilization Full content (see Rapid Mobilization section) Role-by-role duties, bureau exceptions, restraint kits
213-04 Mobilization Readiness Levels Cross-reference only Tiered alert system
213-05 Duties at a Disorder Full content (see Duties at a Disorder section) Deployment, team assignments, mobilization levels
213-07 Unusual Disorder Maps Cross-reference only Map preparation and use
213-08 Unusual Disorder Plan Cross-reference only Overall disorder response plan
213-09 Unusual Disorder Kits Full content Kit contents, storage, inspections

Off-Duty Mobilization — P.G. 213-10

Overview

P.G. 213-10 covers the mobilization of off-duty members, both uniformed and civilian. It establishes primary and alternate mobilization points, defines who responds, and outlines the specific duties of the CO/Ranking Member at the permanent command and at alternate mobilization points.
