Integrity Issues (Additional Data)

  • Improper parking permit use: command concerned handles complaints (tow, 96, etc.) — give disposition (location, plate#, agency of plate, summons #, violation, etc.) to Traffic Management Center
  • Authorized MOS: sign on and sign off when unable to safeguard, even if for a brief period
  • MOS conducting oversight, analysis, investigation should use Dept computer system as appropriate

Administrative

  • Improperly routed requests: reassign via the 311 system
  • Computer issues: notify ITB Service Desk 24/7
  • System failures or program malfunctions: referred to NYC DoITT Citywide Service Desk
  • Document notifications in the Telephone Record

Training Sergeant

  • Conduct command level training on QOL matters, including instruction on operation of the 311 system

ICO

  • Randomly inspect for proper dispositions, compliance with reporting requirements, and system integrity
  • Investigate derelictions of duty or other deficiencies

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Second Platoon Desk Officer: review 311 system at 0800 daily
  • Desk officer: refresh 311 system at least every hour
  • Command Log: entries at beginning and end of each tour
  • Special Ops Lt: monthly audit, weekly chronic conditions update, review deferred requests, maintain deferred list by address
  • CO/XO: review QOL strategies, directly direct NCO Sgt for daily callbacks (min 2 attempts)
  • NCO Sgt: daily callbacks, create/update chronic conditions list weekly

Exam Alert: CHRONIC condition = 5+ requests in 28 days. Immediate response trigger = 5+ calls in 8 hours. Second Platoon Desk Officer reviews at 0800 daily. Desk officer refreshes at least every hour. Special Ops Lt: monthly audit, weekly chronic conditions update. CO directly directs NCO Sgt for daily callbacks with minimum 2 attempts per caller. Deferrals are recorded in 311 system or the Telephone Record (NOT the Telephone Dispatch Log). When referring to another city agency, call 311 directly and obtain a new service request number.

Sergeant Focus: Multiple supervisory layers with different frequencies: hourly (desk officer refresh), daily (0800 review, callbacks), weekly (chronic conditions list), monthly (311 caller audit). The 10-42 code is used to notify Communications Section with location and type of job. Disposition 10-95 for referrals to other agencies. The CO breaks the chain of command by personally directing the NCO Sgt. Give special attention to 311 assignments with media files (photos).

Vehicles and Snow — Emergency Regulations, Snow Removal, Derelict Vehicles, Expired Plates, and Parkway Removal

Overview

These five procedures cover vehicle-related enforcement and snow emergency operations. Sergeants must know the specific timeframes for snow removal summonses, the process for handling derelict vehicles and expired registration plates, and the authority for removing vehicles from parkways and highways. Pay close attention to the 5-day timelines and the role of the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit.

P.G. 214-26 — Snow Emergency Regulations

Key Points

  • Triggered by a FINEST message from the DOT Commissioner
  • Summonses issued for:
    • Traffic Reg 4-12(k)(1): Abandoning or parking illegally on a snow street
    • Traffic Reg 4-12(k)(2): Operating without snow tires or chains and becoming stuck
  • If a vehicle is not immobilized: warn the operator and direct them to leave the snow street at the nearest exit

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Monitor for FINEST messages activating snow emergency regulations
  • Ensure MOS issue summonses for violations on designated snow streets
  • Direct MOS to warn and redirect non-immobilized vehicles rather than immediately summonsing

Exam Alert: Snow emergencies are triggered by a FINEST message from the DOT Commissioner. Vehicles not immobilized receive a warning and are directed to leave at the nearest exit — not an automatic summons.

Sergeant Focus: The activation mechanism is the FINEST message. For vehicles that can still move, the response is a warning and direction to leave, not immediate enforcement.

P.G. 214-27 — Snow Removal

Key Points

  • Summons issued for Admin Code 16-123 if snow is not removed four hours after snow stops falling
  • MOS must prevent the throwing of snow into cleared roadways

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Ensure MOS understand the 4-hour threshold before summons issuance
  • Monitor for snow being thrown into cleared roadways

Exam Alert: The snow removal deadline is four hours after snow stops falling (Admin Code 16-123). Throwing snow into cleared roadways is also a violation.

P.G. 214-29 — Derelict Vehicles with Registration Plates

Key Points

  • Derelict = so dilapidated, burned, or stripped as to have no value except scrap
  • Derelict vehicles are only taken into custody when required as evidence
  • The Department of Sanitation removes all derelict vehicles not required as evidence
  • Notify the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit in ALL cases

Registration Plate Removal

Plate Type Form
New York plates REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL NOTICE (PD571-120)
Out-of-state plates EXPIRED REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL PROGRAM (PD571-124)

Owner Notification and Timeline

  • Owner notified to pick up plates within 5 days
  • If unclaimed after 5 days: forward plates and typed letterhead to DMV
  • Desk officer: assign a check 5 days later, then notify Department of Sanitation if the vehicle is still present

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Ensure the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit is notified in all cases
  • Desk officer assigns the 5-day follow-up check
  • Desk officer notifies Department of Sanitation if the vehicle remains after the follow-up

Exam Alert: Notify the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit in ALL derelict vehicle cases. Owner has 5 days to pick up plates. NY plates use PD571-120; out-of-state plates use PD571-124. Department of Sanitation (not police) removes derelict vehicles.

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer must assign the 5-day follow-up check and then contact the Department of Sanitation if the vehicle remains. Derelict vehicles are only taken into police custody when needed as evidence.

P.G. 214-30 — Removal of Expired Registration Plates

Key Points

  • Applies to plates expired more than 2 months
  • Issue a summons for unregistered vehicle
  • Remove plates and follow the same process as P.G. 214-29
  • Occupied vehicle with expired plates: remove to stationhouse, comply with P.G. 218-12

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Ensure MOS verify plates are expired more than 2 months before taking action
  • Direct proper handling of occupied vehicles with expired plates per P.G. 218-12

Exam Alert: The threshold is plates expired more than 2 months. Occupied vehicles with expired plates are removed to the stationhouse under P.G. 218-12. The plate removal process follows the same steps as P.G. 214-29.

Sergeant Focus: The 2-month expiration threshold is the trigger. Know the distinction between unoccupied vehicles (remove plates on scene) and occupied vehicles (remove to stationhouse per P.G. 218-12).

P.G. 214-31 — Removal of Vehicles from Parkways/Highways/Expressways

Key Points

  • Admin Code 20-511(q) requires removal of vehicles from highways
  • Advise the owner that the tow car operator is entitled to charge towing and storage fees
  • Owner must appear at the precinct with the registration certificate for a written release
  • MOS signs an Authorization to Tow if the owner refuses or the vehicle is unattended
  • Notify the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit immediately

Highway District Vehicles

  • Vehicles with protective bumper guards may push disabled vehicles to the nearest safe parking area
  • May NOT push vehicles to repair shops or private property

Supervisor Responsibilities

  • Ensure the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit is notified immediately
  • Verify the owner is advised of towing/storage fee liability
  • Confirm MOS sign the Authorization to Tow when the owner refuses or is absent

Exam Alert: The owner must present the registration certificate at the precinct for a written release. Highway District vehicles with protective bumper guards may push to the nearest safe parking — NOT to repair shops or private property. Notify the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit immediately.

Sergeant Focus: Two key notifications: the owner about towing fees, and the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit about the vehicle. Highway vehicles can push to safe parking only — never to repair shops or private property.

Key Terms — Section 214: Quality of Life Matters

Definitions

Term Definition
C.U.R.E. Coordinating a United Resolution with Establishments — multi-agency operation targeting problem commercial establishments
M.E.N.D. Program Free mediation offered by the Mayor’s Office of Nightlife as an alternative to C.U.R.E. operations
Chronic 311 Condition 5 or more 311 requests in a 28-day period for the same condition at the same location
Chronic Abuser Alarm Location with 3 or more unnecessary/unfounded alarms in a 3-month period
Dangerous Animal An animal that menaces, threatens, attacks, or bites a person
Derelict Vehicle A vehicle so dilapidated, burned, or stripped as to have no value except scrap
Door Service Method of serving alarm notice by placing in mailbox or under door when no qualified person is available
Deferred Service Method where CPO serves alarm notice the next business day when service cannot be made at the scene
Qualified Person (alarm service) Owner, corporate officer, manager, supervisor, or adult employee/resident
Unlawful Eviction Eviction through force, denial of essential services, removing possessions, changing locks, or interfering with comfort
Access Warrant Court Order signed by a Supreme Court judge allowing DOB to inspect premises
OMAP Designated liaison between NYPD and NYC 311
CVTF Citywide Vandals Task Force — handles graffiti vandal debriefing
ACC Animal Care and Control of NYC — responds to animal complaints
ICAD Number Identifier entered on Aided and Dangerous Animal/Bite Report
LBB Legal Bureau Bulletin — supplemental legal guidance that modifies or clarifies PG procedures
Dwelling Unit Any building or portion used as a home, residence, or sleeping place (includes private hotels with gov’t funding; excludes hospitals, convents, NYCHA)
Legitimate Occupancy Tenant has a lease, occupied 30+ consecutive days, SRO requesting lease (verbal is sufficient), or sublet tenant
Stay of Eviction Court order to halt eviction — unenforceable once the eviction is already complete
72-Hour Notice of Service Required notice before executing Warrant of Eviction; starts day after service, excludes weekends/holidays; NOT required for Repossession Orders
Telephone Record Document where 311 deferrals and notifications are recorded (not the Telephone Dispatch Log)
Chronic 311 Condition 5+ service requests in 28 days for the same specified condition at the same location
NCO Sergeant (311) Directed by CO to make daily callbacks to identified 311 callers; maintains 28-day chronic conditions list

Critical Numbers

Item Value
Graffiti reward maximum $500
Chronic abuser alarm trigger 3+ alarms in 3 months
Alarm reinstatement delay Minimum 48 hours
Vehicle alarm observation period 3 minutes
Vehicle alarm auto-deactivation 10 minutes
Building alarm summons threshold 15 minutes continuous
OATH vehicle alarm mail-in fine $280
OATH vehicle alarm maximum fine $2,100
Unlawful eviction occupancy threshold 30+ consecutive days
Unlawful eviction summons return date minimum 21 days out
TLC follow-up padlocking 10 days after initial sealing
Snow removal summons threshold 4 hours after snow stops
Expired registration plate threshold More than 2 months expired
Derelict vehicle plate pickup deadline 5 days
Narcotics evidence retention (eviction) Minimum 3 months
C.U.R.E. NOTICE lead time At least 30 days before operation
C.U.R.E. CO determination deadline Within 30 days of notice
Chronic 311 condition 5+ requests in 28 days
311 immediate response trigger 5+ calls in 8 hours
311 desk officer refresh interval At least every hour
311 daily review time 0800 by Second Platoon Desk Officer
311 callback attempts Minimum 2 per caller
311 Special Ops Lt audit frequency Monthly
311 chronic conditions list update Weekly
Warrant of Eviction execution hours Mon-Fri 0800-1700
Search Warrant execution hours Mon-Fri 0600-2100
72-hour Notice of Service Starts day after service, excludes Sat/Sun/Holidays
ACC Brooklyn Control Center 24/7
ACC Rescue Units Mon-Fri 0800-2000
ACC Other Control Centers Tues-Sat (business hours)

Key Forms

Form Number Used For
Complaint Report PD313-152 Narcotics eviction documentation
Notice of Unnecessary Alarm PD310-120 Chronic abuser alarm notification
Termination Notice (alarm) PD310-121 Terminating police alarm response
Registration Plate Removal Notice (NY) PD571-120 Derelict/expired NY plates
Expired Registration Plate Removal (out-of-state) PD571-124 Derelict/expired out-of-state plates
Dangerous Animal/Bite Report PD311-152 Every animal incident
C.U.R.E. Notice PD635-121 Notification to problem establishments
Referral to Mediation PD613-181 Non-criminal dispute referral
Notification to Owner MISC. 3930 Commercial premises secured by ESU

Key Radio Codes

Code Meaning
10-11 Alarm Condition
10-42 Notify Communications Section (311 receipt)
10-90N Notice Served (alarm)
10-95 Refer to Another Agency (311 disposition)

Key Legal Bureau Bulletins

LBB Topic Key Point
Vol 49 #1 (Aug 2019) Unlawful Evictions Adds Building/Health Code violations as eviction method; acts must be done for PURPOSE of eviction; police cannot break in to let tenant re-enter unless medicine vital to life is inside
Vol 14 #8 Warrant of Eviction 72-hour Notice of Service required; starts day after service excluding weekends/holidays; no 72-hour notice for Repossession Orders; Order to Seize Property does not authorize forced entry unless explicitly stated

Summons Return Dates — Unlawful Eviction

Borough Day
Manhattan/Bronx Tuesday
Brooklyn/Queens Thursday
Staten Island First Wednesday of the month

Review Questions — Section 214: Quality of Life Matters

Instructions: Answer each question, then expand the details to check your answer.

Question 1

Under the Chronic Abuser Alarm Procedures (P.G. 214-08), what triggers a location being classified as a chronic abuser, and what is the minimum delay before police response can be reinstated after termination?

Answer

A location is classified as a chronic abuser when 3 or more alarms in a 3-month period are unnecessary or unfounded. After a TERMINATION NOTICE (PD310-121) is served, the minimum delay before reinstatement takes effect is 48 hours. The CO may also require an alarm system or premises security survey before reinstatement. (P.G. 214-08)

Question 2

A vehicle alarm has been sounding continuously. At what point can MOS take action, and what is the time of violation recorded on the OATH summons?

Answer

MOS can take action after the vehicle alarm has been audible for 3 minutes. The time of violation recorded on the OATH summons is the end of the 3-minute observation period. The mail-in fine is $280 and the maximum penalty is $2,100. If the alarm resets and sounds again, it is considered one continuous action — only one summons is issued. (P.G. 214-24)

Question 3

What is the critical distinction between MOS authority to deactivate vehicle alarms versus building alarms?

Answer

MOS may attempt to deactivate vehicle alarms (within the first 10 minutes, without intentionally damaging the vehicle). However, MOS are NOT permitted to deactivate building alarms. For building alarms that continue for 15 or more minutes, MOS may issue a summons. ESU should be requested if MOS have difficulty accessing the engine or passenger compartment of a vehicle. (P.G. 214-25)

Question 4

A tenant claims they were unlawfully evicted. They have no lease but have lived in the unit for five weeks. The person who changed the locks is present but refuses to let the tenant back in. What actions should the sergeant direct?

Answer

The law applies because the tenant occupied the unit for 30 or more consecutive days (5 weeks exceeds 30 days), even without a lease. Since the violator is present but refuses to permit reentry, the sergeant should direct an arrest. The subject must be brought to criminal court for prompt arraignment — NO DAT is permitted. Unlawful eviction is a Class A misdemeanor but is NOT fingerprintable. The sergeant should also confirm there is no valid Warrant of Eviction or Government Order to Vacate. (P.G. 214-12)

Question 5

What are the summons return dates for unlawful eviction cases by borough, and what is the minimum lead time?

Answer

All return dates must be at least 21 days out: - Manhattan/Bronx: Tuesday - Brooklyn/Queens: Thursday - Staten Island: First Wednesday of the month

(P.G. 214-12)

Question 6

A dog has bitten a pedestrian but has since fled the scene. The animal is no longer present. What documentation and notifications are required?

Answer

The DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152) must be prepared in every instance, even if the animal is gone. The desk officer must notify the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone because the animal bit a human. If the offender (owner) is identified, process for DAT if eligible. The animal is never placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle. (P.G. 214-33)

Question 7

Under P.G. 214-33, when is a member of the service authorized to use a firearm against a dangerous animal?

Answer

A firearm may be used ONLY to protect from imminent physical injury when there is no opportunity to retreat. General use of force against animals is limited to situations necessary to prevent harm or injury. (P.G. 214-33)

Question 8

What defines a Chronic 311 Condition, and what triggers an immediate response? Describe the different supervisory review frequencies.

Answer
  • Chronic 311 Condition: 5 or more requests in a 28-day period for the same condition at the same location
  • Immediate response trigger: 5 or more calls within an 8-hour period

Supervisory review frequencies: - Hourly: Desk officer manually refreshes the 311 system at least every hour - Daily: Second Platoon Desk Officer reviews at 0800 for the previous 24 hours; CO directs daily callbacks (minimum 2 attempts per caller) - Weekly: Special Ops Lt updates the 28-day chronic conditions list - Monthly: Special Ops Lt conducts an audit of 311 callers

Command Log entries are required at the beginning and end of each tour with the number of pending assignments. (P.G. 214-35)

Question 9

A C.U.R.E. operation is being planned against a nightclub. The nightclub owner’s only issue is refusing to attend community nightlife meetings. Is this sufficient grounds for the operation?

Answer

No. Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is specifically listed as NOT sufficient grounds for a C.U.R.E. operation. Other insufficient grounds include: property crimes beyond the establishment’s control, and crimes where security acted properly. Valid grounds include complaint reports, summonses, arrests, 911/311 calls, general disorder, aided cases from intoxication/overdose, and a documented pattern of problems. (P.G. 214-39)

Question 10

What is the required lead time for a C.U.R.E. NOTICE, and what are the three possible outcomes after the notice period?

Answer

The C.U.R.E. NOTICE (PD635-121) must be served at least 30 days prior to the operation. Prior notice is NOT required when it would compromise an active investigation or pose a health/safety risk.

Within 30 days of the notice, the CO determines one of three outcomes: 1. Conduct the operation 2. Additional monitoring 3. No further action

The Chief of Patrol must approve or disapprove through written endorsement. The operation is coordinated by the Legal Bureau’s Civil Enforcement Unit and overseen by the Mayor’s Office of Nightlife. (P.G. 214-39)

Question 11

During an eviction executed by a city marshal, the marshal asks MOS to help break down a door. Property is being placed on the sidewalk. What should the sergeant direct?

Answer

The sergeant should direct that: 1. MOS will NOT assist in breaking the door or damaging property — the police role is to preserve peace and protect life/property ONLY 2. The marshal/sheriff or landlord is NOT permitted to place property on the sidewalk — a vehicle must be on scene and property loaded immediately 3. Execution of the warrant is the sole responsibility of the city marshal/sheriff 4. The marshal must force entry in the least disruptive way

The sergeant should ensure the Activity Log records: marshal’s name/shield, time of entry, location, tenant name, and who is responsible for property. (P.G. 214-13)

Question 12

MOS discover a derelict vehicle with New York plates. It is not needed as evidence. What is the step-by-step process, and what are the key timelines?

Answer
  1. Remove the registration plates
  2. Prepare the REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL NOTICE (PD571-120) (for NY plates)
  3. Notify the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit (required in ALL cases)
  4. Notify the owner to pick up plates within 5 days
  5. If unclaimed after 5 days: forward plates and typed letterhead to DMV
  6. Desk officer assigns a check 5 days later to verify if the vehicle is still present
  7. If still there: notify the Department of Sanitation for removal

The vehicle is NOT taken into police custody because it is not needed as evidence. The Department of Sanitation removes all derelict vehicles not required as evidence. (P.G. 214-29)

Question 13

MOS encounter potentially obscene material at a storefront. What is the correct chain of action, and what legal distinction must the supervisor understand?

Answer

MOS must NOT take enforcement action. They must immediately notify the patrol supervisor. The patrol supervisor must: 1. Respond to the scene 2. Determine if the material meets legal criteria 3. Make an Activity Log entry 4. Consult the Legal Bureau

No enforcement is permitted without specific direction from the Legal Bureau.

The critical legal distinction: - PL Article 235 (Obscenity): Requires a judicial determination that the item is obscene — no summary enforcement - PL 245.11 (Public Display of Offensive Sexual Material): A Class A misdemeanor — summary enforcement is allowed under appropriate circumstances

(P.G. 214-11)

Question 14

What are the eligibility requirements for the Mediation Referral Program, and what types of disputes are NOT eligible?

Answer

Eligibility requirements: - No threat of violence - No criminal allegation - No Order of Protection between the parties - Parties are not family or intimate partners - Likely an ongoing dispute

NOT eligible: - Criminal conduct - Unlawful evictions (handled under P.G. 214-12) - Intimate partners or family members - Child abuse or neglect - Parties with an Order of Protection between them

The program uses the REFERRAL TO MEDIATION (PD613-181) with a serial number in YYYY-XXX-NNNN format. It must NOT be used in lieu of enforcement action — only when there is no basis to take enforcement action. The Special Ops Lt conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation file. (P.G. 214-40)

Question 15

A DOB inspector arrives at the precinct with an Access Warrant to inspect a commercial building. What must the desk officer verify, and what are the limits on MOS authority during the inspection?

Answer

The desk officer must verify: 1. The warrant is signed by a Supreme Court judge 2. The warrant is not expired

Limits on MOS authority: - MOS role is to preserve peace and prevent crime ONLY - MOS will NOT assist in breaking a door or damaging property - If access is not granted by the occupant, DOB reschedules — this is not a police responsibility

The same principle applies to other civil enforcement (P.G. 214-13, 214-18): police preserve peace but do not participate in the execution of civil process. (P.G. 214-37)

Question 16

A landlord has cut off hot water to a tenant who has lived in the apartment for two months without a lease. The tenant calls police claiming unlawful eviction. Is this an unlawful eviction?

Answer

No. While the tenant has legitimate occupancy (30+ consecutive days), cutting off hot water alone is NOT an unlawful eviction. The exam has tested this directly. Essential services that qualify as unlawful eviction when denied include heat, water, and electricity — but hot water by itself does not meet the threshold. The acts must also be done for the purpose of eviction (per LBB Vol 49 #1). (P.G. 214-12)

Question 17

A city marshal arrives to execute a Warrant of Eviction at 1830 hours on a Wednesday. The tenant has a Stay of Eviction. The eviction was already completed earlier that day. What should the sergeant consider?

Answer

Two key issues:

  1. Time: Warrants of Eviction may only be executed Monday-Friday 0800-1700. At 1830 hours, the execution window has passed. (Do NOT confuse with Search Warrant hours of 0600-2100.)

  2. Stay of Eviction: Once the eviction is complete, a Stay of Eviction is unenforceable. Attempts to pay rent or produce a stay does NOT allow re-entry after the eviction is complete.

Additionally, per LBB Vol 49 #1, police cannot break in to let the tenant re-enter UNLESS there is medicine inside that is vital to life. (P.G. 214-13)

Question 18

During a 311 assignment review, you notice a condition has been deferred. Where should the deferral be indicated — in the Telephone Dispatch Log, the Telephone Record, or the 311 system?

Answer

Deferrals should be indicated in the 311 system or the Telephone Record, as appropriate. The deferred assignment gets an interim disposition of Deferred or Open. Do NOT confuse the Telephone Record with the Telephone Dispatch Log — the exam specifically tests this distinction. Notifications are documented in the Telephone Record. (P.G. 214-35)

Question 19

Under P.G. 214-33, ESU is called to capture a dangerous dog. How is the animal transported, and what specific unit should be contacted if a search warrant is planned at a location known to have a dangerous dog?

Answer

Transport: ESU bags the animal and then an RMP transports the animal to an ACC facility. The kennel is placed in the backseat of the RMP (rear seat may be removed if necessary). An animal is NEVER placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle.

Search warrant: If there is advance information of a dog at a warrant location, confer with the Canine Unit of ESU — not just any ESU team. (P.G. 214-33)

Question 20

Who directs the NCO Sergeant to make daily callbacks to identified 311 callers — the Special Ops Lt or the CO? How many attempts are required per caller?

Answer

The CO personally directs the NCO Sergeant to make daily callbacks to identified 311 callers. This breaks the normal chain of command — it sounds like a Special Ops Lt responsibility, but the PG specifically assigns this to the CO. A minimum of 2 attempts per caller is required, and all contacts and attempts must be documented. The NCO Sgt then interviews callers to determine if assignments were resolved or need further action, and confers with the SOL. (P.G. 214-35)

Question 21

A complainant in an unlawful eviction case refuses to sign the summons. Can the UMOS still proceed? What modifications are needed when a civilian is the complainant?

Answer

Yes. If the complainant refuses to sign, the UMOS can sign based on information and belief — but only if the information is complete.

When a civilian is the complainant, the following modifications are required on the summons: - Cross out Complainant’s and Complainant and substitute Officer’s and Officer on the front - Cross out I personally observed… above the complainant’s name - On the rear of the original summons, enter the complainant’s full name, rank/full signature, and Date Affirmed under the appropriate sections - Complainant’s name, address, and phone number go on the bottom

(P.G. 214-12)

Question 22

When referring a 311 condition to another city agency, what is the correct procedure? Can the referral be made anonymously?

Answer

The referral cannot be made anonymously. The correct procedure is:

  1. Call 311 directly
  2. Provide: member’s name, Dept email, and smartphone number (or TS phone number)
  3. Obtain a new Service Request number
  4. Make the original request disposition 10-95 (Refer to Other Agency)
  5. Put the new service request number in Notes to Customer before finalizing the old request

(P.G. 214-35)

Question 23

A location has had 3 unnecessary alarms in a 3-month period. What action is taken, and when can police response be reinstated?

Answer

The location is classified as a chronic abuser. A TERMINATION NOTICE (PD310-121) is served. Police response can be reinstated after a minimum of 48 hours from service of the notice. The CO may also require an alarm system or premises security survey before reinstatement. (P.G. 214-08)

Question 24

How long must a vehicle alarm sound before MOS can take enforcement action, and how is the violation time recorded?

Answer

MOS can take action after the vehicle alarm has been audible for 3 minutes. The time of violation recorded on the OATH summons is the end of the 3-minute observation period. The mail-in fine is $280 and the maximum penalty is $2,100. (P.G. 214-24)

Question 25

Can MOS deactivate a building alarm that has been sounding for 20 minutes?

Answer

No. MOS are NOT permitted to deactivate building alarms. For building alarms that continue for 15 or more minutes, MOS may issue a summons. However, MOS may attempt to deactivate vehicle alarms (within the first 10 minutes, without intentionally damaging the vehicle). (P.G. 214-25)

Question 26

A person has lived in an apartment for 25 days without a lease. The landlord changes the locks while they are at work. Is this an unlawful eviction under P.G. 214-12?

Answer

No. The law applies to tenants who have occupied the unit for 30 or more consecutive days. At 25 days, the person does not meet the occupancy threshold, even without a lease. This would be a civil matter, not an unlawful eviction under the Patrol Guide. (P.G. 214-12)

Question 27

What are the return date requirements for unlawful eviction summonses?

Answer

All return dates must be at least 21 days out: - Manhattan/Bronx: Tuesday - Brooklyn/Queens: Thursday - Staten Island: First Wednesday of the month

(P.G. 214-12)

Question 28

A dog bites a postal carrier and runs into the residence. The owner refuses entry. What actions are authorized?

Answer

The DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152) must be prepared. The desk officer must notify the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone. If the animal poses an imminent physical injury threat and there is no opportunity to retreat, a firearm may be used. Otherwise, ESU should be requested to capture the animal. The animal is NEVER placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle. (P.G. 214-33)

Question 29

What defines a Chronic 311 Condition and what triggers an immediate response?

Answer
  • Chronic 311 Condition: 5 or more requests in a 28-day period for the same condition at the same location
  • Immediate response trigger: 5 or more calls within an 8-hour period

When an immediate response is triggered, the desk officer must manually refresh the 311 system at least every hour, and the CO directs daily callbacks (minimum 2 attempts per caller). (P.G. 214-35)

Question 30

A nightclub has refused to attend community nightlife meetings. Is this sufficient grounds for a C.U.R.E. operation?

Answer

No. Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is specifically listed as NOT sufficient grounds for a C.U.R.E. operation. Other insufficient grounds include: property crimes beyond the establishment’s control, and crimes where security acted properly. Valid grounds include complaint reports, summonses, arrests, 911/311 calls, general disorder, aided cases from intoxication/overdose, and a documented pattern of problems. (P.G. 214-39)

Question 31

What is the required lead time for serving a C.U.R.E. NOTICE, and who must approve the operation?

Answer

The C.U.R.E. NOTICE (PD635-121) must be served at least 30 days prior to the operation. The Chief of Patrol must approve or disapprove through written endorsement. The operation is coordinated by the Legal Bureau’s Civil Enforcement Unit and overseen by the Mayor’s Office of Nightlife. (P.G. 214-39)

Question 32

During a lawful eviction, the city marshal asks MOS to help break down the door. What should the sergeant direct?

Answer

The sergeant should direct that MOS will NOT assist in breaking the door or damaging property. The police role is to preserve peace and protect life/property ONLY. Execution of the warrant is the sole responsibility of the city marshal/sheriff. The marshal must force entry in the least disruptive way. (P.G. 214-13)

Question 33

A derelict vehicle with NY plates is found. It is not needed as evidence. What is the process for plate removal?

Answer
  1. Remove the registration plates
  2. Prepare the REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL NOTICE (PD571-120)
  3. Notify the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit (required in ALL cases)
  4. Notify the owner to pick up plates within 5 days
  5. If unclaimed after 5 days: forward plates and typed letterhead to DMV
  6. Desk officer assigns a check 5 days later to verify if vehicle is still present
  7. If still there: notify the Department of Sanitation for removal

(P.G. 214-29)

Question 34

MOS discover potentially obscene material at a bookstore. What is the correct chain of action?

Answer

MOS must NOT take enforcement action. They must immediately notify the patrol supervisor. The patrol supervisor must: 1. Respond to the scene 2. Determine if the material meets legal criteria 3. Make an Activity Log entry 4. Consult the Legal Bureau

No enforcement is permitted without specific direction from the Legal Bureau. The critical distinction is between PL Article 235 (requires judicial determination) and PL 245.11 (Class A misdemeanor allowing summary enforcement). (P.G. 214-11)

Question 35

What types of disputes are NOT eligible for the Mediation Referral Program?

Answer

NOT eligible: - Criminal conduct - Unlawful evictions - Intimate partners or family members - Child abuse or neglect - Parties with an Order of Protection between them - Any dispute involving threat of violence or criminal allegations

The program uses REFERRAL TO MEDIATION (PD613-181) with serial number in YYYY-XXX-NNNN format. The Special Ops Lt conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation file. (P.G. 214-40)

Section 215 — Juvenile Matters

Chapter Overview

Section 215 of the NYPD Patrol Guide covers procedures related to juveniles, including lost and runaway children, juvenile delinquency and arrest processing, school-related incidents, child protection and advocacy, and child safety. These procedures are among the most exam-critical for the Sergeant promotion exam due to the complexity of age thresholds, Miranda requirements, notification chains, and supervisor responsibilities.

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 215
  • Source PDF: public-pguide3.pdf
  • Priority: High

Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

  1. Distinguish between Juvenile Delinquent, Juvenile Offender, and Adolescent Offender by age and offense type
  2. Explain the Miranda warning requirements unique to juvenile interrogations, including the role of parents/guardians
  3. Identify the correct notification sequence for lost children, runaway children, and child abuse cases
  4. Describe supervisor responsibilities during juvenile arrests, school-related incidents, and ACS coordination
  5. Recall critical age thresholds (under 11, under 16, under 18) and their procedural significance
  6. Articulate school arrest protocols including School Safety Division notification and on-campus vs. off-campus enforcement
  7. Explain ACS Child Protection Specialist assistance procedures and the 10-68A radio code
  8. State child car seat requirements by age group

Procedure Index

Procedure Title Coverage
P.G. 215-01 Children/Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter covered in section files
P.G. 215-02 Lost Child covered in section-215-lost-runaway-children.md
P.G. 215-03 Emergency Removals covered in section-215-child-protection.md
P.G. 215-04 Family Court Warrant/Remand covered in section files
P.G. 215-05 Runaway Children covered in section-215-lost-runaway-children.md
P.G. 215-07 Truants covered in section-215-school-related.md
P.G. 215-08 On-Line Juvenile Report covered in section files
P.G. 215-09 Offense by Juvenile Under 18 covered in section-215-juvenile-delinquency.md
P.G. 215-10 Arrest of JO/AO covered in section-215-juvenile-delinquency.md
P.G. 215-11 Arrest in Licensed Premises covered in section-215-juvenile-delinquency.md
P.G. 215-12 Personal Recognizance covered in section-215-juvenile-delinquency.md
P.G. 215-13 Handcuffing in Schools covered in section-215-school-related.md
P.G. 215-14 Child Advocacy Referrals covered in section-215-child-protection.md
P.G. 215-16 Reporting School Incidents covered in section-215-school-related.md
P.G. 215-17 Police Actions at Schools covered in section-215-school-related.md
P.G. 215-18 Search Protocols SSA covered in section-215-school-related.md
P.G. 215-22 ACS CPS Assistance covered in section-215-child-protection.md
P.G. 215-23 JO/AO Weekend Processing covered in section-215-juvenile-delinquency.md
P.G. 215-27 School-Related Threats covered in section-215-school-related.md
P.G. 215-28 Child Car Seats covered in section-215-child-protection.md
P.G. 215-29 Electronic Recording covered in section-215-juvenile-delinquency.md

Sergeant Focus Areas

  • Supervisory approval requirements for juvenile arrests
  • Proper detention conditions for juveniles
  • Mandatory notifications (parents, ACS, Family Court)
  • Time limits on juvenile detention
  • Miranda requirements specific to juveniles (parent/guardian role)
  • School arrest protocols and notification chains
  • ACS coordination and 10-68A radio code
  • Child car seat inspection responsibilities

Chapter Files

File Theme Procedures Covered
section-215-lost-runaway-children Lost, runaway, and at-risk children 215-01, 215-02, 215-03, 215-04, 215-05
section-215-juvenile-delinquency Juvenile offenses, arrests, and court processing 215-07, 215-08, 215-09, 215-10, 215-11, 215-12
section-215-school-related School safety, searches, and threats 215-13, 215-16, 215-17, 215-18, 215-27

key-terms Definitions and critical numbers/ages All
review-questions 15 multiple-choice exam questions All

Section 215 — Child Advocacy, ACS Coordination, and Child Safety

Covers: P.G. 215-14, 215-22, 215-23, 215-28, 215-29

P.G. 215-14 — Child Advocacy Referrals

Purpose: To ensure proper notifications and referrals when a child is a victim of abuse or a sex crime.

Applicability

Exam Alert: Know the two triggering conditions — they have different age thresholds.

Condition Trigger
Child abuse victim Child under 11 years of age
Sex crime victim Child under 18 years of age

Notification Chain

Exam Alert: ACS Instant Response Team (IRT) notification is required when a child is removed or an arrest is made.

  1. Notify ACS Instant Response Team (IRT) when:
    • A child is removed from the home, OR
    • An arrest is made
  2. Notify New York State Central Registry
  3. Desk Officer must notify:
      1. Special Victims Unit (first notification)
      1. Appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad

Notification Summary Table

Action Who to Notify
Child removed or arrest made ACS Instant Response Team (IRT)
All cases NY State Central Registry
Desk officer notification (1st) Special Victims Unit (SVU)
Desk officer notification (2nd) Borough Child Abuse Squad

Sergeant Focus: Verify the desk officer has completed the full notification chain: ACS IRT, State Central Registry, SVU, and the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad. Ensure the REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154) is prepared.

Forms and Reports

  • REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154)
  • COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)
  • JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A)

Related Procedures

  • Emergency Removals or Investigations and Reporting of Abused, Neglected or Maltreated Children (P.G. 215-03)
  • On-Line Juvenile Report System (P.G. 215-08)

P.G. 215-22 — Requests for Assistance from ACS Child Protection Specialists

Purpose: To ensure the safety of all persons present when responding to requests for assistance by Administration for Children’s Services (ACS) Child Protection Specialists.

Scope — When ACS May Request Assistance

Police officers may be requested to assist ACS Child Protection Specialists when:

  1. Executing an Order of Entry endorsed by a Family Court judge
  2. Executing a Child Removal Order or warrant endorsed by a Family Court judge
  3. Conducting an emergency removal pursuant to Section 1024 Family Court Act
  4. The ACS Child Protection Specialist is concerned for his/her personal safety due to suspicion of guns, drugs, or prior or threatened acts of violence against ACS Child Protection Specialists

Exam Alert: ACS CPS may only request assistance when a complaint has been lodged with the State Central Registry.

ACS CPS Contact Information

ACS Child Protection Specialists carry cellular telephones issued by their agency. Police officers may request that the communications section contact the caller to obtain the CPS’s exact location.

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Respond to the scene
      1. Give dispatcher 10-84 code upon arrival
      1. Locate the ACS employee
  2. Confer with ACS Child Protection Specialist at the scene
      1. Determine specific reason for request
      1. Obtain copy of Removal/Entry Order(s) or warrant, if applicable

NOTE: In an emergency removal situation, the ACS CPS may not have a warrant or Removal Order.

  1. Request patrol supervisor, if not already present

Patrol Supervisor

Sergeant Focus: A patrol supervisor MUST respond to ACS CPS assistance calls.

  1. Respond and confer with uniformed members of the service and ACS CPS
  2. Determine if precinct detective, borough Special Victims or Child Abuse squads have an open case regarding this family, if possible
  3. Review Entry/Removal order(s) from ACS, if applicable
      1. Confer with Legal Bureau if question as to validity of the order exists. Be guided by response from Legal Bureau personnel.
  4. Determine if additional Department resources are needed

Uniformed Member of the Service (continued)

  1. Ensure the safety of all persons present
  2. Aid in carrying out Entry/Removal order(s), if applicable
  3. Follow P.G. 215-03 if arrest is required for child abuse or neglect
  4. Follow appropriate complaint or arrest procedure for other criminal activities, if appropriate
  5. Make an entry in digital Activity Log including:
      1. ACS Child Protection Specialist’s name
      1. Specific purpose of the request for assistance (see SCOPE statement 1 through 4)
      1. ACS case number (also known as State Connections ID #)

Patrol Supervisor (continued)

  1. Take appropriate action

Desk Officer — Shelter and Child Location

Exam Alert: The Desk Officer must secure shelter for the child and comply with PG 215-01 (Child/Minor Requiring Care/Shelter). The D.O. instructs the P/G to call ACS; if after hours, call ACS Emergency Children’s Services. If the child is a victim of a crime or abused by P/G, NEVER return the child to the home. If another agency with removal authority is present, NYPD will NOT override their decision to remove a child.

Exam Alert: NEVER provide parents or guardian the location of the child after removal. This is a critical rule tested on the exam.

Additional Data

Exam Alert: The radio code 10-68A is specific to ACS CPS assistance requests. This is a commonly tested detail.

  • When an ACS CPS appears at a police facility and requests assistance, the desk officer will ensure that the communications section is notified in a timely manner and a 10-68A code is dispatched
  • The desk officer will direct the CPS to the scene to await patrol response
  • Radio code 10-68A = Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists
  • Nothing precludes a police officer from taking police action if evidence of criminal activity exists

Related Procedures

  • Emergency Removals or Investigation and Reporting of Abused, Neglected, or Maltreated Children (P.G. 215-03)
  • Family Court Warrant for Child Abuse/Neglect Cases (P.G. 215-04)
  • Preliminary Investigation of Complaints (P.G. 207-07)

P.G. 215-23 — Court Processing of Juvenile Offenders/Adolescent Offenders on Weekends/Holidays

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure covers the court processing of Juvenile Offenders (JO) and Adolescent Offenders (AO) during weekends and holidays when Family Court is not in session.

P.G. 215-28 — Use of Child Car Seats

Purpose: To ensure the proper use of child car seats and booster seats in Department vehicles.

Age-Based Requirements

Exam Alert: These age thresholds are frequently tested. Know the specific age ranges and seat types.

Age Requirement Seat Position
Under 2 years Rear-facing child car seat Back seat ONLY
Under 4 years Child car seat Back seat ONLY
Ages 4-7 Booster seat Back seat ONLY
Under 8 years Some form of child restraint required —

Exam Alert: Both child car seats and booster seats must be used in the BACK seat only.

Desk Officer Responsibilities

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer must ensure MOS are trained in proper child car seat installation and use.

  • Ensure uniformed members of the service are trained in proper use of child car seats
  • Ensure child car seats are inspected at the start of every tour

Training Sergeant

Sergeant Focus: The training sergeant is responsible for ensuring ALL members are trained on child car seat usage.

  • Ensure all members of the command are trained in proper child car seat installation and use

Car Seat Inspection Schedule

Task Frequency Responsible
Inspect child car seats Start of every tour Desk officer ensures
Training on car seats Ongoing Training sergeant

P.G. 215-29 — Electronic Recording of Custodial Interrogations of Juveniles

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure addresses the requirement for electronic recording (audio/video) of custodial interrogations of juveniles.

Section 215 — Juvenile Delinquency and Arrest Processing

Covers: P.G. 215-07, 215-08, 215-09, 215-10, 215-11, 215-12

P.G. 215-07 — Truants

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure addresses the handling of truant children encountered during school hours.

P.G. 215-08 — On-Line Juvenile Report System

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure covers the electronic juvenile report system, including completion of the JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A). Referenced extensively in other Section 215 procedures.

P.G. 215-09 — Offense by Juvenile Under 18 (Not JO/AO)

Purpose: To process a juvenile who has committed an offense but does not qualify as a Juvenile Offender (JO) or Adolescent Offender (AO). This is the most detailed and exam-critical procedure in Section 215.

Definitions

Exam Alert: These age definitions are heavily tested. Know them precisely.

Term Definition
Juvenile Delinquent (JD) A person who is at least 12 years of age but less than 18 years of age who commits an act that would be a crime if committed by an adult. For homicide offenses, a child under 12 may also be classified as a JD.
Family/Household Includes persons who: (a) are related by blood or marriage; (b) are or were legally married; (c) have a child in common (regardless of marriage); (d) are or were in an intimate relationship; (e) reside or have resided in the same household

Procedure — Before Arrest

Exam Alert: The Juvenile Strategies Unit must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process. This is a mandatory step.

Uniformed Member of the Service:

  1. Contact the Juvenile Strategies Unit BEFORE beginning arrest process
  2. For robbery designated as a delinquent act: confer with Corporation Counsel regarding the appropriate charge
  3. Notify parent/guardian immediately

Miranda Warnings — Juvenile Specific

Exam Alert: This is one of the most tested topics in Section 215. The parent/guardian plays a critical role in Miranda.

Requirement Detail
When to advise Before ANY questioning
Who must be advised BOTH the juvenile AND the parent/guardian
Form used MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS (PD244-1413)
Parent/guardian presence Must be present when Miranda warnings are read
Parent objects to questioning NO questioning permitted — even if juvenile is willing
Parent requests attorney NO questioning permitted — even if juvenile is willing

Exam Alert: If the parent/guardian objects to questioning OR requests an attorney, questioning MUST STOP — even if the juvenile states willingness to speak. The parent’s objection overrides the juvenile’s consent.

Procedure — After Arrest

Desk Officer:

  • Ensure the REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154) is prepared when abuse is suspected
  • Ensure JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET and COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET are completed
  • Ensure AFFIDAVIT OF NOTIFICATION OF RIGHTS (PD244-1413) is completed with parent/guardian present

Transport Restriction

Exam Alert: A juvenile must NEVER be transported with an adult prisoner.

Family Court Appearance

Exam Alert: The juvenile must appear in Family Court at 0900 hours on the next business day.

Appearance Requirement Detail
Court Family Court
Time 0900 hours
Day Next business day

Designated Felony

For certain serious offenses designated as felonies (robbery, etc.): - Confer with Corporation Counsel regarding appropriate charge designation - The offense may be designated as either a delinquent act or a designated felony

Release and Recognizance

  • Juvenile Delinquent may be released on personal recognizance for designated felonies: appear at 0900 hours on next business day
  • Released on recognizance for non-designated felonies: appear at 0900 hours on next business day
  • Arresting officer excused from court appearance

Sergeant Focus: Ensure the desk officer has verified that (1) the Juvenile Strategies Unit was contacted before arrest, (2) parent/guardian was notified immediately, (3) Miranda warnings were administered with parent/guardian present using PD244-1413, and (4) no questioning occurred if the parent objected or requested an attorney.

Borough Court Section — Family Court Liaison Addresses

Borough Address
Manhattan 60 Lafayette Street
Brooklyn 330 Jay Street
Bronx 900 Sheridan Avenue
Queens 151-20 Jamaica Avenue
Staten Island 100 Richmond Terrace

Additional Data

  • If probable cause exists that a crime has been committed against a child by a family member, additional procedures apply (see P.G. 215-03)
  • All sex crime allegations involving a child: notify Special Victims Unit
  • If child is 11 or older: notify precinct detective squad; if under 11: notify SVU

Forms and Reports

  • JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A)
  • COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)
  • AIDED REPORT
  • MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS (PD244-1413)
  • ON LINE BOOKING SYSTEM ARREST WORKSHEET (PD244-159)
  • AFFIDAVIT OF NOTIFICATION OF RIGHTS

Related Procedures

  • On-Line Juvenile Report System (P.G. 215-08)
  • Emergency Removals or Investigations and Reporting of Abused, Neglected or Maltreated Children (P.G. 215-03)
  • Children or Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter (P.G. 215-01)

P.G. 215-10 — Arrest of Juvenile Offender/Adolescent Offender

This procedure covers the arrest and processing of Juvenile Offenders (JO) and Adolescent Offenders (AO), who are processed through the criminal justice system rather than Family Court.

Definitions

Exam Alert: Know the precise age definitions. An AO is a 16- or 17-year-old charged with ANY felony. A JO is age- and offense-specific.

Term Definition
Adolescent Offender (AO) A 16- or 17-year-old charged with any felony
Juvenile Offender (JO) A 13-, 14-, or 15-year-old charged with specific serious felonies (see below)

13-Year-Old — Very Limited JO Eligibility

Exam Alert: A 13-year-old can ONLY be a JO for two subdivisions of Murder 2nd Degree. Nothing else.

  • Intentional Murder (Murder 2nd, Sub 1) — Intend death, cause death to anyone
  • Deathly Reckless Murder (Murder 2nd, Sub 2) — Figured someone would get killed by their actions but did it anyway, and kills anyone

14- and 15-Year-Olds — MAR BAR MAK + Gun

Memory Aid — MAR BAR MAK + Gun: The felonies that make a 14- or 15-year-old a Juvenile Offender: - Murder 2nd degree (Subs 1, 2, AND 3) + Attempted Murder 2nd - Assault 1st degree (Subs 1 and 2) - Rape 1st degree (Subs 1 and 2) — - Burglary 1st degree (all subs) AND Burglary 2nd degree (Sub 1) - Arson 1st degree (all subs) AND Arson 2nd degree (all subs) - Robbery 1st degree (all subs) AND Robbery 2nd degree (Sub 2) — - Manslaughter 1st degree - Aggravated Sexual Abuse 1st degree - Kidnapping 1st degree (plus attempt) — - Gun: CPW 2nd degree committed within 1000 feet of a school

MAR — Detailed Breakdown

Offense Subdivision Details
Murder 2nd Sub 1 = Intend death, cause death to ANYONE; Sub 2 = Deathly Reckless; Sub 3 = Felony Murder (perp or partner causes death to non-participant during/attempting/fleeing a felony)
Assault 1st Sub 1 = Intend S.P.I., cause S.P.I. with a D.W./I.; Sub 2 = Intend to Disfigure or Amputate
Rape 1st Sub 1 = Force; Sub 2 = Physically helpless

Exam Alert: The Criminal Sexual Act (CSA) has been repealed — those acts are now included in Rape. The 2024 Rape definition update now includes Vaginal AND Oral AND Anal.

BAR — Detailed Breakdown with Shortcuts

Offense Details JO Shortcut
Burglary 2nd Sub 1 = Building + 1 of 4 aggravating factors If there is an aggravating factor, it is a JO. No aggravating factor = No JO.
Burglary 1st = Dwelling + 1 of 4 aggravating factors Whether dwelling or not does NOT matter for JO determination
Arson 2nd Intend damage by Fire, person inside, perp knew/should have known If someone is home, it is a JO
Arson 1st Intend damage by Explosion, person inside, perp knew/should have known; OR Fire/Explosion + (S.P.I. or $$$ or Bomb/Incendiary) —
Robbery 2nd Sub 2 = Cause P.I. If there is an INJURY, it is a JO
Robbery 1st Cause S.P.I.; OR Armed with D.W.; OR Use/Threaten Immediate Use of D.I.; OR Display what appears to be a firearm —

Exam Alert: The 4 aggravating factors for Burglary (1/4): (1) Armed with explosive or Deadly Weapon, (2) Cause P.I. to non-participant, (3) Use/Threaten immediate use of Dangerous Instrument, (4) Display what appears to be a firearm.

Burglary vs. Arson — Building Definition Comparison

Feature Burglary Building Arson Building
Structure, vehicle, watercraft for lodging/business Same Same
Enclosed motor truck/trailer Yes Any vehicle powered by other than Muscle (excludes wheelchairs, rail, tracks, snowmobiles)
Each unit (apt) is a separate Building Yes No — separate units in one structure are NOT separate charges of Arson

Exam Alert: Burglary goes further than Arson and separately spells out a school as a building.

MAK — Detailed Breakdown

Offense Details
Manslaughter 1st Intend S.P.I., kill ANYONE. Also: intend death but under extreme emotional disturbance with reasonable explanation; or kill mom during unlawful abortion (pregnant >24 wks)
Aggravated Sexual Abuse 1st Insert Object, cause Injury by Force, Physically helpless, or Victim under 11 (10)
Kidnapping 1st Abduct + Intent to Compel Ransom ($, Property, Conduct); OR Abduct + Victim Dies; OR Abduct + More than 12 Hrs. AND Intent to commit one of 4: P.I./Sex Abuse, Advance Felony, Terrorize Anyone, Govt. Interference

Exam Alert — Key Note on Sex Crimes: Rape 1st and Agg Sexual Abuse 1st have the same 3 subdivisions (Force, Physically Helpless, Victim <11). However, Rape Sub 3 (Vic <11) does NOT make a JO, but Agg Sex Abuse Sub 3 (Vic <11) DOES make a JO. This is a critical distinction.

Gun — CPW 2nd Degree on School Grounds

  • Loaded Firearm, Machine Gun, Disguised Gun — WITH INTENT
  • Loaded Firearm — OUTSIDE of Home or Office (moved up from 3rd degree)
  • 5 or more firearms

Exam Alert: Loaded means the person has ammo in his possession. It does NOT actually have to be loaded in the gun.

  • Violation of this statute alone does NOT make a 14+ year-old a JO
  • Gun must be possessed on School Grounds (defined in Penal Law Section 220.00 Sub 14):
    • Inside the property line of a school (Elementary up to and including High School), OR
    • Outside the property line but within 1000 feet in a Public Place (includes parked vehicle)

Deadly Weapons — 9 Types

Memory Aid — 9 DEADLY WEAPONS (for Burglary aggravating factor): - 1 Loaded weapon (whose shot can cause S.P.I./D.O.A.) - 4 Slappers: Blackjack, Billy, Metal Knuckles, Plastic Knuckles - 4 Knives: Switchblade knife, Pilum Ballistic knife, Metal knuckle knife, Dagger

Exam Alert: Note that Plastic Knuckles are included as a Deadly Weapon. There are exactly 9 deadly weapons defined in Penal Law Section 10.00.

P.G. 215-11 — Arrest of Juvenile Unlawfully in Certain Licensed Premises

Purpose: To take a juvenile found in certain licensed premises into protective custody.

Definitions

Term Definition
Juvenile An individual actually or apparently under 18 years of age
Licensed Premises Public dance halls, cabarets, catering establishments, bars and grills, pool and billiard parlors, bowling alleys, skating rinks, theaters, motion picture theaters

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Ascertain age of the juvenile
  2. Determine if presence is unlawful
  3. Take juvenile into protective custody if presence in premises is in violation of law and arrest proprietor or person in charge
      1. If a premise is a pool/billiard parlor or bowling alley, issue a summons in lieu of arrest
      1. If proprietor/person in charge is arrested, indicate under Details of ON LINE BOOKING SYSTEM ARREST WORKSHEET (PD244-159) a brief statement indicating person in charge permitted or had knowledge of violation and actions that led arresting officer to this conclusion
  4. Escort juvenile home, if residence is located within city
      1. If residence of juvenile is outside city limits, handle as Child Requiring Shelter until provision is made for returning juvenile home
  5. Inform parents of juvenile of unlawful presence and question concerning reason
  6. Advise parent of legal responsibilities
  7. Prepare JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A) and COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A), per P.G. 215-08, and include under Details section:
      1. Occupation of parent/guardian
      1. Statements of parent/guardian
  8. Submit WORKSHEETS to desk officer

Desk Officer

  1. Review WORKSHEETS for accuracy and sign
  2. Process in normal manner

Commanding Officer

Sergeant Focus: The CO must forward a report to the Chief of Department for specific premises types.

  1. Forward report of action taken to Chief of Department, Investigation Review Section, DIRECT, if premises is:
      1. Catering establishment
      1. Cabaret
      1. Public dance hall

Related Procedures

  • Children or Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter (P.G. 215-01)
  • On-Line Juvenile Report System (P.G. 215-08)

Forms and Reports

  • COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)
  • JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A)
  • ON LINE BOOKING SYSTEM ARREST WORKSHEET (PD244-159)

P.G. 215-12 — Personal Recognizance — Juvenile Delinquent

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure covers the personal recognizance process for juvenile delinquents, including release conditions and court appearance requirements.

Section 215 — Lost, Runaway, and At-Risk Children

Covers: P.G. 215-01, 215-02, 215-03 (S.C.A.M.), 215-04, 215-05

P.G. 215-01 — Children or Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure addresses the handling of children or minors who require care and/or shelter. It is cross-referenced in multiple other Section 215 procedures.

PG Conflict: PG 215-01 says the UMOS should inquire if a relative OR friend will provide care for a child under 18. PG 215-02 (Lost Child) does NOT say friend — only relative. Know this distinction for the exam.

Key Comparisons: 215-01 vs. 215-02

Feature 215-01 (Child Requiring Care/Shelter) 215-02 (Lost Child)
Age listed Under 18 NO age listed
First responder UMOS UMOS
Who can take child Relative OR friend Only relative (no friend)
If not located UMOS notifies ACS Bring child to command
YCO notification UMOS notifies YCO that child is in command Desk Officer notifies YCO always
D.O. special step If left with relative/friend not in household, send copy of aided to Y.O., pct where child located N/A

Exam Alert: The exam may test the difference between 215-01 and 215-02. In 215-01, a friend can take the child; in 215-02, only a relative can. Also note that 215-01 specifies under 18 while 215-02 lists no specific age.

P.G. 215-02 — Lost Child

Purpose: To locate relatives or friends of a lost child.

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Notify desk officer/counterpart AND radio dispatcher
  2. Make brief inquiry in vicinity of place where found
  3. If relative not located (does NOT say friend as in 215-01), bring child to command
  4. Prepare AIDED REPORT

Prior Test: A past promotion test asked: What is the FIRST step when a UMOS finds a lost child? — The correct answer is notify the Desk Officer/counterpart AND the Radio Dispatcher. The exam tested first step language specifically.

Desk Officer

  1. Telephone Missing Persons Squad and give description
  2. Complete captions on AIDED REPORT and process in normal manner:
      1. Assign female uniformed member of the service to watch child in the command, if available, or other available uniformed member of the service, when necessary
      1. Notify youth coordination officer
      1. If child is not claimed within a reasonable amount of time, notify the Administration for Children’s Services (ACS) and request that they send a representative to the stationhouse to provide necessary transportation to shelter (see P.G. 215-03 for ACS borough field office and Emergency Children’s Services addresses)
      1. If ACS is unable to provide transportation, assign a female uniformed member of the service, if available, or other available uniformed members of the service, to escort child to the shelter
      1. Inform Missing Persons Squad if child is moved to another location

Exam Alert: Step 7 is critical — abuse/neglect screening is mandatory.

  1. In cases that could involve abuse, neglect, maltreatment or abandonment, prepare REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154) and related forms, as required:
      1. Notify precinct detective squad if the child is eleven years of age or older OR Special Victims Unit if child is under eleven years of age
      1. For allegations made of any sex crime to any child, notify the Special Victims Unit

Sergeant Focus: Ensure the desk officer completes the full notification chain — Missing Persons Squad in all cases, ACS if child unclaimed, and detective squad/SVU if abuse is suspected.

Age-Based Notification for Suspected Abuse

Child’s Age Notify
11 years or older Precinct detective squad
Under 11 years Special Victims Unit (SVU)
Any sex crime allegation (any age) Special Victims Unit (SVU)

Related Procedures

  • Unidentified Persons (P.G. 216-03)
  • Children or Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter (P.G. 215-01)
  • Emergency Removals or Investigations and Reporting of Abused, Neglected or Maltreated Children (P.G. 215-03)

Forms and Reports

  • AIDED REPORT
  • REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154)

P.G. 215-03 — Suspected Child Abuse/Maltreatment — S.C.A.M. (Emergency Removal Procedure)

This procedure covers emergency removals, investigations, and the reporting requirements for abused, neglected, or maltreated children. It includes addresses for ACS borough field offices and ACS Emergency Children’s Services. It is extensively cross-referenced throughout Section 215.

Exam Alert: PG 215-03 is one of the most detailed procedures in Section 215. Know the role distinctions between UMOS, Patrol Supervisor, Desk Officer, YCO, and DVPO. The Paper Chase of 6 reports is a frequently tested detail.

UMOS — Initial Response

  1. Request response of Patrol Supervisor (ALWAYS)
  2. IF imminent danger AND parental/guardian consent:
    • Take child before supervisor arrives
  3. IF UMOS does not have consent of parent/guardian:
    • Wait for supervisor to arrive and authorize removal

Exam Alert: The Patrol Supervisor must ALWAYS be requested. The only time UMOS can act before the supervisor arrives is when there is imminent danger AND parental consent.

Patrol Supervisor — Emergency Removal Authority

  • IF there is reasonable cause to believe that continued presence in the home is imminently dangerous to child’s life or health AND there is no time to get a Court Order:
    • Direct removal of child without parental consent

Exam Alert: Diplomatic immunity of the person/residence shall NOT be a factor in the emergency removal analysis. The C.O./Duty Captain supervises removal and must comply with PG 212-56.

UMOS — Continued Actions

  • If probable cause a crime was committed: Effect an Arrest (no DAT)
  • Non-abusing parent/guardian: Advise of services for child, provide phone number for SAFE HORIZON
  • Domestic Violence: Look for indicators of other DV; prepare a separate 61 if appropriate

Custody of Child

  • Remove child to command OR Child Advocacy Center (CAC)
    • Child remains at CAC until picked up by relative, friend, or ACS
  • If medical attention needed: take the child to the hospital

Exam Alert: The child is NEVER returned to the home if the child is a victim of a crime or abused by parent/guardian. If another agency with removal authority is present, NYPD will NOT override their decision to remove a child.

Patrol Supervisor — Notice of Removal

  • Direct UMOS to serve or affix Notice-Temporary Removal of Child and Right to Hearing to parent/guardian

The Paper Chase — 6 Reports

Memory Aid — PAPER CHASE (6 Reports): 1. Complaint Report 2. DIR (Domestic Incident Report) 3. SCAM (Report of Suspected Child Abuse or Maltreatment) 4. Aided Report 5. Notice — Temporary Removal of Child and Right to Hearing 6. Affidavit of Service of Notice

  • Complaint but NO removal: Reports 1, 2, and 3 are prepared
  • Complaint AND Removal: All 6 reports are prepared

UMOS — State Central Registry Notification

  • Notify NYS Child Abuse and Maltreatment Register (State Central Registry)
    • If NYS refuses the case: enter time and name in Telephone Record (still make the form)
    • If NYS accepts the case: enter Registry Number on SCAM report in State Registry Number box
  • Notify ACS:
    • Borough field office (9-5) or Emergency Children’s Services (ECS) if after hours
    • ACS Instant Response Team Coordinator

Mail Call — Who Mails What and When

Paper Mailed Out… By Whom To Where
NOTICE In 24 hours UMOS —
AFFIDAVIT In 24 hours (except weekends/holidays) D.O. Family Court in removal borough
SCAM Next department mail D.O. ACS Office in residence borough

Exam Alert: The Affidavit goes to Family Court in the borough of removal. The SCAM goes to ACS in the borough of residence. These are different boroughs — do not confuse them.

SCAM Report Distribution (Desk Officer)

  • Original and 2nd copy to ACS field office in borough where child resides
    • Via Department mail to precinct where ACS office is located
    • D.O. at that command has mail delivered daily except Sat/Sun
  • If child resides outside city or with someone other than P/G (e.g., group home):
    • Report to ACS in Manhattan
  • 3rd copy to YCO, 4th copy with the Aided Report to Youth Strategies Division
  • File copy with DIR attached

Notice of Removal — Service and Affidavit

  • UMOS serves parent/guardian with Notice of Removal OR affixes to door if P/G not present
    • If affixed to door: member must also MAIL a copy out in 24 hours
    • If residence is in another command: D.O. of that command ensures Notice served or posted
  • If P/G not notified of removal: comply with PG 216-03 (Unidentified Persons)
  • If identity of parent(s) or residence is unknown or outside NYC: Detective makes notification

Affidavit

  • UMOS who served Notice OR posted and mailed it must prepare the Affidavit
  • Desk Officer: Countersign Affidavit AND mail to Family Court in borough of removal within 24 hours (except weekends/holidays)
  • Ensure notification efforts are noted on the Affidavit

Exam Alert: Never provide parents or guardian the location of the child.

Referral — Age-Based Squad Notification

Condition Refer To
Victim less than 11 (10) abused by P/G Special Victims Unit
Sex Crime, any age (includes attempts) Special Victims Unit
All other SCAM Reports PDU

Youth Coordination Officer (YCO) Responsibilities

  • Inspect all copies of SCAM including reports prepared by TB/HB — provide SCAM Report Serial Number
  • Review other precinct records: confer with DV Sgt and assigned PDS investigator
    • Check JR, Complaint Index, Aided Index, DIRs for previous contacts from the home
  • Make a folder for each SCAM report (whether removed or not), include all forms
    • If review reveals need for further investigation: Document in ECMS and notify detective squad
    • Enter results of review on rear of SCAM and forward to Youth Strategies Division
  • Maintain liaison with appropriate ACS Field Office

Domestic Violence Prevention Officer (DVPO) Responsibilities

  • Review DIR for accuracy and completeness
  • Record DIR in Precinct Domestic Incident Log
  • Enter information from DIR into Domestic Incident Report Database
  • Forward copy of DIR to Precinct Domestic Violence Investigator
  • Maintain file of DIRs (including copies of SCAM when attached)
  • Monitor at-risk locations and victims
  • Relay information of any other offenses committed within the household to YCO AND the assigned investigator
  • Maintain contact with complainants and advise them of rights and shelter availability

Radio Disposition Codes

Exam Alert: Child abuse uses J codes. If child abuse did NOT take place but other domestic violence occurred, use DV F codes instead.

Code Meaning
10-90J(1) D.I.R. made, no offense
10-90J(2) D.I.R. made, Unfounded
10-92J D.I.R. made, Arrest
10-93J D.I.R. made, 61 Prepared
10-68A ACS escort
10-75F Home visit

Exam Alert: If child abuse did not take place but other types of domestic violence occurred, use DV disposition codes with F suffix: 10-90F(1), 10-90F(2), 10-92F, 10-93F.

SVU — Instant Response Tracking Unit (SVU-IRST)

  • State Central Registry accepts law enforcement referrals from all mandated reporters (teachers, doctors, police) and the general public
  • Reports may be faxed to the SVU-IRTU who will determine the appropriate police response, including referral to a patrol precinct for initial investigation
  • SVU-IRTU may prepare a COMPLAINT REPORT and refer to the appropriate detective squad

See Also: PG 212-56 — Diplomatic immunity procedures related to emergency removal situations.

P.G. 215-04 — Family Court Warrant/Remand Order for Juvenile

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure addresses the execution of Family Court warrants and remand orders for juveniles.

P.G. 215-05 — Runaway Children

Purpose: To report and process runaway children and arrange for their care.

Definitions

  • Runaway Child (New York): A child under 16 years of age who has left home without the consent of a parent or guardian
  • Runaway Child (Interstate Compact): A child from out of state, handled per the Interstate Compact on Juveniles

Uniformed Member of the Service

  1. Take child into custody
  2. Prepare AIDED REPORT
  3. Prepare JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET

Desk Officer

Exam Alert: The desk officer must notify Missing Persons Squad in ALL runaway cases — not just when the child is unclaimed.

  1. Interview the child
  2. Notify Missing Persons Squad in ALL cases
  3. Contact parent/guardian
  4. Arrange shelter if unable to release child to parent/guardian

Sergeant Focus: Verify that the desk officer has made all required notifications. For runaway children, Missing Persons Squad notification is mandatory regardless of whether the parent has been contacted.

Forms and Reports

  • AIDED REPORT
  • JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A)

Related Procedures

  • Children or Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter (P.G. 215-01)
  • On-Line Juvenile Report System (P.G. 215-08)

Section 215 — School Safety, Searches, and Threats

Covers: P.G. 215-13, 215-16, 215-17, 215-18, 215-27

P.G. 215-13 — Handcuffing/Restraining Students in Schools

This procedure addresses the use of handcuffs and restraints on students in school settings, including students in emotional crisis, types of restraint, and the arrest process at schools.

Students in Emotional Crisis

Responding to a 911 call: - Notify the principal (if not aware) - Request the presence of the school’s Crisis Intervention Team - Inquire from school staff whether the crisis intervention plan has been followed prior to the call

Responding to a request from the principal or school’s Crisis Intervention Team: - Inquire: - What de-escalation techniques were used by school staff - Whether the parent/guardian has been notified - Whether the student has a disability or other medical, mental, physical, or emotional condition - Whether the school’s Crisis Intervention Team has been notified - Whether the crisis intervention plan has been followed prior to the request to intervene

Exam Alert: Unless responding to a 911 call or a request to intervene from the principal or school’s Crisis Intervention Team, MOS must first observe the student and circumstances to determine if there is an imminent and substantial risk of physical or serious physical injury to anyone BEFORE engaging.

  • MOS may respond to a student in emotional crisis to prevent imminent and substantial risk of physical injury or serious physical injury
  • Use of mechanical restraints and response of EMS should only be used when a student’s behavior creates an imminent and substantial risk of physical injury or serious physical injury to anyone

Types of Restraint

Type Description When Used
Constructive Restraint Physical control with NO restraining device; using minimal force to control movement Child who is obeying commands; to ensure safety of child
Alternative Restraint Velcro device (alternative to metal handcuffs) for possible safety/flight risk and/or non-compliant child Used only by MOS trained in them; all SSAs carry them and are trained
Metal Handcuffs Standard metal cuffs — LAST RESORT When other means are not effective and risk of bodily harm exists

Restraining Students — Key Rules

Exam Alert: Handcuffs/alternative restraints/Velcro cuffs should NOT be placed on a student while in a classroom, cafeteria, or area where other students are located. Coordinate with school officials to move student to a semi-private location (principal’s office, school security office).

  • When possible and appropriate: MOS should confer and coordinate with school administrative staff (Principal/designee and school’s Crisis Intervention Team) to reduce or eliminate the necessity to use force
  • Absent exigent circumstances, coordinate with school officials to have student removed to a semi-private location

Metal Cuffs — Age Restrictions

Exam Alert: Metal cuffs should NOT be used on students less than 12 (11) years old without prior approval of a Precinct NCO Sergeant, Patrol Sergeant, or School Safety Supervisor. Metal cuffs are a LAST RESORT.

  • Prior to metal cuffs: strongly consider verbal command, constructive or alternative restraint for Non-Criminal Incidents
  • Incidents related to school discipline only are generally not a police matter — MOS will ordinarily defer to school officials

Handcuffed to a Fixed Object — Children Under 12

Exam Alert: A student less than 12 will NOT be handcuffed to a restraining bar, chair, railing, or other fixed objects. Child must be kept under constant observation by UMOS/SSA at all times.

Level of Restraint by Age

Age Restraint Standard
Under 16 Use reasonable belief standard to determine level (Constructive or Alternative). Consider alternatives generally for child less than 12, but any child less than 16 is eligible.
16 and above When necessary to handcuff, metal handcuffs will be used. Alternative restraints will NOT be utilized for students 16 and above.

Factors for Determining Type of Restraint

  • Physical: Age and physical stature of child
  • Offense: Type of offense/nature of incident; use of weapons
  • History: Reputation with school authorities; history of emotional/behavioral difficulties
  • Escape Risk: Possible risk of escape
  • Demeanor and Compliance: Child’s demeanor; compliance with instructions; demonstrating behavior that may cause imminent harm to self or others
  • Parent/Guardian: Presence of child’s parent/guardian
  • Special Circumstances: Special Education Status; medical, mental, physical/emotional condition/limitation of child

Juvenile Removed to Department Facility

Exam Alert: When a juvenile is removed to a Department facility, notification must be made to the Juvenile Crime Desk. The Juvenile Crime Desk will assist supervisors in determining whether a juvenile should be released on a Juvenile Report System Worksheet or processed as a JD.

The Arrest Steps — UMOS/SSA Level I-III

  1. Inform school personnel of decision to arrest and basis UNLESS fight, flight, etc.
  2. Notify, confer & coordinate with SSD personnel AND principal/designee prior, UNLESS exigents exist
    • If conferrals not possible due to exigents: notify SSD and principal/designee ASAP after arrest/restraint for emotional issues
    • Discuss the option of referring low-level non-violent crimes/violations to school officials in lieu of arrest or summons
  3. Remove student if possible to semi-private location (principal’s office, school security office)
  4. Inform student of authority and cause for arrest or summons, UNLESS physical resistance, flight, or other factors render impractical
  5. Restraint — Use appropriate restraint when applicable
  6. Arrest Effected:
    • Request Pct NCO Sgt to respond OR, if NCO Sgt n/a, request Patrol Supervisor

Exam Alert — PRIOR to arrest: Absent exigents, notify School Safety Division’s Operations Unit with info per PG 215-17 (name of school, suspect, charge, date of incident, date reported). Put Log # obtained from Ops in Activity Log.

  1. Violation — If offense is a violation, do NOT remove subject from a school to a Department facility
  2. Observation — Keep student under constant supervision regardless of level of restraint used

Rear-Handcuff Policy

Exam Alert: Department policy is to rear-handcuff students who are arrested in a school facility UNLESS mitigating circumstances dictate otherwise. Consideration should be given to student’s age or special needs status. A decision may be made not to rear-handcuff when special circumstances exist.

Low-Level Misdemeanors or Violations
