  • After review of incident by MOS and conferral with school principal/designee, non-violent misdemeanors may be referred to school officials for appropriate action

Precinct NCO Sergeant/Patrol Supervisor — Arrest Situation

  • Respond to school and verify probable cause exists to support the arrest
  • Restraint: Assess the type of restraint used and direct change in level as appropriate
    • Metal cuffs used on student less than 12: Notify CO/XO/Duty Captain
  • School Objects: If school administrator objects to arrest, advise them to contact their Legal Department

Exam Alert — Metal Cuffs on Child Under 12: The C.O./X.O./Duty Captain must investigate and prepare a Report on Typed Letterhead including determination of necessity and appropriateness. Forward report to Chief of Patrol with copy to Commanding Officer, School Safety Division.

Precinct NCO Sergeant — School Meeting

  • At start of school year, meet with principals in command to apprise them of Department’s cuffing guidelines

SSA Level I-II — Police Action

  • Be aware of all police action taken on school grounds
  • Document any police action taken on SCHOOL SAFETY DIVISION CRIMINAL INCIDENT REPORT

Probable Cause Determination

Exam Alert: Whether probable cause to arrest exists will be determined by the Police Department. While the desires of school personnel may be considered, the views of school personnel are NOT controlling. If conflict arises between UMOS and school personnel, UMOS will request response of Precinct NCO Sergeant or Patrol Supervisor. Under no circumstances will school personnel be permitted to interfere with an arrest the NCO Sgt or Patrol Supervisor has deemed warranted.

Legal Considerations

  • Whether the school is a DOE facility or NOT, MOS will adhere to the procedure and fulfill their duties as outlined within their scope of responsibilities

See Also: PG 215-17 — Police Actions at DOE Schools for pre-arrest notification requirements.

P.G. 215-16 — Reporting School-Related Criminal Incidents

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure covers the reporting requirements for criminal incidents occurring in or near schools.

P.G. 215-17 — Police Actions at DOE Schools

Purpose: To establish procedures for police actions at Department of Education (DOE) schools, including arrests, investigations, and student questioning.

Pre-Arrest Notification

Exam Alert: The School Safety Division Operations Unit must be notified PRIOR to any arrest at a school. A log number must be obtained.

Requirement Detail
Notify School Safety Division Operations Unit
When PRIOR to arrest
Obtain Log number
Consult Principal or designee

On-Campus vs. Off-Campus Arrests

Exam Alert: Arrests for off-campus incidents should be effected OFF school property whenever possible.

  • Arrests of students for off-campus incidents should be effected OFF school property when possible
  • Arrests on school property are appropriate only for specific offenses

Offenses Permitting On-Campus Arrest

The following offenses warrant arrest ON school property:

  • Any felony (except certain property crimes below)
  • All sex offenses
  • Menacing with firearm display
  • Other serious offenses involving weapons or violence

Sergeant Focus: Evaluate whether the offense justifies an on-campus arrest. For property crimes that are felonies, consider whether the arrest can be deferred to off-campus.

Student Questioning

Requirement Detail
Parent/guardian Contact must be attempted before questioning
Miranda Required if student is a suspect
Principal/designee Must be consulted
Student records Require court order or subpoena unless health/safety emergency

Exam Alert: Student records require a court order or subpoena to access. The only exception is a health or safety emergency.

Supervisor Responsibilities

Sergeant Focus: When responding to school incidents, ensure: - School Safety Division Operations Unit was notified and log number obtained - Principal/designee was consulted - Parent/guardian contact was attempted before student questioning - Miranda warnings given if student is a suspect - Off-campus arrest was considered for off-campus incidents - Student records were not accessed without proper legal authority

See Also: PG 215-13 — Handcuffing/Restraining Students in Schools, which details the arrest steps, restraint types, and rear-handcuff policy.

Related Procedures

  • On-Line Juvenile Report System (P.G. 215-08)
  • School Safety Division procedures
  • Reporting School-Related Criminal Incidents (P.G. 215-16)
  • Handcuffing/Restraining Students in Schools (P.G. 215-13)

Forms and Reports

  • JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET (PD377-159A)
  • COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)
  • ON LINE BOOKING SYSTEM ARREST WORKSHEET (PD244-159)

P.G. 215-18 — Search Protocols for School Safety Agents

Purpose: To establish search protocols and limitations for School Safety Agents (SSAs) at DOE schools.

Authority to Search

Exam Alert: School Safety Agents conduct searches on behalf of the principal/designee — NOT on their own authority (except in emergencies).

Factor Requirement
Authority source Principal or designee
Standard Reasonable suspicion
Gender requirement Same-gender search to the maximum extent
Strip-searches Absolutely prohibited
Emergency exception Immediate intervention for safety — no principal authorization required

Search Standards

  • Searches are conducted only on behalf of the principal or designee (except emergency)
  • The reasonable suspicion standard applies
  • Same-gender search required to the maximum extent possible
  • NO strip-searches under any circumstances

Exam Alert: Strip-searches of students are absolutely prohibited, regardless of the circumstances.

Emergency Searches

An emergency is defined as a situation requiring immediate intervention for safety. In an emergency: - The School Safety Agent may act without prior principal/designee authorization - The search must be limited to what is necessary to address the immediate safety concern

RIGHT TO KNOW Business Card

  • Not required at security checkpoints unless specifically requested by the individual
  • Must be provided if requested

Sergeant Focus: Ensure School Safety Agents understand that their search authority derives from the principal/designee, not from independent police authority. Monitor for compliance with the strip-search prohibition and same-gender search requirement.

P.G. 215-27 — School-Related Threats

Purpose: To establish procedures for evaluating and responding to threats directed at schools.

Pre-Enforcement Consultation

Exam Alert: The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted BEFORE any enforcement action is taken regarding school-related threats.

Requirement Detail
Consult School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit
When BEFORE enforcement action
Central repository Counterterrorism Unit maintains all school threat records
Final determination Commanding Officer

Definition of School-Related Threat

A school-related threat is a threat with no corroborating evidence. This distinguishes it from an active threat situation that would require an immediate law enforcement response.

Processing School Threats

  1. Confer with School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit BEFORE enforcement action
  2. The Counterterrorism Unit serves as the central repository of all school threats
  3. The Commanding Officer makes the final determination on the response
  4. Low-level threats are referred back to the school for disciplinary action

Sergeant Focus: The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted before taking enforcement action. The CO makes the final call on the threat level and response. Low-level threats should be handled through school discipline, not enforcement.

Threat Assessment Flow

Threat Received
    |
    v
Confer with School Safety Division
Counterterrorism Unit
    |
    v
Counterterrorism Unit reviews
(central repository of all threats)
    |
    v
CO makes final determination
    |
    +--> Low-level: Refer to school for discipline
    |
    +--> Elevated: Appropriate enforcement action

Section 215 — Key Terms and Critical Numbers

Definitions

Term Definition Source
Juvenile Delinquent (JD) A person at least 12 but less than 18 years of age who commits an act that would be a crime if committed by an adult. A child under 12 may qualify for homicide offenses. P.G. 215-09
Juvenile Offender (JO) A juvenile charged with specific serious felonies who is prosecuted in the criminal justice system rather than Family Court. Age and offense-specific. P.G. 215-10
Adolescent Offender (AO) A category under Raise the Age legislation for certain offenders aged 16-17 charged with felonies, processed through a Youth Part of Superior Court. P.G. 215-10
PINS (Person in Need of Supervision) A person under 18 who is truant, incorrigible, ungovernable, or habitually disobedient and beyond the control of a parent/guardian. Processed through Family Court. General PG
ACS (Administration for Children’s Services) NYC agency responsible for child welfare, protective services, foster care, and adoption services. Multiple
IRT (Instant Response Team) An ACS team that responds when a child is removed from the home or an arrest is made in a child abuse/neglect case. P.G. 215-14
SVU (Special Victims Unit) NYPD unit handling sex crimes and crimes against children under 11 years of age. P.G. 215-02, 215-14
Corporation Counsel NYC’s Law Department attorney who represents the city in Family Court proceedings involving juvenile delinquents. Consulted for robbery charges designated as delinquent. P.G. 215-09
Family Court Court with jurisdiction over juvenile delinquency, child abuse/neglect, PINS, and family offenses. JDs appear at 0900 hours on the next business day. P.G. 215-09
Miranda (Juvenile Context) Warnings that must be read to BOTH the juvenile AND the parent/guardian using form PD244-1413. Parent/guardian must be present. If parent objects or requests attorney, no questioning is permitted. P.G. 215-09
School Safety Agent (SSA) Civilian agents who conduct security at DOE schools. Search authority derives from the principal/designee, not independent police authority. P.G. 215-18
Reasonable Suspicion The legal standard for School Safety Agent searches of students. Lower than probable cause but requires articulable facts. P.G. 215-18
Strip-Search Prohibition School Safety Agents are absolutely prohibited from conducting strip-searches under any circumstances. P.G. 215-18
Licensed Premises Public dance halls, cabarets, catering establishments, bars/grills, pool/billiard parlors, bowling alleys, skating rinks, theaters, motion picture theaters. P.G. 215-11
State Central Registry New York State database of child abuse/maltreatment reports. ACS CPS may only request police assistance when a complaint is lodged here. P.G. 215-14, 215-22
10-68A Radio code for Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists. P.G. 215-22
Juvenile Strategies Unit NYPD unit that must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process for a juvenile delinquent. P.G. 215-09
School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit Unit that must be consulted BEFORE enforcement action on school-related threats. Serves as central repository of all school threats. P.G. 215-27
School Safety Division Operations Unit Unit that must be notified PRIOR to any arrest at a DOE school. A log number must be obtained. P.G. 215-17

Critical Numbers and Ages

Number/Age Significance Source
Under 11 Abuse cases: notify SVU (not precinct detectives) P.G. 215-02, 215-14
11 or older Abuse cases: notify precinct detective squad P.G. 215-02
Under 12 Only qualifies as JD for homicide offenses P.G. 215-09
12 to 17 (at least 12, less than 18) Juvenile Delinquent age range P.G. 215-09
Under 16 Runaway child definition (New York) P.G. 215-05
Under 18 Juvenile definition for licensed premises; sex crime victim threshold for child advocacy P.G. 215-11, 215-14
Under 2 years Rear-facing child car seat required P.G. 215-28
Under 4 years Child car seat required P.G. 215-28
Ages 4-7 Booster seat required P.G. 215-28
Under 8 years Child restraint of some form required P.G. 215-28
0900 hours Family Court appearance time (next business day) P.G. 215-09
10-68A Radio code: ACS CPS assistance request P.G. 215-22
10-84 Radio code: arrival at ACS CPS scene P.G. 215-22
Section 1024 FCA Authorizes emergency removal of children P.G. 215-22
MAR BAR MAK + Gun Mnemonic for felonies making a 14/15-year-old a Juvenile Offender: Murder/Assault/Rape, Burglary/Arson/Robbery, Manslaughter/Agg Sexual Abuse/Kidnapping, + CPW 2nd within 1000 ft of school P.G. 215-10
S.C.A.M. Suspected Child Abuse or Maltreatment — the report (and procedure PG 215-03) for investigating and documenting child abuse/neglect cases P.G. 215-03
Paper Chase (6 Reports) The 6 documents prepared in a child removal case: Complaint Report, DIR, SCAM, Aided Report, Notice of Removal, Affidavit of Service. Only 3 are prepared if no removal (Complaint, DIR, SCAM). P.G. 215-03
Constructive Restraint Physical control with no restraining device, using minimal force necessary to control movement. Used for a child who is obeying commands. P.G. 215-13
Alternative Restraint Velcro device used as an alternative to metal handcuffs for possible safety/flight risk and/or non-compliant child. Used only by MOS trained in them; all SSAs carry and are trained. P.G. 215-13
Crisis Intervention Team School-based team that should be requested when responding to a student in emotional crisis. MOS should inquire about their involvement before engaging. P.G. 215-13
Deadly Weapon (9 types) 1 loaded weapon (shot can cause S.P.I./D.O.A.), 4 slappers (blackjack, billy, metal knuckles, plastic knuckles), 4 knives (switchblade, pilum ballistic, metal knuckle, dagger). Defined in Penal Law Section 10.00. P.G. 215-10
School Grounds For CPW 2nd degree JO purposes: inside school property line (elementary through high school) OR outside property line but within 1000 feet in a public place (includes parked vehicle). Penal Law Section 220.00 Sub 14. P.G. 215-10
SVU-IRST (Instant Response Tracking Unit) SVU unit that accepts law enforcement referrals from State Central Registry, determines appropriate police response, and may prepare complaint reports. P.G. 215-03
Safe Horizon Organization whose phone number is provided to the non-abusing parent/guardian for child services. P.G. 215-03
Juvenile Crime Desk Notified when a juvenile is removed to a Department facility. Assists supervisors in determining whether juvenile should be released on JR Worksheet or processed as JD. P.G. 215-13
10-90J(1) Radio disposition: D.I.R. made, no offense (child abuse J code) P.G. 215-03
10-92J Radio disposition: D.I.R. made, Arrest (child abuse J code) P.G. 215-03
10-93J Radio disposition: D.I.R. made, 61 Prepared (child abuse J code) P.G. 215-03
10-75F Radio disposition: Home visit P.G. 215-03

Critical Numbers and Ages (Additional)

Number/Age Significance Source
13-year-old JO Can ONLY be a JO for Murder 2nd degree, Subs 1 and 2 (Intentional and Deathly Reckless) P.G. 215-10
14- and 15-year-old JO Full MAR BAR MAK + Gun list applies P.G. 215-10
Under 12 (restraint) Metal cuffs should NOT be used without prior supervisor approval; NOT handcuffed to fixed objects P.G. 215-13
16 and above (restraint) Metal handcuffs used when necessary; alternative restraints will NOT be utilized P.G. 215-13
1000 feet Distance from school that defines School Grounds for CPW 2nd degree JO P.G. 215-10
24 hours Time limit for mailing Notice of Removal (UMOS) and Affidavit (D.O. to Family Court) P.G. 215-03
9 Number of Deadly Weapons defined in Penal Law Section 10.00 P.G. 215-10

Key Forms

Form Number Used For
AIDED REPORT — Lost child, runaway child
REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT PD377-154 Suspected abuse/neglect
JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET PD377-159A All juvenile incidents
COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET PD313-152A Criminal complaints involving juveniles
MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS PD244-1413 Juvenile questioning
ON LINE BOOKING SYSTEM ARREST WORKSHEET PD244-159 Juvenile arrests
DIR (DOMESTIC INCIDENT REPORT) — Child abuse cases involving DV; part of Paper Chase
NOTICE — TEMPORARY REMOVAL OF CHILD AND RIGHT TO HEARING — Served on P/G or affixed to door when child is removed
AFFIDAVIT OF SERVICE OF NOTICE — Documents who served/posted/mailed the Notice; D.O. countersigns and mails to Family Court
SCHOOL SAFETY DIVISION CRIMINAL INCIDENT REPORT — SSA Level I-II documents police action on school grounds

Section 215 — Review Questions

15 multiple-choice questions in exam format covering the most testable content from Section 215.

Question 1

Under P.G. 215-09, a Juvenile Delinquent is defined as a person who is:

    1. At least 7 years of age but less than 16 years of age
    1. At least 12 years of age but less than 18 years of age
    1. At least 13 years of age but less than 18 years of age
    1. At least 10 years of age but less than 16 years of age
Answer

B) At least 12 years of age but less than 18 years of age

P.G. 215-09 — A Juvenile Delinquent is a person who is at least 12 but less than 18 years of age who commits an act that would be a crime if committed by an adult. The exception is for homicide offenses, where a child under 12 may also be classified as a JD.

Question 2

When processing an arrest of a juvenile under P.G. 215-09, what unit must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process?

    1. Special Victims Unit
    1. Borough Child Abuse Squad
    1. Juvenile Strategies Unit
    1. School Safety Division Operations Unit
Answer

C) Juvenile Strategies Unit

P.G. 215-09 — The Juvenile Strategies Unit must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process for a juvenile delinquent. This is a mandatory step that cannot be skipped.

Question 3

Under P.G. 215-09, a juvenile’s parent/guardian objects to questioning but the juvenile states willingness to speak. What should the officer do?

    1. Proceed with questioning since the juvenile consented
    1. Continue questioning with the parent/guardian removed from the room
    1. Not question the juvenile — the parent’s objection overrides the juvenile’s consent
    1. Contact Corporation Counsel for guidance before proceeding
Answer

C) Not question the juvenile — the parent’s objection overrides the juvenile’s consent

P.G. 215-09 — If the parent/guardian objects to questioning OR requests an attorney, NO questioning is permitted, even if the juvenile is willing to speak. The parent’s objection controls.

Question 4

Under P.G. 215-09, Miranda warnings for a juvenile must be administered using which form, and who must be present?

    1. PD244-159, with the juvenile’s attorney present
    1. PD244-1413, with the parent/guardian present
    1. PD377-154, with the desk officer present
    1. PD244-1413, with the patrol supervisor present
Answer

B) PD244-1413, with the parent/guardian present

P.G. 215-09 — Miranda warnings must be read using MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS (PD244-1413) with the parent/guardian present. Both the juvenile AND the parent/guardian must be advised of rights.

Question 5

Under P.G. 215-17, prior to effecting an arrest at a DOE school, which unit must be notified?

    1. School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit
    1. Borough Command
    1. School Safety Division Operations Unit
    1. Special Victims Unit
Answer

C) School Safety Division Operations Unit

P.G. 215-17 — The School Safety Division Operations Unit must be notified PRIOR to any arrest at a DOE school, and a log number must be obtained.

Question 6

Under P.G. 215-17, arrests of students for off-campus incidents should be:

    1. Made at the school to minimize disruption to the community
    1. Effected off school property whenever possible
    1. Made only with the written consent of the school principal
    1. Deferred until the student reaches age 18
Answer

B) Effected off school property whenever possible

P.G. 215-17 — Arrests of students for off-campus incidents should be effected OFF school property when possible. On-campus arrests are reserved for specific qualifying offenses.

Question 7

Under P.G. 215-18, School Safety Agents conduct searches under what authority and legal standard?

    1. Independent police authority; probable cause
    1. On behalf of the principal/designee; reasonable suspicion
    1. Under ACS supervision; preponderance of evidence
    1. As peace officers; probable cause
Answer

B) On behalf of the principal/designee; reasonable suspicion

P.G. 215-18 — School Safety Agents conduct searches on behalf of the principal/designee (except in emergencies) under the reasonable suspicion standard. They do not have independent police search authority.

Question 8

Which of the following is TRUE regarding School Safety Agent searches under P.G. 215-18?

    1. Strip-searches are permitted with supervisor approval
    1. Same-gender searches are preferred but not required
    1. Strip-searches are absolutely prohibited under any circumstances
    1. Searches may only be conducted with a court order
Answer

C) Strip-searches are absolutely prohibited under any circumstances

P.G. 215-18 — Strip-searches of students are absolutely prohibited regardless of the circumstances. Same-gender searches are required to the maximum extent possible.

Question 9

Under P.G. 215-14, when a child under 11 is a victim of abuse, the desk officer must notify:

    1. Precinct detective squad, then ACS
    1. Special Victims Unit, then the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad
    1. Missing Persons Squad, then SVU
    1. Borough Command, then the precinct detective squad
Answer

B) Special Victims Unit, then the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad

P.G. 215-14 — For a child under 11 who is a victim of abuse, the desk officer must notify the Special Victims Unit first, then the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad. ACS Instant Response Team must also be notified when a child is removed or an arrest is made.

Question 10

Under P.G. 215-02, a lost child who may be a victim of abuse is 13 years old. Who should the desk officer notify regarding the potential abuse?

    1. Special Victims Unit
    1. Precinct detective squad
    1. ACS Instant Response Team only
    1. Missing Persons Squad only
Answer

B) Precinct detective squad

P.G. 215-02 — For suspected abuse cases, the desk officer notifies the precinct detective squad if the child is 11 years of age or older, or SVU if under 11. Since this child is 13, the precinct detective squad is the correct notification. However, for any sex crime allegations regardless of age, SVU is notified.

Question 11

Under P.G. 215-22, the radio code designated for Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists is:

    1. 10-84
    1. 10-13
    1. 10-68A
    1. 10-85
Answer

C) 10-68A

P.G. 215-22 — Radio code 10-68A has been created specifically for Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists. When an ACS CPS appears at a police facility requesting assistance, the desk officer ensures a 10-68A code is dispatched.

Question 12

Under P.G. 215-28, a 3-year-old child being transported in a Department vehicle must be placed in:

    1. A booster seat in the front passenger seat
    1. A rear-facing car seat in the back seat
    1. A child car seat in the back seat
    1. A regular seat belt in the back seat
Answer

C) A child car seat in the back seat

P.G. 215-28 — Children under 4 years of age require a child car seat, and children under 2 require a rear-facing child car seat. A 3-year-old requires a child car seat (not necessarily rear-facing since the child is 2 or older). Both child car seats and booster seats must be placed in the back seat only.

Question 13

Under P.G. 215-27, before taking enforcement action regarding a school-related threat, an officer must first confer with:

    1. School Safety Division Operations Unit
    1. Borough Command
    1. School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit
    1. The school principal only
Answer

C) School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit

P.G. 215-27 — The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted BEFORE any enforcement action on school-related threats. This unit serves as the central repository of all school threats. The Commanding Officer makes the final determination.

Question 14

Under P.G. 215-09, a juvenile delinquent must appear in Family Court at what time?

    1. 1000 hours on the next calendar day
    1. 0900 hours on the next business day
    1. 0830 hours on the day of arrest
    1. 1400 hours on the next business day
Answer

B) 0900 hours on the next business day

P.G. 215-09 — A juvenile delinquent must appear in Family Court at 0900 hours on the next business day following arrest.

Question 15

Under P.G. 215-22, which of the following is NOT a reason ACS Child Protection Specialists may request police assistance?

    1. Executing an Order of Entry endorsed by a Family Court judge
    1. Conducting an emergency removal pursuant to Section 1024 Family Court Act
    1. Serving a civil summons related to a custody dispute
    1. Concern for personal safety due to suspicion of guns, drugs, or threatened violence
Answer

C) Serving a civil summons related to a custody dispute

P.G. 215-22 — ACS CPS may request police assistance for four specific reasons: (1) executing an Order of Entry, (2) executing a Child Removal Order or warrant, (3) conducting an emergency removal under Section 1024 FCA, or (4) personal safety concerns. Civil custody disputes are not among the authorized reasons for police assistance.

Question 16

Under P.G. 215-02, what is the FIRST step a UMOS should take upon finding a lost child?

    1. Bring the child to the command
    1. Prepare an Aided Report
    1. Notify the desk officer/counterpart AND radio dispatcher
    1. Make inquiry in the vicinity of where the child was found
Answer

C) Notify the desk officer/counterpart AND radio dispatcher

P.G. 215-02 — A past promotion test asked this exact question. The first step is to notify the Desk Officer/counterpart AND the Radio Dispatcher. Making inquiry in the vicinity comes second, followed by bringing the child to the command if a relative is not located.

Question 17

Under P.G. 215-01 (Child/Minor Requiring Care/Shelter), who may take custody of a child? Compare this to P.G. 215-02 (Lost Child).

    1. Both procedures allow a relative or friend to take the child
    1. 215-01 allows a relative or friend; 215-02 allows only a relative
    1. Both procedures allow only a relative to take the child
    1. 215-01 allows only a relative; 215-02 allows a relative or friend
Answer

B) 215-01 allows a relative or friend; 215-02 allows only a relative

P.G. 215-01 states the UMOS should inquire if a relative OR friend will provide care. P.G. 215-02 says only relative — it does NOT say friend. This is a critical distinction between the two procedures.

Question 18

Under P.G. 215-03 (Suspected Child Abuse/Maltreatment), when a complaint is made but NO child removal occurs, how many reports are prepared?

    1. 1 report
    1. 3 reports
    1. 4 reports
    1. 6 reports
Answer

B) 3 reports

P.G. 215-03 — The Paper Chase requires: Complaint but NO removal = 3 reports (Complaint Report, DIR, and SCAM). When there IS a removal, all 6 reports are prepared (adding Aided Report, Notice of Removal, and Affidavit of Service).

Question 19

Under P.G. 215-03, the Affidavit of Service of Notice must be mailed by the Desk Officer to Family Court in which borough?

    1. The borough where the child was found
    1. The borough where the child resides
    1. The borough of removal
    1. The borough where the precinct of arrest is located
Answer

C) The borough of removal

P.G. 215-03 — The Affidavit goes to Family Court in the borough of REMOVAL (within 24 hours, except weekends/holidays). The SCAM report, by contrast, goes to the ACS Office in the borough of RESIDENCE. Do not confuse the two.

Question 20

Under P.G. 215-13, metal handcuffs should NOT be used on a student less than what age without prior approval of a supervisor?

    1. 10 years old
    1. 11 years old
    1. 12 years old
    1. 16 years old
Answer

C) 12 years old

P.G. 215-13 — Metal cuffs should NOT be used on students less than 12 (11) years old without prior approval of a Precinct NCO Sergeant, Patrol Sergeant, or School Safety Supervisor. Metal cuffs are a last resort. Also, a student less than 12 will NOT be handcuffed to a fixed object.

Question 21

A 14-year-old commits a Robbery 2nd degree, Sub 2 (causing physical injury). Under P.G. 215-10, is this person a Juvenile Offender?

    1. No, because Robbery 2nd is not serious enough
    1. Yes, because Robbery 2nd Sub 2 (causing P.I.) is on the JO list
    1. No, because a 14-year-old can only be a JO for Murder 2nd
    1. Yes, but only if a deadly weapon was used
Answer

B) Yes, because Robbery 2nd Sub 2 (causing P.I.) is on the JO list

P.G. 215-10 — Under the MAR BAR MAK + Gun mnemonic, Robbery 2nd degree Sub 2 (causing physical injury) makes a 14- or 15-year-old a Juvenile Offender. The shortcut: if there is an INJURY in a robbery, it is a JO.

Question 22

Under P.G. 215-13, when a conflict arises between a UMOS who wishes to arrest a student and school personnel who object, what is the proper course of action?

    1. Defer to the school principal’s wishes
    1. Contact the School Safety Division Operations Unit for guidance
    1. Request response of the Precinct NCO Sergeant or Patrol Supervisor; school personnel may NOT interfere with an arrest deemed warranted
    1. Release the student and file a report for follow-up
Answer

C) Request response of the Precinct NCO Sergeant or Patrol Supervisor; school personnel may NOT interfere with an arrest deemed warranted

P.G. 215-13 — Whether probable cause exists is determined by the Police Department. While school personnel’s views may be considered, they are NOT controlling. Under no circumstances will school personnel be permitted to interfere with an arrest the NCO Sgt or Patrol Supervisor has deemed warranted. If the school administrator objects, advise them to contact their Legal Department.

Section 216 — Aided Cases

Chapter Overview

Section 216 of the NYPD Patrol Guide governs all aided cases — incidents where a person (other than a prisoner) requires medical aid or assistance. These procedures cover situations ranging from routine sick-at-home calls to dead body investigations, psychiatric admissions, defibrillator use, and involuntary removals under Mental Hygiene Law.

Sergeants must understand when to respond, what notifications are required, how to supervise aided scenes, and the specific rules that govern specialized scenarios such as Department of Correction deaths, carbon monoxide alarms, and Mobile Crisis Team removals.

Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

  1. Define aided case and distinguish it from routine sick-at-home and nursing home calls.
  2. Identify the correct steps for handling a dead human body, including Medical Examiner notifications and body identification at the morgue.
  3. Explain firearm safety station procedures at psychiatric wards.
  4. Describe the patrol supervisor’s role in drug overdose, carbon monoxide, and EDP-related aided cases.
  5. State the rules for animal bite reporting and when firearms may be discharged at an animal.
  6. List the notifications required for aided cases involving DOC facilities, firefighters, and MTA incidents.
  7. Explain the AED program, including kit contents, utilization definition, and documentation requirements.
  8. Describe the procedures for TB shelter client removals and the Department’s limited role.
  9. Articulate the requirements for Mobile Crisis Team / ACT Team removals under Section 9.58 of Mental Hygiene Law.
  10. Identify key forms, reports, and their associated procedure numbers.

Procedure Index

File Procedures Covered
section-216-general-procedure 216-01, 216-02 (cross-ref), 216-03 (cross-ref)
section-216-dead-body 216-04, 216-05 (cross-ref), 216-06 (cross-ref)
section-216-special-aided 216-07, 216-08, 216-09, 216-10, 216-11, 216-12 (cross-ref)
section-216-auxiliary-transit-notifications 216-13, 216-14, 216-15, 216-16
section-216-medical-programs 216-17 (cross-ref), 216-18 (cross-ref), 216-19, 216-20, 216-21, 216-22
section-216-overdose-incidents 216-23
key-terms Definitions, forms, mnemonics, critical numbers
review-questions 28 multiple-choice exam prep questions

Section 216 — Auxiliary Police, Transit, Notifications, and DOC Deaths

P.G. 216-13: Line of Duty Injury or Death — Auxiliary Police Officers

Date Effective: 02/03/25 | Revision: R.O. 8 | Pages: 3

Purpose

To report and record line of duty injuries and deaths of auxiliary police officers.

Key Definitions

LINE OF DUTY — An incident that occurred while a member was on duty. Auxiliary police officers are considered ON DUTY after they sign-in on the Auxiliary Police Roll Call (APS #10) until they sign out at the end of their tour.

Exam Alert: Line of duty designation will NOT be given for any off-duty incidents. There is no off-duty designation for auxiliary police.

INVESTIGATING SUPERVISOR — Determined by a hierarchy based on severity:

Priority Investigating Supervisor When
1st Lieutenant platoon commander, precinct of occurrence If available
2nd Patrol supervisor, precinct of occurrence If lieutenant platoon commander unavailable
3rd Precinct CO/XO/duty captain If injured auxiliary sustains serious physical injury, is likely to die, or dies

Exam Alert: The investigating supervisor hierarchy escalates based on injury severity. CO/XO/duty captain handles the most serious cases.

Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 1-3)

Step Action
1 Request patrol supervisor to respond
2 Notify desk officer of: (a) circumstances of injury, and (b) request witnesses to await arrival of supervisor and precinct auxiliary coordinator (if on duty)
3 Remain at scene with auxiliary police officer unless: (a) hospitalization/medical attention required, and/or (b) further police action necessary

Note: If injury results from a reportable use of force incident, comply with P.G. 221-03 and P.G. 221-06.

Patrol Supervisor (Steps 4-7)

Step Action
4 Respond to scene
5 Interview auxiliary police officer; if removed to hospital, obtain name of hospital, attendant, and doctor
6 Have auxiliary police officer prepare, in own handwriting if possible, two copies of Employee’s Notice of Injury (Law Department Form WCD 23)
6a If member unable, patrol supervisor will prepare and submit report to CO, precinct of occurrence
7 Request witnesses to await arrival of investigating supervisor, if necessary

Exam Alert: The Employee’s Notice of Injury must be prepared in the auxiliary officer’s own handwriting when possible. Two copies are required.

Patrol Supervisor — Continued (Steps 8-11)

Step Action
8 Obtain and safeguard shield, identification card, portable radio, and personal property of auxiliary police officer, if hospitalized
9 Report circumstances to desk officer
10 Prepare Police Accident Report (MV104AN), if vehicle collision involved
11 Have AIDED REPORT prepared, if vehicle not involved

Desk Officer (Steps 12-17)

Step Action
12 Notify investigating supervisor as indicated in definitions
13 Make Command Log entry
14 Notify Operations Unit and supervisor at Auxiliary Police Section
14a Notify member’s command, if not assigned to precinct of occurrence
15 Notify precinct CO/XO/duty captain and Medical Examiner, if member dies or is likely to die
16 Notify borough commander, if member dies
17 Enter results of investigation in Command Log, indicating whether injury was sustained in line of duty

Investigating Supervisor (Steps 18-23)

Step Action
18 Interview injured auxiliary police officer and investigate circumstances
19 Notify Operations Unit promptly if injured member requires hospital treatment
19a Additional notification to Operations Unit required before final reports to update status
20 Make initial report to desk officer without delay
21 Prepare THREAT, RESISTANCE OR INJURY (T.R.I.) INCIDENT REPORT, as appropriate
22 Interview witnesses; request they prepare and sign WITNESS STATEMENT — INJURY TO MEMBER OF THE DEPARTMENT (PD429-065)
23 Prepare report on Typed Letterhead or UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT (PD370-152); forward to precinct commander

CO, Auxiliary Police Section (Steps 24-25)

Step Action
24 Designate supervisory member to respond and assist precinct CO/XO/duty captain if injured auxiliary dies or is likely to die
25 Prepare T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORT per P.G. 221-06 for each auxiliary member injured as a result of force

Auxiliary Police Coordinator (Step 26)

Step Action
26 Ensure all required reports and forms are forwarded to Auxiliary Police Section within ten (10) days, including copies of UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORTS

Exam Alert: Reports must reach the Auxiliary Police Section within 10 days.

CO/XO/Duty Captain (Steps 27-28)

Step Action
27 Prepare report on Typed Letterhead or UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT for LOD death or serious injury/likely to die; forward per P.G. 212-09 (exclude copy for Police Pension Fund)
28 Forward two copies of Employee’s Notice of Injury and WITNESS STATEMENT to CO, Auxiliary Police Section

Additional Data

In LOD cases where an auxiliary police officer dies or is likely to die, the command auxiliary police coordinator where the member is assigned shall maintain liaison with the Patrol Borough Auxiliary Police Section. In the absence of the coordinator, the precinct CO will designate an alternate.

P.G. 216-14: Aided Cases on New York City Transit Trains

Date Issued: 10/18/16 | Pages: 1

Purpose

To provide procedures for handling aided cases occurring on trains.

Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

Step Action
1 Ascertain condition of aided (unconscious, pregnant, epileptic, intoxicated, bleeding, etc.)
2 Transmit information immediately via radio dispatcher
3 Remove aided from train, if feasible, unless not advisable due to nature of illness/injury
3a Seek assistance of train crew or passengers, if necessary
4 Keep radio dispatcher informed of pertinent developments
5 Avoid unnecessary interruptions of train service
6 Notify passengers of delay in service and alternate routes, when possible
7 Prepare AIDED REPORT

Exam Alert: The primary goal is to remove the aided from the train when feasible. If not feasible, the radio dispatcher and NYC Transit Command Center will coordinate moving the train to a location with adequate assistance.

Exam Alert: Unnecessary interruption of train service can increase temperatures significantly in trains/stations and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels. Service will be interrupted only when absolutely necessary.

Radio Dispatcher (Steps 8-12)

Step Action
8 Obtain all relevant information concerning aided
9 Provide all relevant information to field units responding
10 Dispatch sufficient personnel and assistance promptly
11 Notify other city agencies as necessary
12 Notify Operations Unit for serious incidents

P.G. 216-15: Notifications

Date Issued: 10/18/16 | Pages: 2

Purpose

To ensure that relatives or friends are notified, where necessary, in aided and collision cases.

Procedure — Desk Officer

Step Action
1 Direct notifications be made by telephone, if possible

Exam Alert: Make notifications tactfully and in person, if possible, when the aided/collision victim is deceased or seriously injured and likely to die. Efforts should be made to have a relative, friend, or neighbor present.

Sergeant Focus: In death or likely-to-die cases, the desk officer will ensure a notification is made in a timely manner and document the time, date, and name of the member making notification on the AIDED REPORT or Police Accident Report.

Notification Assignments (Step 2)

Scenario Who Makes Notification
Relatives/friends within precinct of occurrence Member assigned to precinct
Relatives/friends outside precinct but within NYC Member assigned to resident precinct
Relatives/friends outside NYC Inter-City Correspondence Unit
Firefighter killed/injured at fire DO NOT notify without permission of fire officer in charge
Inmate who dies in DOC facility DO NOT notify unless requested by Correction supervisor in charge
Homicide victim Assigned detective makes notification

Exam Alert: Three DO NOT rules: (1) No firefighter family notification without fire officer permission, (2) No DOC inmate family notification unless Correction requests it, (3) Homicide notification is always the detective’s responsibility.

Special Notifications (Step 3)

Scenario Desk Officer Notifies
Aided killed/injured on MTA bus NYC Transit
Aided killed/injured on scaffold Department of Buildings
Aided killed/injured at construction site Operations Unit
Aided killed/injured on Parks Department property Operations Unit
Aided killed/injured in factory or store Operations Unit

Exam Alert: MTA bus goes to NYC Transit, scaffold goes to Buildings, everything else (construction, Parks, factory/store) goes to Operations Unit.

Documentation (Steps 4-10)

Step Role Action
4 MOS Record notification details on AIDED REPORT or MV104AN
4a Under Details, enter rank/name of fire officer or DOC supervisor, as applicable
4b Indicate which agency will make notifications
5 MOS If notification cannot be made, electronically save AIDED REPORT and notify desk officer
6 D.O. Review and sign AIDED REPORT; process per P.G. 216-02
7 D.O. Print and retain duplicate copy at desk if notifications not yet made; mark Supplementary
8 D.O. Enter notification into FORMS once obtained; file Supplementary AIDED REPORT
9 D.O. Enter detective’s name on AIDED REPORT for homicide victim
10 D.O. Ensure appropriate notifications are made once aided info is inputted into FORMS

Note: In all other cases, notifications will be made even though other authorities (hospital, etc.) indicate they have already made the notification.

P.G. 216-16: Suspicious Death or Suicide in Department of Correction Facility

Date Issued: 10/18/16 | Pages: 1

Purpose

To investigate suspicious deaths or suicides in Correction Department facilities.

Procedure — Desk Officer

Step Action
1 Notify:
1a Precinct detective squad (if unavailable, notify detective borough command)
1b Commanding officer/duty captain
1c Patrol borough
1d Internal Affairs Bureau (obtain log number)
1e Operations Unit

Exam Alert: IAB must be notified for DOC suspicious deaths/suicides — and you must obtain a log number.

Exam Alert: Uniformed members of the service assigned to duty in uniform WILL NOT respond to Correction facility.

Step Action
2 Direct preparation of AIDED REPORT; include under Details name/rank of DOC supervisor present and identity of person making death pronouncement
3 Have COMPLAINT REPORT (PD313-152) prepared; include IAB log number
4 Verify that Medical Examiner’s office has been notified
4a Enter time of notification, identity of person receiving call, and name of ME on AIDED REPORT
4b Make follow-up call if ME does not respond within one hour
4c Obtain ME case number for entry on AIDED REPORT
5 Record all calls to ME in Telephone Record
6 Notifications WILL NOT be made to relatives/friends of an inmate who dies in DOC facility, unless requested by Correction supervisor in charge
6a Indicate on AIDED REPORT that DOC will make notification, where applicable
7 Request permission of Medical Examiner prior to moving body

Exam Alert: Two key WILL NOT rules for DOC deaths: (1) Uniformed members will NOT respond to the facility, and (2) Notifications will NOT be made to family unless DOC requests it.

Key Forms

Form Number
AIDED REPORT (Electronic)
COMPLAINT REPORT PD313-152

Section 216 — Dead Human Body

P.G. 216-04: Dead Human Body General Procedure

Date Effective: 02/03/25 | Revision: R.O. 8 | Pages: 4

Purpose

To ensure that notifications to concerned authorities and relatives are properly made and recorded, evidence is preserved, and proper disposition is made of dead human bodies.

Procedure — Upon Arrival at Scene

Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 1-7)

Step Action
1 Request ambulance and patrol supervisor to respond
2 Exclude unauthorized persons from scene
3 Obtain names of witnesses and detain at scene if death is suspicious
4 Screen area from public view, if possible
5 Cover body with waterproof covering, if publicly exposed

See Also: P.G. 219-01 — The Patrol Supervisor’s car must have 4 waterproof body covers per P.G. 219-01. | 6 | Ascertain facts and notify desk officer as soon as possible | | 7 | Request aided be removed to hospital or doctor dispatched if pronouncement of death by ambulance attendant, EMT, or paramedic is questioned by anyone |

Exam Alert — EMS 90Z: FDNY EMS policy permits EMS personnel to depart a location of a deceased person prior to police arrival if: (1) a responsible adult is present, (2) no reasonable belief a crime was committed, and (3) the deceased is in a secure location. If EMS left prior to police arrival: Communications Section notifies D.O. by dept phone, Patrol Supervisor ensures assigned sector responds, and if sector has not responded after more than 1 hour after EMS left, notify C.O./X.O./Duty Capt. UMOS tells radio to have EMS come back if crime scene exists, integrity is an issue, or EMS left and no responsible adult is present per P.G. 216-04.

Communications Section (Step 8-9)

Step Action
8 Notify Medical Examiner or assistant ME
8a Obtain ME Case Number for entry on AIDED REPORT
8b Provide ME with: (1) officer’s name, command, form numbers; (2) place of occurrence; (3) decedent info (name, age, DOB, address); (4) family/next-of-kin info; (5) if plastic sheet/bag may be factor in death; (6) circumstances of death
8c Make follow-up call to ME if they fail to respond within 1 hour
8d Notify desk officer of actions taken
9 Notify desk officer, command of occurrence, via department phone if EMS departed location prior to police arrival

Exam Alert: The 1-hour follow-up to the Medical Examiner is a critical testable point.

Patrol Supervisor (Step 10)

Step Action
10 Ensure assigned sector responds to location if EMS departed prior to police arrival
10a Notify CO/XO/duty captain if sector has not responded after more than one hour has passed after EMS departed

Uniformed Member of the Service (Step 11)

Step Action
11 Notify Communications Section to have EMS return if questions arise regarding: (a) existence of a crime scene, (b) integrity of a crime scene, or (c) EMS departure and no responsible adult is present
11(1) Notify Operations Unit for FDNY EMS Operations Center phone number if not present in ICAD

Desk Officer (Steps 12-13)

Step Action
12 Notify: (a) detective squad
12b Harbor Unit, if aided is removed from navigable waters by other than Harbor Unit personnel
12c Operations Unit, if apparent homicide, suicide, or suspicious death
12d Communications Section, if death is unusual or homicide
13 Record all calls to ME in Telephone Record

Exam Alert: The responding detective (not the desk officer) will notify Crime Scene Unit if an apparent homicide, suicide, or suspicious death. However, the Patrol Supervisor (P/S) notifies CSU, and PDS notifies if the P/S is unavailable per P.G. 212-04. Crime Scene will be called by PDS if it is an apparent homicide, suicide, or suspicious death per P.G. 216-04.

Exam Alert: Desk Officer notifications for dead body: (1) PDS ALWAYS for crime scene, (2) If Homicide/Suicide/Suspicious — notify Operations, (3) If Homicide/Unusual — notify Communications, (4) If floater pulled from navigable waters by other than Harbor — notify Harbor Unit. The D.O. records all calls to M.E. in the Telephone Record (D.O. no longer calls the M.E. directly — UMOS does that now) per P.G. 216-04.

Persons Who May Disturb Body

Exam Alert: This is one of the most frequently tested topics in this section.

The uniformed member will prevent anyone from disturbing body or its effects, EXCEPT:

Letter Authorized Person
a. Paramedic, E.M.T., or doctor (caution not to disturb evidence)
b. Medical Examiner or assistant
c. District Attorney or assistant
d. Members of the Detective Bureau, detective squad, or Crime Scene Unit
e. Member of Highway District assigned as collision technicians or collision investigators
f. Member of Emergency Service Unit, if body is on train tracks or in subway tunnel

Memory Aid — TED the M.D. from D.C.: Technician (Highway collision technician/investigator), ESU (subway tunnel/train tracks), Doctor/Paramedic/EMT, the Medical examiner (or assistant), District attorney (or assistant), Detective Bureau/squad, Crime Scene Unit. These are the ONLY persons who may disturb a dead body per P.G. 216-04. Caution: the civilians (Doctor, ME, DA) should be cautioned not to disturb evidence.

Sergeant Focus: Memorize all six categories. Note that Highway District members must be specifically assigned as collision technicians/investigators — not just any Highway member.

Identification Tag (Step 15-16)

Step Action
15 Prepare and attach IDENTIFICATION TAG (PD317-091) on body
16 Obtain receipt on lower half of IDENTIFICATION TAG for body when removed to morgue, and for body and death certificate if released to a funeral director or other authorized person

Exam Alert: An IDENTIFICATION TAG (95 Tag) will be placed on dead human bodies, parts of bodies, and human fetuses that are to be delivered to the morgue. If the morgue takes the body, UMOS keeps the Death Certificate and obtains the lower half of 95 Tag as receipt, then gives both to the D.O. If a funeral director takes the body, the funeral director takes the Death Certificate and obtains the lower half of 95 Tag as receipt for body AND Death Certificate. The D.O. puts the 95 Tag receipt for body and Death Certificate (if received) in the Property Receipt Book per P.G. 216-04.

Death Notification (Step 17)

A notification to a member of the immediate family of the deceased will be made: - Tactfully and in person, if possible - Efforts should be made to have a friend or neighbor present when making notification

Reports and Documentation (Steps 18-21)

Step Action
18 Prepare AIDED REPORT or Police Accident Report (MV104AN), including details of who removed body
19 Prepare COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A)
20 Safeguard body and effects until instructions from patrol supervisor

Prior Test: Correct answer on a recent test was that MOS should not search the body and effects until the Patrol Supervisor instructs them to do so per P.G. 216-04. | 21 | Deliver receipt for body and death certificate to desk officer |

Patrol Supervisor Responsibilities (Steps 22-23)

Step Action
22 Ascertain that detective squad, Crime Scene Unit, and Operations Unit have been notified, when necessary
23 Have body and premises searched in the presence of witnesses, when possible, if deceased resided alone or died at a location other than residence

Exam Alert: A police officer of either sex may search a dead body. Any gender can search a DOA — don’t be bashful per P.G. 216-04.

Sergeant Focus: The patrol supervisor must confer with the ME or assistant ME prior to directing a member to search the body to safeguard property. The P/S also examines UMOS Digital A/L and certifies accuracy by signing rank and name, and makes their own Digital Activity Log entry per P.G. 216-04.

Desk Officer Responsibilities (Steps 27-31)

Step Action
27 Direct command clerk to prepare COMPLAINT REPORT from worksheet via OMNIFORM System
28 Close complaint at precinct level when: deceased is properly identified, ME certifies death as natural, and relatives are present and notified

Exam Alert — TO CLOSE THE UF61 — NEED 3 THINGS: (1) DOA is identified, (2) M.E. states cause is natural, and (3) Relatives are present and notified. All three must be met per P.G. 216-04. | 29 | Direct officer who discovered body in a homicide case to report to the morgue at 0900 hours the next day to identify the body to two Medical Examiners | | 29a | Assign officer first on scene, or if not available, another UMOS who is NOT RDO | | 30 | If DOA remains unidentified OR no notification can be made, prepare MISSING/UNIDENTIFIED PERSONS REPORT and a new UF 61 number; comply with P.G. 216-03 | | 31 | File receipt for body and death certificate in the Property Receipt Book |

Exam Alert: If a person dies as the direct result of a police action, the uniformed member involved in or actually witnessing the incident WILL NOT be assigned to identify the body at the morgue. Another member who can make the identification will be assigned.

Additional Data

  • A body offensive to public decency may be moved from a public place. However, if death is suspicious, permission of both the M.E. AND the assigned detective must be obtained before moving.
  • All notifications and preparation of Department reports are still required even if a body is removed by OCME or to UMOS on scene.

Exam Alert — Notifying Operations: Don’t wait for the whole story — make the call with preliminary facts for all unusual incidents including homicide and/or suspicious death. Make subsequent calls as more info comes in per P.G. 216-04.

Exam Alert — DOA as Direct Result of Police Action: The UMOS involved in or who actually witnessed the incident WILL NOT be assigned to identify the body at the morgue. DON’T use UMOS involved or who witnessed per P.G. 216-04.

Exam Alert — Civilian Identifier: The D.O. may direct the identifying UMOS to arrange for a relative or other person who knew the deceased to go to the morgue to ID the body in their presence per P.G. 216-04.

Exam Alert — Medical Examiner Identification: UMOS will ID body to 2 M.E.s at the morgue per P.G. 216-04.

Key Forms

Form Number
AIDED REPORT (Electronic)
IDENTIFICATION TAG PD317-091
COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET PD313-152A
COMPLAINT REPORT PD313-152
MISSING/UNIDENTIFIED PERSONS REPORT PD336-151
Police Accident Report MV104AN

P.G. 216-05

(Title and text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

P.G. 216-06

(Title and text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

Section 216 — General Procedure

P.G. 216-01: Aided Cases General Procedure

Date Effective: 09/29/25 | Revision: R.O. 88 | Pages: 4

Purpose

To render necessary aid, take corrective or summary action, and prepare the prescribed forms.

Key Definitions

AIDED CASE — Any occurrence coming to the attention of a uniformed member of the service which requires that a person, OTHER THAN A PRISONER, receive medical aid or assistance because such person is:

Category Description
a. Sick or injured (except vehicle or bicycle collision)
b. Dead (except vehicle or bicycle collision)
c. Lost person
d. Mentally ill
e. Abandoned, destitute, abused, or neglected child
f. Runaway child
g. Adult/child requiring care due to arrest, hospitalization, or death of parent/guardian
h. Homeless individual

Memory Aid — CHILD-ERS: Child (runaway/abandoned/destitute/abused/neglected), Homeless, Injured, Lost, DOA, EDP, Requires care (adult/child due to arrest/97H/death of parent/guardian), Sick. This mnemonic covers all eight aided case categories per P.G. 216-01.

Exam Alert: An aided case specifically excludes prisoners (use Medical Treatment of Prisoner form instead) and NEVER includes vehicle or bicycle collisions (use a PAR instead). Per P.G. 216-01.

Exam Alert: If from homeless Outreach efforts, check Other and explain in details on the Aided Report per P.G. 216-01.

ROUTINE SICK AT HOME — Requires ONLY a digital Activity Log entry if the aided is at any residence AND: - Is conscious and properly identified - No other police service or notification is required - No dependent adults or uncared-for children are in household - No other investigation is needed

Memory Aid — I C no P.A.I.N.: Identified and Conscious, no Police service needed, no dependent Adults or uncared-for children, no Investigation needed, no Notifications required. If all conditions are met = Routine Sick at Home = Activity Log entry only, NO Aided Report per P.G. 216-01.

Exam Alert: All four conditions must be met for Routine Sick at Home — if any one fails, a full AIDED REPORT is required. Note the aided must be at any residence (not just their own).

Prior Test: Past test had an old woman in a nursing home burnt by her morning coffee. The correct answer is that it requires a response and Aided Report because it is an injury, not a routine sick case per P.G. 216-01.

ROUTINE SICK IN NURSING HOMES — Uniformed members WILL NOT respond to routine sick cases in nursing homes. No response and NO Aided Report. However, for anything other than routine sick (injury, cardiac, D.O.A., etc.), a uniformed member will respond, prepare an AIDED REPORT, and make necessary notifications.

Exam Alert: Two situations that produce NO Aided Report: (1) Routine Sick at Home (Activity Log only), and (2) Routine Sick in Nursing Home (no response at all). If it is an injury, cardiac, or DOA at a nursing home, treat it as a regular aided case per P.G. 216-01.

Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

Step Action
1 Render reasonable aid to sick or injured person
1a Refer individual to the NYC 9-8-8 hotline who seeks mental health support, is not in need of immediate medical attention, and is not a danger to themselves or others
2 Request an ambulance or doctor, if necessary
2a If not assigned to patrol, request dispatcher to assign patrol unit concerned
2b If aided person is wearing a Medic Alert Emblem (diabetes, heart disease, etc.), notify radio dispatcher and bring to the attention of ambulance attendant. Do not remove Emblem
3 Wait in view to direct the ambulance or have a responsible person do so
4 Make a second call in twenty (20) minutes if ambulance does not arrive
5 Make digital Activity Log entry (include Medic Alert notification name; include ACR/PCR number)
6 Accompany unconscious or unidentified aided to hospital in body of ambulance (see 3 UNs below)

Exam Alert — The 3 UNs: Ride in the back of the ambulance with the aided when any of these apply: (1) UN-identified, (2) UN-conscious, (3) UN-sound mind (EDP per P.G. 221-13). Per P.G. 216-01. | 6a | Witness search by hospital authorities and sign hospital property record as witness | | 6b | Examine property removed in attempt to determine identity of aided | | 6c | Record in digital Activity Log a list of property removed and identity of person conducting search | | 7 | Obtain name, address, and telephone number of relative or friend for notification | | 8 | Prepare AIDED REPORT |

Exam Alert: If the aided has been removed from the scene prior to MOS arrival (10-54 cannot be given back as 10-90Z), the member will find out where the aided was removed to, obtain all information necessary for the AIDED REPORT, and prepare it per P.G. 216-01.

Exam Alert: Medic Alert Emblems — DO NOT REMOVE the emblem. Notify both the radio dispatcher AND the ambulance attendant. Document in the Digital Activity Log who was informed of the Medic Alert. On the Aided Report, note in Details who was notified. For prisoners (P.G. 210), MOS does NOT enter the Medic Alert on the Medical Treatment of Prisoner form per P.G. 216-01.

All Aided Cases

Step Role Action
9 MOS Have relatives/friends notified if aided is admitted to a hospital or dies
10 MOS Classify aided Unidentified if unable to make identification; inform desk officer
11 MOS Submit AIDED REPORT to desk officer
12 Desk Officer Ascertain that notification has been made or comply with P.G. 216-03, Unidentified Persons
13 Desk Officer Review AIDED REPORT for accuracy and completeness
14 Desk Officer Process AIDED REPORT in accordance with P.G. 216-02, Preparation of Aided Report
15 Desk Officer Notify NYC HRA Adult Protective Services to obtain care for a dependent adult due to arrest, hospitalization, or death of a guardian

Sergeant Focus: In cases where a death has occurred or the person is seriously injured and likely to die, the desk officer will ensure that notification is made in a timely manner and document the time, date, and name of the member making notification under caption Notification on the AIDED REPORT.

Additional Data — Critical Rules

Medical Authority Transfer: - When an aided is placed in care of ambulance or medical personnel at a hospital, the authority and responsibility for medical care rests solely with hospital personnel.

Unconscious Person in Manhole: - Request Emergency Service Unit immediately — ALWAYS call ESU whether going down or not - Member WILL NOT DESCEND into the manhole unless equipped with a Scott Air Pack (self-contained oxygen breathing apparatus) — NOT a filter mask, NOT PPE given by the Department - May descend only if: (1) person is unconscious, (2) atmosphere is believed safe, (3) rope tied securely around body, and (4) a capable person holds the free end outside the manhole

Exam Alert: Two conditions must be distinguished for manhole descent per P.G. 216-01: The Scott Air Pack is required due to toxic gases/no oxygen. If no Scott Air Pack but atmosphere appears safe, member must still use a rope and have someone hold it.

Difference of Opinion with EMS: - If not life-threatening and there is a dispute between aided/family and EMS regarding treatment/removal, IMMEDIATELY request patrol supervisor and EMS supervisor - If patrol supervisor unavailable, Communications Section dispatcher will automatically direct adjoining command patrol supervisor

Sergeant Focus: The patrol supervisor will immediately respond, ascertain facts, and attempt resolution. If unresolved, make a second request for an EMS supervisor.

Domestic Violence Aided: - Ensure photographs are taken of visible injuries per P.G. 208-39

NYC 9-8-8 Hotline: - Free DOHMH treatment referral program for counseling assistance (crisis intervention, substance/alcohol abuse, mental health) - Available 24/7 by calling 9-8-8, texting 9-8-8, or live chat - Services available in more than 200 languages - Accessible via Department smartphone app - NOT an alternative means for handling EDPs who pose a danger to themselves or others

Exam Alert: 9-8-8 is for voluntary mental health support only. If the person is a danger to self or others, comply with P.G. 221-13, Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons. The 9-8-8 program is NOT an alternative for dealing with an EDP who poses a danger. Per P.G. 216-01.

Exam Alert: The 9-8-8 hotline is a free NYC Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH) treatment referral program. Available 24/7 in more than 200 languages. MOS may access the 9-8-8 app on the Department smartphone. Reached by calling, texting 9-8-8, or live chatting via nyc988.cityofnewyork.us per P.G. 216-01.

Crime Victim Compensation (Domestic Violence and Aided Cases): - MOS will notify innocent victims or dependents/surviving relatives of Crime Victim Compensation - Additional info/applications are available at any Pct/PSA/TD - For domestic violence aided cases, take pictures of visible injuries or property damage per P.G. 208-39

Desk Officer — Guardian Responsibilities: - If the guardian of a dependent adult is arrested, DOA, or hospitalized, the Desk Officer will notify Human Resources Administration Adult Protective Services to obtain care per P.G. 216-01

Drug Overdose / Cocaine Psychosis: - MOS: Request ambulance AND patrol supervisor - If ambulance not immediately available, supervisor determines if aided should be removed to hospital via RMP or await ambulance - Aided is NOT to be brought to a police facility — NEVER bring an aided OD to the station house - Cocaine psychosis may cause paranoia, visual/auditory/tactile hallucinations, high body temperature, and seizures - Sudden death may ensue — bring to hospital, NOT police facility

Sergeant Focus: The patrol supervisor decides whether to transport via RMP or wait for ambulance. Either way, destination is the hospital — never a police facility. The Sgt. can decide to use the RMP or await the bus per P.G. 216-01.

Volunteer Medical Professional: - Only an E.D.P. can volunteer — that is, an EMT, Doctor, or Paramedic - Allow treatment if the member reasonably believes the volunteer is a professional - Treatment should occur under MOS observation (if possible) - Once situation is under control, ask for ID (name and address) - Document volunteer’s identity in Digital Activity Log and in Details section of the AIDED REPORT - Volunteer’s role is limited to medical assistance ONLY — the volunteer does NOT dictate method of transport. Neither does NYPD. EMS decides transportation

Exam Alert: Do NOT say NURSE — nurses are not included. Only EMT, Doctor, or Paramedic may volunteer. Per P.G. 216-01.

See Also: P.G. 303-15 — Crime Prevention Officer notifies victims under 60 of Crime Victims Compensation within 5 days of receipt of the Complaint Report (UF 61). In absence of the CPO, the Operations Coordinator ensures it gets done.

P.G. 216-02: Preparation of Aided Report

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure covers the detailed steps for completing and processing the AIDED REPORT form, including required fields, distribution, and electronic submission via FORMS.

P.G. 216-03: Unidentified Persons

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure addresses the handling of unidentified aided persons, including preparation of the MISSING/UNIDENTIFIED PERSONS REPORT (PD336-151) and entry into the OMNIFORM System.

Key Forms

Form Number
AIDED REPORT (Electronic)
MISSING/UNIDENTIFIED PERSONS REPORT PD336-151

Section 216 — Medical Programs and Specialized Removals

P.G. 216-17: Child Window Fall Report

(Text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

This procedure covers reporting requirements when a child falls from a window.

P.G. 216-18

(Title and text unavailable in source PDF — review original Patrol Guide)

P.G. 216-19: Public Access Defibrillation Program

Date Effective: 11/18/21 | Revision: I.O. 104 | Pages: 3

Purpose

To account for and properly document the use of Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) in assigned commands, and to provide for training and certification of uniformed, civilian, and auxiliary members of the service in their use.

Key Definitions

AUTOMATED EXTERNAL DEFIBRILLATOR (AED) — An electronic device used to defibrillate the heart by applying an electric shock to it.

UTILIZATION OF AED — Once an AED is placed on an aided, the Department considers that as utilized, even if a shock is not administered. When the AED pads are placed on an aided, the machine will perform an analysis and advise what actions should be taken.

Exam Alert: Utilized means pads are placed on the patient — a shock does NOT need to be delivered for it to count as utilized. Once AED pads are placed on aided, whether you shock or not, it is considered USED per P.G. 216-19.

CARDIO PULMONARY RESUSCITATION (CPR) ADMINISTERED — The Department considers CPR administered once a member performs rapid chest compressions OR mouth-to-mouth ventilation on an aided. Either one counts as CPR per P.G. 216-19.

AED Kit Contents

Exam Alert: Memorize the kit contents — this is frequently tested.

Item Quantity
Operational AED device 1
Electrode pads with wire leads (sealed packages, each containing two pads) 2 packages
Pocket mask 1
Gloves 1 pair
Cloth/gauze compress 1
Razor 1

Procedure — Desk Officer (Steps 1-6)

Step Action
1 Account for all AED kits assigned to command at the beginning of each tour
2 Assign an AED at start of each tour to a member trained and certified as AED qualified
3 Notify radio dispatcher of the unit(s) assigned an AED kit
4 Make a Command Log entry accounting for all AED kits and notification to radio dispatcher
4a Note assignment of AED kits by their serial numbers, with prefix AED (e.g., AED 1234)
5 Ensure assignment of AED kits are noted on the roll call
6 Secure AED kits not in use in appropriate location (e.g., radio room, property room)

Exam Alert: AED serial numbers are prefixed with AED (e.g., AED 1234) in the Command Log. Ensure assignment is also documented on the Roll Call per P.G. 216-19.

Sergeant Focus: AED accountability happens every tour — the desk officer must account for all kits, assign them, notify dispatch, and make Command Log entries.

Member of the Service — Kit Inspection (Steps 7-8)

Step Action
7 Inspect AED kit to ensure all items present
8 Notify desk officer of any deficiencies/missing equipment; make digital Activity Log entries

Upon Arriving at Scene of Cardiac Aided Case (Steps 9-13)

Step Action
9 Advise radio dispatcher of aided’s condition and request EMS response
10 Utilize AED in compliance with Department guidelines/training
10a AED will only be attached to an unconscious adult patient after confirming: not breathing and no pulse
10b Provide care until relieved by advanced-level emergency medical personnel
11 Brief responding EMS crew of: (a) condition when found, (b) nature of care provided (rescue breathing, CPR, defibrillation, etc.)
12 Prepare AIDED REPORT; note in Details if AED utilized or CPR administered and results achieved; include AED serial number if utilized
12b If CPR administered, check appropriate caption on AIDED REPORT
13 Submit AIDED REPORT immediately to desk officer after returning to command

Exam Alert: AED is only for unconscious adults with no breathing and no pulse. All three conditions must be confirmed.

Prisoner AED/CPR Use

Exam Alert: If AED/CPR is administered on a prisoner in custody, a MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150) form will be completed instead of an AIDED REPORT. This is the same distinction as Medic Alert emblems on prisoners per P.G. 216-19.

Desk Officer — Post-Use (Steps 14-15)

Step Action
14 Telephone ESU desk to obtain an AED/CPR Log number; enter on AIDED REPORT or MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER form
14a Forward copy of completed form to ESU-AED Coordinator via fax (to one person’s attention) and Department mail to ESU, Attention: EMT School (mailed to another)
14b Ensure AED is secured until delivery and is not reassigned
15 Schedule appointment; ensure AED is delivered to ESU-AED Coordinator for downloading of information after each use

Training — BLASTT Course

Exam Alert: AED training is a one-day course at the Police Academy called BLASTT — Basic Life Support and Trauma Treatment CPR-AED-Naloxone-BTK.

  • The commanding officer designates the command’s training sergeant as the AED liaison
  • The command training sergeant schedules members via T.A.C.T.I.C.S. (Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integrated Collection System)

Loss or Theft

If an AED kit or its contents (except cloth/gauze compress and plastic gloves) are lost or stolen, comply with P.G. 219-20, Loss or Theft of Department Property.

Exam Alert: Replacement supplies = call ESU AED coordinator. Questions about training = call the Police Academy. Someone wants to donate an AED = confer with ESU per P.G. 216-19.

See Also: P.G. 305-08 — If trained in opioid antagonist kit, it is mandatory to carry it per P.G. 216-19.

Key Forms

Form Number
AIDED REPORT (Electronic)
MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER PD244-150

P.G. 216-20: Handling Shelter Clients with Tuberculosis

Date Effective: 11/18/21 | Revision: I.O. 99 | Pages: 2

Purpose

To assist the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene in the removal of recalcitrant patients sheltered in Human Resources Administration facilities, who have tuberculosis and are refusing treatment.

Procedure

Operations Unit Personnel (Steps 1-2)

Step Action
1 Upon notification by DOHMH that a removal and detention order has been issued: (a) ascertain location of removal, (b) enter in Command Log the name of DOHMH personnel and pedigree information of subject
2 Notify Communications Section to assign a patrol supervisor, an RMP unit, and ESU to respond

Patrol Supervisor (Steps 3-6)

Step Action
3 Respond to scene and assess the situation
4 Determine if services of ESU are necessary; cancel ESU if not required
5 Confirm that the Removal and Detention Order is possessed by the DOHMH physician/peace officer at the shelter site
6 Assign uniformed member to accompany subject and DOHMH physician/peace officer in DOHMH transportation vehicle to the pre-designated hospital

Sergeant Focus: The patrol supervisor must confirm the Removal and Detention Order before proceeding. This is a legal document that authorizes the removal.

Department’s Role — Critical Limitations

Exam Alert: The Department’s primary responsibility is to assist in the arrest and preserve the peace. Members of the service are:

Allowed NOT Allowed
Use physical force to restrain subject until hospital delivery (only extent necessary) Personally assist in forcing entry to premises
Accompany patient to hospital Safeguard property at location
Wear protective equipment Secure premises afterward

Exam Alert: The Department does NOT force entry, safeguard property, or secure premises — those duties are performed by other agencies at the direction of the DOHMH physician/peace officer.

Protective Equipment

Responding members will take appropriate precautions including wearing plastic gloves and facial masks. Facial masks will be provided at the shelter site by DOHMH.

Transportation

  • DOHMH provides the vehicle
  • If HRA cannot provide a vehicle, an ambulance will be requested through Communications
  • Except in extreme cases, Department vehicles will NOT be used for transportation
  • Transport only to the designated HHC facility (even if subject is arrested for other conduct)

Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 7-10)

Step Action
7 Accompany patient along with DOHMH physician/peace officer to pre-designated hospital
8 Enter details of removal and transport in digital Activity Log
9 Ensure DOHMH physician/peace officer name and patient name are recorded in Activity Log and Department forms
10 Comply with A.G. 330-09, Exposure of Members of the Service to Infectious Diseases or Hazardous Materials

If Patient Becomes Violent or Barricades

Comply with P.G. 221-13, Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons and P.G. 221-14, Hostage/Barricaded Person(s), as appropriate.

P.G. 216-21: Response to Carbon Monoxide Alarms

Date Issued: 10/18/16 | Pages: 2

Key Definition

CARBON MONOXIDE — A colorless and odorless gas that interferes with the delivery of oxygen to the cells of the body.

Signs and Symptoms
Headache
Nausea
Vomiting
Confusion and disorientation
Cherry red skin (severe cases)

Carbon monoxide is created by incomplete combustion of burning fuels (gasoline, oil, wood, paper).

Exam Alert: CO is colorless and odorless. Cherry red skin indicates a severe case.

Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service (Steps 1-8)

Step Action
1 Promptly respond; identify type of alarm (residential, central station monitored, EMS call)
2 Interview persons with information regarding location of alarm
3 Refrain from entering location
4 Request response of patrol supervisor, if alarm confirmed
5 Establish frozen area
6 Render reasonable aid to victims, if present
7 Request EMS to respond if necessary
8 Observe affected individuals for signs/symptoms of CO poisoning; direct them to EMS for treatment

Exam Alert: Members DO NOT enter the location. This is critical — CO is invisible and can incapacitate quickly.

Patrol Supervisor (Steps 9-11)

Step Action
9 Respond to location
10 Evaluate situation upon arrival
11 Maintain frozen area and extend if necessary, until hazard is removed

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisors should be guided by P.G. 212-37, Hazardous Materials, when responding to CO alarms and setting up frozen areas.

Patrol Supervisor — Continued (Steps 12-16)

Step Action
12 Establish crime scene, if necessary
13 Request response of ESU personnel
14 Prevent all non-ESU police personnel from entering location
15 Notify duty captain and precinct desk officer
16 Direct Communications to notify Department of Buildings if location is City-owned

Emergency Service Unit Member (Steps 17-19)

Step Action
17 Respond to location and determine whether CO gases are present
18 Advise patrol supervisor on appropriate frozen area boundaries
19 Determine whether location is safe for re-entry

Exam Alert: Only ESU determines whether the location is safe for re-entry. Not the patrol supervisor, not FDNY — ESU.

After ESU Assessment (Steps 20-25)

Step Role Action
20 MOS Remain outside location until ESU deems location habitable
21 MOS Assist EMS with removal of aided(s) if location properly vented and ESU advises safe to enter
22 MOS Comply with P.G. 216-01, Aided Cases General Procedure
23 P.S. Direct preparation of AIDED REPORT and COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET (PD313-152A), if appropriate
24 P.S. Confer with ESU to determine if location is safe for occupants to re-enter prior to allowing re-entry
25 P.S. Comply with P.G. 212-37, Hazardous Materials, if a continuing CO threat exists

Key Forms

Form Number
AIDED REPORT (Electronic)
COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET PD313-152A

P.G. 216-22: Mobile Crisis Outreach Teams and Assertive Community Treatment Teams

Date Effective: 06/11/24 | Revision: R.O. 39 | Pages: 2

Purpose

To safeguard and remove mentally ill or emotionally disturbed persons at the direction of Mobile Crisis Outreach or Assertive Community Treatment Teams pursuant to section 9.58 of the New York State Mental Hygiene Law.

Key Definitions

MOBILE CRISIS OUTREACH TEAMS — Independently operated by local hospitals or community-based mental health organizations; usually funded by DOHMH; may be attached to NYS-licensed comprehensive emergency programs. Goal: assess, stabilize, and rapidly refer patients to longer-term providers.

ASSERTIVE COMMUNITY TREATMENT TEAMS (ACT) — Similar to Mobile Crisis Outreach Teams but are hospitals on wheels, more richly staffed by mental health workers. Provide a wide range of ongoing services. May order involuntary transport of patients to hospital ERs. Receive contract funding from DOHMH and are licensed by the NYS Office of Mental Health.

QUALIFIED MENTAL HEALTH PROFESSIONAL (QMHP) — Members of a Mobile Crisis Outreach or ACT Team who are:

QMHP Eligible Professionals
Licensed psychologists
Certified social workers
Registered professional nurses

Exam Alert: Only these three types of professionals qualify as QMHPs eligible to direct a removal under Section 9.58.

Procedure — Uniformed Member of the Service

Step Action
1 Comply with P.G. 221-13, Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons, as required
2 Request the physician or QMHP on scene to present section 9.58 identification card along with signed and completed OMH 482 form (Mobile Crisis Outreach Team Authorization for Transport)
2a Record pertinent information in digital Activity Log and AIDED REPORT, including identity of physician/QMHP ordering removal and length of time aided was in police custody
3 Inform communications dispatcher that the removal is involuntary, when appropriate
4 Prepare AIDED REPORT and include:
4a Upload photographs of section 9.58 identification card and OMH 482 form to AIDED REPORT
4b Note that individual was removed pursuant to section 9.58 of NYS Mental Hygiene Law
4c Name of QMHP who directed the removal and the mobile crisis team name

Exam Alert: Two documents must be presented: (1) Section 9.58 ID card and (2) OMH 482 form. Both must be photographed and uploaded to the AIDED REPORT.

AIDED REPORT Coding
